reading_mnemonic: Oh! Don't forget your <reading>hats</reading> (はつ) either. Wouldn’t want to start a <kanji>departure</kanji> without hats or a lantern. That'd be a short journey.
meaning_hint: Feel the heat from the lantern. It's not quite as comforting as being inside a tent, but it's better than nothing. The lantern will light your way on your departure.
reading_hint: How cold and unprotected would your head be without a hat? Very. Taking hats along with departures is integral to their success. Imagine how unsafe you would feel with no hats. That's no departure I want to set out on.

meaning_mnemonic: You need to look a <radical>tree</radical> in the <radical>eye</radical> to gain a <kanji>mutual</kanji> respect for each other. Stand up straight, look the tree directly in its eye(s). If it looks right back, you know things are mutual.
reading_mnemonic: If your respect for the tree is <kanji>mutual</kanji> with the respect the tree feels for you, you'll be able to see the tree's <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Right now its soul looks pretty sad, like it's trying to say, "Why are your people cutting down all my friends? I thought we had a mutual understanding."
meaning_hint: It's the same with humans, you know? If you want to have a mutual relationship you need to be able to look each other in the eyes. If you're too embarrassed, or you look down on them, then that isn't mutual respect!
reading_hint: This is basically a FernGully moment, you know? You can feel the tree's pain like it's your pain. It's that kind of mutual sharing of souls you need.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>eye</radical> of a <radical>lion</radical>, still <radical>small</radical> and curious, gazes upon the <kanji>prefecture</kanji> that will one day become his. He's a small lion, but once he grows up, everything reflected in his eye will be his prefecture to rule over.
reading_mnemonic: This small lion's <kanji>prefecture</kanji> is being protected by <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai, because the lion is too small to protect all of this land himself. Ken the samurai is used to wandering far and wide, so one prefecture is nothing to him.
meaning_hint: Imagine the small lion casting his eye across his future prefecture. His small eye is twinkling, because he's proud that he'll one day be prefect, and this prefecture will be his.
reading_hint: Picture Ken protecting the prefecture, punching hyenas in the face, and yelling, "This may just be a prefecture, not a country, but I'll give it my all for my lion prefect-to-be!"

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>sheep</radical> that's <radical>big</radical> is a true <kanji>beauty</kanji>. Big is <kanji>beautiful</kanji>, and that's all the more true for sheep.
reading_mnemonic: The big sheep's <kanji>beauty</kanji> has attracted a swarm of <reading>bee</reading>s (び). The sheep is so beautiful that the bees have mistaken it for a flower.
meaning_hint:
Imagine a great big beautiful sheep. See the sheep's beauty.

This sheep is so big and fluffy that you can't see its tail, which is why it looks bit different in this kanji.
reading_hint: The bees are swarming around the sheep, marvelling at its beauty and trying to figure out how to get nectar from it.

meaning_mnemonic: When you're in <radical>prison</radical>, whoever ends up with the <radical>shellfish</radical> will <kanji>lose</kanji>. It's the game all prisoners play. It's like keep away from the shellfish. And if you end up with the shellfish BZZZZTT that's it, you're the loser.
reading_mnemonic: If you <kanji>lose</kanji>, you're a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). All you had to do was not hold the prison shellfish. It really shouldn't have been that hard to not lose, ya fool.
meaning_hint: Imagine that it's your first day in prison. You're walking around the yard, minding your own business, when suddenly a shellfish smacks you right in the head and falls into your open hands. "HAHA YOU LOSE!" someone shouts.
reading_hint: Now you have to try to make some other fool lose. Otherwise you'll be the loser fool forever.

meaning_mnemonic: On its <radical>scooter</radical>, a creature with <radical>horns</radical> from <radical>heaven</radical> will take things you want to <kanji>send</kanji> there.
reading_mnemonic: You want to <kanji>send</kanji> your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) to heaven. I mean, you don't want it to go to some of those other places you've heard of, so why not send your soul?
meaning_hint: Picture the horned thing on its scooter, a big messenger bag over its shoulder with the kanji for heaven on it. "You got somethin' you want me to send?" it mutters.
reading_hint: You put your soul into an envelope, stick a stamp on it, and hand it off to be sent to heaven. Bye-bye! I hope this doesn't have any negative effects on your body!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>heavy</radical> radical and the <kanji>heavy</kanji> kanji are the same.
reading_mnemonic: In your hand is a very <kanji>heavy</kanji> <reading>jew</reading>el (じゅう). Real jewels are always heavier than they look.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Imagine holding a real jewel in your right hand and a fake jewel in your left. The one in your right hand is suuuuupeerrr heavy!

meaning_mnemonic: You  <radical>say</radical> things around a  <radical>fire</radical>. In fact there are two  <radical>fire</radical>s, and you're <kanji>talk</kanji>ing with a group of people, all sitting around two nice campfires.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>talk</kanji> in this intense, fiery way all the way until <reading>dawn</reading> (だん), but no longer! Once dawn comes the discussion needs to be concluded.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself next to the two campfires, having a heart-to-heart talk. What are you saying?
reading_hint: If you try to talk all night and past dawn, you'll burn out. Take the day, think, then tonight you can try to have an all-night talk again.

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>helicopter</radical> is piloted by a <radical>woman</radical>, it stirs an insatiable <kanji>need</kanji> in her. She doesn't know what it is. But she needs it. And that's why she's flying around looking for it!
reading_mnemonic: Finally she knows what she <kanji>need</kanji>s! It's <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう)! Delicious, creamy yogurt!
meaning_hint: Picture the woman in the helicopter needing something. She's looking around. Searching. But she doesn't know what it is!
reading_hint: What does she need? Yogurt!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> grants a <radical>gladiator</radical> its <radical>power</radical> so he will <kanji>win</kanji>! <kanji>Victory</kanji> will be his!
reading_mnemonic: He <kanji>win</kanji>s over and over again, and soon the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) is even rooting for him. In fact, the Shougun claims him as his personal gladiator of choice.
meaning_hint: Watch as the moon's power fills the gladiator. He starts to glow, grows larger, and has some craters on his face now. Well.. okay.
reading_hint: The Shougun is only interested in people who win. But you probably could have guessed that already.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> is very <radical>anti</radical>-everything, making it all <kanji>temporary</kanji>. You get a new shirt, "I'm anti shirts!" she yells, and out it goes.
reading_mnemonic: The leader's most <kanji>temporary</kanji> possession is her <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Or... cars, because they're all very temporary. She buys one, drives it around, and then suddenly she's anti red car. She needs a blue one!
meaning_hint: She tires of things so quickly, you hardly have time to get used to them before they're gone.
reading_hint: Picture all of the temporary cars she's tried filling up an entire junkyard. They're all a little different, but in the end they were all just temporary fads.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Run</radical> into <radical>oneself</radical> in a dream and you'll definitely <kanji>wake up</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: If one runs into oneself and you don't <kanji>wake up</kanji>, just look down at your waist. If you're wearing an <reading>o</reading>bi (お) you know it's a dream and you'll eventually wake up. If you aren't wearing an obi then it isn't a dream and you don't have anything to wake up from.
meaning_hint: If you run into yourself, it really messes you up, you know? You won't be able to handle what you're seeing so you have to wake up.
reading_hint: Your obi is like a special object you only have in dreams. If you have your obi, it's okay, it's a dream and you can wake up now! Make sure you memorize exactly how it feels so dream-you can't trick you or something.

meaning_mnemonic: You jump on your <radical>scooter</radical> with a big <radical>bundle</radical> to go as <kanji>fast</kanji> as you can! You have a bundle of pizzas in here, and they need to be delivered as fast as you can get them there!
reading_mnemonic: You were going so <kanji>fast</kanji>, you only put on <reading>sock</reading>s (そく). So now you're barreling down the street at top speeds in just your sad, white socks.
meaning_hint: Imagine how fast you can go on your scooter holding this bundle. It's way faster than trying to go on foot, that's for sure!
reading_hint: Imagine riding the scooter as fast as you can in just your socks. You can feel the wind pulling them off your feet!

meaning_mnemonic: You don't want to keep all the <radical>alcohol</radical> to yourself (<radical>oneself</radical>), so you <kanji>distribute</kanji> it to all of your friends. Heck, not just your friends, to everyone you can find!
reading_mnemonic:
As you <kanji>distribute</kanji> the alcohol you say a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい):

Here you go my friends
Alcohol for everyone
Never drink alone
meaning_hint: Don't be that person who keeps the alcohol all to themselves. Distribute it to all of the legally-aged people you know!
reading_hint: This becomes the haiku you say before every party, every potluck, and every night out at the bar. You even get it tattoo'd to your arm.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> is an <radical>alcohol</radical>ic beverage containing a lot of <kanji>alcohol</kanji>. It’s 3 different kinds of rums, pineapple juice, and grenadine. It’ll hit you like a tsunami though. This alcoholic drink has a kick to it.
reading_mnemonic: As you lie on this beach, the <kanji>alcohol</kanji> keeps coming down a <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ), straight into your mouth. You can feel the sand in your toes and the alcohol sliding down your throat from this special tsunami chute.
meaning_hint: It’s made with spiced rum, coconut rum, and dark rum. Imagine yourself on a sandy beach drinking this cocktail. Feel the sun on your face and the wind in your hair as you sip on this fruity and rummy alcoholic beverage known as a tsunami. You could even picture an actual tsunami in the water coming right for you. I wouldn’t recommend it though—actual tsunami are scary, not drinkable and delicious.
reading_hint: Picture the alcohol flowing down this long chute from somewhere far away. You try to see where it's coming from, but all you can see is this long, long chute stretching along the entire beach, until it disappears into the distance. You should probably be concerned that you're nonstop drinking alcohol from a random chute, but it tastes so good!

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>building</radical> has a <radical>roof</radical> and will teach you about the <radical>origin</radical> of many things. It's an <kanji>institution</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: What does <kanji>institution</kanji> start with? In (いん)!
meaning_hint: Institutions are places of learning, but they have to be a physical place with a roof and all that. If you're learning out in a field somewhere, that's probably not a proper institution.
reading_hint: Just remember, it's an IN-stitution, and you'll know this reading easy-peasy.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> and <radical>ice</radical> has brought this year to its <kanji>end</kanji>. Once you're in the long thread of winter and ice, you know the year will end soon.
reading_mnemonic: Whenever winter <kanji>end</kanji>s, you take the thread covered in ice, and lace it into your <reading>shoe</reading> (しゅう).
meaning_hint: Picture the season of winter as a long thread, embalmed in ice. At the end of it is the year’s end. The thread of winter and ice brings you closure, it marks the end.
reading_hint: This thread isn't just a metaphor, it's real! Feel the cold ice on it, as you lace it into your shoe. An age old tradition, the lacing of icy threads as winter ends. Your shoes will look so cool, or should I say… iced out.

meaning_mnemonic: You find some <radical>feathers</radical> that are so perfectly <radical>white</radical>, you want to <kanji>learn</kanji> more about where they came from and what they're made of.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>learn</kanji> that you should tie these white feathers to your <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). You do it before you learn what it will do, and then suddenly you're jump-flying out of the building.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself in a library poring through huge tomes on feathers, specifically for completely white ones. You want to learn everything you can!
reading_hint: Oh geez, oh no. You better learn how to control these shoes before you—CRASH!!! Welp, you just shot through a plane. You need to learn more before you act next time.

meaning_mnemonic: If your <radical>car</radical> runs into a <radical>cloud</radical> you'll <kanji>revolve</kanji> around it instead of going straight through. Clouds are really hard to see through, so even though you're trying not to, you revolve in a tight circle up and around the cloud anyway.
reading_mnemonic: You have to <kanji>revolve</kanji> around the cloud <reading>ten</reading> (てん) times before it disappears and you're able to go through.
meaning_hint: Imagine driving your car around and around the cloud, instead of going through it. At least it's soft and cool.
reading_hint: Unfortunately, not long after you get through that cloud another one comes down and gets in your way. Now you're counting each revolution, one... two... three... all the way up to ten.

meaning_mnemonic: You give your <radical>scooter</radical> to a <radical>turkey</radical> so that it can <kanji>advance</kanji>. The turkey was trying and failing to walk through a crowded street, so out of the goodness of your heart you gave it a scooter so it could advance.
reading_mnemonic: You wave as the turkey <kanji>advance</kanji>s into the street at top speeds, collides with a car, and scrapes up its <reading>shin</reading>s (しん) almost immediately. Ah!
meaning_hint: Imagine picking up the turkey and putting it on your scooter. Of course, you give it your helmet as well, safety first.
reading_hint: Silly turkey, you can't advance that fast, of course you'll hurt your shins. It's better to advance slowly, but I guess I couldn't expect you to know that.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> are knocked down by a violent <radical>tsunami</radical>, and swirl through the air to <radical>kiss</radical> your face gently as they <kanji>fall</kanji>. With the tsunami came a great windy storm that blew the flowers down. Amongst all the destruction, it's a pretty sight to watch them fall.
reading_mnemonic: You want to save these flowers that <kanji>fall</kanji> around you, so you take out a drying <reading>rack</reading> (らく) and place them carefully on it. Any flowers that fall will go on the rack like this, so can save them for later.
meaning_hint: Imagine being out in this tsunami-induced storm. You're wet, cold, and scared, but you're calmed and mesmerized by how the flowers dance in the wind before they finally fall.
reading_hint: Imagine running around catching the flowers as they fall. Imagine placing each fallen petal onto the rack to keep it safe and dry.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> bring beauty to the <radical>world</radical> of <radical>trees</radical>, but the <kanji>leaf</kanji> is what matters the most. <kanji>Leaves</kanji> are how flowers and trees get their nutrients from the sun!
reading_mnemonic: Picture a little flower working really hard to grow its first leaf. The <kanji>leaf</kanji> pops out and it shouts, "<reading>Ha</reading> (は)!"
meaning_hint: Leaves, no matter how little, are the most important thing in the world for them.
reading_hint: は is the sound of a leaf coming into existence. Whenever you see a plant from now on, hear the little "ha" sound it makes when each leaf pops out of its body.

meaning_mnemonic: For a <radical>car</radical>, a <radical>tombstone</radical> is <kanji>lightweight</kanji>. Cars are very strong, you see, so something heavy like a tombstone is no match for them. All cars find tombstones to be <kanji>light</kanji> and easy to transport.
reading_mnemonic: What do you wish you had to transport this tombstone? Oh, that’s right, a car. So you need some kind of <reading>car</reading> <reading>loo</reading>phole (かる). If you had a car, this tombstone moving business would be no problem. To cars, tombstones are naught but <kanji>lightweight</kanji> pebbles! But the rules say you can't use cars to carry tombstones, so you need to find a car loophole.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself trying to move a huge slab tombstone. It’s difficult, no? I bet you wish you had a car or some other vehicle to make the process much less painful. Your arms are getting sore from carrying the tombstone and you’re getting sweaty and hot from all the work. What you would do for a car with air conditioning.
reading_hint: Remember all that hard work you were doing before and how you got all sweaty and gross? Now imagine yourself inside of a nice car with air conditioning, and the tombstone in the trunk. It feels great doesn’t it? The air conditioning is perfect, and driving a car is much easier than carrying a tombstone around. Thank goodness you found a car loophole in the tombstone carrying rules.

meaning_mnemonic: While riding your <radical>scooter</radical>, you bang your <radical>forehead</radical> on the <radical>car</radical> in front. Your scooter's wrecked, so you have to <kanji>carry</kanji> it home. You'll need a lot of <kanji>luck</kanji> to get that scooter home safely.
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>carry</kanji> the scooter, your <kanji>luck</kanji> turns, and it starts to rain, so you pull out your <reading>um</reading>brella (うん). Now you're holding your scooter, your forehead, and an umbrella.
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to carry your scooter down a busy road, with all the cars whizzing past. Your forehead is still hurting from that nasty bang. Good luck!
reading_hint: It's hard enough to carry a scooter in heavy traffic, but now you have to try to keep yourself and it dry with an umbrella too.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>gate</radical> with a <radical>lantern</radical> is <kanji>open</kanji> for you. That's convenient, since it's pretty dark out and you might not have seen the open gate without the lantern's help.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>open</kanji> gate is in the water though, so you'll need a <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) to get in. Luckily, you've been carrying your kayak with you just in case.
meaning_hint: Imagine being a traveler, walking around the outside of a huge walled city. You really need to get inside but all of the gates are closed. Finally, you see a single, bright lantern marking the open gate! What a relief.
reading_hint: Imagine kayaking in the dark toward an open gate. Finally, you've made it!

meaning_mnemonic: You chase a <radical>turkey</radical> up a <radical>tree</radical> to <kanji>collect</kanji> it. At least, you will once you've <kanji>gather</kanji>ed enough turkeys into this one tree.
reading_mnemonic: Once you've <kanji>collect</kanji>ed enough turkeys, you're going to turn them into a <reading>shoe</reading> (しゅう). Maybe even two shoes, but that will mean <kanji>gather</kanji>ing even more turkeys!
meaning_hint: Collect another turkey, send it flying and gobbling up into the tree next to the other one.
reading_hint: Picture the nice, feathered shoes you'll be able to make once you've gathered all of these turkeys together. Ohoho, you can't wait! Go collect another turkey!

meaning_mnemonic: You can <radical>eat</radical> all you want, but you'll still <radical>lack</radical> something to <kanji>drink</kanji>. You can't live just from eating, you have to drink things too!
reading_mnemonic: You get desperate for a <kanji>drink</kanji> and put some <reading>no</reading>ri (の) seaweed in a blender, trying to create a drink of your own.
meaning_hint: Imagine eating and eating and lacking a delicious, cold, thirst-quenching drink. Your mouth is dry. You have to drink something, anything!
reading_hint: Luckily, this nori isn't dried and pressed like they usually are, and you manage to make a bright green nori drink. You gulp it down, and even though it tastes like the ocean, it hits the spot!

meaning_mnemonic: You have <radical>spikes</radical> growing out of a <radical>sheep</radical> with <radical>fins</radical>. Turn this thing into a <kanji>business</kanji>! You could totally charge people money to look at it. Sure, you could also donate it to science, but a business-minded person like you knows a business opportunity when you see one. Step right up! Just five dollars for a glance at the rare spiky mer-sheep!
reading_mnemonic: As you evolve your <kanji>business</kanji>, you let customers feed the sheep <reading>gyo</reading>za (ぎょう) dumplings, its favorite snack. For a nominal fee, of course. People love it when the mutant sheep eats gyoza out of their hands. Wouldn't you?
meaning_hint: Imagine all the money you'll rake in with this new business. People will come from far and wide to see the spiky mer-sheep sideshow. And for an extra charge you'll let 'em touch those pointy spikes and slimy fins. Oh yes, business will be booming.
reading_hint: Imagine the smell of garlicky gyoza filling the sideshow tent, as customers try to feed the sheep and compete for its attention. Business is so good that the sheep gets tired of gyoza and won't eat it anymore. Oh well… Skewer them on its spikes and save 'em for later.

meaning_mnemonic:
A <radical>tsunami</radical> hit ancient <radical>China</radical> and formed the <kanji>Chinese</kanji> country we know now as... <kanji>China</kanji>!

Sometimes this kanji also means <kanji>man</kanji>. In fact, it used to refer specifically to people of the Han dynasty, but over time evolved into a term for men in specific contexts.
reading_mnemonic: You peer closer at <kanji>China</kanji> and see one man, Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん), leading his troops into the vast country, ready to conquer all the Chinese people living in China!
meaning_hint: Picture the world way far away, from space. You see a giant tsunami carve out a massive chunk of land to the west of Japan on the main continent. Slowly the tsunami waters recede, leaving the outline of China.
reading_hint: Khan is only one man, but watch as he takes China for himself. A tiny little flag with a horse on it is raised in the middle, for all of the Chinese people to see. Hmm, this could be a problem.

meaning_mnemonic: If you've been walking all day, your <radical>foot</radical> is the last thing anyone would want to <radical>kiss</radical>, especially after wandering barefoot down a <kanji>road</kanji>. Your feet are filthy, sweaty, and stinky. Nobody is going to want to kiss those feet.
reading_mnemonic: What was this person walking down again? Oh, that's right – a <reading>ro</reading>ad (ろ). They were walking down a filthy road.
meaning_hint: Imagine kissing someone's foot. Now imagine kissing someone's foot after they've been walking down a road for hours. It's disgusting. Smell the stench of sweat wafting up into your nostrils as the foot approaches your lips. It's wretched.
reading_hint: Walking down a road barefoot like this is a sure way to dirty up your feet. Feel the dirt, rocks, and twigs under your feet. The road is hard and dry and is not making your journey very comfortable at all.

meaning_mnemonic: You blast your <radical>music</radical> loud so it causes a <radical>landslide</radical>, revealing the perfect soil for <kanji>farming</kanji>! Musically moved dirt is perfect for <kanji>agriculture</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The first person to come up with this <kanji>farming</kanji> method was <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう), the famous seer and prophecy man. He predicted humans of the future would use special "yell cubes" to "move that good earth."
meaning_hint: Imagine slowly turning up your big boombox until your ears hurt and the ground beneath your feet is vibrating with the beat. Soon you and the boombox are sliding with all of the earth down the hill and onto your farmland. Hooray, now the farming can begin!
reading_hint: Picture Nostradamus flailing his arms and trying to explain his vision to the farmers at the time. He knows that if they listen their farming techniques will improve, but they're looking at him like he's nuts.

meaning_mnemonic: You look at some <radical>gold</radical> and notice a <radical>fault</radical> in it. You scrape at the fault and see that it's just sprayed with gold paint. It's not gold, it's (comparatively) worthless <kanji>iron</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: Do you know that movie, <reading>Tetsu</reading>o (てつ) the <kanji>Iron</kanji> Man? We talked about it on Tofugu a while back. It’s one of the wackiest Japanese movies of all time. If you don’t know about Tetsuo the Iron Man, you should definitely look it up (if you aren't averse to horror/violent films). Tetsuo is one of the most insane movies in the history of ever.
meaning_hint: You think you've found a ton of gold bars! Imagine being so happy about this. Then, imagine your heart dropping in your chest when you notice the fault. Then, it dropping down even farther when you figure out it's merely iron.
reading_hint: Watch a trailer on YouTube for Tetsuo the Iron Man. Go ahead, do it now – I’ll wait. Did you watch it? Remember that nonsense every time you see this kanji.

meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>lip ring</radical> (or two in this case) is what you <radical>lack</radical>, it's going to be way easier for you to <kanji>sing</kanji> a <kanji>song</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Where are you always singing <kanji>song</kanji>s, by the way? In your <reading>ca</reading>r (か). It's not just you, either. Pretty much everyone likes to sing in their car.
meaning_hint: Imagine singing a song with a couple lip rings in your mouth. The articulation will be all wrong! It's definitely better to lack lip rings if you're going to sing.
reading_hint: There's something about your car that makes your singing sound so much better than usual, you know?

meaning_mnemonic: If someone sticks <radical>bamboo</radical> in your <radical>eye</radical> <radical>twenty</radical> times, the first thing your brain does is <kanji>calculate</kanji> how painful it is. Your brain's pain <kanji>calculation</kanji> will determine how you calculate your next move.
reading_mnemonic: Next you want to <kanji>calculate</kanji> how much punishment your bamboo attacker deserves, so you call on <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん). Santa-san is always calculating how many pieces of coal the naughty children deserve, so he's perfect for this <kanji>calculation</kanji> job.
meaning_hint: Your next move is to go to the doctor, because your brain has calculated that the pain is extreme. Imagine the doctor asking how much it hurts. Well, on a scale of one to ten, doctor, I'd calculate this is about a twenty.
reading_hint: Imagine trekking through the frozen wastes of the North Pole (with your eye patch on) to obtain Santa-san's calculation advice. After much discussion, he concludes that your bamboo attacker deserves twenty. No, not twenty years in prison — just twenty pieces of coal. Oh, how disappointing…

meaning_mnemonic: If you go up to a <radical>gate</radical> and press your <radical>ear</radical> to it, you'll <kanji>hear</kanji> what's going on on the other side. The people on the other side will have no idea you can hear their conversation.
reading_mnemonic: From the other side of the gate, you <kanji>hear</kanji> one of the people start to play a song on a recorder. You recognize the song as "Hot Cross <reading>Bun</reading>s (ぶん)." You haven't heard this song since grade school. "Hot cross buns, hot cross buns~"
meaning_hint: I'm sure you've pressed your ear up against a wall, door, or gate before to hear what's going on on the other side. Remember back to a time you did this and how sneaky you felt listening in on people without them knowing. You sneaky frood, you.
reading_hint: I'm sure many of you were forced to play "Hot Cross Buns" on the recorder in school at some point. Picture yourself playing this song and remember the melody when you see this kanji.

meaning_mnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> between <radical>five</radical> <radical>mouth</radical>s is a <kanji>language</kanji>. As long as there are five different people saying these same words with their five mouths, it's considered its own language.
reading_mnemonic: Another mouth joins in speaking the <kanji>language</kanji>, but it's green and scaled and... it's <reading>Go</reading>dzilla's (ご) mouth! Godzilla speaks this language too.
meaning_hint: Picture these five mouths saying the same language to each other. They can understand each other! The mouths are smiling and laughing too.
reading_hint: Godzilla speaks a language some people understand! You wish you knew this earlier, then you might have been able to stop some of his destruction. Try to say, "Hey Godzilla, please stop destroying my city," in his language. It's a lot of growling and yelling. He doesn't seem to understand you. You better practice your pronunciation.

meaning_mnemonic: You have to <radical>say</radical> what you’re <radical>sell</radical>ing because people don’t like to <kanji>read</kanji>. If they walk right by and don’t read, you better say what you have for sale so they’ll know! Read it out loud to them.
reading_mnemonic: What are you forced to <kanji>read</kanji> aloud to sell? <reading>Yo-</reading>yo (よ) instructions! You’d think they’d be self explanatory, but there are actually a lot of rules and tricks that you have to read to get good at yo-yoing. Read all of these out loud so you can sell them.
meaning_hint: Imagine reading what you have for sale out loud to people walking by because they’re too lazy to read it for themselves.
reading_hint: Imagine reading through your list of yo-yo tricks. Do you know how to pronounce those weird yo-yo names?

meaning_mnemonic: When the <radical>mouth</radical> of a <radical>bird</radical> opens, you're most likely to hear a <kanji>chirp</kanji>. This is how birds communicate. They open their mouths and let out a series of chirps. Chirp chirp chirp!
reading_mnemonic: The best way to experience the <kanji>chirp</kanji>-chirping of birds is to offer them <reading>na</reading>chos (な). Birds love nachos so much, it's a guaranteed way to make them chirp with delight.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself out in the wilderness on a sunny day. The weather is perfect and the scenery is beautiful. The only sound you can hear is the chirp of birds throughout the woods around you. It's very peaceful.
reading_hint: Picture yourself surrounded by birds of all kinds, handing out nachos. Just listen to their happy chirping. They're sitting on your arms, shoulders, and even your head, trying to get closer to the nachos. That chirping is so loud!

meaning_mnemonic: You can use a long piece of <radical>thread</radical> during <radical>white</radical> <radical>water</radical> rafting as a safety <kanji>line</kanji>. Sure it's thin, but if someone goes overboard even a line of thread is better than nothing. Throw them a line and drag them back aboard!
reading_mnemonic: The person who threw you that <kanji>line</kanji> of thread is actually a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). How kind of him. After you are safe you hop on the centaur's back and ride around to get the wind to dry you off.
meaning_hint: Imagine you're the person who fell overboard. The white water is rushing violently and it is deathly cold. Feel the water and freezing temperature on your skin. But look! Someone has thrown you a line of thread! You're saved!
reading_hint: Coming out of the rushing water, this centaur is like an angel sent from heaven. He's just saved your life. Imagine the happiness and gratitude you feel towards this centaur as you are pulled to safety by that line of thread.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tree</radical> marked with a <radical>yellow</radical> X on the <kanji>side</kanji> of the house is going to get cut down. You’ve marked it with a yellow X so the hired help knows which tree to cut down. There are a lot of trees on the side of the house, but the tree marked with a yellow X is the only one in danger of causing damage to your roof in the event of a storm.
reading_mnemonic: Wait, that woman with the chainsaw is <reading>Yoko</reading> (よこ) Ono! Yes, the same Yoko Ono who’s married to John Lennon of the Beatles. What’s she doing chopping down trees on the <kanji>side</kanji> of your house? Who can say. Silly Yoko.
meaning_hint: Picture the scene of a woman with a chainsaw cutting down this tree marked with a yellow X. Hear the sound of the chainsaw buzzing and the grinding of wood as it meets the side of the trunk. It’s very loud. Woodchips go flying everywhere.
reading_hint: Now picture the same scene as before only imagining Yoko Ono wielding that chainsaw. Maybe she’s dancing. Maybe there’s some Beatles music playing in the background. Either way it’s a strange sight – remember it.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>say</radical> something about the <radical>circumference</radical> of this area. You've looked around this whole area, making a big circle around it, because you are trying to <kanji>investigate</kanji> something that happened here. You also say this thing in a really quiet <kanji>tone</kanji> of voice, because you don't want the person or thing you're investigating to know you're on to them.
reading_mnemonic: "I came here to <kanji>investigate</kanji> this place, and I did indeed find out who the criminal is... it's none other than Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう)!"
meaning_hint: Because the radical <radical>circumference</radical> is in play, you can think of the crime scene you're investigating as a statue of a giant globe, and you're doing a circumference around it. Then, in the reading, we'll think about what you'll <radical>say</radical>.
reading_hint: From what you know about evil Mrs. Chou, you probably could have guessed it was her. The "Circumference Investigation" is now over, though you do still have to catch the perp, Mrs. Chou.

meaning_mnemonic: If you're <radical>stand</radical>ing under a <radical>tree</radical> and you <radical>see</radical> your <kanji>parent</kanji>, it's a good chance for a moment of <kanji>intimacy</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Standing under the tree, you go to give your <kanji>parent</kanji> a big hug, but you manage to bang your <reading>shin</reading> (しん)  on a huge tree root. Your parent pulls you in for a warm hug and rubs your shin. Despite the pain, it becomes an unforgettable "shin-timate" moment.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself standing in the shade of a beautiful, big, old tree. You see your parent walk past and shout out to them. They come and join you, and it's like the two of you are the only two people in the world right now. That's intimacy.
reading_hint: Busting your shins on things really hurts. I'm sure you've hit your shins on coffee tables and the like all your life. Remember that pain and think how much worse it would be banging your bare shins on pointy tree roots just when you were enjoying a moment of parental intimacy. Not enjoyable.

meaning_mnemonic: If you feed a lot of <radical>beans</radical> to a <radical>geoduck</radical>, its <kanji>head</kanji> will grow larger. To a geoduck, beans are a brain food, so by eating beans, it can grow an incredibly large head. Geoducks already look strange enough as it is, let alone with engorged heads.
reading_mnemonic: As the geoduck eats more and more beans its <kanji>head</kanji> gets bigger and bigger, until finally the head explodes like an <reading>atomi</reading>c (あたま) bomb. BOOM! Geoduck parts go flying everywhere and pieces of its massive head float to the ground as atomic fallout.
meaning_hint: Imagine, if you will, a geoduck. Now imagine it with a massive head. It looks ridiculous, and more disgusting than ever. It's actually really creeping you out, because the head keeps getting larger as the geoduck eats more beans. Ew.
reading_hint: Remember how gross geoducks are? Of course you do. Now imagine one exploding its atomic head right in front of you. You've got geoduck mess all over yourself. It's gross, slimy, sticky, and covering you from head to toe. It's wretched.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>stand</radical> on a <radical>cliff</radical>, a <radical>hair</radical>'s breadth away from the edge, when out of nowhere a <radical>geoduck</radical> slaps you in the <kanji>face</kanji>. The force makes you back away from the edge of the cliff, and your face is all slimy from the geoduck's residue.
reading_mnemonic: But where did that geoduck come from? As you turn from the cliff edge, you find yourself <kanji>face</kanji>-to-face with a <reading>cow</reading> (かお). This cow is the one who hit you in the face with a geoduck. You don't know why, but something about your face has upset the cow.
meaning_hint: Imagine being slapped in the face with a geoduck — a sharp hot pain, which then mingles with the cold geoduck residue left behind on your face. It's a strange, contradictory sensation.
reading_hint: Cows are big animals. So you probably got hit in the face with that geoduck pretty hard. Imagine the lingering pain, and how indignant you'd feel. Imagine getting in that cow's face and demanding an explanation.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>sick</radical> stick of <radical>dynamite</radical> is going to be <kanji>sick</kanji> forever. Unfortunately there are no medicines that are effective on sticks of dynamite, so once they contract a sickness, they’re sick for good.
reading_mnemonic: Being nervous all the time about spontaneously exploding would probably make you sweat profusely. And you know what sweating causes? <reading>B.O.</reading> (びょう). You’d be one smelly stick of dynamite. B.O. is a very unpleasant smell, and being <kanji>sick</kanji> is no excuse.
meaning_hint: Imagine if you were a sick stick of dynamite. Every time you sneezed you ran the risk of blowing up. It would be a terrifying way to live. Just imagine it.
reading_hint: Picture this as your life. You could explode at any moment, you’re sick, and you have B.O. Nobody wants to be around you because you might explode and kill them, and you stink. What a terrible way to live!

meaning_mnemonic: Let the <radical>sun</radical> shine into your <radical>ear</radical> while sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> and make sure <kanji>most</kanji> of it gets in your head. You want to fill your brain with the most sun anyone ever has.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Most</kanji> of this is for your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). You're testing out a new way to power it, but if you can't get most of the sun into your ear, it won't be enough to power most of the cyborg's robo-parts.
meaning_hint: Most of the sun is getting into your ear, but not all of it. Darn, maybe you should try angling yourself a different way.
reading_hint: Install the part into your cyborg. As long as most of the sun gets into its human ear, most of its cyborg bits will have power.

meaning_mnemonic: In <radical>prison</radical>, <radical>Wolverine</radical> gets picked on for the <radical>barb</radical>s coming out of his hands. This creates a lot of <kanji>conflict</kanji> between him and the other prisoners. They don't take kindly to mutants, and Wolverine doesn't take kindly to bigots — hence the conflict.
reading_mnemonic: This constant <kanji>conflict</kanji> between Wolverine and the other inmates is really affecting his <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Despite what people think, Wolverine really doesn't like to fight. He just hates injustice so much, he feels he has to do something about it.
meaning_hint: Picture one Wolverine versus, like, a hundred prisoners. Who would win? Yeah, my money's on the mutant in this conflict.
reading_hint: Put yourself in Wolverine's shoes. Imagine what it would feel like to have conflicting values, what it would do to your soul. Wanting to be peaceful on the one hand, but on the other not wanting to allow bigotry of any kind. It's a conflict in your soul.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> who takes the <radical>middle</radical> of the road on every issue is likely to have an average <kanji>relationship</kanji> with everyone. He doesn't really take sides in matters, and just stays in the middle. This makes him seem like a wishy-washy people-pleaser, and as such he's only able to maintain average, mid-tier relationships.
reading_mnemonic: Imagine a flip-flopping politician you have an average <kanji>relationship</kanji> with. Now imagine that there's a <reading>knocker</reading> (なか) on their forehead and you get to knock that knocker as hard as you like. Maybe you can knock some integrity into them and form a better relationship.
meaning_hint: I'm sure you've heard of flip-flopping in politics, when people try to take both sides of an issue, staying in the middle ground and never taking a stand. Imagine one of these people to represent this kanji.
reading_hint: Also, this kanji is pronounced exactly like the vocab word 中 (なか). If you can remember that, it'll make remembering 仲 easier.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>cloud</radical> that he uses to <kanji>transmit</kanji> all of the messages he needs to send to his people. The cloud floats above him and when he wants to tell people something he just whispers into the cloud and it transmits his message really, really loudly to all of them.
reading_mnemonic: As the leader <kanji>transmit</kanji>s his latest series of messages to you and the others, you notice a pretty large <reading>den</reading>t (でん) in it. That dent is probably a clue to what this cloud is really made of.
meaning_hint: Picture this special cloud floating above your leader. It's probably just a speaker attached to a drone covered in cotton balls.
reading_hint: You highly doubt this is a magical cloud that can transmit messages. You inch closer to the big dent and try to poke it. Your finger goes right in! Hmm, you're still pretty sure there's a trick to this thing.

meaning_mnemonic: The card game fish are best at is <radical>blackjack</radical> because they can easily hold the cards with their <radical>fins</radical> <kanji>together</kanji>. In blackjack you don't have to have a huge hand, full of cards. It's just a few, so fish fins can keep them together and just hit or stay.
reading_mnemonic: Once a year trillions of fish get <kanji>together</kanji> in <reading>きょう</reading>と to play blackjack. They have a huge tournament there once a year.
meaning_hint: Imagine a fish trying to hold a full hand of cards together. Impossible! Fish don’t have fingers to keep them spread properly. Now imagine a fish trying to hold two cards together and nodding to hit. It's much easier to keep things together this way!
reading_hint: Imagine a bunch of fish gathering together in きょうと to participate in this blackjack tournament. With so many fish all together in one place, it probably smells pretty fishy.

meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>woman</radical> and a <radical>child</radical> in front of you. You <kanji>like</kanji> them very much.
reading_mnemonic: "I <kanji>like</kanji> you two," you say to the woman and child. They both turn their heads 180 degrees back towards you. They both have faces that look just like <reading>こう</reading>いち's. "We like you too," they say in unison.
meaning_hint: Imagine a woman and a child you like very much. Just appreciate them, and think about how much you like those two. The reading mnemonic definitely won't ruin your image of them or anything.
reading_hint: And you DO like them. You have always been a fan of こういち's kanji learning methods, and you feel so lucky to have married a woman who looks just like こういち and then (this is the real miracle!) gave birth to a child that looks like こういち too. Hashtag blessed.

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>drunkard</radical> holds a <radical>sword</radical> it <kanji>becomes</kanji> something else. The drunkard doesn't become a swordsman, they're still just a drunkard. But the sword in their hands does.
reading_mnemonic: The sword has <kanji>become</kanji> a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい)! It's just like the saber you have! Hey, wait.
meaning_hint: Picture the drunkard standing in front of you. He picked up the sword and is holding it above his head. It's becoming... becoming...
reading_hint:
If a sword becomes a saber in the hands of a drunkard... then what are you? Geez this became pretty deep.

You slice down the drunkard with your saber and become a little more depressed than you were before.

meaning_mnemonic: By the time you finish digging a <radical>coffin</radical> hole with nothing but a <radical>spoon</radical>, you’ll be an old <kanji>elderly</kanji> person. Digging with nothing but a spoon takes a long time, especially when the hole needs to be big enough for a coffin. You might as well be digging it for yourself at this rate. By the time you finish, you’ll be all wrinkled and elderly and just about ready to keel over.
reading_mnemonic: Now that you’re <kanji>elderly</kanji> (and probably about to die) you suddenly crave some delicious <reading>roe</reading> (ろう). Salmon roe, sea urchin roe, tofugu roe, doesn’t matter. Your old, tired bones need roe after all that hard work!
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself digging a coffin hole with nothing but a spoon. Your hands get all cramped, your wrist starts to hurt, and you get painful blisters on your hands. It takes forever to dig a hole big enough for a coffin!
reading_hint: You hated roe when you were still young, but now that you’re an elderly old fart you really want to eat it. Roe is for old people, you guess, as you slurp it up.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> <radical>stand</radical>s at the highest <kanji>rank</kanji> in an organization. The leader is of the highest rank and stands above all others.
reading_mnemonic: This great leader is actually the leader of the boy scouts. And as we all know, the highest <kanji>rank</kanji> in boy scouts is the rank of <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) scout. This great leader has attained the rank of eagle scout and stands above all other scouts.
meaning_hint: Picture this great leader. Imagine him actually standing on the shoulders of his supporters who are a lower rank than he is. He’s standing atop this great pyramid as a fearless leader.
reading_hint: Picture the same ranking pyramid from before, only all the members are boy scouts, and the leader at the top is an eagle. Hear the piercing screech of the eagle with your ears.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of your <radical>clan</radical> wants to live close to the <radical>ground</radical> so he demands you get <kanji>low</kanji>. Low, low, with your belly to the ground. That way you're always below him, since he's the leader.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Low</kanji> people like you can only eat low food like potatoes. But that's boring, so you low people learned to make <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots. You roll them to each other and eat them without lifting your heads.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself, face smooshed into the ground, and you can hear the leader shouting above you, "GET LOW!"
reading_hint: Get low to the ground right now and ask your mom or dad to roll some tater tots towards your face. Eat them up, mm mm.

meaning_mnemonic: You wear a special <radical>outfit</radical> and equip a <radical>sword</radical> whenever you do something for the <kanji>first</kanji> time. Doing something for the first time is always scary, but the right outfit and a sword at your side can give you the confidence and courage to overcome your jitters!
reading_mnemonic: So, what is it you're doing for the <kanji>first</kanji> time? A fashion <reading>show</reading> (しょ). Your special outfit and sword aren't just to calm your nerves — they're also what you'll be showing off on the catwalk.
meaning_hint: Imagine the butterflies in your stomach as you prepare to do something for the first time. Now look down at your badass outfit and the sword on your belt. Feel the nervousness subside a little. You've got this.
reading_hint: Picture yourself strutting down the runway at this fashion show. You're doing pretty well for your first show — until you trip over your sword and fall flat on your face. Darn it — now everyone's gonna know this was your first time.

meaning_mnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> you put a <radical>sword</radical> and a <radical>knife</radical>. Now that you have them in there, you have to make them <kanji>separate</kanji> from each other.
reading_mnemonic: Why are you trying to make a sword and knife <kanji>separate</kanji> in your mouth? Because someone <reading>bets</reading> (べつ) you can't do it.
meaning_hint: Anyone can put a sword and knife in their mouth. But not everyone can make them separate while still in your mouth.
reading_hint: "I bet you can't separate the sword and knife," they say in an annoying voice. Alright, time for you to show them. Nobody bets you anything without you blindly trying to do it!

meaning_mnemonic: Cut <radical>grain</radical>s with a <radical>knife</radical> to make them smaller and you'll make a great <kanji>profit</kanji>. By cutting them, now you can get three grains worth of money for what was once a single grain.
reading_mnemonic: To make an even better <kanji>profit</kanji>, you decide to cut up <reading>ree</reading>ds (り) too. You stuff the grains in the reeds and sell that as "self-cooking" sets.
meaning_hint: Imagine buying some rice. Now cut all the grains with a knife into smaller grains. Now sell them for two, three, four times the profit!
reading_hint: Picture all of your reeds full of grains in a row. The entire world will benefit from your crafty invention. And you'll turn a great profit too, which certainly doesn't hurt.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> sits on a <radical>stool</radical> to conserve her <radical>power</radical>, as her servants <kanji>toil</kanji> away in the kitchen. They toil away preparing a feast for this powerful woman while she kicks back on her cozy stool. She's a queen and deserves to be pampered.
reading_mnemonic: As a servant you <kanji>toil</kanji> not just for the woman, but also for her <reading>do</reading>g (ど). Anything she gets, the dog gets too. That means double the toil for you. But hey, at least the dog is cute.
meaning_hint:
Imagine you're one of this woman's minions, toiling away in the kitchen. The heat from the stoves is intense, and you're hoping she doesn't see the sweat dripping from your forehead into the food. Man, you've never toiled so hard in your life.

Note: There are a few other kanji with similar meanings. This one is used in words that refer to doing work, with a focus on making a concerted effort.
reading_hint: Secretly it's this dog you toil for, not the woman. Try to picture what kind of dog it is. And imagine sneaking extra portions to it under the table when the woman isn't looking. Yes, if it weren't for this innocent dog, you'd have done with this life of toil!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> in a position of <radical>power</radical> does not need to <kanji>labor</kanji> in the fields. Labor is for the weak! A Viking of power takes what he wants, and need not labor for it. A powerful Viking plunders all and takes what he wants!
reading_mnemonic: It takes a lot of <kanji>labor</kanji> to harvest <reading>roe</reading> (ろう) so that’s what the Viking decides to take first! Roe is delicious, but it’s small and delicate. Vikings don’t labor for their own roe! They take it and eat it!
meaning_hint:
Imagine yourself as a powerful Viking. You have the power to take anything you want, pillaging and plundering to your heart’s content. Imagine all the things you would acquire with this sort of power.

Note - there are a few other kanji that have a similar meaning to this one. This kanji is used in words related to physical, or manual labor, with an emphasis on how darn tiring it is!
reading_hint: Picture this powerful Viking laying siege on a small fishing village and taking all the roe and stuffing it into his bearded face. So much labor went into harvesting that roe! Vikings are so mean!

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Orders</radical> coming from your <radical>mouth</radical> will determine the <kanji>fate</kanji> of your soldiers. You are their commanding officer and your orders determine their <kanji>life</kanji> and death.
reading_mnemonic: You are the commanding officer on the <reading>May</reading>flower (めい). You give orders to colonize America. The orders spewing from your mouth hole determine the <kanji>fate</kanji> of the settlers and the fate of America and the fate of the world! You and the Mayflower have changed everything forever.
meaning_hint: Pretend you are a high ranking officer and imagine yourself giving orders to your underlings. Imagine all the power and influence you have. It feels good.
reading_hint: Students of American history are very familiar with the Mayflower. Recall learning about it in school whenever you see this kanji. Or look it up on Wikipedia if you don’t know about it. The Mayflower is a big deal!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>mountain</radical> on top of the <radical>cliff</radical> is <radical>dry</radical> from the <kanji>coast</kanji> below. Just from looking at the raging <kanji>shore</kanji> below, you'd think everything would be wet, it's splashing everywhere!
reading_mnemonic: Sitting up there, watching the <kanji>coast</kanji> with you, is <reading>Gan</reading>dhi (がん). The world famous Gandhi, is just hanging out up here with you, looking down at what you now realize must be a coast in India.
meaning_hint: You're on top of the mountain and the entire coast is stretching out below. The shore goes as far as your eyes can see.
reading_hint: Sit down next to Gandhi and close your eyes. You can hear the water hitting the shore below. The coastal birds are screaming. You crack open an eye and see Gandhi still sitting perfectly still.

meaning_mnemonic: As the year starts moving in the <radical>direction</radical> of <radical>winter</radical>, the trees must <kanji>release</kanji> their leaves.
reading_mnemonic: The trees <kanji>release</kanji> all of their leaves onto your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Slowly, you watch in horror as your home is consumed by the released leaves. They enter in through the windows, doors, and the chimney.
meaning_hint: Picture winter heading straight for you and frosting everything over. Now picture the leaves being released by the trees.
reading_hint: "Release my home from this leaf prison!" you shout at the now bare trees.

meaning_mnemonic: You've been playing <radical>blackjack</radical> in the <radical>sun</radical> since <kanji>long ago</kanji>. This is the longest game of blackjack you've ever played and it feels like ages since you started. You've been sitting out in the sun playing blackjack since long, long ago.
reading_mnemonic: Once you finally finish the game, you walk the long walk back home, in the sun, barefoot, on a dirt road that hasn't been maintained since <kanji>long ago</kanji>. Your feet are all sweaty from being out in the sun, and walking on this dirt road gives you <reading>muck ashi</reading> (むかし). Your ashi (feet) are all mucky and muddy from the combination of dirt and sweat. Your muck ashi are really gross, man.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself playing a never-ending game of blackjack out in the hot sun. You’re sticky, sweaty, and tired. But all you can do is continue to play your game of blackjack. It’s miserable.
reading_hint: Imagine your muck ashi, all dirty and muddy from walking on that old road from long ago. Feel the muck between the toes of your ashi, squishing with every step. Hear the sound the mud makes as it slides between your sweaty mucky toes.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits your <radical>skin</radical> and creates smaller <kanji>wave</kanji>s. Those hit you too!
reading_mnemonic: When each <kanji>wave</kanji> hits your body, you shout, "<reading>Ha</reading> (は)!" It's not like it's on purpose. When the wave crashes into you, the wind gets knocked right out of your mouth, making a HA sound.
meaning_hint: If you’ve ever been to the ocean or a large lake, you know what kind of forces even small waves can hit with. Now imagine the force with which a tsunami wave would strike you. You’d have the wind knocked out of you for sure.
reading_hint: The waves are basically punching you in the gut, forcing the air out of your lungs, and making you shout HA!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>master</radical> has the power to <kanji>pour</kanji> water wherever he pleases. Tsunami masters are masters over the most powerful water force in the universe, so it makes sense that they have the ability to pour water at will, wherever they please. Best not tick off the master, unless you want a tsunami poured on your head.
reading_mnemonic: If <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう) was trying to attack you, you’d be able to defend yourself easily. Simply <kanji>pour</kanji> a tsunami on him and Chewbacca would surrender in a heartbeat. Chewbacca doesn’t like having water poured on him, especially not all the water from a tsunami.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself as this tsunami master. You’re like a water bender from Avatar, only more awesome. Feel the cold wet water pouring out of your fingertips as you create water out of nothing. Imagine what it would be like to have this power.
reading_hint: Imagine Chewbacca soaking wet. All his fur is matted down and he looks pretty depressed. He smells like wet dog too. Smell this smell in your nostrils as you imagine how pathetic this drenched Chewbacca feels.

meaning_mnemonic: Give <radical>trash</radical> to the <radical>moon</radical> and it will <kanji>nurture</kanji> and <kanji>raise</kanji> that trash like it's its own little moon baby.
reading_mnemonic: The moon <kanji>nurture</kanji>s the trash ball until one day, it speaks! "<reading>行く</reading> (いく), 行く！" it says. It wants to go live with mom moon!
meaning_hint: Picture the moon raising this ball of trash, giving it love and attention.
reading_hint: Watch as the little trash ball 行くs up into the sky and floats around its moon mama. If you raise your child right it will want to 行く wherever you go too.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> always seem to <radical>suit</radical> the random things you <kanji>pick up</kanji> off the ground. An old thimble. A discarded onion ring. A dead worm you tie in a knot. Your incredible fingers suit them beautifully, almost as if they were jewelry, so of course you pick up all of them.
reading_mnemonic: You're walking around looking for something to <kanji>pick up</kanji> when you see a sword in a rock. Yes, this will suit you. You pick up the sword and people jump out around you and tell you that you are the new <reading>hero</reading> (ひろ)! Uh oh. Maybe you shouldn't have picked this up.
meaning_hint: Imagine finding something that suits your life and your style. You're not just going to leave it there, right? No, you're going to pick it up! Go on, use your fingers and pick up that thing.
reading_hint: You picked up the hero sword and now you're the new hero. Time to go kill a dragon. Good luck, and try not to die, hero person!

meaning_mnemonic: Try to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to <radical>spoon</radical> out some of the <radical>sun</radical> and you'll come back with just one <kanji>finger</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Don't worry, you aren't the only one out there with just one <kanji>finger</kanji>, <reading>shee</reading>p (し) only have one finger too. I mean, they're hooves, which are really just a single animal finger. It's just big and kind of flat.
meaning_hint: Look down at your finger and the burned stumps where the rest of your fingers used to be. It hurts. And you're sad. But at least you still have one finger left.
reading_hint: Picture sitting with a sheep. It's right across from you, staring into your eyes. It reaches out its sheep finger hoof and you reach out with your finger. They touch. It's magical.

meaning_mnemonic:
This <radical>tsunami</radical> is ridden by a <radical>sheep</radical> in classic <kanji>Western style</kanji>. And by Western style I mean the sheep is dressed in a cowboy outfit and has put a saddle atop the tsunami wave to ride it. It's a tsunami rodeo and this crazy sheep is riding that tsunami wave in classic Western style.

This kanji also means <kanji>ocean</kanji>, because presumably the people from the West, with their Western style, came from across the ocean. You'll want to be familiar with both meanings for vocab later on.
reading_mnemonic: After a whole day of riding this tsunami, the sheep is hankerin' for some <kanji>Western-style</kanji> food, namely <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Yogurt is Western-style food, right? If it helps you can imagine the yogurt is flavored like apple pie, to make it more American and Western.
meaning_hint: Imagine how ridiculous a sheep would look all decked out in Western-style cowboy gear. Now imagine it riding a tsunami wave as it screams, "Baa~ baa~ baa~!"
reading_hint: Imagine yourself eating this Western-style apple pie yogurt. It tastes just like apple pie, only creamier and fruitier. Taste the sweet yogurt in your mouth as you imagine yourself scarfing some down.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>spirit</radical> should feel <radical>humble</radical> before a <kanji>god</kanji>'s <kanji>spirit</kanji>. You never really feel humble in front of anyone else, but if you meet a god's spirit… Well, you should probably lower yourself appropriately. Gods have powerful spirits.
reading_mnemonic: When you meet a <kanji>god</kanji>'s <kanji>spirit</kanji>, you need to get down on your <reading>shin</reading>s (しん). Get down on your ankles? No, not low enough. Knees? No, no, get your shins flat on the ground to show how humble you really are. Luckily, you're wearing <reading>jean</reading>s (じん), so at least that will stop your poor shins getting chafed!
meaning_hint: Imagine a spirit appearing before you. It's a god. You should be as humble as possible. Out of respect and also because you don't want to be cursed or something.
reading_hint: If you don't get down on your shins before god's spirit, it could take them away. Even your jeans couldn't protect you then. You don't want to live a shin-less life, do you?

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Grain</radical>s are <radical>few</radical>. Imagine the last few grains trickling down the hourglass. You only have a <kanji>second</kanji> left.
reading_mnemonic: After the final <kanji>second</kanji>s go by, your awful <reading>B.O.</reading> (びょう) appears.
meaning_hint: Normally an hourglass has sand to count the seconds, but yours uses grains. Imagine just a few grains left, and watch them trickle down as you count a second, and then another second.
reading_hint: Your hourglass timer was a timer to specifically let you know when your B.O. was set to return.

meaning_mnemonic: There are pieces of <radical>thread</radical> on each <radical>escalator</radical> showing you what <kanji>level</kanji> they go to in your school. Each escalator takes you to a different <kanji>grade</kanji>, so make sure you follow the right thread or you'll end up in a class that's either way above or way below your level.
reading_mnemonic: You want to see what the highest <kanji>level</kanji> is, so you go up the wrong escalator. At the top you find a giant <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう).
meaning_hint: When you level up your Japanese skills, you'll take this escalator to rank up too!
reading_hint: A teacher comes over and says, "Excuse me, this is advanced level cucumber studies. I don't think you're at this level yet... please take the escalator down and follow the threads to find your level."

meaning_mnemonic: On a <radical>scooter</radical> there is a <radical>bear</radical> with a <radical>drop</radical> on its head trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> and <kanji>chase</kanji> you! You accidentally splashed it with water and it's not happy about it. You're on your own scooter, luckily, but that doesn't make this any less terrifying.
reading_mnemonic: The bear is trying to <kanji>follow</kanji> you because it wants your <reading>o</reading>bi (お) as compensation for its injured pride. The obi is waving in the wind behind your scooter, and the bear is reaching out with its teeth to try and grab it.
meaning_hint: Picture the bear on its scooter with the offending drop of water on its head following really closely behind your scooter. You can almost feel his bear breath on your neck!
reading_hint: Picture the bear chasing you on its scooter, trying to reach out and grab the obi off your waist. The drop of water on its head is jiggling from the exertion. Woah, it's getting a little too close!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>simple</radical> <radical>drunkard</radical> starting a <kanji>war</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The simple drunkard started a <kanji>war</kanji> with the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん)! This is especially unfortunate, because the centaurs are really good at <kanji>battle</kanji>s because they're all automatically cavalry troops.
meaning_hint: Imagine what the simple drunkard does, to start the war. They're not in their right mind, and the centaurs (getting you ready for the reading mnemonic!) are an easily offended people. Go ahead and run through the simple drunkard's actions toward the centaurs that start this war.
reading_hint: Imagine the war: humans vs centaurs. Tens of thousands clashing in battle.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Stand</radical> on your <radical>older brother</radical>. Now <radical>stand</radical> on your <radical>older brother</radical> again. You wish to stand on, and above, your older brother. You <kanji>compete</kanji> over and over again, to stand atop your older brother.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>compete</kanji> with your older brother because only one of you may go to <reading>きょう</reading>と. Whoever stands on top the most gets to go to きょうと, so you must compete for a spot.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself standing on your older brother. They push you off. So you do it again. You want to be on top, but your older brother doesn't want you to. You compete for the top spot over and over again, throughout your whole life, because that's what one does with their older brother.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself competing to stand on top of your older brother. Do you win? Do you get the one ticket to きょうと?

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>drop</radical> of water to a <radical>root</radical> is <kanji>good</kanji>. It's good for the roots to grow and live and thrive. And what's good for roots is good for our plants and us humans too.
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>good</kanji> way to get a lot of drops of water for your roots is to buy a <reading>row</reading> boat (りょう). Then just row out to the middle of the lake and plonk all the roots right into the source!
meaning_hint: Imagine putting a little drop of water over a root you want to grow. Doing this is good for the plant and also good for your soul.
reading_hint: Rowing even feels good on your arms and back. Probably just as good as all these roots will feel.

meaning_mnemonic: Using your <radical>construction</radical> <radical>power</radical> you build something great. A true <kanji>achievement</kanji> built for the achievement of making kanji learning easier!
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>achievement</kanji> was made possible by <reading>こう</reading>いち, so of course this construction project is a giant statue of the man himself. What an achievement of a statue. What an achievement of an achievement.
meaning_hint: Just constructing this thing was an achievement. Feel proud like it was, well, an achievement.
reading_hint:
Thanks こういち. Now everyone will know of your achievements around kanji learning and inside-out earth theory.

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading. Remember that, because it comes up quite a bit!

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>cow</radical> <radical>temple</radical> is <kanji>special</kanji>. This cow must have been very important to have an entire temple dedicated to it.
reading_mnemonic: This cow is <kanji>special</kanji> because it belonged to <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu, the first shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate and uniter of Japan! He's a super important figure in Japanese history. You should absolutely look him up and read a bit about him (when you're caught up on these lessons, that is).
meaning_hint: Picture a temple dedicated to a single cow. All of the people who come and worship here have a special place in their hearts for the cow.
reading_hint: You walk up to a statue of the special cow in the temple and see a collar around its neck that says "Tokugawa Moo Moo" on it. How... special!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> stands upon the <radical>ground</radical> of the <radical>rice paddy</radical>. She is searching for <radical>treasure</radical>. It is said that this treasure here in this rice field will make things much easier for the leader (that's what leaders really want, after all). If she gets this treasure, it will be a great <kanji>convenience</kanji> for her, because convenience is everything when you're the leader.
reading_mnemonic: She finds a place to dig in the ground. It looks like there's some fresh dirt here. Finally, the treasure of <kanji>convenience</kanji> that will make this leader's life so much easier. She digs into the ground and pulls out a box. When she opens it, she finds out it's a <reading>ben</reading>to (べん). "My god," she thinks. "This really will make my life more convenient. My people can take their lunches with them now instead of coming back to base to eat, which will waste way less time!"
meaning_hint: Imagine the leader looking around the rice paddy. She keeps saying, "Ugh, it would be convenient if I didn't have to walk around out here looking for the treasure that's going to make things more convenient for me. Ugh." Go ahead and say this phrase out loud, too, pretending to be the leader in the rice paddy. Feel the cold and the wet, making it less convenient for you.
reading_hint: Feel the happiness that the leader would feel at this revelation. It will make everything much more convenient for her, since she's not the one doing all the manual labor. She won't have to eat out of a bento, that's for sure.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> makes you lift <radical>heavy</radical> loads with all your <radical>power</radical>, since this is what you do for <kanji>work</kanji>. Why do you do this kind of work? Well, you're good at it and the leader makes you do it, I guess.
reading_mnemonic: Where do you get the power to do all this <kanji>work</kanji>? From <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts (どう). You eat nothing but doughnuts. If you had no doughnuts, you couldn't work. In fact, your leader pays you for your work… in doughnuts.
meaning_hint:
Picture this leader asking you (yet again) to work for them. "Can you move those heavy objects with all that power of yours?" Typical leader — as soon as they know you're good at a certain kind of work, they ask you to do it all the time. 

Note: There are a few other kanji that have a similar meaning to this one. This kanji is used in words that refer to doing work, with a focus on movement or operating something.
reading_hint: Picture yourself receiving a doughnut as payment after you've been working. It's delicious. Doughnuts are fantastic. Taste the fresh doughnut in your mouth as it crumbles and melts on your tongue. It's the best doughnut you've ever had.

meaning_mnemonic: If you know the radical meaning <radical>orders</radical> you know the kanji meaning <kanji>orders</kanji> as well!
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>orders</kanji> are to get a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun.
meaning_hint: Here's an order: study your radicals.
reading_hint:
Imagine an authority figure giving you this order as an official order. Ray guns don't really exist, so you think it's a pretty strange order.

Hint: when you see the <radical>orders</radical> radical, the reading will often be れい. The mnemonic will likely involve orders to get a ray gun of some kind.

meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>sound</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> gives me an <kanji>idea</kanji>. The methodic thump thump thumping of a heart puts me in a trance that gives me great ideas. It’s a very inspirational sound, the sound of a heart is. It gives me an idea. 

Alternatively, you could look at this kanji and see a "stand" radical and something that kind of (almost) looks like the kanji for "think." When you stand up and think, you come up with your very own <kanji>idea</kanji>s!
reading_mnemonic: The heart of an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) is very strong. The person who knew this probably came up with the <kanji>idea</kanji> to make the bald eagle a symbol of the United States. Eagles are always flying around and doing things, so their hearts are very strong.
meaning_hint: Put your hand over your heart, or on your wrist, or somewhere you can feel your pulse. Your heart is pumping blood through your body, hear the sound of your heart beat and try to picture and get an idea for how all the blood flows through your body.
reading_hint: Picture the majestic animal that is the bald eagle. Imagine the sound of an eagle screeching as it zooms through the air, flapping its wings in the wind. It’s pretty impressive.

meaning_mnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> there is a <radical>jet</radical> dropping <kanji>flavor</kanji> bombs. Every time you <kanji>taste</kanji> something, it's actually because a tiny jet has flown into your mouth to bomb you with flavor.
reading_mnemonic: And what has more <kanji>flavor</kanji> than a big slab of <reading>me</reading>at (み)? You love meat and its flavor nearly as much as you love the <kanji>taste</kanji> of its juice. So many good things to put in your mouth!
meaning_hint: What's your favorite food? Imagine that, as you bite into it, tiny jets are released from inside the food to attack your tongue with flavor bombs, stimulating your sense of taste.
reading_hint: Of course, if you're vegetarian imagine the flavor of the meat being terrible. Up to you. Make the mnemonic work for your lifestyle.

meaning_mnemonic: You'll have to <radical>excuse</radical> me, having all this <radical>power</radical> puts a lot of strain and <kanji>exertion</kanji> on my body. My power comes from exertion of muscle, so please excuse me if I start to perspire.
reading_mnemonic: Excuse me, I don’t think I’ve introduced myself yet. My name is <reading>Ben</reading> (べん) and I use powerful muscle <kanji>exertion</kanji> to accomplish every task. Ben knows what Ben wants and how to get it. Remember that, kids.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself under great exertion. You're attempting to use your power to move something and it's really requiring a lot of exertion to move. Picture yourself doing this action and imagine how difficult it is.
reading_hint: Picture this Ben in your head. Ben always uses lots of exertion to do tasks. Imagine him using an unnecessary amount of power and exertion in carrying out normal everyday tasks.

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>canopy</radical> and a man called the <radical>Yoga</radical> <radical>King</radical> standing in the middle of your <kanji>garden</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Not only is this Yoga King taking over your <kanji>garden</kanji>, but he's ordering you to serve food to his class. "You there, peasant, fetch these people your freshest <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots from this puny garden to give them strength."
meaning_hint: Somehow your little garden is now being used by the Yoga King to teach yoga to a whole group of random people under this canopy... you have no idea how they got back here.
reading_hint: You're not sure what else to do, so you end up serving tater tots in your garden all day to stretchy strangers.

meaning_mnemonic: Imagining one’s <radical>self</radical> without a <radical>heart</radical> can make it hard to draw <kanji>breath</kanji>. Without a heart, you’d have no blood pumping and you would start to die. This would make it increasingly harder to draw breath and get oxygen to stay alive.
reading_mnemonic: You know how when people are unconscious and their <kanji>breath</kanji> slows or comes to a stop, they use smelling salts to revive them? Well a smelly <reading>sock</reading> (そく) can work just as well. Socks get really stinky and their stench is powerful enough to revive someone who isn’t drawing breath.
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to live without a heart. You couldn’t. Your blood stops flowing and your body shuts down. You would no longer be able to draw breath.
reading_hint: Imagine smelling this smelly sock. Imagine the smelly stench of the dirty sock wafting up into your nostrils and filling your lungs with its odor. Gross.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>direction</radical> your <radical>gun</radical> is facing leads straight to a block of <radical>tofu</radical>. This might be a sign you need to take a <kanji>trip</kanji>. If you're out there pointing guns at tofu, you're probably a little… tired… and in need of a change of scenery. Take a trip!
reading_mnemonic: So, where do you go on your <kanji>trip</kanji>? To <reading>Rio</reading> (りょ). You had to go way down south to get as far away as possible from tofu. Ahh, nothing like a nice trip to Rio!
meaning_hint: Imagine coming to your senses after shooting up a block of tofu for stress relief or whatever you were doing. Yeah, you gotta get away from tofu for a while… It's definitely time for a relaxing trip.
reading_hint: Imagine soaking up the sun on the beaches of Rio de Janeiro. Rio has its fair share of guns, but at least there's a lot less tofu around these parts.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> <radical>root</radical> is still just a <kanji>root</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: Tree <kanji>root</kanji>s are the perfect roosting place for <reading>con</reading>dors (こん). Unfortunately, living on roots is also why they're always endangered. Condors can be killed pretty easily living on the ground on roots like this.
meaning_hint: Roots are roots, ね.
reading_hint: Picture a family of condors nestled at the bottom of a tree in its roots. Then a huge machine comes over and mows them all down as it cuts all the trees off their roots. Darn you humans! Do you have to ruin everything?!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> lifts all the <radical>trash</radical> out of the <radical>river</radical> and it <kanji>stream</kanji>s out onto the shores.
