reading_mnemonic: Next to the trash <kanji>stream</kanji> you suddenly see <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter and he looks pissed. He's furious that so many people throw their trash in the river and it causes streams like this.
meaning_hint: As the tsunami recedes, watch as all of the trash comes streaming out of the river and crowds the bank.
reading_hint: Now Ryu is punching and kicking and tatsumaki senpukyaku-ing all of the trash into burnable and non-burnable piles. But the stream doesn't end. Now he's trying to get his other Street Fighter pals to help him beat it back!

meaning_mnemonic: A long time ago a huge <radical>tsunami</radical> swept a <radical>triceratops</radical> up into the <radical>moon</radical> and <kanji>extinguish</kanji>ed its life.
reading_mnemonic: This famous event where almost all life was <kanji>extinguish</kanji>ed is reenacted every year for the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). He's got a bit of an unhealthy obsession with extinguishing life...
meaning_hint: I’m sure you’ve heard many theories on how dinosaurs went extinct. Well, this is the real story. Imagine this poor triceratops and all his friends being extinguished by this powerful tsunami, sweeping them up into the moon where there's no air to breathe or leaves to eat.
reading_hint: Watch the Shougun's eyes light up as an actor dressed like a tsunami crashes into an actor dressed as a triceratops and all of the lights on the stage are extinguished. Spooky...

meaning_mnemonic:
A <radical>leader</radical> who hires his own personal <radical>clown</radical> will likely <kanji>double</kanji> his entertainment. Clowns are funny and make people laugh, so the leader decided to hire one. Since that time, the leader's amount of mirth has doubled. 

This also means <kanji>times</kanji>, which makes sense when you think about the kind of math you'll need to double something. What is A * 2? It's A doubled, first of all, but you're using multiplication. You're "timesing" something.
reading_mnemonic: When the clown stops being funny, it's time to say <reading>bye</reading> (ばい). At first, the clown <kanji>double</kanji>d the leader's entertainment, but now the clown is just annoying. It's time to say bye-bye.
meaning_hint: Picture a clown and all the ridiculous funny things they do. Imagine the clown throwing a pie at someone's face and how funny that is. Chuckle to yourself and remember this kanji.
reading_hint: This clown has served its purpose. Wave your hand in the air and say bye-bye to this worthless clown.

meaning_mnemonic: Even a <radical>mouth</radical>-bearing <radical>shellfish</radical> is a <kanji>member</kanji> of the invertebrate family. Yes, shellfish do have mouths, look it up!
reading_mnemonic: If you were a <kanji>member</kanji> of the same shellfish family, you'd be able to go to the special shellfish <reading>inn</reading> (いん). Every species has a members-only inn.
meaning_hint: Imagine a shellfish opening and closing its mouth, talking to you and telling you what a proud member of its family it is. It's a strange sight, but picture it in your mind anyway.
reading_hint: Imagine trying to go to the shellfish inn. Imagine all the members-only signs telling you that you can't come in. Womp womp.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>bird</radical> sits upon a <radical>mountain</radical>. If you look at the radicals, the bird is quite large and the mountain quite small. Think of this in the mnemonic as well. The reason the mountain is small is because you can only see the top of it, sticking out of the water. On it is the bird. This small mountain with the bird on it is an <kanji>island</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: But this <kanji>island</kanji> doesn't only belong to the bird. In fact, the bird shouldn't be on this island, because it belongs to the <reading>She</reading>ep <reading>Ma</reading>ma (しま), who is like Mother Nature but for sheep. The all-powerful Sheep Mama comes to kick the bird off the island, because this place is only for sheep! All hail the Sheep Mama.
meaning_hint: Imagine the mountain as small as you'd like. Maybe it's only barely sticking out of the sea, and the bird is having trouble standing on it. Whatever you do, make sure you think of it as an island, possibly owned by the bird.
reading_hint: The Sheep Mama is a huge, majestic sheep with magical powers. She kicks the bird off the island with her hooves, and the bird squawks and flies away as fast as it can.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> shines down on a <radical>stool</radical> and a <radical>jackhammer</radical> which are there for the upcoming <kanji>festival</kanji>. Festivals need activities, and at this festival, you can sit on a stool in the moonlight and use a jackhammer to make a lot of noise. Perfect for a festival!
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>festival</kanji> is for <reading>cy</reading>borgs (さい) only. Picture the cyborg you made getting excited that you're taking it to the festival. That's why all of the activities have to do with heavy machinery. The rides are mostly just cyborgs letting other people ride them, but it's still pretty fun.
meaning_hint: Imagine being at a moonlit festival and waiting in line for the stool and jackhammer. You don't want to eat candy apples or ride rides, you want to wait your turn to try out the jackhammer (while comfortably seated on a stool, of course).
reading_hint: Imagine your cyborg going to this festival. Everyone here has some type of cyborg enhancement, like robotic arms, legs, or braces. Your cyborg is really excited to be around its people and play with jackhammers.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>stand</radical> at a podium <radical>early</radical> in the morning to practice reading the <kanji>chapter</kanji> from your latest book.
reading_mnemonic: You're practicing this specific <kanji>chapter</kanji> because you've been asked to read it for the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). There will be a bunch of other authors reading chapters of their books to him too, and you want to make a good impression.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself, standing tall in the light of the early morning. You hold your head up high, open to your bookmark, and read the name of the chapter aloud: "Chapter 42: The Most Important Number."
reading_hint: Picture yourself in a line with other authors, standing in the early morning, waiting to read chapters from your respective books. You really hope the Shougun likes yours. If he does, you'll be famous! If he doesn't, you might be beheaded.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Bamboo</radical>'s <radical>younger brother</radical> is counted with an <kanji>ordinal number prefix</kanji>. An ordinal number is one that represents a position or rank in sequential order (like first, second, third, etc.). We use this ordinal number prefix before counting these things in Japanese sometimes. Like with bamboo, going down in order toward the youngest.
reading_mnemonic: To remember you need to use the <kanji>ordinal number prefix</kanji> before you count these bamboo, you <reading>dye</reading> (だい) them.
meaning_hint: Imagine a line of bamboo shoots arranged from oldest to youngest. They look like pipe organ pipes, or a xylophone, and now you need to count them.
reading_hint: Now they're in descending order already, and you can count them nice and easy thanks to your dye method.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Someone</radical>s and <radical>building</radical>s, someones and buildings, someones and buildings. So many someones and buildings. With so many someones and buildings, we have ourselves a <kanji>metropolis</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: In this <kanji>metropolis</kanji> everything is a <reading>toe</reading> (と). The someones are toes, walking around. The buildings are toes, too.
meaning_hint: Imagine just a ton of someones and buildings all packed together in a small area. More than a village or town or city. With enough someones and buildings, you end up with a metropolis.
reading_hint: Imagine this huge metropolis. Now imagine every building just being a toe. Tall skyscraper toes. Smaller house toes. Wide apartment building toes. And then the people walking around. They are toes, too. Toes walking around and going into toe buildings, with toe cars and toe dogs. If you imagine the toe metropolis deeply enough, it should be weird enough to burn into your memory.

meaning_mnemonic: When something is <radical>heavy</radical>, you need a lot of <radical>power</radical> to <kanji>move</kanji> it. It’s not going to move unless you use a lot of power. That thing is heavy and unless you use all the power you got, it’s not moving.
reading_mnemonic: Remember that 1 ton weight? Well, actually it’s a <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう). A huge, massive, 1 ton doughnut. It’s super heavy and hard to move, but it’s delicious.
meaning_hint: Picture a massive 1 ton weight. It’s heavy. You can’t even begin to move it without some sort of powerful machine to help you. Imagine yourself trying to move it with just your bare hands.
reading_hint: Okay, so you don’t have enough power to move this doughnut as is. In order to move it, you’re going to have to use your superhuman power of eating. Once you eat enough of it, the remainder will be easy to move. Imagine yourself digging into this massive doughnut and taste its fresh baked goodness on your tongue. It’s mighty tasty.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>stand</radical>ing <radical>mustache</radical> with <radical>legs</radical> is able to sell <kanji>merchandise</kanji>. Most mustaches can’t stand because they don’t have legs. This mustache, however, does. It also has a keen business sense, which is why it’s selling merchandise.
reading_mnemonic: The mustache is selling his <kanji>merchandise</kanji> so well that even the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) notices. He leans down really close to the standing mustache man's merchandise and stays very still. Then he snaps his fingers and his servants come rushing out to buy it all!
meaning_hint: Imagine this mustache entrepreneur, standing at his merchandise stand, selling his wares. He looks quite dignified, legs and all.
reading_hint: Now all of the merchandise belongs to the Shougun. The mustache has nothing left to sell, but at least he's rich!

meaning_mnemonic: In <radical>Asia</radical> they assume everyone's <radical>heart</radical> is automatically <kanji>bad</kanji> and <kanji>evil</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Luckily, people in Asia know how to get the <kanji>evil</kanji> <kanji>bad</kanji>ness out of your heart with <reading>acu</reading>puncture (あく).
meaning_hint: You don't want a bunch of evil in your heart, do you? No, I didn't think so.
reading_hint: Acupuncture was originally invented in Asia a long, long time ago, to stop the bad chi from going through your heart.

meaning_mnemonic: If someone is headed in your <radical>direction</radical> with a <radical>gun</radical>, nock an <radical>arrow</radical> in your bow and gather your <kanji>tribe</kanji> behind you because it’s about to get real! This man with a gun is headed your direction and he doesn’t even know what you and your tribe are gonna do to him. If he gets any closer, you’re likely to shoot that arrow right at him.
reading_mnemonic: When he starts to get closer, you notice that this guy is a boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang member. Bosozoku means “running <kanji>tribe</kanji>” and this guy is running right at you. With a gun. So gather your own tribe behind you and prepare to defend yourself against this bosozoku member.
meaning_hint: This guy headed your direction with his gun doesn’t look friendly at all. Hear the sound of the bowstring as you nock your arrow and prepare your shot to defend yourself. Then imagine the sound of everyone in your tribe doing the same.
reading_hint: You remember what a bosozoku is, right? We wrote about them on Tofugu a while ago. They’re bike gang members and the “zoku” in bosozoku means tribe. Remember that article when you see this kanji.

meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> is higher than your <radical>forehead</radical> you better move those <radical>legs</radical> and find a <radical>tree</radical> to climb, because that thing is <kanji>deep</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: You wait until the tsunami is only as <kanji>deep</kanji> as your <reading>shin</reading> (しん). Then you climb down the tree and wade home in the shin-deep water.
meaning_hint: Picture this tsunami rushing past the tree you managed to climb up. It's a deep one, way higher than your forehead, but you're safe here. Legs exhausted, sure, but safe and sound.
reading_hint: Imagine all the debris floating in the shin-deep water now, bumping up against your shins. Probably hurts, considering how sensitive shins are.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> <radical>request</radical>s a <kanji>sphere</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>sphere</kanji> is a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) sphere. A strange sphere shaped cucumber.
meaning_hint: Imagine the king holding a sphere in his hand, and looking at it. It's a green sphere, and that is because...
reading_hint: Now imagine the king requesting a cucumber sphere, and holding it up to look at it. If you want, have him take a bite out of the sphere, and say "this sphere is a cucumber?" Imagine the taste of it. Imagine the smell, and its bumpy cucumber skin.

meaning_mnemonic: Those who <radical>stand</radical> on top of a <radical>village</radical> are <kanji>juvenile</kanji>. Imagine a bunch of young people standing on the roofs of buildings in the village. That's a juvenile thing to do.
reading_mnemonic: Mostly, though, the  <kanji>juvenile</kanji>s are standing on top of village buildings, eating <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts (どう).
meaning_hint: Picture all those juveniles standing on top of buildings in your village, being bored, and doing juvenile things.
reading_hint: Imagine looking up to the top of the buildings in your village. Thousands of juveniles, staring down at you and everyone else, eating doughnuts. The crumbs falling down on top of you. But, the doughnuts smell good, at least. Maybe you try to catch some crumbs.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>building</radical> has <radical>easy</radical> access to <kanji>sunshine</kanji>. The <kanji>sunlight</kanji> shines right in, because it's a clear building.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>sunshine</kanji> gets into the building so easily, in fact, that it hits your <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). The sunshine makes the yogurt... gross.
meaning_hint: Feel the sunshine coming into the building. It gets into this building so easily, and makes it very bright and warm. No matter where you look, sunshine is everywhere.
reading_hint: Picture your yogurt glistening in the sunlight. Watch as it slowly curdles and starts to smell sour. You still eat your yogurt, but it's hot and lumpy now. Sunlight and yogurt do not mix.

meaning_mnemonic: Look at a <radical>building</radical> and <radical>compare</radical> the <radical>white</radical> parts. Each of those white parts is a <kanji>floor</kanji>. Multi-<kanji>story</kanji> buildings have tons of different floors that you can see from the outside.
reading_mnemonic: One <kanji>floor</kanji> is just all <reading>kay</reading>aks (かい). You're looking at this building, and every other <kanji>story</kanji> is normal, but that one floor is kayak after kayak after kayak.
meaning_hint: All buildings have white marks that you can compare with the rest to see where the floors are.
reading_hint: Kayaks working at desks. Kayaks that are desks. Kayaks walking around checking on other kayaks. The kayak floor is quite odd. It's odd there is a floor only about kayaks.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical>, even though it's almost <radical>spring</radical>, is still covered in <radical>ice</radical> so it's <kanji>cold</kanji>. Inside the house is cold, outside the house is cold. Everything is cold!
reading_mnemonic: You can't stand the <kanji>cold</kanji> anymore, so you hire a <reading>samu</reading>rai (さむ) to come try to hack the ice off of your roof with his sword. You stand outside shivering, and watching as the samurai tries to stab the ice off of your roof.
meaning_hint: Picture the entire roof covered in ice. It's supposed to be spring right now, but it's too cold for the ice to melt off your roof.
reading_hint: The samurai isn't doing a good job stopping the cold, and soon he and his sword have frozen into your ice roof. So now it's cold and you have a dead samurai stuck to your house. Great.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>criminal</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical> will always be <kanji>sad</kanji>. No one chooses a life of crime if they're happy. And even if they seem like they're doing fine on the outside, deep down they're really sad.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>sad</kanji> feelings fill the criminal's body with <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). This heat spreads and radiates out. If you walk next to them on the street, you'll feel the sad heat floating by you.
meaning_hint: Imagine watching a criminal after he's done stealing from a bank. He's all alone. He stops throwing the money around. And cries. So so sad.
reading_hint: Imagine being so sad that your entire body fills with heat. That heat even makes squiggly heat lines pop out above and around you.

meaning_mnemonic:
If the <radical>sun</radical> is on <radical>someone</radical>, they will get <kanji>hot</kanji>. That's just how it is. Someone standing out in the sun for a long time will inevitably get hot. The sun is hot, and it makes you get hot too.

Note that this usually refers to hot weather or the heat of an area you're in and doesn't refer to a hot "thing."
reading_mnemonic: One sun was <kanji>hot</kanji> enough, but then a second one shows up. "<reading>Ah, two</reading> (あつ)!" you scream. If you were sweaty and sticky from the heat of just one sun, imagine what this second one is going to do to you. Ah, two hot suns!
meaning_hint: Imagine you're this someone, standing out in the hot sun. Feel the hot rays beating down on you. You're getting all sweaty and sticky from the heat now, ugh.
reading_hint: Feel the hot rays of sunlight beating down on you from two directions. "Ah, two hot suns is more than I can take," you gasp as your body slowly melts into a human puddle of sweat.

meaning_mnemonic: A "<radical>crab</radical> <radical>moon</radical>" is a specific <kanji>period of time</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: When the crab moon <kanji>period of time</kanji> is upon us, we must take a special <reading>key</reading> (き) and lock everything in such a way that crabs cannot get in.
meaning_hint: Imagine looking at the moon and seeing a crab on it and thinking: "oh, it's the crab moon <kanji>time period</kanji>."
reading_hint: Imagine a key with a little crab moon on it, denoting the Crab Moon period of time. Then, go around and lock everything with it and watch thousands of crabs trying to get into everything. Luckily, they can't, because you have a special key that locks things so crabs can't get in. When the Crab Moon time period is over, you can use your key to unlock everything.

meaning_mnemonic: If you want a <radical>tree</radical> to grow <radical>straight</radical>, you have to <kanji>plant</kanji> it properly. Everyone knows that poorly planted trees grow all crooked and twisted. That's just how plants work. If your tree's not straight, start over and plant it again.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>plant</kanji> and re-plant your tree over and over, utterly <reading>shock</reading>ed (しょく) at how hard it is to plant it right. You thought planting trees was easy, so this is a real shock to you.
meaning_hint: After four or five attempts, you've finally planted a perfectly straight tree. Pat yourself on the back for a job well done.
reading_hint: After planting your tree and failing again for the 100th time, you stand there in total shock. How could it be this difficult?

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Stop</radical>! That <radical>box</radical> of <radical>rice</radical> is actually full of <kanji>teeth</kanji>! I know it says "rice" on the cover, but I promise you those are all teeth and if you bite into them you'll break a <kanji>tooth</kanji> of your own.
reading_mnemonic: Why do I have a rice box full of <kanji>teeth</kanji>? <reading>Ha</reading> (は)! Wouldn't you like to know. Ha! I definitely have an answer that isn't weird or creepy. Ha... ha ha...
meaning_hint: Stop what you're doing and look into the rice box. Nope, it's definitely full of teeth. There's a grain or two of rice, but it's like 98% teeth.
reading_hint: Ha! Wouldn't you like to know why there are teeth in there? Ha. Ha ha. HA HA HA HA!

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>tsunami</radical> which washes a <radical>plate</radical> out into the <radical>sun</radical>. Because it's in the sun, it becomes  <kanji>warm</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The plate starts to get so <kanji>warm</kanji> that you could fry an <reading>on</reading>ion (おん) on it. Who doesn’t love fried onions? That plate sure is warm enough, so start cookin’ ‘em!
meaning_hint: Right after the tsunami, this plate was wet, but the sun is warming and drying the plate. Imagine looking at the plate and seeing the beads of water evaporate off it as it warms up. Feel the warm steam on your face as this happens.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself frying onions on this plate warmed by the sun. Smell the onion smell as the onion fumes get into your eyes and your sinuses. It makes your eyes water as you are very sensitive to onions.

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>tsunami</radical> coming and you want to be <radical>together</radical> with it by <radical>yourself</radical> (oneself) so you go to the <kanji>harbor</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: As you look around at the <kanji>harbor</kanji> in your last moments you suddenly see someone in the water. It's <reading>こう</reading>いち! He's sitting in an inner tube in the harbor waiting for the tsunami too.
meaning_hint: Picture the harbor. You're the only one there. The wind is whipping violently and all of the boats docked around you are being bounced around. You can see the tsunami rising out of the ocean and coming for you and the harbor.
reading_hint: Well this sucks. You wanted to enjoy being alone together with the tsunami and the harbor, but now こういち is here ruining your peace. He's floating around in the harbor like everything isn't about to be destroyed, sipping on a fancy drink in his inner tube.

meaning_mnemonic: If there's a <radical>tsunami</radical> it's <radical>easy</radical> to get <kanji>hot water</kanji>. Just stick your hand out the window as the tsunami goes by and poof! That water's HOT.
reading_mnemonic: As the <kanji>hot water</kanji> comes, you just lift up the bottom of the <reading>yu</reading>rt (ゆ) you live in, and it comes flowing in. All of the cups, pots, and barrels you laid on your floor are now full of hot water.
meaning_hint: Picture the boiling hot tsunami water as it comes for you. Psh, this will be easy.
reading_hint: You decided to live in a yurt because of the easy access it provides to hot water. They're so mobile!

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>tent</radical> of <radical>beans</radical>. You try to <kanji>climb</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>climb</kanji> the bean tent, and from this vantage point you can see <reading>とう</reading>きょう in the distance.
meaning_hint: Imagine this tent of beans. Make it huge, bulging with beans! Now, get climbing. Feel the beans on the other side of the tent's wall.
reading_hint: It's beautiful! It's breathtaking! All your climbing makes this view of とうきょう even more worthwhile!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>horn</radical>ed <radical>king</radical> on a <radical>slide</radical> catches your <radical>eye</radical> because you love what he's chosen to <kanji>wear</kanji>. You can't wait for him to <kanji>arrive</kanji> at the bottom of the slide so you can check out his outfit close-up.
reading_mnemonic: How does this king decide what to <kanji>wear</kanji> each day? He uses <reading>chalk</reading> (ちゃく) to sketch out his wardrobe ideas on a chalkboard. This helps him <kanji>arrive</kanji> at a conclusion faster.
meaning_hint: Try to picture what this king is wearing as he comes down the slide and arrives at the bottom. He can surely afford to wear the finest garments — plus, his horns give him all sorts of options for accessorizing.
reading_hint: Imagine the sound of horrible chalk squeaks as the king furiously sketches all sorts of crazy outfits on his massive royal chalkboard — all the while muttering "What to wear, what to wear?"

meaning_mnemonic: If you use an <radical>arrow</radical> to shoot a stalk of <radical>beans</radical>, the bean plant will end up <kanji>short</kanji>. When the beans are hit with that arrow, it severs the plant in two, and you are left with a stalk of beans that is much shorter than before.
reading_mnemonic: All this shooting of arrows to make the bean plants <kanji>short</kanji> is taking a long time. Since you're out in the sun, you're starting to get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). After the day is finally over, your tan is really, really dark.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself shooting this arrow. Imagine yourself nocking the arrow to the bow, and pulling the string back. That bean plant is not short enough, and you're going to do something about it.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself being out in the sun for so long that you get an amazing tan. Your skin feels warm all over from all the sun you were getting throughout the day. Feel the warmth coming off of your tanned skin.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>village</radical> <radical>beforehand</radical> (what the village was before it was a village) was a <kanji>field</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: On this <kanji>field</kanji>, the <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や) used to play.
meaning_hint: It makes sense for a big open field to have been what was there before the village was made.
reading_hint: It was sad that they had to destroy the natural habitat of the Yankees (a field) to put up the village.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>White</radical> <radical>water</radical> shoots out from a <kanji>spring</kanji> in the ground. Due to the force the water shoots from the spring, the water looks white with all the air bubbles.
reading_mnemonic: From the <kanji>spring</kanji>, a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) emerges.
meaning_hint: Imagine these geysers and the white water shooting from the springs under the ground. Now imagine yourself standing over one when the water shoots out. You’d get super wet and probably knocked over due to the force of the spring water. Imagine this happening to yourself.
reading_hint: Imagine this centaur pushing its way out from the hole in the ground, plugging the water as it squeezes through the hole. Why was there a centaur in the spring? Once it gets out, imagine the centaur standing in front of the spring as it bubbles up and starts shooting water out again. This spring belongs to the centaur.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> radical and the <kanji>life</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.
reading_mnemonic: You can save <kanji>life</kanji> or take it away with your new <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). This is the saber you got from doing the correct thing (remember?), and now you need to figure out how to use it with this life. So what do you do?
meaning_hint: Study your radicals and you'll read good.
reading_hint: Imagine having someone's life in the palm of your hands. It's up to you whether you protect it with your saber or... well... do the other thing sabers are for.

meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>death</radical> and the kanji <kanji>death</kanji> are the same.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>death</kanji> will be because of a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) accident. Oh, you didn't know? You're going to be hit by a giant bowling ball and crushed flat.
meaning_hint: This thing is deceased. It ain't alive anymore.
reading_hint: You didn't want to know what would cause your death, you say? Well that's a shame. I guess you could try to avoid bowling from now on, but that won't do any good. Mark my words, your death will be bowling-related.

meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>suit</radical> and the kanji <kanji>suit</kanji> are exactly the same! This kanji can also mean <kanji>join</kanji> which makes sense. When something suits you well, like a hat or something, it joins together with you quite well too. Join me, my hat! You suit my head well. It just <kanji>fit</kanji>s, you know?
reading_mnemonic:
You need someone who will <kanji>suit</kanji> you. Someone who can <kanji>join</kanji> you on your life journey. So you call Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち.

Note: For the ごう reading mnemonics, we use Grandpa ごういち because こういち is used for the こう reading. So just remember that ごう comes from Koichi's grandpa ごういち.
meaning_hint: Just remember that this "suit" means "to suit you" - not the thing you wear, okay!
reading_hint: Imagine you two standing next to each other, posing for a photograph. You two suit each other well. Grandpa ごういち with his gray beard, and you with your [well, you have to fill in this part by yourself].

meaning_mnemonic:
You look at a <radical>table</radical> and notice it is being used as a <radical>slide</radical> by <radical>insect</radical>s to launch themselves into the <kanji>wind</kanji>.

This kanji can also mean <kanji>style</kanji>. To remember this, consider how styles can change quickly, just like the <kanji>wind</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: You wonder what's pushing these insects to jump into the <kanji>wind</kanji> in such a manner, and you realize that it's because they've had way too much <reading>foo</reading>d (ふう).
meaning_hint: Picture a table tilted on its side with some of its legs longer than others, a perfect slide for insects! Imagine all the different insects jumping into the wind each in their own particular style.
reading_hint: The insects ate all the food that was left on the table, and now they can't just fly away into the wind like they normally do. Instead, they have to try this new style by using the table as a slide to help them take off into the wind.

meaning_mnemonic: What does your <radical>mama</radical> tell you to do in the <radical>street</radical>? Look before you cross! You gotta do it <kanji>beforehand</kanji>, or it could be too late.
reading_mnemonic: As you look <kanji>beforehand</kanji>, you see a <reading>yo-</reading>yo (よ) lying in the street.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself about to cross the street, and your mama saying in your ear: "always look beforehand"!
reading_hint: As you cross the road, you pick up the yo-yo and put it in your pocket. You're really glad you looked beforehand, because you now have a yo-yo!

meaning_mnemonic: Under a <radical>cliff</radical> there's a <radical>stool</radical>. If someone has a stool deep inside a cliff, they are most likely pretty <kanji>anti</kanji>-society, hiding away like that.
reading_mnemonic: Turns out, when you go to this guy and pull off his beard, its the <kanji>anti</kanji>-hero from Star Wars, <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo! What is Han doing here, so hidden away? He's obviously hiding from Jabba because he doesn't want to get frozen in carbonite again.
meaning_hint: Imagine someone who looks super anti-social (and a little feral). Ask them why they're sitting on this stool inside a cliff. They reply it's because they hate people. They're anti-people. In fact, they're anti-everything.
reading_hint: Make sure you get to the point where you pull this anti-social guy's beard off, to reveal Han Solo beneath. Be surprised... not just because he's Han Solo, but because he's real, too! Maybe you can yell out "Han! Your people need you!"

meaning_mnemonic:
This is a strange kanji because it doesn't really mean anything at all. In fact, what it does is it repeats the kanji that comes before it. For example: 人々 is actually 人人. This kanji shows up whenever there's a repeating kanji, because it's often easier to write than writing a more complicated kanji twice. That's why this kanji is the <kanji>repeater</kanji>. It repeats what comes before it.

To remember that this kanji is the repeater, though, think of a <radical>prison</radical> with <radical>drop</radical>s of water dripping over and over in it. Drip drop drip drop. It does that over and over again, driving the prisoners nuts. But it never stops. It just repeats over and over.
reading_mnemonic:
This symbol doesn't really have a name, but it's sometimes called the のま because it looks like the katakana characters ノ and マ put together. If you know katakana then this will be easy to read. If you don't, think of it this way:

Your mom is the <kanji>repeater</kanji>. She is trying to force feed you over and over again until you explode (literally). You keep yelling "<reading>No ma</reading> (のま)!" but she doesn't stop. She's on a repeating track, never stopping, repeating forever.
meaning_hint: Since this kanji doesn't really have a meaning as much as it does a function, try to change the way you think about it when you're memorizing it.
reading_hint: If you feel like you're so full you're about to burst, and if you imagine your mom doing this to you (how could you, ma?), you should be able to remember the reading of this kanji. That being said, the reading is mostly only useful in the event that you have to identify a 々 — otherwise, you won't see it much.

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>stand</radical>ing <radical>tree</radical> that you're taking an <radical>axe</radical> to. You're going to cut down this tree to let a <kanji>new</kanji> tree grow.
reading_mnemonic: You cut down the tree to make room for a <kanji>new</kanji> one, but it falls on your <reading>shin</reading> (しん).
meaning_hint: As you imagine yourself cutting down the standing tree, think about what new tree you'll plant in its place.
reading_hint: Only your shin got hit, so focus all your imaginary pain down there. Try to pull out the splinters. Ouch!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>scooter</radical> is <radical>anti</radical> scoot. It doesn't want to run. So you have to <kanji>return</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>return</kanji> to the store and wait in line to return the scooter but the person working returns doesn't want you to return it. He stares you dead in the eyes, waves his hand, and turns you into a <reading>hen</reading> (へん)!
meaning_hint: Unfortunately, now you have to return to the store to return this scooter, but you have no scooter to get there with. Ugh.
reading_hint: The employee leans down toward you in hen form and says, "Ooh, I'm sorry. We don't take returns from hens. You'll have to just return home."

meaning_mnemonic: If you run into a <radical>gate</radical> <radical>mouth</radical> first, you’re going to have a <kanji>problem</kanji>. Smashing your mouth into a gate is going to hurt. A lot. The problem you’re now in is one of getting to a hospital and having your mouth sewn back on. The <kanji>question</kanji> is, how are you going to get to the hospital?
reading_mnemonic: The reason you ran mouth first into this gate was because you were being chased by a <reading>mon</reading>ster (もん). A monster in and of itself is a big enough <kanji>problem</kanji> as is, let alone smashing your mouth into a gate. You poor, hapless soul.
meaning_hint: Imagine having your mouth bashed into a gate. It’s going to hurt. Imagine how painful this would be and how problematic it is. Imagine that the only question in your mind is how to get to the hospital as quickly as you possibly can.
reading_hint: Monsters are scary. Maybe you’ve had a dream where you were being chased by one. Remember how scary that is when you see this kanji.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> under which the <radical>leader</radical> can house one <radical>hundred</radical> of his people is at the local <kanji>lodge</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>lodge</kanji> <reading>shook</reading> (しゅく) as the hundred people came tumbling in through the door. It shook, but it didn't fall over. The roof stayed firmly in place, and everyone got to sleep under it okay.
meaning_hint: Back in the old days a lodge had to be huuuuge so when a leader brought a hundred people through town they'd have a place to stay.
reading_hint: Imagine being someone staying at the lodge as they came in and everything shook around you. This lodge would probably last through an earthquake too!

meaning_mnemonic: Only a <radical>tree</radical> with <radical>eye</radical>s and a <radical>heart</radical> has the ability to <kanji>imagine</kanji> and to <kanji>contemplate</kanji>. For a tree to imagine and contemplate things, it needs eyes to see and a heart to feel.
reading_mnemonic: If a <radical>tree</radical> can <kanji>imagine</kanji>, it likely has a <reading>soul</reading> (そう). Its soul is what makes it possible for the tree to <kanji>contemplate</kanji> things, you see?
meaning_hint: Can you picture a tree without eyes and a heart imagining something? Contemplating? Probably not. To imagine or contemplate, a tree definitely needs eyes to observe and a heart to experience feelings.
reading_hint: Use your soul to imagine what this tree is imagining with its soul. Is it contemplating the deep mysteries of the universe? Is it imagining where that squirrel has gone off to?

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>canine</radical> puts its <radical>mouth</radical> on your <radical>heart</radical>, it's a pretty unforgettable <kanji>feeling</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Overall, though, it's a good <kanji>feeling</kanji> — so good that you feel like Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! With this canine lovingly slobbering on your heart, you feel like you could go out and conquer the world!
meaning_hint: The feeling is kind of hard to describe. On the one hand it's warm and loving, but on the other hand it's also kind of wet and slobbery.
reading_hint: Just be careful not to let in all the Genghis Khan feelings. The confidence and motivation are good, but the feeling of wanting to wantonly slaughter your enemies is probably one you ought to repress.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>bundle</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> is the <radical>correct</radical> way to <kanji>arrange</kanji> things. Just make sure you <kanji>organize</kanji> it all before winter comes, or it'll be too late.
reading_mnemonic: But the most important things to <kanji>arrange</kanji> and <kanji>organize</kanji> are your <reading>sa</reading>bers (せい). You have an extensive saber collection that you simply must organize before winter arrives.
meaning_hint: Picture all the things you've arranged correctly ahead of winter — logs bundled for the fire, blankets for the night, and yourself, all bundled up in your neatly organized home — just as the first snowflakes begin to fall.
reading_hint: Last winter you tripped and fell on a saber you'd left lying around. Ouch, lesson learned. That's when your passion for organizing things in bundles started. You know better than anyone the value of arranging your sabers as safely and tidily as possible.

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>sun</radical> starts to make a <radical>sound</radical>, you know it's about to go <kanji>dark</kanji>. The sun isn't supposed to make a sound, so if you can hear it, it's probably about to fizzle out or something. After it's gone, obviously everything will be pitch black. Get ready to be in the dark!
reading_mnemonic: Now wandering around your house in the <kanji>dark</kanji> you start to feel hungry, so you reach blindly for some food and take a bite. Yuck! It's <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん). You don't even like anchovies, how did they get in your house? Hmm… Dark forces are at work here…
meaning_hint: Imagine a weird sound coming from the sun. Maybe a rumble, or something like a balloon deflating? Then everything goes totally dark. Welp, guess you'll be living in the dark for the rest of your life.
reading_hint: And because it's so dark, the anchovy taste is all you can concentrate on. Imagine that fishy, salty taste on your tongue, the little softened bones sticking between your teeth. Ugh.

meaning_mnemonic:
By a <radical>tree</radical>, there's a <radical>sheep</radical> in the <radical>water</radical> with a <kanji>formal name ender</kanji>. That means everyone calls him Sheep-sama. No one calls him just "Sheep," since he's kind of a big deal. He's actually like sheep royalty. When he's not in his castle, Sheep-sama enjoys playing in the water by the trees.

This can also mean <kanji>manner</kanji>, as in your manner of doing things. I guess someone with a formal name ender has a different manner of doing things.
reading_mnemonic: This sheep with the <kanji>formal name ender</kanji> only likes to play outside in the water during the <reading>summer</reading> (さま). Summer is the best season for this fancy sheep. The other seasons just can't compare.
meaning_hint: Imagine what Sheep-sama must have done to become such important and distinguished sheep royalty. Some pretty amazing things, probably, especially for a sheep.
reading_hint: Imagine playing in the water with Sheep-sama in the summer. It's crazy hot outside in the summer, so going in the water feels nice. Imagine the coolness of the refreshing water envelop your body.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>tree</radical> must've been sent from <radical>heaven</radical>, because it has a <radical>mouth</radical> and a <radical>mustache</radical>. Like some kind of special heaven tree that talks. And why was it sent? To be a <kanji>bridge</kanji>. To be the great heavenly bridge that we all, someday, must cross.
reading_mnemonic: Actually, to get across this <kanji>bridge</kanji> you have to give it some <reading>hash</reading> (はし) browns. That's what the bridge's mouth is for. Hash browns to get across the heaven bridge? Small price to pay, really.
meaning_hint: Picture this majestic tree bridge laid on its side, beckoning you into heaven with its mustachioed mouth. "Cross over!" it says in its bridge voice. Do… do you just step over the mouth? Can you wipe your shoes on the mustache, like it's a bristly doormat to heaven?
reading_hint: Hear the bridge shouting "HASH BROWNS" in its bridge voice. You stuff some hash into its mouth and it starts to chew loudly. Oh, ew… Little hash brown bits are getting stuck in its mustache. Fortunately, the bridge lets you across. Unfortunately, it turns out all of heaven smells like hash browns.

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>spirit</radical> decides to live in your <radical>scarecrow</radical>, you'll be granted good <kanji>luck</kanji> and good <kanji>fortune</kanji>. Crows won't hang out in your farm. Everything you grow will become large and delicious. But not everyone is so lucky that a spirit will decide to live in their scarecrow. So maybe the luck was in you all along!
reading_mnemonic: You want to check the scarecrow to make sure your recent <kanji>luck</kanji> is coming from it, so you kick it. "<reading>F***</reading> (ふく) you!" it yells. Oh yeah, there's a spirit in there.
meaning_hint: Nope, the luck is definitely coming from the spirit in your scarecrow. You aren't lucky on your own.
reading_hint: If you get a luck spirit to say f*** you, you might accidentally convince it to move somewhere else. If it does, you lose your luck. So be careful.

meaning_mnemonic: If you tie a <radical>thread</radical> around <radical>Wolverine</radical> and dip him in <radical>water</radical>, he'll turn <kanji>green</kanji>, like the Hulk. Some kind of weird chemical reaction. But I… I wouldn't try it if I were you. You wouldn't like him when he's green.
reading_mnemonic: As the flailing Wolverine begins to turn even more <kanji>green</kanji>, he calls out, "HELP <reading>ME DORY</reading> (みどり)!" Me, Dory? No, not you. He's calling out to that fish from Finding Nemo, another Disney IP. "Help me Dory, I'm green!" Then Dory leaps out of the water to bite through the thread and save Wolverine… THE END. Fade to green.
meaning_hint: Still, imagine pulling Wolverine from X-Men out of the water with that thread, and seeing him looking kinda green. Pretty green, but he could be greener. We have to go greener! We have to make the Hulk green with envy! So you put him back in the water, again and again…
reading_hint: These Marvel crossovers are getting ridiculous.

meaning_mnemonic: Pointing a <radical>thread</radical> to the <radical>east</radical> is what you <kanji>practice</kanji> every day. You're incredibly passionate about eastward thread-pointing, so you make sure to practice every day.
reading_mnemonic: You do all of this <kanji>practice</kanji> to try and afford all of your <reading>ren</reading>tals (れん). One day, all of this practice will pay for all of your rentals.
meaning_hint: You're absolutely obsessed with pointing your thread towards the east, so all you do is practice. Practice, practice, practice.
reading_hint: Everything in your life is a rental, so you better get to practicing. Your house, your car, your friends — all of these rentals need to be paid off!

meaning_mnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> at a <radical>temple</radical> is a <kanji>poem</kanji>. It has to be a poem, because if you say anything else it's just plain disrespectful. All of the monks and priests at this temple have to read and recite poems all day, and if they can do it forever, you can while you're visiting.
reading_mnemonic: As you recite a long <kanji>poem</kanji> at the temple you see a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in the crowd. It's opening and closing its mouth like it wants to say the poem too.
meaning_hint: Picture all of the people at the temple reciting poems together. You join in too, and it's a really powerful moment!
reading_hint:
You turn to the person next to you and realize you have to say everything in poem form.

Excuse me
I'm not a creep
so hear my plea
is that a sheep?

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Gold</radical>, at its <radical>root</radical>, is made up of <kanji>silver</kanji>. Using silver as the root of a coin is a great way to save on gold! That's why, often, if you just scratch gold, you'll find there's silver hidden underneath, at its root.
reading_mnemonic: If you want to hunt for some <kanji>silver</kanji> for yourself, all you need is a <reading>guin</reading>ea (ぎん) pig. Guinea pigs can smell silver, so get a couple and set them loose in the woods and they'll lead you straight to it.
meaning_hint: Imagine taking a gold coin and scrubbing it with some soap and water. As you scrub, you slowly see that it's really silver underneath. Dang it, silver again!
reading_hint: Guinea pigs are to silver as normal pigs are to truffles.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> shines down on your <radical>coat rack</radical> to reveal your <radical>geoduck</radical>-patterned jacket, which then becomes the major <kanji>topic</kanji> of discussion all around town.
reading_mnemonic: After a few days, the <kanji>topic</kanji> changes from the cool geoduck design to what kind of <reading>dye</reading> (だい) you used to get those real-to-life geoduck colors.
meaning_hint: Everywhere you go, you overhear someone talking about your stylish geoduck-patterned jacket. It's the only topic anyone wants to talk about.
reading_hint: Imagine how annoying it is to constantly be asked about the dye you used in your jacket. You'd really like to change the topic — some might even say you're dye-ing to change it…

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Eat</radical>ing the <radical>roof</radical> is a <radical>bear</radical>, because the roof is a <kanji>public building</kanji>. Because it is a public building bears are allowed to eat the roof.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Public building</kanji>s must make their roofs available for eating because of a law passed by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).
meaning_hint: Picture a public building, like a museum or a library, and look at the roof. There's a bear eating the roof!
reading_hint: Imagine Genghis Khan signing something and giving a speech talking about how important it is that all bears are able to eat the roofs of public buildings, as a bear in the background eats the roof.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> and a <radical>shrimp</radical> are sitting next to each other. They are both at the train <kanji>station</kanji>, waiting for a train to come and take them home.
reading_mnemonic: The train pulls into the <kanji>station</kanji>. They walk up to the train doors, but they don't open. "Perhaps <reading>a key</reading> (えき) would help?" asks the station master, as he goes over to open the door to the train.
meaning_hint: Imagine the horse and the shrimp sitting next to each other on a small bench. There is nobody else at the train station. It is awkward.
reading_hint: Instead of saying "a key" he says "eh key." How strange. Imagine the station master saying it like this with his weird accent. You ask him if he has "a key" and he replies with, "What is a key?" Then you say "eh key." He totally understands you now.

meaning_mnemonic: A cult <radical>leader</radical> has a great <radical>idea</radical> to recruit a <kanji>hundred million</kanji> new members. Since he’s the guy with all the ideas, he is the leader of the cult. His idea is to give every new member a free puppy. He’ll have a hundred million new recruits in no time.
reading_mnemonic: The cult leader has his headquarters in <reading>Ok</reading>lahoma (おく). Are there a <kanji>hundred million</kanji> people in Oklahoma? No, probably not. But hey, this leader is shooting for the stars. First Oklahoma, then the universe!
meaning_hint: Imagine a hundred million free puppies. That’s a lot. Imagine playing with all of them. You’d be pretty happy. Unless you hate puppies. Then you’d be pretty unhappy, I imagine.
reading_hint: Picture this cult leader gathering a hundred million people into Oklahoma. It’d be awfully crowded. Imagine yourself being stifled by the bodies of a hundred million other people.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Big</radical> <radical>products</radical> need to be put in a big <kanji>container</kanji>. A small container just won’t do. These products are big and they need a big container to be contained in.
reading_mnemonic: Once you get the big products into a <kanji>container</kanji>, you then lock it with a <reading>key</reading> (き). You wouldn’t want these products to bust out of their containers, so it’s best to lock them up with a key.
meaning_hint: Imagine having all these big products. How will you contain them all!? Imagine yourself stuffing all of these big products into their respective containers.
reading_hint: Imagine the size of these big keys you have to use to lock these containers. It’s a really big key. Picture yourself straining to turn the key in the lock.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>samurai</radical> radical and the <kanji>samurai</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: The most famous <kanji>samurai</kanji> in Japanese history was the illustrious <reading>shee</reading>p (し) samurai. He wasn't JUST a sheep who learned the ways of the sword, he would use the wool from the sheep enemies he killed to create his armor!
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Picture the sheep samurai. Maybe draw him on a piece of paper. He's a sheep wearing wool armor, and he has ram horns on the hilt of his katana.

meaning_mnemonic: You have a <radical>rice</radical> <radical>ladle</radical> that's specifically for paying the rice <kanji>fee</kanji>. Each scoop of something has an extra fee tacked on, so when you use a <kanji>material</kanji>, there's a fee you have to pay in that material as well.
reading_mnemonic: One day, the <kanji>fee</kanji> ladle is suddenly the size of <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat oars! What! You're supposed to pay a row boat sized fee now?!
meaning_hint: Imagine ladling up a nice big scoop of rice and then seeing the little fee ladle next to it. Ugh. The material fee.
reading_hint: That's a lot of material you have to give up every time you want to eat some rice. Maybe you should take this row boat oar ladle and row out into the ocean where no one can tax you.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>tree</radical> with a <radical>helicopter</radical> and a <radical>jackhammer</radical> tied to it. You look closer, because this is an odd sight, and realize that there is a sign here too. It's telling you to beware of helicopters and jackhammers in these woods. It's a poorly made <kanji>signpost</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: As you look at this weird <kanji>signpost</kanji> you notice a button. You press it because it will supposedly tell you more about these dangerous helicopters and jackhammers. Instead, it just yells out in a loud obnoxious voice: <reading>HEYO</reading> (ひょう)!!
meaning_hint: This is not the best signpost you've ever seen, but it's better than nothing at all, you suppose.
reading_hint: Press it a few more times and hear the sound in your head. Also spell it out in kana as you hear it as a nice little additional visual.

meaning_mnemonic: There's <radical>treasure</radical> in this <radical>tree</radical> so you take out your <radical>weapon</radical> and <kanji>kill</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: But after the <kanji>kill</kanji>, <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) appears and yells at you. She likes trees — remember how she protected one in the 札 mnemonic? — so Satsuki's pretty upset you'd kill one like that.
meaning_hint: You didn't just cut the tree down, you stabbed it and sliced it and killed it to death.
reading_hint: Here's that little girl Satsuki again. Remember how you imagined her last time? Try to have a picture of her in your mind so these さつ kanji are easier to remember.

meaning_mnemonic: Under a misshapen crescent <radical>moon</radical>, a <radical>dog</radical> <radical>boil</radical>s as much <kanji>nature</kanji> as it can find.
reading_mnemonic: Destroying <kanji>nature</kanji> like this is the dog's way of practicing <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん) Buddhism. As it breathes in the fumes from all of the different pieces of nature being boiled down, the dog gets really zen and chill.
meaning_hint: Picture a weird-looking, misshapen crescent moon casting its light onto the scene of a dog boiling up everything you'd find in nature. Trees, sticks, mud, little animals. All of that is being boiled by this dog by the light of a weird-looking moon.
reading_hint: Picture the dog kneeling in front of the pot of concentrated nature and breathing deeply as it practiced Zen. You kind of wish you could join it, considering how peaceful it looks.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>womb</radical> spins in a <radical>circle</radical> and starts to <radical>boil</radical> with <kanji>heat</kanji>. Uh oh, I think you have a <kanji>fever</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>heat</kanji> from your <kanji>fever</kanji> is making your insides and your head and your entire body spin and boil, until your doctor throws some <reading>nets</reading> (ねつ) around you. You were flailing around and they couldn't get you to stop, so they had to catch you in a series of nets.
meaning_hint: Even if you don't have a womb, just picture one anyway: it's turning around in a circle faster and faster, as the heat bubbles and boils in you. You can feel this heat spread through your entire body as you burn up with a fever.
reading_hint: These nets aren't making the fever heat go away, but they have made the spinning stop at least.

meaning_mnemonic: I always <radical>say</radical> the <radical>fruit</radical> <kanji>section</kanji> at the grocery should be at the front of the store. It’s the perfect place for the fruit section, I say.
reading_mnemonic: To get to the fruit <kanji>section</kanji>, you have to go to the store. To get to the store you have to drive a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Driving a car is the fastest way to get to the fruit section at the store.
meaning_hint: Think about it. Every grocery store has the fruit section right at the front, wouldn’t you say?
reading_hint: Picture yourself driving a car and salivating over the fruit waiting for you in the fruit section. Imagine your favorite fruit as you put the pedal to the metal in your awesome car.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>Viking</radical> opens his <radical>mouth</radical>, and below it, a <radical>shellfish</radical> sits. Grab that shellfish and throw it into his mouth to win a <kanji>prize</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>prize</kanji> you're working so hard to win is lunch with the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). This is a pretty huge prize, considering all you're doing is knocking something over.
meaning_hint: Imagine a game at a fair where you have to throw a shellfish into the mouth of a hungry Viking. You want this prize just as much as the Viking wants his shellfish.
reading_hint: You want this prize. You want to meet the Shougun. Soon you'll both be eating shellfish together!

meaning_mnemonic: For your <radical>car</radical> you use a <radical>hat</radical>, some <radical>ground</radical> and a <radical>bookshelf</radical> to build a new <kanji>wheel</kanji>. I mean, it's generally <kanji>ring</kanji> and <kanji>loop</kanji> shaped, it just needs to be a <kanji>circle</kanji>, so it should work, right?
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>wheel</kanji> is... generally <reading>rin</reading>g (りん) shaped, so that's good.
meaning_hint: Imagine this wheel you made out of a hat, some ground (presumably to keep it all together), and a bookshelf. You install it on your car and hope for the best. This is why you should buy wheels from professional wheel makers.
reading_hint: A ring-shaped wheel is better than nothing. Hopefully it will get you where you need to go.

meaning_mnemonic: You want to get a <radical>scooter</radical>, so you have one self (<radical>oneself</radical>) and another self working <radical>together</radical> to <kanji>choose</kanji>. The two tiny yous sit on each of your shoulders, like angel and devil, and argue about which scooter you should choose.
reading_mnemonic: Suddenly your selfs both gasp and <kanji>choose</kanji> a scooter in front of you that looks like a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). The scooter is designed like a human on the top, the bars are its arms, and the bottom with the seat and the wheels are all horse. This centaur scooter is what you choose to ride.
meaning_hint: Your angel self says you should choose the safest scooter. The devil self says safety is for suckers, and you should buy one that's loud and dangerous and covered in flames.
reading_hint: The angel self says, "I choose this centaur scooter because of its strong arms." The devil self says, "I choose this scooter because it looks like a badass horse monster, wooh!"

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Gold</radical> reveals itself when you <radical>stand</radical> and <radical>see</radical> into this <kanji>mirror</kanji>. Yes, even you are made of gold. You're so great and valuable, standing in front of that mirror. It's not hard to see, especially in a mirror – you're golden.
reading_mnemonic: As you look into the <kanji>mirror</kanji> you think, "<reading>Kaga</reading>… <reading>me</reading> (かがみ)?" You always forget that you're Chairman Kaga from the Iron Chef TV show. That's why you say "Kaga… me?" every time you look in the mirror.
meaning_hint: Imagine looking at yourself in the mirror only to see you're made of gold. It would be pretty shocking. Imagine how surprised you would be.
reading_hint: Picture yourself in front of a mirror, except your reflection is Chairman Kaga Takeshi. (Look up his picture if it helps.) Imagine not recognizing yourself at first, how weird that would feel, then suddenly remembering: "Kaga… me!"

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>original</radical> <radical>geoduck</radical> had a <kanji>request</kanji>. He wants there to be more geoducks in the world!
reading_mnemonic: The only person that would even hear the geoduck's <kanji>request</kanji> was <reading>Gan</reading>dhi (がん). Gandhi closed his eyes, sat down, and spread the geoduck's request to the rest of the world.
meaning_hint: Remember, this is the OG = original geoduck. There are no other geoducks yet.
reading_hint:
People listened when Gandhi said, "My friends. We must stop trying to eat the geoduck, and instead allow it to breed. Join me in this fast!"

And that's why we have so many geoducks on the earth today. Thanks Gandhi.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Sheep</radical> <radical>fins</radical> are <radical>good</radical> for <kanji>foster</kanji> children. The sheep have been genetically combined with fish, so the sheep have fins (and they've lost their tails — which is why the tail part of the sheep radical is missing in this kanji). These sheep fins are rich in nutrients, so they're good for fostering big, strong foster children.
reading_mnemonic: The best way to <kanji>foster</kanji> a better relationship with these foster kids would be to give them some <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Maybe they can dip the sheep fins in the yogurt to help mask the strange taste. Yogurt is also very nutritious, so it's a good supplement to the foster kids' diet.
meaning_hint: Imagine eating a fish fin with sheep wool on it. It tastes fishy like the ocean but the wool tastes like clothes. It's probably the worst thing ever, but try to imagine you're tasting it right now.
reading_hint: Remember how bad those sheep fins tasted? Now imagine eating your favorite yogurt flavor instead. This yogurt is delicious.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>elephant</radical>s is so great, they put up an <kanji>image</kanji> or <kanji>statue</kanji> of him everywhere. Every house, every street corner — his image looks down on all the other elephants at all times.
reading_mnemonic: When they unveil the <kanji>image</kanji>s and <kanji>statue</kanji>s of the leader elephant, everyone gasps. They look like him, but he's a <reading>zo</reading>mbie (ぞう). Gasp!
meaning_hint: For now, just imagine the leader of the elephants being told images and statues of him are being put up everywhere. Don't think about what the statue looks like, because that's next.
reading_hint: But this is exactly what the leader of the elephants asked for. You see, the leader of the elephants was a big fan of zombies. Imagine how excited he'd get to have this zombie version of himself appearing as images and statues, all across the land.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> is <radical>blue</radical>. As in, it's sad and not feeling well. This is because your soul changes colors depending on your <kanji>feeling</kanji>s or what <kanji>emotion</kanji>s you're expressing.
reading_mnemonic: Why is your soul <kanji>feeling</kanji> blue? Because <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big local farmhand, was extremely mean to you.
meaning_hint: Start with a blue soul. What feeling is that? Now a red soul. What emotion is that? Go through as many colors as you can think of, focusing on the fact that each color represents a different emotion or feeling.
reading_hint: Maybe he was just having a bad day, but that still really hurt… and now your soul’s all blue too. Seems you’ll have to walk around with a blue soul for the next few days.

meaning_mnemonic: You thought it would be a good idea to <radical>say</radical> the <radical>body</radical> <radical>measurement</radical>s of everyone in your village and now you have to <kanji>apologize</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You were told you had to <kanji>apologize</kanji> by the village <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ). At first you didn't even know why everyone was glaring at you, but the shaman took you aside and explained. And now she's standing behind you with her arms crossed as you apologize to everybody.
meaning_hint: When you do a bad thing you should apologize. And saying everyone's private body measurements is definitely something you should not have done. So it's apology time.
reading_hint: "What do we say?" the shaman says, poking you in the back. "Ugh, I'm sorry I told everyone how tiny your socks are, I guess." "And?" "Annnddd I'll never do it again."

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> in the <radical>center</radical> of the sky gives off its light, which <kanji>reflect</kanji>s off of everything around it.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>reflect</kanji>ed light is important for <reading>a</reading>corns (えい), in particular. Without light reflecting off everything, the sun's light would never make it down to the ground where they've fallen. They would never grow into trees. And we'd all suffocate.
meaning_hint: Look around you, everything you see is reflecting the light of the sun. Unless it's night time, then the tiny sun in the center of your lightbulb is creating the light that reflects there.
reading_hint: Picture the light of the sun reflecting off a single acorn on the forest floor. So much symbolism!

meaning_mnemonic: You reach for a <radical>spoon</radical> to start eating, but your <radical>mama</radical> hands you an <radical>arrow</radical> instead and points towards the <radical>coat rack</radical>. Hm, that's not like her… Suddenly you're filled with <kanji>doubt</kanji>. Is this really your mama? You doubt it.
reading_mnemonic: Now full of <kanji>doubt</kanji>, you decide to test your mama by putting her in a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). It sounds harsh, but the guillotine is the only way to clear up your doubts.
meaning_hint: She's just acting so weird. You doubt she would ever make you hunt for your own meal like this. What are you going to do?
reading_hint: With her head in the guillotine, it's your mama who starts to look doubtful now. "How could you ever doubt me?" she cries. Ugh, now you're really not sure. This guillotine thing has just compounded your doubts.

meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>compare</radical> <radical>white</radical> light to the other colors you'll realize... white consists of <kanji>all</kanji> the colors in one. It contains all the colors in the spectrum in one color. It is the one color that is <kanji>everything</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You decide white light is the ultimate color. You decide you want to put it on <kanji>all</kanji> things to make everything white. "What a great idea," you think. You try to put it on everything and you nearly succeed... except, the <reading>miners</reading> (みな). They keep getting dirty doing all their mining things. NOOO, your plan is ruined!
meaning_hint: Just look at the color white. Compare it to other colors. Now look at white again. Split it up into all the colors, creating a beautiful spectrum. Wow, it's <kanji>all</kanji> the colors!
reading_hint: Imagine everything covered in white. Everything in the world. Then, you see a dark splotch. It's the miners, they're all covered in coal, ruining all your ambitions. Walk up to them and see all the dirt on the perfectly white uniforms you gave them just a few minutes earlier. Feel enraged at them. How could they ruin all this white you made for <kanji>all</kanji> the people of the world!?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>yakuza</radical> shows you his <radical>knife</radical>. He says, "This is an <kanji>example</kanji> of a knife."
reading_mnemonic: "This is an <kanji>example</kanji>," the leader of the yakuza tells you. "Next time, I'll show you an example of a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun."
meaning_hint: The leader of the yakuza is just showing you an example of a knife. Maybe to scare you? Why would an example of what a knife is scare you, though? Coming from the leader of the yakuza, this is a weird tactic.
reading_hint: Why is the leader of the yakuza showing you examples of weapons, not to mention weapons that aren't real, like ray guns?

meaning_mnemonic: You give a <radical>lid</radical> to a <radical>person</radical> (two of them, in fact), then they put it on a <radical>cross</radical> when you <kanji>graduate</kanji>. It's a symbolic ceremony, putting a lid over a cross, but it's just what you do when you graduate around these parts.
reading_mnemonic: Uh oh, looks like you forgot your lid. They decide not to let you <kanji>graduate</kanji>. <reading>So two</reading> (そつ) more years now until you can graduate, it seems. So two more years?! Just for forgetting a lid? Nonsense!
meaning_hint: Try to picture the lid you give to the person(s), then their putting it on a cross. Why does this ceremony symbolize graduation? Maybe the persons are your teachers, and it's about the divine knowledge you've now finished collecting under your "lid," or something. Congrats?
reading_hint: Imagine walking up to the stage with no lid, and the person(s) just say, "Sorry, you can't graduate. So two more years. Next!"

meaning_mnemonic: Next to the <radical>cross</radical> we combine our <radical>power</radical> <radical>power</radical> <radical>power</radical> so that we have <kanji>cooperation</kanji>. And with our powers combined, we become stronger than we ever could have been apart, lifting the cross above our heads!
reading_mnemonic: Nowhere is the power of cooperation more apparent than in <reading>きょう</reading>と. When invaders are attacking, when the townspeople want to rebel, when there is a kitty up a tree, the people combine their power from all reaches of Japan and cross into きょうと to put a stop to the madness through cooperation.
meaning_hint: The power of cooperation is a *ahem* powerful thing.
reading_hint: Imagine being a citizen of きょうと. Watch as the powers from your neighboring prefectures march across the border to come cooperate together to stop the bandits that raided your farm. You admire their cooperation. You admire きょうと!

meaning_mnemonic: You try to be <radical>private</radical>, but because of your <radical>big</radical> <radical>hair</radical> you end up having to <kanji>participate</kanji> in everything. Can you imagine people asking for volunteers or something, and you trying to look inconspicuous with your big hair? Of course they're going to notice you and make you participate.
reading_mnemonic: You know who has big hair and loves to <kanji>participate</kanji> in everything? <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん)! Well, his hair is more big on the bottom (it's a beard) than whatever you have on your head, but hair is hair. And he participates in present-giving in every house in the world! See? Big hair = participation!
meaning_hint: When you're thinking about your next hair style, if you don't like participating in things, you should think about something you can actually keep more private.
reading_hint: Would you want to participate in Christmas if it meant looking like Santa and visiting every single house on earth?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>circumference</radical> radical and the <kanji>circumference</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: Practice how to measure the <kanji>circumference</kanji> of an item by using your <reading>shoe</reading> (しゅう). Your shoe is kind of round, and it's free. I mean it's right there on your foot.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Try to find the circumference of your shoe and then email us with the measurement. I promise you'll never forget this kanji if you actually do it.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> of a <radical>well</radical> will <kanji>surround</kanji> you if you fall into it. The mouth of a well is the opening of the well, and if you fall into that well, the mouth as well as the rest of the well will surround you.
reading_mnemonic: The well walls <kanji>surround</kanji> you as you look up to the mouth once again. This time you see an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) flying overhead. You call out to the eagle to save you and the eagle comes swooping down into the well and flies you out to safety. What a nice eagle!
meaning_hint: Picture yourself surrounded by this well on all sides. You look up and you can barely see the mouth of the well where you fell in and now you are trapped. Imagine how scared you are. Imagine trying to claw your way up the sides of the well but all you do is break your nails on the walls.
reading_hint: This eagle is basically a superhero. Imagine an eagle dressed in a superman outfit. He’s your savior. Imagine your gratitude towards this eagle superhero.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>mouth</radical> that is <radical>old</radical> is going to be <kanji>hard</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You touch the old man's <kanji>hard</kanji> mouth and it crumbles away, revealing the face of a <reading>子</reading> (こ) inside.
meaning_hint: Go ahead and touch that old man's mouth. It's super hard feeling when you touch it.
reading_hint: "You pressed the hard skin holding me captive! Thank you!" the 子 says, as they break free from their hard mouth prison.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>grain</radical>'s <radical>child</radical> will grow with the <kanji>seasons</kanji>. In spring the grain is born, it grows in summer, is harvested in fall, and dies in winter.
reading_mnemonic: All of the <kanji>seasons</kanji> have their own special <reading>key</reading> (き). As they change, that season's key appears and unlocks the next step in the grain's life.
meaning_hint: Watch the grain children live their entire lives throughout the seasons. Though there are 72 seasons in Japan, so the life of a grain is actually really long there.
reading_hint: Picture these seasonal keys floating through the sky, unlocking the weather changes, the growth of new grains, and the aging of us all.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> that became the <radical>origin</radical> of all roofs was <kanji>perfect</kanji>. It was tall and wide so it could fit and protect everyone. And the dimensions were perfect too.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>perfect</kanji> roof was commissioned by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). We got all of the carpenters and home makers and construction workers he'd conquered together and tasked them with making the first roof, but only if they made it absolutely perfect.
meaning_hint: Picture this absolutely perfect roof. They really got it right on their first try.
reading_hint: Genghis Khan was a perfectionist, and by conquering and bringing all of these different people together, he was able to create something perfect by all standards.

meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>treasure</radical> on top of a <radical>narwhal</radical> who wears a <radical>towel</radical> that will grant your greatest <kanji>wish</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: In order to open the treasure and get your <kanji>wish</kanji>, you need the <reading>key</reading> (き). The narwhal knows where it's hidden, so you'll have to convince it to tell you where the key is.
meaning_hint: Picture this narwhal: above its long horn there is a huge, shiny treasure floating. It pulls the towel tight around itself, "Brururu you found me I see!" it whistles at you.
reading_hint: If you find the key, what will you wish for? Think about your single, greatest wish. Close your eyes and picture it as clearly as you can and you'll find the key.

meaning_mnemonic: If you live in the <radical>now</radical>, there’s a good chance your <radical>heart</radical> controls your <kanji>thought</kanji> process. You don’t have time to think with your brain, you need an answer now so you use your heart to make decisions and thought process.
reading_mnemonic: You're living in the now and letting your heart control all your <kanji>thought</kanji>s. Why are you following your heart? You found you only have one <reading>年</reading> (ねん) to live, so you have to live for the now.
meaning_hint: Think about the last time you thought it was right to make a decision with your heart. What was it? Use these emotions to remember this kanji.
reading_hint: Think of all the things you'd do with your life if you only had one 年 left. Maybe you should go do some of them anyways, right now?

meaning_mnemonic: If your <radical>fingers</radical> are strong enough, you can take an <radical>axe</radical> and <kanji>fold</kanji> it in half. But be careful not to <kanji>bend</kanji> it too far, or you might just <kanji>break</kanji> it!
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, as you <kanji>fold</kanji> these axes in half you realize they come in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ), so there are twice the axes you thought there would be. To compensate, you start folding them in sets too.
meaning_hint: Imagine holding an axe between your fingers and folding it like a pretzel. Or some silly putty. Or a really soft axe.
reading_hint: How many axes can you bend at once? A set of two? What about a set of four? Or a set of forty-two?

meaning_mnemonic: Causing <radical>death</radical> at every turn, the <radical>moon</radical> <radical>king</radical> sits inside his moon, getting ready to cause death on anything in his sights. Sounds a lot like the Death Star from a certain movie. One of which is "Star Wars: A New <kanji>Hope</kanji>."
reading_mnemonic: The name A New <kanji>Hope</kanji>, as we all know, comes from the ending where the moon king's son sends a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball into that little opening and knocks it out of the sky.
meaning_hint: Now picture the death moon king as the Emperor, sitting in his Death Star. There is no hope, except for that little opening you can shoot through to destroy the whole thing. That's hope, I guess!
reading_hint: In the end, the real hope was bowling all along. I hope you've learned a valuable lesson from this space movie.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> with <radical>talent</radical> can become <kanji>lumber</kanji>. Not all trees can do this. Only the talented ones are able to turn themselves into lumber or <kanji>material</kanji>, to be used for a million different things.
reading_mnemonic: Trees can be turned into all kinds of things, but this one wants to use its <kanji>lumber</kanji> and <kanji>material</kanji> to build a <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい). It's the most difficult instrument to create, so it suits the talent level of this tree!
meaning_hint: Picture a tree standing in the forest. It's thinking so hard you can see the math equations flying around in its branches, until finally a cartoon light bulb appears above its head. This talented tree has figured out how to become lumber!
reading_hint: Watch as the tree becomes lumber and that lumber is used to craft a ginormous xylophone. Each key is its own plank of lumber!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>bundle</radical> radical and the <kanji>bundle</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: You should always <kanji>bundle</kanji> your <reading>sock</reading>s (そく). If you don't, there is a 100% chance you'll lose all of the left ones.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Imagine doing laundry and making sure to bundle your socks together so none of them will be lost.

meaning_mnemonic: When climbing a <radical>tree</radical> in <radical>public</radical>, it’s best to choose a <kanji>pine</kanji>. Pine trees offer the most coverage, so you’ll be able to hide, even out in public.
reading_mnemonic: In case you fall, someone has laid <reading>mats</reading> (まつ) out around the base of the <kanji>pine</kanji> tree. That way, you’ll land on the mats should something unfortunate happen.
meaning_hint: Imagine climbing up a pine tree. The pine needles are prickly, so your hands get pricked as you climb. Smell the pine tree smell and feel the pine sap on your hands.
reading_hint: Picture yourself falling out of this pine tree and onto the mats. The mats are nice and soft so it’s nice. Hear the whoosh of air as you collapse onto the mats.
