reading_hint: This ketsup is exactly what you were looking for, but now it won't stop. You need to get out of this bind by untying the thread on your finger. But it won't budge. It's covered in ketsup. Help! You're going to drown!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> is so <radical>big</radical> that it's the <kanji>cause</kanji> of all your problems.
reading_mnemonic: Your big mouth is the <kanji>cause</kanji> behind all your friends and family moving to an <reading>inn</reading> (いん). They move to the inn so they don't have to live with you and hear you talk anymore.
meaning_hint: Imagine all the dumb things you blabber about, with your mouth open big and wide. Saying things you shouldn't say, causing all kinds of drama. Shame on you.
reading_hint: Who knew you could cause people to live in an inn instead of their own homes with your big mouth...

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>viking</radical> with a <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>towel</radical> is a totally <kanji>normal</kanji> thing. It's <kanji>ordinary</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: It's <kanji>normal</kanji> for a viking to have a mouth towel, but your sloppy farmhand friend <reading>Joe</reading>(じょう) has a mouth towel too. That's a habit that's only <kanji>normal</kanji> for vikings, so why does he have one?
meaning_hint: Vikings get a lot of nasty stuff on their mouths from their messy eating and pillaging, so it makes sense that they'd all have mouth towels. It's as ordinary and normal as it gets, at least amongst the vikings.
reading_hint: When you ask Joe about his Viking mouth towel, he just tells you, "It's normal for everyone to have mouth towels. Totally ordinary." Now that you think about it, he really is a messy dude, so he probably needs it.

meaning_mnemonic: If you can <radical>say</radical> what <radical>sound</radical> a <radical>drunkard</radical> makes, you must be very <kanji>discerning</kanji>. Few people are able to discern what sound a drunkard makes, but you — you can say, because you're a very <kanji>discriminating</kanji> listener.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Discerning</kanji> individuals like you are considered <reading>chic</reading> (しき). What's more chic than your <kanji>discriminating</kanji> way of listening?
meaning_hint: Yes, you can even tell a tequila drunk from a vodka drunk based on the sounds they make. (Imagine how exactly they'd be different.) To be able to say what sound each kind of drunkard makes, it takes a very discerning, discriminating mind.
reading_hint: All chic people show some kind of discernment. For some it's about discerning what's fashionable. For you, it's about discriminating one kind of drunk sound from another. That's your version of chic.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>criminal</radical> has done an <kanji>injustice</kanji> on the world or other people. Being a criminal is a <kanji>negative</kanji> thing, and even if it was just a <kanji>mistake</kanji>, it was still a crime.
reading_mnemonic: When you commit an <kanji>injustice</kanji>, you'll know because <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) fills your body. Maybe you've never done any kind of big thing, but when you make a <kanji>mistake</kanji> your face fills with heat and you turn red!
meaning_hint: Think of all the criminals you know. Even if what they did was a mistake, it was still some type of injustice, right? It was something the rest of the world sees as a negative thing.
reading_hint: This is the heat of embarrassment, of knowing you did something negative or wrong.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>dry</radical> radical and the <kanji>dry</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: You need to <kanji>dry</kanji> Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). He hates being wet, all of the Mongols do, so your job is to dry his entire body. And his clothes, and his horse. In fact, when it rains, you have to ride next to him keeping him dry by holding an umbrella above his head.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Imagine keeping Genghis Khan completely dry all day every day. He won't even get wet to bathe. Instead he makes you rub his body with dry sand and salts.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> is <radical>correct</radical>, <kanji>absolutely</kanji>. The mighty sun is never wrong. It is absolutely correct, always.
reading_mnemonic: Then, a <reading>ze</reading>ppelin (ぜ) flies in front of the sun, blocking it out. <kanji>Absolutely</kanji>, the sun cannot ignore this transgression. Therefore, the sun corrects the zeppelin’s evil ways by frying it with sunshine and bringing it to the ground.
meaning_hint: Imagine the sun in the sky. Its rays blind you as it corrects all of the errors you’ve made.
reading_hint: Imagine the sun being so absolutely consumed with rage that it catches a zeppelin on fire and crashes it. Feel the heat from the grounded zeppelin as it smolders.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> is coming, and you want to <radical>walk</radical> over it, so you have to <kanji>ford</kanji> it. You don’t walk across a tsunami, you <kanji>wade</kanji> across it.
reading_mnemonic: Your ability to <kanji>ford</kanji> this tsunami impresses everyone who witnesses it, especially the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). Now that you've taught him a tsunami can be forded, he's going to start sending his troops through them instead of waiting for them to pass.
meaning_hint: Imaging taking your shoes off, hiking up your pants, and holding all of your things above your head as you ford across the tsunami.
reading_hint: Your new fording techniques have increased the Shougun's military power! Congrats, I guess.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tiger</radical>, clad in <radical>spikes</radical>, exists in the <kanji>void</kanji>. This tiger is from another dimension where tigers rule the earth, clad in armor with spikes. The void has allowed this tiger to cross dimensional planes.
reading_mnemonic: The aforementioned <kanji>void</kanji> has opened up in Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). The spiked void tigers chose to come here in order to face off against the Hanshin Tigers baseball team in battle. It will be a vicious match here in Little Kyoto. Since this is taking place in Little Kyoto and not Japan itself, the Hanshin Tigers won't have the home field advantage. Who will win?
meaning_hint: Tigers are pretty intimidating just on their own, let alone with spikey armor. Imagine this tiger, covered in spikes. It’s roaring at you. Pretty scary, huh?
reading_hint: Picture this grand match taking place in Little Kyoto. Actual tigers versus the Hanshin Tigers. It’s truly a sight to behold. The void tigers are armed with their spikes and the Hanshin Tigers are armed with their bats. Hear the crack of wood and the roar of tigers in the air as the battle commences.

meaning_mnemonic: Under this <radical>roof</radical> is a <radical>bear</radical> who works for the <kanji>government</kanji>. If you saw a bear out in the wild you might be afraid and try to run, but if you see a bear inside a building under a roof, don't be scared! It's just a government bear.
reading_mnemonic: The first <kanji>government</kanji> that allowed bears was set up by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). He was all about conquering people and then using them as efficiently as possible. He discovered that bears are incredibly useful in government buildings as government workers.
meaning_hint: Picture the government bear looking through government documents and snorting gruffly as it decides what to sign and what to reject. It has a little government sash and hat.
reading_hint: Picture Genghis Khan patting the first bear government worker on the back as they start their first day working for the government.

meaning_mnemonic: If you're on the <radical>roof</radical> of the <radical>festival</radical> you'll have to <kanji>guess</kanji> what's actually going on below you. You can kind of see that there are rides and maybe food? It smells like food. But you have to guess on the specifics because you're above it and not in it.
reading_mnemonic: To get a better <kanji>guess</kanji>, you ask <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) for help. She's that little girl you know from some earlier mnemonics, and she happens to be up on the roof with you. Picture her as you ask, "What's your guess, Satsuki?"
meaning_hint: Imagine being on the roof above a festival. Can you guess what's happening below you based on the sounds and smells?
reading_hint: And what's Satsuki doing up here, anyway? Your guess is as good as mine. Now imagine you and her trying to guess what kind of festival it is.

meaning_mnemonic: Under a <radical>canopy</radical> your <radical>clan</radical> digs in the <radical>ground</radical>, trying to get to the <kanji>bottom</kanji>. They want to build a nice home your entire clan can live in, but they need to get to the bottom before they'll start building.
reading_mnemonic: Suddenly someone down in the hole shouts that they've reached the <kanji>bottom</kanji>, but it's full of water and they're all <reading>soak</reading>ed (そこ)!
meaning_hint: Picture all of the people in your clan under this canopy digging with all their might, trying to find the bottom. "Isn't there... no bottom though?" you think to yourself.
reading_hint: The bottom is full of water, and everyone and everything down there is soaked through now.

meaning_mnemonic: Put on <radical>cleat</radical>s so you can plant a kiss on your sweetheart's <radical>forehead</radical> in the <radical>heart</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> — that's <kanji>love</kanji>. You'll be braving the harsh winter elements for love, so you put on your cleats to get a better grip on the snow. Sure, you could just wait for better weather to go see them, but then it wouldn't be true love, would it?
reading_mnemonic: Walking through the winter snow with your cleats on, you've gotta keep your <reading>eye</reading> (あい) on the prize. It's a really hard journey, but you keep your eye on the prize, which is your <kanji>love</kanji> and the time you'll get to spend with them.
meaning_hint: Picture a person you love. Feel the joy of love in your heart as you brave the winter to see them. Your cleats crunch the snow underfoot, it's cold outside, but you're kept warm and happy by that feeling of love.
reading_hint: Imagine seeing your love with your own two eyes after a long time apart. It feels great to be reunited and see eye to eye once again. Imagine this feeling.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>net</radical> is for <radical>someone</radical> who works in a <kanji>government office</kanji>. You'll also take someone from a <kanji>political office</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Government office</kanji> and <kanji>political office</kanji> people put on the best <reading>show</reading>s (しょ). That's why you want to capture someone from there with your net.
meaning_hint: Doesn't matter who, but you're going to catch someone from a government office using this net, it doesn't matter who.
reading_hint: Imagine the show they put on. You'll call your show "Exactly 8 Hours of Bureaucracy."

meaning_mnemonic: You have a <radical>poem</radical> about <radical>winter</radical> that you <radical>say</radical> to <kanji>warn</kanji> people against going outside. It's cold and dangerous in winter! You <kanji>admonish</kanji> anyone and everyone you see going out there with your scary winter poem.
reading_mnemonic: To make sure people listen to your <kanji>warn</kanji>ing, you threaten to take away their <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). People will do anything for cake, so when you bring it up, they actually take your attempts to warn them about winter seriously.
meaning_hint: Imagine the things you'd warn people about. What is it about winter that's so dangerous?
reading_hint:
The winter cold will make you ache
Your whole body will start to shake
Don't go outside
I'll eat your cake

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Again</radical> and again, your <radical>heart</radical> comes back to the same person. Is this <kanji>romance</kanji>? Are you in <kanji>love</kanji>?
reading_mnemonic: But, remember that your <kanji>romance</kanji> isn’t a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん). You really wanna reserve it for the right person!
meaning_hint: Perhaps you have a person in mind that your heart comes back to again and again. Associate that person with this notion and kanji, and this'll be easy to memorize!
reading_hint: Don’t let your love become a rental. Wait for the right person to come around.

meaning_mnemonic:
<radical>Viking</radical> <radical>see</radical>, Viking <kanji>memorize</kanji>. That’s how Vikings learn things. They see something, and then they memorize it. Quite simple, really.

It can also mean <kanji>awake</kanji>. Just remember that Vikings can't see if they aren't awake. There won't be anything to memorize if their eyes are still closed, after all!
reading_mnemonic: Every morning, a <reading>cock</reading> (かく) crows and rouses the Vikings. Seeing this, one of the Vikings <kanji>memorize</kanji>s the cock crow so that he can mimic it and play pranks on his fellow Vikings.
meaning_hint: How else would a Viking memorize something? Imagine having to memorize sea charts for your Viking navigation. Look and see the maps in your brain as you memorize them.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself memorizing the sound of a cock crow and using it to play jokes on your fellow Vikings. Cock-a-doodle-doo!

meaning_mnemonic: What do you <radical>say</radical> to a <radical>guard</radical>? Your <kanji>theory</kanji> about how the thing he's guarding isn't even real. Yeah, you have this whole thing figured out. You have for a while. At this point, it's only fair that you tell your theory to the guard who's wasting his time.
reading_mnemonic: The guard listens to your <kanji>theory</kanji> but then he says his OWN! He thinks there are two <reading>sets</reading> (せつ) of guarded items, and he's only guarding one. According to his theory, either one could be real! His theory is more plausible, don't you think?
meaning_hint: Imagine saying your theory out loud to a guard about how what he's guarding isn't even there. Imagine how he would react.
reading_hint: Which theory do you believe? That there is nothing or that there are sets of things?

meaning_mnemonic: You hide <radical>poop</radical> in a <radical>cape</radical> as part of the <kanji>illusion</kanji> in your magic show. Your acts are kind of gross and this one involves creating the illusion of disappearing poop by hiding it within the folds of your cape.
reading_mnemonic: Somehow the poop cape <kanji>illusion</kanji> goes over really well. The crowd is giving you a standing ovation and right in the center is <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん). He marches up to you, wipes a tear from his eye, and says, "Your illusion cape is wonderful. I must have it!"
meaning_hint: Imagine having poop in your cape. It would smell bad and anyone close to you would be able to smell right through the illusion.
reading_hint: Genji grabs the cape before you can stop him and he puts it on. You can see the illusion fade, he keeps smiling but more tears start streaming down his face. "A wonderful illusion, the best I've ever seen," he chokes out and marches away, without looking back.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>say</radical> stuff to a <radical>river</radical>, giving it <kanji>instruction</kanji>. This river is a new river so it needs you to say things, giving it instruction on what to do. Otherwise the river wouldn’t know how to behave.
reading_mnemonic: While giving <kanji>instruction</kanji> to the river, you see a small boat coming down it. Inside the miniature boat is a rac<reading>coon</reading> (くん) captain and his raccoon crew. The raccoon captain is instructing them, “Stroke! Stroke!”
meaning_hint: Imagine saying things to this river. What would you say? Flow harder! Babble better! Picture yourself shouting at the river as the water moves along.
reading_hint: Picture this raccoon captain and his raccoon crew coming down the river. The raccoons are all chittering about as they paddle the boat down the river.

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to <radical>say</radical> something at a <radical>ceremony</radical> for <radical>construction</radical> workers you're going to have to <kanji>try</kanji> really hard. Construction is loud, so if you want to be heard you have to <kanji>attempt</kanji> to be louder than the drilling and smashing and digging and handing out of awards.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>try</kanji> to get their attention the only way you know how, by lifting a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) above your head. The sheep baas and suddenly everything is silent, all eyes are on you. Well, they're on the sheep.
meaning_hint: Try to imagine trying this hard to be heard. The construction workers are all screaming at each other with ease. It's so hard for you to try and keep up with their volume!
reading_hint: Huh. You should have thought to try this sheep technique earlier.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>bow</radical> radical and the <kanji>bow</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
reading_mnemonic: You have a <kanji>bow</kanji> in one hand and in the other... a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう). You're going to shoot this cucumber with this bow into the salad you're making. Many people don't know this, but this makes your cucumbers taste better.
meaning_hint: Make sure you know those radicals!
reading_hint: Imagine fitting a cucumber into the string of your bow. Imagine the smell of it as you draw the string back. Mm, take a quick bite, then let it loose.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>slide</radical> covered in <radical>dirt</radical> and use your <radical>mouth</radical> to clean it off. Then, you <kanji>announce</kanji> to everyone that the slide is clean and they can use it safely again.
reading_mnemonic: After you <kanji>announce</kanji> the slide is clean, you take a big swig of <reading>Coke</reading> (こく). The dirty slide left a nasty taste in your mouth, but the Coke is helping to wash it away.
meaning_hint: Imagine announcing to all the denizens of the playground that you've licked their slide clean. For some reason, no one seems to be rushing to play on it…
reading_hint: Honestly, after that big announcement, you deserve a Coke. Public speaking's hard, so you should treat yourself to a Coke whenever you announce something — regardless of whether you had to lick something gross beforehand.

meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>grain</radical> seems extra <radical>heavy</radical>, you know it's a different <kanji>kind</kanji>. Not all <kanji>type</kanji>s of grains are the same. So when you feel the heavy type, you know it isn't the same as the light kind.
reading_mnemonic: You sort all of the different <kanji>kind</kanji>s and <kanji>type</kanji>s by putting them in the right <reading>chu</reading>tes (しゅ). They slide away, probably to some big type-based piles you can't see.
meaning_hint: Imagine sorting all the different types of grains by their weight.
reading_hint: Picture the two chutes you use to separate the different types of grains. One has a sign that says "Light Chute" and the other says "Heavy Chute."

meaning_mnemonic: You ride your scooter through the dirt while carrying a sheep because you're determined to attain something. Greatness? Glory? Everlasting fame? Maybe you'll attain a plural combination of all those things. After all, you are the best sheep-carrying off-road scooterist the world has ever seen.
reading_mnemonic: After you <kanji>attain</kanji> that <kanji>plural</kanji> combination of glory, greatness, and fame, you get a <reading>tattoo</reading> (たつ) to celebrate: "world's greatest," in gothic lettering, above a picture of you and your sheep on your scooter.
meaning_hint: Feel the dirt spatter your face as you zip around off-road trails with your sheep under your arm. You will attain glory! You will attain greatness!
reading_hint: Feel the pain of the tattoo needle as you get this commemorative tattoo. You're getting this tattoo on your forehead, of course, because you want to make sure everyone sees it.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>rice</radical> you feed to a <radical>big</radical> <radical>geoduck</radical> can be of any <kanji>type</kanji>, <kanji>kind</kanji>, or <kanji>category</kanji>. It doesn't matter what kind of rice it is, the big geoduck will eat it.
reading_mnemonic: While eating every <kanji>type</kanji> and <kanji>kind</kanji> of rice from every <kanji>category</kanji>, the big geoduck has become even bigger. Its body is <reading>rui</reading>ned (るい). It's painfully obese. This geoduck has eaten itself to ruin.
meaning_hint: Picture this big geoduck shoveling rice down in a flurry of geoduckness. Hear the sloshing and goopy glugging as the geoduck gorges itself on every type of rice.
reading_hint: Picture this big, rice-engorged geoduck, too big to move. It's now in a wheel chair and on a rice IV. Imagine its wheelchair squeaking by as it wheezes and groans — ruined by all those types of rice.

meaning_mnemonic: You want to share <radical>happiness</radical> with people so you <radical>stamp</radical> <radical>stool</radical>s with the <kanji>news</kanji>. Now when people want a <kanji>report</kanji> of all the happy things going on, they can just look down before they sit.
reading_mnemonic: You research and <kanji>report</kanji> the <kanji>news</kanji> from <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Only after, once all of the news is stamped onto the stools, do you leave your home.
meaning_hint: Stand up and look at the happy news today! It definitely isn't sad or depressing.
reading_hint: Because your reporting is done from home, most of the news is about how your plants are growing. What the cat did this morning. And it certainly isn't the news without the weather!

meaning_mnemonic: If you see a <radical>spirit</radical> with an <radical>axe</radical>, you better <kanji>pray</kanji> for your life. Spirits only hold axes if they're thinking about murder, so pray as hard as you can to try and get it to spare you.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>pray</kanji> and pray and pray and nothing happens. You stop praying and dare to open your eyes. There is a <reading>key</reading> (き) in front of you. Grab the key and hold it before you and pray for the spirit to take it. Moments later, both the key and the spirit are gone.
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to pray as hard as you can as a spirit with an axe closes in on you. Can you hold your prayer concentration long enough?
reading_hint: I guess the key was prayer all along. ( ͡° ͜ʖ ͡°)

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>temple</radical> is <kanji>equal</kanji> to all of the other non-bamboo temples. Just because it's made of bamboo doesn't make it any less special and spiritual.
reading_mnemonic: The bamboo temple is also an <kanji>equal</kanji> distance away from the other temples in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. When you look at a map of とうきょう temples, you can measure them out and they'll all be perfectly equal.
meaning_hint: Picture this bamboo temple compared to some temples made of stone or wood. It's the same size, shape, and it has the same number of worshipers. It's equal in every way.
reading_hint: While many things in とうきょう may not be created equal, the temples are, at least.

meaning_mnemonic: The water from a <radical>tsunami</radical> can create a lot of <radical>energy</radical> when made into <kanji>steam</kanji>. Steam power used to be all the rage back in the day. Steam comes from water, water comes from tsunamis, so logically, tsunamis are a great source of energy.
reading_mnemonic: In order to contain this <kanji>steam</kanji> energy and store it for later, you lock it up in a box with a <reading>key</reading> (き). That way the energy can be used at a later time. Provided you don’t lose the key, that is.
meaning_hint: Imagine how much steam a tsunami could create. Hear the steam hissing off of the water and feel the moisture from the steam washing over your body.
reading_hint: Steam is very light, so carrying this box of steam energy will be no problem. Picture yourself wandering about with a box of steam in one hand and a key in the other.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> from <radical>long ago</radical> thought to <kanji>borrow</kanji> something precious from you. This leader did not return the thing he borrowed, and now he will pay.
reading_mnemonic: In your rage, you decide to find this leader, take back what's yours, and in return, <kanji>borrow</kanji> the leader's life. So you found him, grabbed him, and threw him into a <reading>shack</reading> (しゃく) — where you "borrow" a few parts of the leader himself, and do other terrible things to him.
meaning_hint: Think about what precious thing this leader borrowed from you so long ago. What was it? Think about how angry you are about not having the thing returned to you.
reading_hint: Come up with all the terrible things you do to this leader in the shack. Hey, it's just a little payback for the borrowing he did.

meaning_mnemonic:
In a dangerous <radical>fire</radical>, a <radical>gambler</radical> will always try to <kanji>bake</kanji> something before fleeing to safety. It could be his own house on fire, and the gambler would still use the free heat source to get some baking done. Maybe he's gambling that those baked goods will offset the cost of a new home…

This kanji also means <kanji>burn</kanji>, because that's what happens when you expose something to a lot of heat.
reading_mnemonic: He used to <kanji>bake</kanji> for the <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や). Baseball cakes, bat baguettes, that kind of thing. A career-ending kitchen injury forced him to retire. That's when he developed a gambling problem. But he still likes to bake whenever the Yankees are on TV.
meaning_hint: Imagine you're the gambler. Feel the heat intensify as your nostrils fill with the smell of baked goods.
reading_hint: Imagine this guy running around baking in the fire of his home, only this time with a Yankees hat and jersey on. He doesn't bake for them anymore, but he still loves the team.

meaning_mnemonic: Under a <radical>canopy</radical>, there are two <radical>person</radical>s on the <radical>dirt</radical>. What do they do there? They <kanji>sit</kanji>. The people use the dirt under the canopy as a <kanji>seat</kanji> for their butts.
reading_mnemonic: Who decided people have to <kanji>sit</kanji> like this? The C<reading>za</reading>r (ざ). The Czar wants to be above everyone, so he makes them sit on the dirt under canopies while he goes around on horseback or something, towering over them as they sit.
meaning_hint: Imagine how it feels sitting on dirt. Is it comfortable? Probably not. Maybe you feel a little uneasy about just sitting on plain dirt. Try to recall this feeling whenever you encounter this kanji.
reading_hint: This Czar you met is ruthless and has formally banned all non-sitting activities. Imagine the excruciating pain, the stiffness, the cramping in your legs — all caused by this 24-7 sit-a-thon the Czar has cruelly decreed.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>death</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> will cause you to <kanji>forget</kanji> all of the most important things. People think memories are kept in your brain, but that's not true. Your heart is where all of your most precious memories are kept, and when it dies, you forget them all.
reading_mnemonic: When someone <kanji>forget</kanji>s everything they're left with only one thing to do: go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). Bowling is easy, and there's nothing to forget. You pick up a ball. You drop it. Everything else is automatically taken care of.
meaning_hint: Imagine someone close to you experiencing the death of their heart. They look right at you but they've already started to forget everything. I know this is not a fun thing to imagine, but use that emotion to help you remember this kanji, not forget it.
reading_hint: You know, that's probably why so many old people go bowling. They forgot everything else.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>previous</radical>ly gave everything in this town a good <kanji>wash</kanji>. There are many laundromats in this town, so when the previous tsunami hit, all of the detergent mixed with the water and gave everything a good wash.
reading_mnemonic: It wasn't just the town that got a <kanji>wash</kanji>, it was the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん) too. (Centaurs happen to live in this town.) And being half horse, centaurs have a lot of hair that needs washing. Luckily, the soap tsunami gave them a good wash too.
meaning_hint: Imagine this soapy tsunami, washing all the things in town. Hear the soapy waves bubbling with cleanliness as they crash down around you.
reading_hint: Imagine these centaurs frolicking around in the aftermath of the tsunami previous. It's just like a big bathtub for them. Hear them splashing and neighing in the water.

meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>moon</radical> <radical>prison</radical>, there lies a <radical>treasure chest</radical> containing a golden <kanji>chest</kanji> piece. This mighty chest armor will grant you protection from nearly any attack, because it is imbued with moon magic.
reading_mnemonic: Where is this <kanji>chest</kanji> armor from? It's from the moon. Now how would you say that if you were Canadian? It's from the <reading>moon, eh</reading> (むね). Right, this here chest armor is from the moon, eh.
meaning_hint: Imagine finding this treasure chest and donning the mighty chest armor. Feel the weight of the golden chest piece and the vast protection it grants you.
reading_hint: If you can remember this kanji's reading and meaning with a Canadian accent, you'll have no problem, eh.

meaning_mnemonic: Nestled in the light of the <radical>moon</radical> in the <radical>grass</radical> is a <radical>treasure chest</radical> that you can see with your <kanji>brain</kanji>. You close your eyes, and you can see the spot, but it's kept deep in your brain, and you have to use its power to find it.
reading_mnemonic: If you can unlock the secrets of your <kanji>brain</kanji> you'll have powers like <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう). Nostradamus was able to look inward at his own hidden brain treasure chest and see the future, so maybe you can be like him too.
meaning_hint: As you visualize the treasure chest you realize that the moon was your face, the grass your hair, and the treasure chest your brain! It was you all along!
reading_hint: Use your brain like Nostradamus and try to see the future. You see... you see... huh, your brain is just surrounded by kanji.

meaning_mnemonic: A religious <radical>leader</radical> wearing a <radical>mask</radical> could be either a <kanji>monk</kanji> or a <kanji>priest</kanji>. You don’t know, their face is covered by a mask!
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>monk</kanji> or <kanji>priest</kanji> is wearing a mask to protect their <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). You aren't a monk or a priest, so they don't want your not-religious-enough eyes to dirty their soul.
meaning_hint: Picture this leader in their mask. Listen to them chant prayers that reverberate throughout the building.
reading_hint: Imagine the monk/priest's bright, pure soul shining behind the mask. You'll never actually see it, unless you're becoming a monk/priest yourself, and then your soul will need protecting too.

meaning_mnemonic: You want your <radical>spirit</radical> to be <radical>simple</radical> so you practice <kanji>Zen Buddhism</kanji>. Zen is all about meditating and letting earthly things go. There's nothing more simple for your spirit than being <kanji>Zen</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The reading for this kanji is <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん), just like the <kanji>Zen</kanji> in <kanji>Zen Buddhism</kanji>. Pretty easy to remember, no? Just remember that the reading is the same as the meaning. It’s easy!
meaning_hint: Try out Zen meditation for a little bit. Look away from your computer or phone or tablet and let your life be simple for a second. Just let it all go and free your spirit from all the noise.
reading_hint: Without a new reading to learn, you should have time to close your eyes and clear your mind. Zen it out, friend.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> and a <radical>squid</radical> are going to <kanji>test</kanji> you.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>test</kanji> is being supervised by <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai.
meaning_hint: Imagine the horse and squid in a big testing room, getting ready to test you. What are they testing you on? Weirdly, they're going to test you on all the kanji that use horse and squid radicals. You ready?
reading_hint: To make sure you don't cheat on the horse and the squid test, Ken the samurai is watching you closely, pacing up and down. Every now and then, he waves his katana menacingly.

meaning_mnemonic: With a <radical>lip ring</radical>, anything is <kanji>possible</kanji>. Having a lip ring grants you the power to conquer any obstacle, achieve any goal. As long as it's a <kanji>passable</kanji> lip ring, that is. If it isn't good enough, it isn't possible to be passable.
reading_mnemonic: Since anything and everything is <kanji>possible</kanji> with your new <kanji>passable</kanji> lip ring, you use it to get a new <reading>ca</reading>r (か). You go to the car dealership and ask for a free (passable) car. The manager is impressed with your lip ring and gladly hands over the keys to the latest model of your favorite car. Not bad.
meaning_hint: Imagine getting a magical lip ring. First, you have to find a passable one. Then, you have to go through the pain of having your lip pierced. Is it even possible to imagine how painful that would be?!
reading_hint: It was possible to get this passable car thanks to your lip ring. Hooray!

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Say</radical> it at <radical>noon</radical>. That's the only time I will <kanji>allow</kanji> you to say it. That's the only time I will <kanji>permit</kanji> you to say it. Other than that, no saying anything. Noon is your window.
reading_mnemonic: Where do these archaic horrible rules exist? The smaller version of Kyoto, Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). Normal Kyoto is just fine. This smaller one is where a group of people went to create their own little town with their own dumb rules.
meaning_hint: Imagine a world where you're not allowed to say anything except for at noon. At that moment, after you've been staring at the clock, waiting for that opportunity, you burst forth with things you want to say. But, once it hits 12:01, you are grabbed and your mouth is covered. You are not permitted to say anything outside of that time.
reading_hint: Imagine going to big Kyoto where everything is fine. Then, go to little Kyoto where it's not. Little Kyoto has the short きょ (instead of the long one), and this is how you can remember the difference.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> that's been sliced up in <radical>winter</radical> can be used as a <kanji>flat object counter</kanji>. Since it's winter, all the leaves have fallen off the tree, so it's easy to cut into slats. These slats can be used as counters for flat objects.
reading_mnemonic: As you count each <kanji>counter for flat objects</kanji>, a bunch of <reading>mi</reading>ce (まい) show up and start climbing all over you. This won't do, so you take two slats and start smashing and flattening all the mice. Now that the mice are flat too, you can also use them as a counter for flat objects. Good job, mice.
meaning_hint: What with the snow and wind, winter also tends to knock trees down flat… which is probably why the tree radical on the left looks all flat and squished here. That's another way to remember this kanji is a counter for flat stuff!
reading_hint: Now imagine yourself counting with these mice you just smashed. They're freshly dead, so there's not much of a stench yet, but you still feel bad about using dead mice to help you count. Imagine yourself counting with these mice as you try to hold back tears from all the murder you just did.

meaning_mnemonic: Within the <radical>blue</radical> water, a <radical>conflict</radical> stirs, but is made <kanji>quiet</kanji> by the muffling power of liquid. Under this blue water is a great conflict between two sea monsters, but on the surface it appears peaceful and quiet as the water prevents sound from rising above the surface.
reading_mnemonic: As you watch below the <kanji>quiet</kanji> water, every once in a while a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) breaches the surface and quietly slices back down.
meaning_hint: Imagine watching this conflict from above the blue water on a boat. You can kind of see the action, but you can't hear a thing.
reading_hint: Sabers are sharp, curved, and totally quiet. Great for underwater blue conflicts, and sneaky assassinations.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>paragraph</radical> radical and the <kanji>paragraph</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
reading_mnemonic: You have to write an entire <kanji>paragraph</kanji> about a topic, so you choose <reading>coo</reading>kies (く).
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Imagine actually getting an assignment to write a paragraph all about cookies. Would you write about your favorite cookies? Your eating methods?

meaning_mnemonic: Killing two <radical>tree</radical>s with a <radical>jackhammer</radical> is expressly <kanji>prohibit</kanji>ed. Don't get me wrong, you can rip up one tree with your jackhammer, but not two.
reading_mnemonic: The person who decided to <kanji>prohibit</kanji> jackhammering two trees is the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. He's the only person who's able to prohibit certain things, so it had to be him.
meaning_hint: Imagine standing in front of a forest with your jackhammer, staring at a sign with just this kanji on it. Tree plus tree with jackhammer equals prohibited. Hmm.
reading_hint: Picture the King angrily covering his ears as his subjects jackhammer trees. He screams and shouts, his crown bouncing on his head, and points at his new sign that prohibits the jackhammering of trees.

meaning_mnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> you put your <radical>life</radical> on the line by putting in a <radical>sword</radical> that is <radical>big</radical>. You put it all the way down there, so you <kanji>consume</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: The "<kanji>consume</kanji> a sword" illusion came with a cheap magic <reading>kit</reading> (きつ) you had.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself doing a magic trick where you put a big sword in your mouth. You say: "Now, for my next illusion, I will consume a sword!"
reading_hint: It's odd that there would be a "consume a sword" trick in your magic kit. It seems so real for a kit you can buy at the store.

meaning_mnemonic: If you have a <radical>fire</radical> and a <radical>helicopter</radical> hovering beside it, there's going to be a lot of <radical>dirt</radical> and <kanji>smoke</kanji> billowing around. Mostly smoke, on account of the fire. Now it's in your eyes and mouth. It gets in your lungs and you can't breathe. Why, helicopter, why!
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>smoke</kanji> is too much and you realize this is the <reading>en</reading>d (えん) for you. You're gonna die here!
meaning_hint: Imagine the smoke stopping up your lungs and getting in your eyes. All you know other than the smoke is the sound of the helicopter that's causing it to happen.
reading_hint: Imagine choking on the smoke and accepting that this is the end. Boohoo, if only you'd learned more kanji before the end!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>power</radical> of your <radical>mouth</radical> lets you <kanji>add</kanji> words together to form a sentence. Marvel at the mighty additive power your mouth holds! You add words together in an amazing fashion!
reading_mnemonic: Your parents are so proud of you being able to <kanji>add</kanji> words together that they surprise you with a new <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Wow! You add some stickers to your bumper and add your favorite playlist to the car radio. Let's drive.
meaning_hint: Say this sentence out loud. Incredible! You've used the power of your mouth to add words together in a sentence. Your parents must be very proud of you.
reading_hint: Imagine riding around in your new car, singing songs with your powerful mouth to the playlists you added to the fancy new radio.

meaning_mnemonic:
On the bottom of <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>root</radical>s, there’s a <radical>stamp</radical> that tells you the <kanji>season</kanji> in which it was cut.

Additionally, the stamp also has information on the condition of the bamboo’s <kanji>joint</kanji>s, because no one wants to buy bamboo with damaged joints!
reading_mnemonic: These <kanji>season</kanji> stamps are marked on bamboo <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). You can't buy a single banana or a single grape, and just like that, if you want some nice seasonal bamboo, you have to buy them in sets.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself in a store, looking at a bunch of different bamboo stamps to make sure you don’t buy one that’s out of season, or one that has damaged joints. The season and the joints, that’s what quality bamboo is all about, and it’s why we have these stamps!
reading_hint: How many bamboo sets will you be buying this season? I'm getting 42 sets.

meaning_mnemonic: Direct a <radical>tsunami</radical> into a <radical>canine</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical> to <kanji>decrease</kanji> the amount of water that strikes the surrounding area.
reading_mnemonic: After you and your canine <kanji>decrease</kanji> the tsunami's power, <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん) comes out to thank you. That tsunami was on track to wash away Genji's house, which would have forced him to majorly decrease his frivolous playboy activities.
meaning_hint: Just make sure you get a really thirsty canine, because they're going to need to drink a LOT of water to decrease the tsunami's power.
reading_hint: Now you're wondering if you made the right choice. The world might actually be a better place if Genji decreased his silly lover-boy antics a bit.

meaning_mnemonic: When they migrate to a <radical>river</radical>, a group of <radical>geoducks</radical> must do so in a certain <kanji>order</kanji> and <kanji>sequence</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Actually, they go in birth-month <kanji>order</kanji>, starting from <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). No one knows why they follow this June-based <kanji>sequence</kanji>. But the geoducks born in June always go first in the migration order.
meaning_hint: Geoducks usually live in the ocean, but when they migrate to a river, they do so in a specific, predetermined order. Imagine what that sequence might be. Maybe in order of neck length, with the longest-necked geoducks going first? Or maybe in alphabetical order?
reading_hint: Imagine it's June, and you're on a beach somewhere watching a bunch of geoducks make their way to a river. Marvel at the biological clock that determines the June-based migration sequence for these fascinating creatures.

meaning_mnemonic: If you put a <radical>roof</radical> over a <radical>valley</radical> it will change its <kanji>form</kanji>. Its <kanji>appearance</kanji> will alter completely!
reading_mnemonic: Its new <kanji>appearance</kanji> looks familiar to you, like it's a big <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう) cup! A valley with a roof on it looks exactly like a yogurt cup. Huh!
meaning_hint: Picture a valley in your mind. Now put a roof on it. Hmm. It isn't <kanji>shape</kanji>d like a valley anymore.
reading_hint: Now imagine this taking shape before your eyes and becoming actual yogurt! You grab a giant spoon and take a bite.

meaning_mnemonic: To a <radical>narwhal</radical>, a <radical>towel</radical> is just normal <kanji>cloth</kanji>. They're always wet so towel cloth is all they know.
reading_mnemonic: If you use this type of <kanji>cloth</kanji> for your clothes and blankets people will call you a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). They think you're a fool who can't tell the difference between human cloth and towel cloth.
meaning_hint: Picture a narwhal wrapped in a fuzzy towel blanket, with a towel tail cover, and some towel underpants. Everything is made of this type of cloth.
reading_hint: "You fool, you aren't a narwhal, that cloth isn't for you!" they say. They're so mean.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>easy</radical> radical and the <kanji>easy</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: What would be an <kanji>easy</kanji> reading for this kanji? <reading>い</reading>! The sound easy starts with!
meaning_hint: Easy is easy!
reading_hint: I told you easy was easy.

meaning_mnemonic: Give some <radical>shellfish</radical> to a <radical>talent</radical>ed person and they'll have no problem accumulating <kanji>wealth</kanji>. People with talent can make money out of pretty much anything, so shellfish are like easy-mode.
reading_mnemonic: This talented person really starts accumulating <kanji>wealth</kanji> when their <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい) is unveiled. It's a half-man, half-shellfish cyborg that plays the <reading>xy</reading>lophone  (ざい), and everyone wants to buy it.
meaning_hint: Picture the talented person huddled over a pile of shellfish, whispering to them about the wealth they'll soon be turned into.
reading_hint: You'd be wealthy too if you sold that cyborg you made. It may not have any shellfish in it and it can't play the xylophone, but you could change that…

meaning_mnemonic: There are <radical>flowers</radical> on your <radical>right</radical> to show that you're still <kanji>young</kanji>. Your body still works, you're happy, you have your entire life ahead of you! And everything smells like flowers.
reading_mnemonic: When <kanji>young</kanji> people look to the right, they'll see their future: a <reading>walke</reading>r (わか). You know, those metal contraptions with tennis balls on the bottoms to make sure they don't scrape the floor. Everything may be flowers and happiness now, but your future is in a walker.
meaning_hint: Look to your right, if you see flowers (metaphorically, maybe) then you're still young. Congratulations!
reading_hint: All young people have a walker looming just off to the side. A sign that your youth won't last forever, dear summer child.

meaning_mnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> to the <radical>director</radical> is judged immediately. You're having trouble understanding this <kanji>part of speech</kanji>, so he's trying to help you. But you say it over and over and he keeps saying you're wrong.
reading_mnemonic: The director asks you again, "What is the part of speech for the word <reading>shee</reading>p (し)?"
meaning_hint:
"Is it an adjective?"
"No, that's the wrong part of speech."
"Is it a verb?"
"N-no! Of course not!"
reading_hint: You stare blankly at him. What part of speech is that?! It's an animal, you know that. Oh! A noun. Duh.

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>sun</radical> is <radical>compare</radical>d to an <kanji>insect</kanji>, you’ll see that they're both shiny, but that the sun is much shinier than the insect.
reading_mnemonic: While you’re holding up an <kanji>insect</kanji> to the sun for comparison, a flock of <reading>con</reading>dors (こん) flies by, blocking out the sun. There are so many condors that they manage to totally block out the sun.
meaning_hint: Imagine holding up an insect to the sun to compare the two. See how they both shine, but the sun shines more brightly than the insect.
reading_hint: Feel how dark and cold it is suddenly with all those condors blocking out the sun.

meaning_mnemonic: Passed out in front of your <radical>gate</radical> lies a former <radical>leader</radical>. He's now a <radical>drunkard</radical>, but once ran a popular <kanji>clique</kanji> called the Cool Kids <kanji>Clan</kanji>. The clan has fallen apart, and he no longer has a clique to call his own.
reading_mnemonic: Eventually you get sick and tired of this washed up <kanji>clan</kanji> leader and you gather your own <kanji>clique</kanji> to chase this guy away from your gate and your property. You and your friends grab some <reading>bats</reading> (ばつ) and use these bats to beat the drunkard leader. Being beaten with bats frightens him, and he scurries away.
meaning_hint: Imagine this washed up drunkard leader passed out in front of the gate to your house. You have to pass him whenever you enter or exit your house. He's gross and he smells. Experience the stench in your nostrils. How he was ever the leader of a clan you have no idea.
reading_hint: Hear the crushing blows of bats against flesh as you imagine this scene in your mind. You're beating the crap out of this guy with a bunch of bats, but hey — he had it coming.

meaning_mnemonic: At the edge of the <radical>cliff</radical>, two <radical>tree</radical>s mark the spot where you must <radical>stop</radical>, since any <kanji>continuation</kanji> requires a way across. The trees seem long enough to reach to the other side, but how are you going to chop them down?
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Continuation</kanji> is impossible unless you chop these trees down to make a bridge to the other side. You decide to use a <reading>wrecki</reading>ng (れき) ball. Luckily, there's one right by you. So you take the wrecking ball and knock the trees over, making a bridge with them.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself on this cliff. You desire a continuation for your journey, not wanting to stop here. Feel the strong wind on your body, blowing your hair about as you stand on the edge of the cliff. The leaves on the trees rustle in the breeze.
reading_hint: Picture yourself using the wrecking ball to knock down the two trees on the cliff. Hear the the trees crack and splinter from the force of the wrecking ball. That wrecking ball is the sound of continuation.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tongue</radical> radical and the <kanji>tongue</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Yee-haw!
reading_mnemonic: You’re sound asleep in bed when your <kanji>tongue</kanji> drops out of your mouth and you start to drool, getting saliva all over the bed <reading>sheet</reading> (した). You wake up with a soggy patch of sheet near your mouth and wipe the drool off your face. Your significant other sees the whole thing.
reading_hint: Your significant other had been watching you sleep with your tongue out for a while now, so you're super embarrassed you wet the sheets with your nasty tongue drool. You feel very judged and embarrassed.

meaning_mnemonic: You use a <radical>bookshelf</radical> as a <kanji>book counter</kanji>. Duh. What, are you gonna count them one by one? Okay, well maybe, but you should count them while they're nice and organized on the shelves. Otherwise counting is going to be a huge pain.
reading_mnemonic: You use this <kanji>book counter</kanji> method to count all the books <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) has in her house. Remember her? She's got, like, a ton of books, but she's just a little girl so she needs some help. That's where your book counter know-how comes in handy.
meaning_hint: Imagine counting books on a bunch of bookshelves. One book, two book, red book, blue book!
reading_hint: Imagine how tedious it'd be to watch her counting one by one. Teach her your book counter ways!

meaning_mnemonic: You use a <radical>roof</radical> to keep yourself <radical>dry</radical> and safe from <kanji>outer space</kanji>. Look outside, above your roof, and you'll see outer space stretching above you.
reading_mnemonic: Why isn't it dry in <kanji>outer space</kanji>, you ask? Because of all the <reading>u</reading>ni (う) swimming around up there. They have nasty, wet, pointy spikes and they're all over the place.
meaning_hint: You don't want to float up into outer space and get all wet, do you? No? Then you better make sure you have a roof above your head.
reading_hint: Look closely up at outer space and you'll see all of the uni floating around up there. They're really dark, so it can be hard to notice them, but I promise you, they're there.

meaning_mnemonic: You jumped off your <radical>roof</radical> for some <radical>reason</radical> and now you're floating in <kanji>midair</kanji>! You kick your legs a bit and move your arms like you're in water, but you're still just stuck there in midair.
reading_mnemonic: As you spin a bit in <kanji>midair</kanji> you look up and see <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう) looking down at you from a spaceship. He's laughing his Chewbacca laugh and you realize he has you trapped in his tractor beam.
meaning_hint: At the moment you can't remember the reason you did it, but when you jumped off your roof you just floated in midair.
reading_hint: Oh Chewbacca, you lovable fuzzball. You better not drop me in midair, or I'll die. I hope you bring me up there!

meaning_mnemonic: You'll work your <radical>soul</radical> to <radical>death</radical> if you're too <kanji>busy</kanji>. Being busy just isn't good for your soul. Take breaks. Go on vacation. Don't be so busy that you destroy yourself.
reading_mnemonic: Take time out of your <kanji>busy</kanji> life and go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) once in a while. In fact, you should probably go bowling once a week at least. Nothing brings energy back to your life like some relaxing bowling.
meaning_hint: Look around you. See all those busy people? Their souls are dying. Don't end up like them.
reading_hint: Think about your busy schedule this week. Now cancel everything and just go bowling instead.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>flag</radical> <radical>loiter</radical>s in a <radical>black hole</radical>. This flag marks (in the black hole) where you left your <kanji>footwear</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You grab your <kanji>footwear</kanji> from the black hole. In your footwear (thanks to the shelter they provided), some <reading>ree</reading>ds (り) have grown in!
meaning_hint: If you look into the black hole, you can see your footwear right there, marked with the flag.
reading_hint: It's amazing that these cute little reeds could grow in your footwear in the middle of a black hole. As a great philosopher once said, life finds a way.

meaning_mnemonic: Use your <radical>mouth</radical> to take a <radical>measurement</radical> of this <kanji>group</kanji>. Just stretch your mouth out and around every single person. Get 'em all in there.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>group</kanji> is so big it takes you till <reading>dawn</reading> (だん) to get your measurement: they weigh a <reading>ton</reading> (とん) too!
meaning_hint: I hope this group is full of people who enjoy being inside someone else's mouth, because they're gonna be pretty mad when you let them out if they aren't.
reading_hint: Imagine letting this group of, now very wet, people out of your mouth one by one. They don't seem to care that they weigh a ton! That's a large measurement, they should be proud.

meaning_mnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> gets <radical>together</radical> with <radical>water</radical>, only <kanji>violence</kanji> can ensue. They don’t mix well together. The sun loves to fight water and water loves to fight the sun. Lots and lots of violence.
reading_mnemonic: But after a while, the sun and the water have had enough of <kanji>violence</kanji>. They decide to settle their differences with a friendly game of <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). Bowling is a great way to relieve stress and in the end, they bond over it.
meaning_hint: Picture this violent fight scene in your brain. Imagine water locked in battle with the sun as the sun’s fire flares up and the water starts to sizzle.
reading_hint: Imagine the sun and the water trying to bowl. Hear the sound of bowling balls rolling down the lane and pins rattling around and crashing together.

meaning_mnemonic: A raging <radical>tsunami</radical> rises next to the calm <radical>sun</radical>. When you try to <radical>compare</radical> these two things, it's clear that they cannot <kanji>mix</kanji>. If a tsunami is coming, it will likely get in the way of the sun.
reading_mnemonic: Just before the tsunami crashes over you, a <reading>con</reading>dor (こん) swoops out of the sky and grabs you up. This condor must be a <kanji>mix</kanji> — a normal condor is not strong enough to pick up a human. The condor must be mixed with something else. Like a pterodactyl.
meaning_hint: Imagine the sun getting blocked out as a tsunami rises up from the ocean. Feel the cold fear of death on you as the tsunami approaches. That tsunami is gonna mix you up.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself getting mixed up in this situation and the pterodactyl-sized condor picking you up. Feel the condor's talons digging into your shoulders as it grabs you up and flies you towards the sun.

meaning_mnemonic: If you stab somebody in the <radical>tongue</radical> with an <radical>umbrella</radical>, it’s sure to start a <kanji>riot</kanji> and cause <kanji>disorder</kanji>. Nobody likes to get stabbed in the tongue with an umbrella, so they’re probably going to retaliate, and before you know it, you’ve a full scale riot on your hands. Look what you’ve done.
reading_mnemonic: Just now you realize that the man you stabbed in the tongue with your umbrella was none other than Macho Man <reading>Ran</reading>dy Savage (らん). Being a professional wrestler, Macho Man Randy Savage is ready to throw down at a moment’s notice. A <kanji>riot</kanji> immediately erupts. It’s a battle royal with you and Randy at the epicenter.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself getting stabbed in the tongue with an umbrella. It’s very painful. Taste the blood and metal in your mouth as this happens to you.
reading_hint: Picture yourself squaring off with Macho Man Randy Savage in the middle of this riot. You have the advantage of having an umbrella, but Randy has the advantage of being himself. Picture yourself getting into a fight with him.

meaning_mnemonic: “Don’t <radical>loiter</radical> – <radical>run</radical>, <kanji>Junior</kanji>!” said Indiana Jones Senior to Indiana Jones Junior. They’re escaping from a sticky situation and Junior was just loitering around like a fool. It’s no time to loiter, it’s time to run!
reading_mnemonic: Deciding not to loiter any longer, Indiana Jones <kanji>Junior</kanji> takes his dad’s advice and starts to run. Unfortunately he wasn’t paying attention to where his feet were and he stubbed his <reading>to</reading>e (と) on a jagged rock. His toe is in so much pain that he can’t bear to run any longer.
meaning_hint: Imagine Sean Connery saying “Don’t loiter – run, Junior!” and you should have a much easier time remembering this phrase if you remember it in his voice.
reading_hint: Imagine that you are Indiana Jones Junior and this is happening to you. Feel the terrible pain of your throbbing toe as you clutch it, with the bad guys catching up with you.

meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around the <radical>sun</radical> <radical>temple</radical>, it's likely you'll <kanji>acquire</kanji> something. When you loiter around like that it looks like you're waiting for something, so if someone puts something in your hands and you just happen to acquire it, well, no one can fault you for that.
reading_mnemonic: You managed to <kanji>acquire</kanji> some tobacco, but just as you're about to leave <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu comes marching up to you!! Or at least, his ghost does. He stares you dead in the eyes and says, "I didn't found the shogunate for you to acquire illegal goods and kill your body toking on my soil."
meaning_hint: Imagine loitering around near the sun temple. Maybe you acquire some illegal goods, maybe you just acquire a tan (it is a SUN temple, after all).
reading_hint: Your eyes fill with tears under Tokugawa's gaze and you drop everything you acquired to the ground. You bow before him apologizing. You'll never do this again!

meaning_mnemonic: It's important to focus <radical>oneself</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> so that one can emerge <kanji>renew</kanji>ed! You can't really go outside in winter without getting cold or sick, so spend winter focusing on YOU.
reading_mnemonic: Winter ends and, <kanji>renew</kanji>ed, you come out ready to start a new hobby: <reading>kay</reading>aking (かい)! Nothing says renewal like a brand new kayak and sense of purpose in life.
meaning_hint: Imagine sitting in your house all winter, meditating and thinking about how you can renew yourself.
reading_hint: Imagine being back in your house in winter. You're thinking about all the kayaking you're gonna do when winter ends. You're even making the movements with your arms, so you'll be a really renewed you.

meaning_mnemonic: You have to gather <radical>thread</radical> to <radical>sell</radical> if you want to <kanji>continue</kanji> living. You want to make a living, don't you? Make sure you continue to sell that thread every single day!
reading_mnemonic: You have to <kanji>continue</kanji> to sell that thread because a Boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang is forcing you to do it. You see? If you want to continue to live, you have to appease this Bosozoku gang. You better keep selling!
meaning_hint: Imagine selling thread so you can continue living. You search for thread continually now. But are you gathering thread to continue to live, or living to continue gathering thread?
reading_hint: Continue selling thread and paying off the Bosozoku. It's the only way you can continue to survive!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>scooter</radical> and your <radical>car</radical> is what you always <kanji>take along</kanji> with you. Make sure they're <kanji>connect</kanji>ed well so you don't lose them!
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>take along</kanji> your scooter and your car. Anything else, you can do a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん) for!
meaning_hint: Your scooter and your car, connected by a tiny little string, are the two things you take along with you wherever you go.
reading_hint: The roof over your head? Rental. The clothes on your body? Rental. All of your friends? Rental. The only thing you permanently take along with you are your scooter and your car.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>sheep</radical> loses its <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> so it picks them up in its <radical>mouth</radical> to take them to someone who's <kanji>morally good</kanji> enough to attach them back onto its head. Sheep don’t have hands, so the sheep had to pick the horns up off the ground with his mouth. Someone evil would just murder the sheep and steal his horns, so the sheep must find someone <kanji>good</kanji> to reaffix them.
reading_mnemonic: Since you are <kanji>morally good</kanji>, you decide not to murder the sheep and harvest its organs. Instead, as a practitioner of the <kanji>good</kanji> teachings of <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん) Buddhism, you decided to help the sheep out. Your Zen teachings have increased the power of your healing abilities, so you fix the sheep up in no time.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself as the good person being approached by this forlorn sheep without horns. Hear the clack of the horns together as the sheep drops them out of his mouth onto the ground and lets out a pathetic bleat. “Baa~aa~ah~”
reading_hint: Imagine yourself using your Zen magic to heal this sheep. Place your hands on the sheep’s horns and imagine returning them to their rightful location on the sheep’s head. Feel the soft wool of the sheep as he bleats in happiness.

meaning_mnemonic: If you put your <radical>mouth</radical> on a <radical>tree</radical> and try to eat it, you’re probably a <kanji>distressed</kanji> and <kanji>troubled</kanji> individual (unless you’re a woodchuck). You don’t need to eat this tree, you have plenty of food at home. Get your mouth off that tree you distressed child.
reading_mnemonic: You thought you were the most <kanji>distressed</kanji> and <kanji>troubled</kanji> person in this situation, but then you look up and see a very equally distressed and troubled <reading>con</reading>dor (こん). The tree you've been mouthing is its home. And now you've slobbered all over it, ruining this condor's day.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself as this distressed and troubled person. Taste the taste of sap and bark in your mouth as you nom on the tree with all your might.
reading_hint: Picture a very distressed condor watching you munch your mouth all over its tree house. You've made a condor enemy this day.

meaning_mnemonic: If you put a <radical>thread</radical> in the middle of a <radical>kiss</radical>, you’re going to <kanji>entangle</kanji> it. Things can get pretty messy in a kiss, so adding a thread to the mix will definitely entangle it.
reading_mnemonic: This isn't going well, so you pull out the weird kiss thread and <kanji>entangle</kanji> it around a <reading>rack</reading> (らく). Good thing that rack was there! Much better to have it entangled around a rack when you're trying to kiss someone.
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to kiss someone with a piece of thread between your lips. Feel the thread getting all entangled between the two of you. Thread entangled, tongues entangled, lips entangled — just a thready kiss of entanglement.
reading_hint: Imagine pausing this kiss to pull a thread out of your mouth and entangle it around a random rack in your room. Smooth move, Casanova! Your partner will totally love that.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>compare</radical> radical and the <kanji>compare</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Huzzah!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Compare</kanji> the <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) today with the heat yesterday. Is it hotter today? It's up to you to compare.
reading_hint: Feel today's heat. Now compare it to yesterday. You might want to check your thermometer if you're not sure!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>flood</radical> and a <radical>fire</radical>! At the same time? This is a <kanji>disaster</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>disaster</kanji> was caused by your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい)! It's malfunctioning, running around leaking large amounts of water and breathing fire. I guess you didn't work out all the kinks before you let it roam wild.
meaning_hint: Imagine a disaster so bad that there are floods AND fires. That's too much!
reading_hint: You better figure out a way to stop your cyborg from causing more disasters like this. Fix it now, or you're gonna be in big trouble.

meaning_mnemonic: Next to a <radical>tree</radical>, you spot <radical>poop</radical>, not once but twice. Only a <radical>drunkard</radical> would be the type of <radical>person</radical> to not use the nearby <kanji>machine</kanji> toilet. There's a perfectly good outhouse with a machine toilet right there, but this drunkard is so drunk that he decides to poop under the tree instead. And not only does he poop once, he poops twice.
reading_mnemonic: Oh, I see — the door to the outhouse with the <kanji>machine</kanji> toilet is locked, and the drunkard doesn't have the <reading>key</reading> (き). If he had a machine like a jackhammer, or a circular saw, or some other heavy duty machine, he might be able to get in without a key, but since he has none of those or a key, he's reduced to pooping under the tree instead.
meaning_hint: Imagine this drunkard, squatting down under the tree to poop. You can smell the poop and a faint stench of alcohol in the air. How could he not have realized that there was a machine toilet right there?
reading_hint: Listen to the drunkard grumbling about the lack of a key as he poops under the tree. He doesn't sound happy, and every time he stresses to push out the poop, his voice fluctuates. Imagine hearing this in your mind and being a wee bit disgusted.

meaning_mnemonic: Sitting on the <radical>lid</radical> of a toilet, you're praying your <radical>poop</radical> <radical>explosion</radical> will be flushed away, beseeching the <radical>cross</radical> decorating the bathroom wall. The poop needs to be one hundred <kanji>percent</kanji> flushed away because you're on a date, and you don't want even one percent of your explosion left for them to see.
reading_mnemonic: It was extra important that there was zero <kanji>percent</kanji> poop left, because you're not on a date just anywhere, you're at the <reading>Ritz</reading> (りつ)!
meaning_hint: You take a peek under the lid to check, and your poop explosion is one hundred percent gone! Phew!
reading_hint: Look around you at the classy surroundings of the Ritz Hotel bathroom. You feel one hundred percent relieved now, and ready to enjoy your date at the Ritz!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>fly</radical> radical and the <kanji>fly</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Awesome!
reading_mnemonic: When you <kanji>fly</kanji>, you generate a lot of <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) because you’re moving so fast through the atmosphere. Normally this heat would be a nuisance, but you’re a superhero so it doesn’t bother you. This is actually the reason you’re able to fly – you’re a superhero and super resistant to heat.
reading_hint: You’re flying through the air super fast. Feel the air rushing past you as heat builds up all over your body from rocketing through the air so fast.

meaning_mnemonic: You slip off the <radical>roof</radical>, your <radical>life</radical> flashes before your eyes, and you smash your <radical>mouth</radical> on the ground causing some serious <kanji>damage</kanji>. Oof, this is a really bad <kanji>injury</kanji>, you fell all the way off the roof and onto your mouth.
reading_mnemonic: As you try to assess the <kanji>damage</kanji>, you hear someone shout, "<reading>Guy</reading> (がい), hey, you okay, guy?" They run up to you, but your mouth is too <kanji>damage</kanji>d, you can't respond. "Okay guy, I'm gonna call you Guy now. Let's get you to the hospital."
meaning_hint: Imagine that moment as you fall off the roof. In slow motion you can see the roof moving away, you see yourself as a little kid, a teen, an adult, then BAM you've injured your mouth. Ow ow ow!
reading_hint: No matter how much you try to wave your hands and gesture, they keep calling you Guy. It's really annoying, actually, because you aren't a guy and you aren't Guy either. But your mouth is too damaged for you to tell him.

meaning_mnemonic: Regardless of whether or not you wear a <radical>hat</radical> while you <radical>jackhammer</radical>, you’re going to have a <kanji>surplus</kanji> of noise. Jackhammers are loud, and your hat is doing nothing to dampen the racket. There’s a surplus of commotion coming from the jackhammer and onlookers are not amused.
reading_mnemonic: One of the people walking by is the owner of a push cart <reading>yo</reading>-yo (よ) stand. Having a <kanji>surplus</kanji> of yo-yos, he starts throwing them at you. This makes you stop using the jackhammer as you need both hands to fend off the yo-yo barrage.
meaning_hint: The hat you’re wearing is ridiculous, giving you a surplus of bad style. Imagine a bunch of people scowling at you, not only because of your crazy hat, but because of the annoying sound of the jackhammer. Hear the obnoxious noise around you as the jackhammer rattles your bones.
reading_hint: Your hat is doing little to defend you from the onslaught of yo-yos being tossed at you. Feel the yo-yos hitting your body and listen to them roll away after striking you.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>Chinese</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> is <kanji>difficult</kanji> to find because they are so rare. Chinese turkeys are the rarest variety of turkey, so naturally, finding one is exceedingly difficult.
reading_mnemonic: Do you know the actual reason Chinese turkey are so <kanji>difficult</kanji> to find? It’s because they’re all made out of <reading>Naan</reading> (なん). Of course they’re elusive when they blend in so well with all the other Naan!
meaning_hint: Try and picture a Chinese turkey in your brain. Now imagine yourself trying to catch it, but you just can’t. It’s too difficult. The Chinese turkey is too elusive.
reading_hint: Picture yourself entering a bakery, frantically scouring through all the Naan in an attempt to find one that’s actually a Chinese turkey. "WHERE ARE THE TURKEYS?", you scream, to everyone else’s bewilderment. Those elusive, difficult to find Chinese Naan turkeys…

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> with <radical>direction</radical> will have things <kanji>obstruct</kanji> her path. It's a sad fact that it's still a man's world out there!
reading_mnemonic: This woman is truly magnificent. Some compare her to a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball, because of how she knocks down the obstructions in her path like bowling pins.
meaning_hint: Picture this woman with direction, knocking down obstruction after obstruction on her path to success.
reading_hint: Now imagine that she actually is a bowling ball. Watch her roll down the bowling lane and smash all those stupid pins!

meaning_mnemonic: That <radical>outfit</radical> on your <radical>skin</radical> is there to <kanji>cover</kanji> you up. In polite society, it's expected that you cover your skin with some kind of outfit — even if you have to wear something that causes you to <kanji>incur</kanji> damage, like circulation-cutting skinny jeans or a shirt with a really scratchy tag.
reading_mnemonic: Another reason to <kanji>cover</kanji> yourself is to retain <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). Actually, retaining heat and staying warm is probably the reason people started covering themselves up in the first place.
meaning_hint: Imagine the most uncomfortable outfit you can. Your body is incurring some minor damage right now, but hey — at least you're covering your skin properly.
reading_hint: Even if you incur some damage from uncomfortable clothing, it's better to cover up and retain heat than to underdress and incur damage from frostbite or something.

meaning_mnemonic: You have an <radical>outfit</radical> <radical>valley</radical>, which is a valley where you store your <kanji>abundant</kanji> number of outfits. Yes, you're so <kanji>wealthy</kanji> that not only can you purchase a valley for yourself, you can also afford to use it like a walk-in closet.
reading_mnemonic: This valley is also <kanji>abundant</kanji> with <kanji>wealthy</kanji> <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) — trendy, wealthy youths who come from around the world to descend on your valley and peep your outfit collection.
meaning_hint: See the green mountains rising on either side of the valley, hear the birdsong in the distance… and behold the miles of clothes racks that hold all your outfits. This valley is the only space big enough to contain your material abundance and wealth.
reading_hint: Picture these wealthy youths as they peruse your valley of outfits. They're mostly influencers making short-form video content for their socials, basically leeching off your abundance. Ugh. Is this how people get wealthy in the 21st century?

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Rain</radical> causes a <radical>landslide</radical> that causes an <kanji>earthquake</kanji>!!! The earth doesn't just slide, it <kanji>shake</kanji>s and <kanji>quake</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>earthquake</kanji> shakes you so violently that you fall over, banging your <reading>shin</reading> (しん) on the coffee table. The edge of the coffee table is very sharp, so it cuts into your shin pretty badly.
meaning_hint: Picture this happening in real time. First it starts to rain really hard, then the ground starts to shift and run, then everything is shaking and quaking so violently it's an earthquake!
reading_hint: You grab your shin, but the earthquake shakes the coffee table toward you to hit your shin yet again! Ow ow ow stop!

meaning_mnemonic: You take some <radical>flag</radical>s, <radical>nine</radical> in total, and put them one by one up your <kanji>butt</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Now that you have nine flags inside your <kanji>butt</kanji>, you have to get them out. But, you don't know how to. So you ask <reading>Siri</reading> (しり), "How do I get nine flags out of my butt?"
meaning_hint: Imagine taking these nine flags... count 'em... and putting them up your butt. With each one that's up there it gets harder and harder to fit the next one in.
reading_hint: In your head, doing the Siri voice, come up with an answer for your nine-flags-in-the-butt predicament.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>flag</radical> stuck in the ground with a little bit of <radical>fur</radical> coming out from it and on closer inspection you realize it’s actually a <kanji>tail</kanji>. Someone stuck a flag into some sort of animal, pushing it under the ground. All that’s visible now is some fur and part of the tail.
reading_mnemonic: You try to pull the flag out of the ground and hear a rumbling buzzing sound. At the end of the flagpole you see a huge <reading>bee</reading>hive (び)! These bees made their home under the <kanji>tail</kanji>! Maybe that’s why the flag was sticking in it… to warn people. Oops.
meaning_hint: What kind of animal could it be? You start to feel the soft fur with your hand and then the stench of death hits your nostrils. Ew.
reading_hint: See the bees swarming out from around the tail and toward your face. Run! Run before there’s nothing left of you but your own tail!

meaning_mnemonic: Under a <radical>tree</radical> a <radical>drunkard</radical> sits with <radical>twenty</radical> little <kanji>contraptions</kanji>. There's little electronic ones, little bopping ones, little walking ones... twenty of them.
reading_mnemonic: You want to know what these <kanji>contraptions</kanji> are for, so you ask. The drunkard says, "These are <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) contraptions *urp* they're for your uh *blarp* kayak... things."
meaning_hint: Can you even imagine twenty completely unique contraptions? Why don't you try, just for fun.
reading_hint: "Kayak contraptions" is pretty vague, but you do have a kayak, so you're tempted anyway. If only you could figure out what each contraption did so you could justify buying some.

meaning_mnemonic: You take a <radical>stone</radical> to the <radical>forehead</radical> of a <radical>turkey</radical>. It is <kanji>certain</kanji> that you will damage it.
reading_mnemonic: You're killing this turkey, and you need to make sure he's dead for <kanji>certain</kanji>. The reason you need to make sure this turkey is dead is because of the <reading>cock</reading> (かく) in your life. Male chickens get really jealous when turkeys are around.
meaning_hint: Try hitting a sponge on a turkey's forehead. Not certain. Now a stone. <kanji>Certain</kanji>.
reading_hint: As you smash the stone into the turkey's forehead, yell out "This is for you, my cock! THIS IS FOR YOU!" Your cock lover watches from the shadows.

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>woman</radical> sees the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical>, she feels a strong feeling of <kanji>dislike</kanji>. All women dislike the Mona Lisa because of how much attention she gets.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>dislike</kanji> the Mona Lisa so much you hire <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai to cut her up.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself feeling this strong dislike towards the Mona Lisa. She gets all the attention and you get none. It’s not fair and you’re upset.
reading_hint: Ken walks slowly toward the Mona Lisa, dislike shines in his eyes, but then! He falls for her too. No! Ken, destroy her!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical> has grown <radical>old</radical> from saying so many things. This is what makes them different from everyone else, they speak truly like an <kanji>individual</kanji>.
