reading_hint: Imagine goofing off in your row boat and then seeing one of the colleagues waiting on the shore for you. No! She's gonna take your row boat away!

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Grain</radical>s get <radical>blame</radical>d for how much they <kanji>accumulate</kanji>. If the grains accumulate too much then they make more work for the farmers. If they accumulate too little, then they're starving the people. Ugh, grains just can't win with you people. They're blamed for everything.
reading_mnemonic: Now the grains have <kanji>accumulate</kanji>d a ton, they're overflowing in their grain sacks, and the farmer freezes and says, "Now those are some <reading>sexy</reading> (せき) grains."
meaning_hint: Picture a pile of grains accumulating in size while a person watches. No matter how large the pile gets, the person keeps blaming them for something!
reading_hint: Is that why people become farmers? Because they think... accumulating a lot of grains is sexy? People are weird.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Above</radical> you on a <radical>small</radical> <radical>stool</radical> is the <radical>eye</radical> of the one who will <kanji>direct</kanji> and <kanji>supervise</kanji> you at work. He’s really high above you, so all you can see is the glint of his supervising eye. But you can hear him shouting directions clearly, telling you exactly what to do.
reading_mnemonic: A man who can <kanji>direct</kanji> others so well must be <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu. Sure enough, when he finally comes down off his stool you see that he’s the ghost of Tokugawa Ieyasu!!! That explains why you could only make out the ghostly gleam of his supervising eye before.
meaning_hint: Look up and see that eye, shining down and supervising you closely. Hear the man shouting as he directs you in one task after another. It makes you uncomfortable, honestly.
reading_hint: Tokugawa lingers before you in full shogun garb, then nods his head in approval, winks, and disappears.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>servant</radical> radical and the <kanji>servant</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Just remember that the kanji also means <kanji>retainer</kanji> and you’ll be fine.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>servant</kanji>, also known as your <kanji>retainer</kanji>, bangs his <reading>shins</reading> (しん) all the time so you make him wear <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) to work now. He bangs his shins on all the furniture in the house, knocking over all your valuables. But at least when he’s wearing jeans it doesn’t leave bruises like it did when he wasn’t wearing pants.
meaning_hint: Retainer and servant are basically the same thing, so remembering this one shouldn’t be too hard for you.
reading_hint: Imagine your servant banging his shins on everything in your house. Hand him a pair of jeans and tell him to put them on to soften the blows.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>rice paddy</radical> you'd want to <radical>kiss</radical> is one that would probably have a cool <kanji>abbreviation</kanji> for their name, like Ri'-Pad. Yeah, that's definitely a cool abbreviation!
reading_mnemonic: An <kanji>abbreviation</kanji> like Ri'-Pad is sure to get a strong <reading>reac</reading>tion (りゃく) — some will find it a little pretentious, some will hate it, but most will probably think it's really cool.
meaning_hint: Try to imagine how cool and confident Ri'-Pad is, they probably grow and wear their rice in a cool and trendy way, like a middle part or something.
reading_hint: Try to visualize the different reactions people have to the name Ri'-Pad. What reaction did you have? What face did you make when you saw it?

meaning_mnemonic: On every <radical>boat</radical> there is a <radical>lid</radical> on the <radical>table</radical> that's used for <kanji>navigation</kanji>. When you take the lid off, there is a big map, a sextant, and some little boat miniatures. All the navigation tools.
reading_mnemonic: You watch the <kanji>navigation</kanji> equipment roll away and then you feel a slap on the back of your head. Ouch! You turn around and there stands <reading>こう</reading>いち, who you took with you as the navigator of this expedition. And, you just lost all his stuff!
meaning_hint: You forget to put the lid back on the navigation station and all those tools and boat pieces go flying around the boat and into the water. Oops.
reading_hint: Picture this seafaring こういち angrily chasing you around the ruined navigation station, trying to hit you with a telescope.

meaning_mnemonic: Under this <radical>roof</radical> someone <radical>odd</radical> is trying to <kanji>draw near</kanji> to you. You back up, but they continue to draw near anyway.
reading_mnemonic: They continue to <kanji>draw near</kanji> until you can't take it anymore. You run out from under the roof, slam the door closed, and lock it with a <reading>key</reading> (き).
meaning_hint: Imagine being under the same roof as an odd person who's always trying to draw near to you. You don't want anything to do with them, but they won't back off.
reading_hint: You hold the key tightly in your hand and press your ear to the door. You can still hear the odd person drawing near. Hopefully this key keeps them away.

meaning_mnemonic: If you're a <radical>tree</radical> <radical>anti</radical> (someone who's against trees), then you won't mind if I turn all of them into <kanji>board</kanji>s! Hahaha!
reading_mnemonic: The Tree Lovers Association has one word when they see you turning trees into <kanji>board</kanji>s: "<reading>Ban</reading>ned (ばん)," they shout at you. "Boards mean you're banned!"
meaning_hint: "Boards are much more useful than trees." – The Anti-Tree Association. Now picture this sign on a big machine that's cutting up all the trees into big boards.
reading_hint: You couldn't care less about getting banned from the Tree Lovers Association. You and your Anti-Tree Association still keep on turning every tree into boards, ban or no ban. Damn those trees!

meaning_mnemonic: If <radical>dirt</radical> and more <radical>dirt</radical> is all you see as you <radical>go</radical> from place to place, you must be roaming the <kanji>streets</kanji> of a <kanji>city</kanji>. Cities are notoriously dirty places, so watch your step when you head out into the streets.
reading_mnemonic: As you roam the <kanji>streets</kanji>, brushing the dirt off yourself, a <reading>guy</reading> (がい) bumps into you: "Hey guy, watch where you're going!" As he says this he bumps into another guy. "Whoa there, guy — you think you own these streets or somethin'?" "As a matter of fact, my guy, I run these streets, so you'd better check yourself."
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself caked in dirt as you make your way through this urban jungle — someone really needs to clean up these streets!
reading_hint: These two guys seem like they might be wise guys, so you hightail it out of there. You don't want to be another victim to the streets.

meaning_mnemonic: Under this <radical>roof</radical> you <radical>jackhammer</radical> your <kanji>religion</kanji> into people.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>religion</kanji> requires everyone to take off their <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). Mostly because the shoes will help them resist the jackhammering, so you tell them that to join your religion they have to leave their shoes at the door.
meaning_hint: It's a religion building. They have a lot of different names, churches, mosques, temples, cathedrals, but none of those matter. All that matters is that you really drill whichever religion you want into the heads of the people who come under your roof.
reading_hint: Picture the pile of shoes left outside. If you see a bunch of shoes like this you can be sure there's a religious place around here.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>servant</radical> sits on a <radical>stool</radical>, holding a piece of <radical>thread</radical>, looking very <kanji>tense</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You ask the servant why they're so <kanji>tense</kanji>, and they say they've been assigned to assassinate the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England with this piece of thread.
meaning_hint: You walk by this tense servant and pause for a moment. They raise their tense eyes up at you and you can see they're shaking.
reading_hint: Imagine how tense you would be if you were about to try to murder the King! Your entire body would tense up, maybe to the point that you wouldn't be able to actually go through with it.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> who is <radical>good</radical> is my <kanji>daughter</kanji>. My daughter is a good woman, I guarantee it.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>daughter</kanji>, on the other hand, is not a good woman. Your daughter looks like a <reading>moose who may</reading> (むすめ) not be able to find its way out of a wet paper bag. Your daughter looks like a moose who may lose its way without a map. Your daughter looks like a moose who may do lots of stupid things.
meaning_hint: Look at how good this woman is! She’s my daughter! She’s good! You wish your daughter was as good a woman as mine is!
reading_hint: Think about how your daughter is basically a moose who may do dumb things. How ashamed you feel. How very, very ashamed. Unlike how I feel about my wonderful, perfect, not a moose-like daughter.

meaning_mnemonic: Under a <radical>roof</radical>, but still in the <radical>sun</radical>, a <radical>woman</radical> is laying out a fabulous <kanji>banquet</kanji>. The woman doesn't want anyone to burn from the sun, but banquets are best outside, so she's set up a roof to protect her and her guests.
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, the <kanji>banquet</kanji> roof comes crashing down right before the guests arrive, and it <reading>end</reading>s (えん) before it can start.
meaning_hint: Picture a large banquet of food on a sunny day, covered by an outdoor roof, and a woman carefully setting it all up.
reading_hint: Honestly though, have you ever been to a banquet that ended well? Something always goes wrong when you have that many people under one roof.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>soul</radical> of a <radical>narwhal</radical> was sucked into its <radical>towel</radical>, which is <kanji>scary</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: As you watch this <kanji>scary</kanji> scene you can't help but mutter, "<reading>Foo</reading>l," (ふ) under your breath. Then you hear "Fool, fool, fool, it's too scary you fool!" and you realize all of the other narwhals are chanting.
meaning_hint: Watch as the narwhal's face contorts as you see its soul get slurped out by the towel it was using.
reading_hint: You don't know what's more scary. That the narwhal's soul got sucked out, or that all of its kind is calling it a fool now that it's dead.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>saw</radical> cuts into your <radical>heart</radical> and you are filled with <kanji>fear</kanji>. Well, you were filled with fear before the saw reached your heart too, but now that the saw is cutting into your heart, you’re feeling more fear than ever.
reading_mnemonic: Even more so than your <kanji>fear</kanji> of dying, you are filled with the fear that you’ll never get to vacation in <reading>きょう</reading>と. You always wanted to take a trip to きょうと, but if you die from this saw wound to the heart, you’ll never get the chance to go.
meaning_hint: Try and imagine this saw slicing into your heart. Are you filled with fear? You should be.
reading_hint: I fear you’ll never make it to きょうと. Think of きょうと and how you long for it as you slowly die from that saw wound to the heart.

meaning_mnemonic: There’s a <radical>tsunami</radical> in <radical>heaven</radical> and you take a <radical>small</radical> <radical>drop</radical> of water and <kanji>append</kanji> it to the tsunami. Now the tsunami is big enough to destroy all of heaven. The small drop that you appended to the tsunami was just enough to push it over the edge, reaching full destruction capability.
reading_mnemonic: Just after you <kanji>append</kanji> the small drop of water to the tsunami, it grows <reading>ten</reading> (てん) stories high. Before it was only nine, but since you appended that drop, the tsunami is now a full ten stories tall.
meaning_hint: Take a small drop of water and append it to the tsunami. Feel the drop of water on your finger and visualize yourself flicking it at the tsunami as it races towards heaven.
reading_hint: Look up at this ten story tall tsunami. It looks big and powerful. Feel the water spray from the tsunami as it towers over you.

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>animal</radical> eats a <radical>child</radical> from a <radical>plate</radical> in a very <kanji>fierce</kanji> manner. This is a fierce animal, so it makes sense that it would eat the child in a fierce style.
reading_mnemonic: The reason this <kanji>fierce</kanji> animal is eating the child from the plate is because the child forgot to <reading>mow</reading> (もう) the animal’s lawn. The fierce animal had hired the child to mow his lawn today, and when the child forgot to do it, the fierce animal hunted the child down and ate him.
meaning_hint: See the fierce animal fiercely eating the child from the plate. It’s very gruesome. See blood and body parts flying everywhere as the animal devours the child.
reading_hint: That child really shouldn’t have forgotten to mow the lawn. Imagine you are the child, being eaten by the fierce animal and wishing that you would have just mowed the lawn like you said you would.

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>yakuza</radical>'s <radical>knife</radical> <radical>boil</radical>s, you know something very <kanji>violent</kanji> and <kanji>intense</kanji> is about to happen.
reading_mnemonic: When someone gets <kanji>violent</kanji> or <kanji>intense</kanji>, they always yell "<reading>Let's</reading> (れつ) get violent and intense!"
meaning_hint: The yakuza member is so upset his knife boils. Right after, they will definitely get violent and intense.
reading_hint: In the case of our mnemonic, it's a yakuza member getting violent. So, imagine a yakuza member saying "Ret's get violent!" before doing just that.

meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>cross</radical> in your <radical>forehead</radical> with a <radical>thread</radical> attached to it, forcing you to <kanji>search</kanji> where it leads. You’re not sure how the cross got stuck in your forehead, but you are interested in seeing where this piece of thread leads. The search is on.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>search</kanji> is over. You found the end of the thread. The thread leads you to a <reading>sack</reading> (さく). A sack filled with more crosses with more threads attached to them. What does it all mean!?!
meaning_hint: It hurts to have this cross stuck in your forehead, but try to ignore that as you search for where the thread leads. Imagine gathering the thread in your hands as you move along.
reading_hint: Why are there more crosses and threads in the sack? Are you supposed to take them from the sack and throw them at other people’s foreheads? Maybe. Imagine yourself reaching into the sack and throwing the crosses at other people.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Say</radical> <radical>good luck</radical> to one person enough and they'll be <kanji>stuffed</kanji> full of that luck.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>stuffed</kanji> them by saying good luck <reading>two</reading> (つ) whole times!
meaning_hint: Better safe than sorry. Don't just say good luck once, make sure they're stuffed!
reading_hint: Picture a person with the actual words "good luck" stuffed into their body.

meaning_mnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> <radical>sheep</radical> are <kanji>detailed</kanji> creatures. I mean, I never thought sheep were very detailed, but that’s what they say.
reading_mnemonic: The reason these sheep are so <kanji>detailed</kanji> is that they are trying to impress the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). If they're fancy enough, perhaps the Shougun won’t kill them for their meat and make their wool into clothing.
meaning_hint: Imagine getting really close to a sheep and seeing all the tiny detailed curls in their wool. There are hidden patterns you'd never noticed before now!
reading_hint: Picture the Shougun, leaning in close to inspect the detailed patterns all over the sheep. His nose is so close, he can probably smell all the little detailed nuances of the sheep's musk too.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>demon</radical> on a <radical>jet</radical> is pretty <kanji>alluring</kanji>. Demons themselves are pretty intriguing, but put one on a jet? Now that’s alluring.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>alluring</kanji> demon on a jet wants one thing and one thing only. <reading>Me</reading>at (み). Human meat. This demon needs human meat to survive so he is taking his jet into the city to feast on some meat.
meaning_hint: See this demon flying around in his jet. Do you feel allured? You should. Hear the roar of the jet engine as the demon cackles.
reading_hint: The demon won’t be so alluring once you see him scarfing down humans, tearing the meat from their bones. How gross.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits the <radical>sun</radical>, where you're in <radical>prison</radical> with a <radical>spoon</radical> trying to catch some of it before it starts to <kanji>dry up</kanji>. Being in prison on the sun means your <kanji>thirst</kanji> is truly great, and this spoon will help you quench it when the tsunami comes.
reading_mnemonic: This method of quenching your <kanji>thirst</kanji> reminds you of being in a <reading>ca</reading>r <reading>wa</reading>sh (かわ). The water pours over you in waves, and you're stuck inside a metal cage. Totally the same thing as a car wash, right?
meaning_hint: Imagine the feeling of drinking spoon after spoon of refreshing tsunami water, how it replenishes your dried up body. You've been in this sun prison for so long, holding onto this spoon and waiting for this moment. Better take advantage of it — even a tsunami will dry up real quick on the sun.
reading_hint: Imagine quenching your thirst in a real car wash. Open your mouth and let the soapy water dump on over and in you. You'll never be thirsty again!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>lineage</radical> radical and the <kanji>lineage</kanji> kanji are the same. Sometimes this kanji can also mean <kanji>system</kanji>. I guess a lineage is a kind of system, if you think about it.
reading_mnemonic: You trace your <kanji>lineage</kanji> back through your whole family <kanji>system</kanji> and realize every single relative loved <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). Most of them were cake bakers too!
meaning_hint: Basically, think of 系 as linking things that belong together in some kind of group. Lineages, solar systems, genres, styles… Anything along those lines.
reading_hint: You come from a lineage of cake makers and eaters. No wonder you love cake so much! (And if you hate cake, no wonder your family doesn't like you.)

meaning_mnemonic: You meet a <radical>woman</radical> from another <radical>clan</radical> under the rising <radical>sun</radical> to prepare for <kanji>marriage</kanji>. Your clan and her clan are standing together as the two of you watch the sun rise together.
reading_mnemonic: For the <kanji>marriage</kanji> to be official, you need to wait for a <reading>con</reading>dor (こん) to appear. If it doesn't show up before the sun goes down again, the marriage was a failure. You have to try again tomorrow with a different woman.
meaning_hint: Marriage is a big deal and it's going to connect your two clans, so you have to do the ceremony in the sun in front of everyone so they can see.
reading_hint: Do you want to go through with this marriage? You better hope you see a condor then! It's the official bird of love and unification, ya know. That's why so many people wait until they're older to get married these days, because there aren't as many condors thanks to deforestation.

meaning_mnemonic: On a <radical>scooter</radical> running in one <radical>direction</radical> is a <radical>gun</radical>-toting <radical>child</radical>. This is a terribly dangerous situation, but to the child it is just <kanji>play</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Everything is <kanji>play</kanji> for <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう), even if it's a terrible, scary, not good situation like this one.
meaning_hint: Imagine a child riding a scooter with a gun having a good time. "Come play with me!" they shout. "No!" you yell back.
reading_hint:
Imagine trying to explain to the youth that this is not play and that they should stop, but them youths can't stop playing.

P.S. From the WaniKani staff to you: Don't let youths play with guns, please.

meaning_mnemonic: Traveling in the <radical>direction</radical> of the ocean, you take a <radical>gun</radical> and shoot a <radical>crab</radical>, then stick a <kanji>flag</kanji> in the beach and claim it as your own. The crab was the only thing standing in the way of you conquering the area. Now that the flag is in the ground, no one can dispute who it belongs to.
reading_mnemonic: To make sure that no one removes your <kanji>flag</kanji>, you lock it to one of the lifeguard posts and you throw away the <reading>key</reading> (き). Since no one will have the key to unlock the flag, no one will be able to remove it.
meaning_hint: Look at your flag and hear it flapping in the wind. Also hear your ears ringing a bit from when you shot that crab. Look at all the bits of exploded crab strewn around the area. Yowza.
reading_hint: Picture yourself locking this flag up with your key. Imagine turning the key in the lock and hearing the click when it is secured.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> is being <radical>call</radical>ed on to <radical>boil</radical> the earth by <kanji>illuminating</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: The person who calls the sun asking it to <kanji>illuminate</kanji> the earth is the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). It's very specifically the Shougun's responsibility to make sure the earth is illuminated every morning.
meaning_hint: We don't want the earth to actually boil, but we do want some of that boiling hot sun light to illuminate all of the darkness of the universe and let us see.
reading_hint: Picture the Shougun calling out to the sun, asking it to illuminate our planet with its boiling hot rays. You better thank the Shougun the next time you see him for everything he does for you.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>soul</radical> of a <radical>person</radical> has a <radical>hook</radical> that can make them <kanji>pleasant</kanji>. When you see, hear, or feel something pleasant, can't you feel that hook pressing into the middle of your body?
reading_mnemonic: If you want to feel this <kanji>pleasant</kanji> feeling just look at a <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい). Kayaks are the most pleasant boats in the world. Feel that pleasant feeling in your chest just from reading about kayaks in this mnemonic right now.
meaning_hint: Imagine that feeling you get when everything is just SO pleasant. There is a pressure from your soul's hook digging in. It's a good feeling though, a pleasant one! It isn't too hard or too soft.
reading_hint: Go look up pictures of kayaks to get some of that pleasant high. Mm, your soul is hooking you real good.

meaning_mnemonic: If you're a <radical>one-sided</radical> paper <radical>anti</radical> (someone against one-sided paper), then you should bind all those one-sided papers into an <kanji>edition</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The first <kanji>edition</kanji> you create is a fan fiction all about the adventures of <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo. You hated how short and one-sided his story in Star Wars seemed to be, and trying to keep track of it all was really hard. But not anymore!
meaning_hint: One-sided paper is just anti-everything. It's bad for the environment. It's bad for easy reading. Put all those one-sided papers together into one, big edition instead.
reading_hint: Please read my super special Han Solo edition of the Star Wars story. I ripped out all the one-sided pages that had him in it and put them into a single edition together so it's just the good stuff now.

meaning_mnemonic: Those that can only eat a <radical>part</radical> of a <radical>shellfish</radical> are probably a bit <kanji>poor</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: And since they are <kanji>poor</kanji>, they are getting these shellfish parts from the trash <reading>bin</reading> (びん). It's quite unfortunate.
meaning_hint: Poor people can't even afford a whole shellfish. They have to settle with just a part.
reading_hint: Imagine a poor person going through a garbage bin to find only a part of of a shellfish. What a sad sight.

meaning_mnemonic: If you look for a <radical>leaf</radical> among the <radical>hills</radical> you will notice they are <kanji>scarce</kanji>. The hills have no trees around them, so there are no leaves. The leaves are scarce here on the hills.
reading_mnemonic: Still looking for a leaf, you determine that they are far too <kanji>scarce</kanji> to find here. Just before you give up, you discover a giant <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball. As you pick it up, another one comes thundering down the hill knocking over a tiny tree.
meaning_hint: Imagine being on the hills. You want a leaf to find and take back as a souvenir but there are none. Imagine looking feverishly for a leaf even though you don’t really expect to find one.
reading_hint: Leaves are scarce here because someone is using these hills for their bowling. The bowling balls knock over everything, and leaves can't grow.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>scooter</radical> with a <radical>mohawk</radical>, now that's the most <kanji>suitable</kanji> hairstyle you could ever think of for a scooter!
reading_mnemonic: You want this <kanji>suitable</kanji> hairstyle for your scooter too, and luckily you're a <reading>techie</reading> (てき), so it's easy for you to install one.
meaning_hint: You keep seeing scooters all over the city with mohawks and you can't get it out of your head that it's the most suitable thing you've ever seen.
reading_hint: Imagine going online to check out some of your techie message boards and learning the most suitable mohawk for your scooter then installing it yourself.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Beforehand</radical>, to buy a <radical>geoduck</radical>, you have to go to the bank to <kanji>deposit</kanji> enough money. If you don't deposit your money you won't have enough in your account to buy one! They're expensive, after all.
reading_mnemonic: You go to the bank to make the <kanji>deposit</kanji>, and there is a kid standing in your way playing with a <reading>yo</reading>-yo (よ). You try to squeeze past and he hits you in the face with the yo-yo. Now you can't even see, let alone make the deposit.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself depositing your money beforehand. Imagine the delicious geoduck you'll be able to buy now that the money has been deposited into your account.
reading_hint: Imagine trying to make a deposit to your bank after getting hit in the face with a yo-yo. You can hardly see what you're shoving your money into. Just hope it isn't the hands of the kid that hit you.

meaning_mnemonic: If you do <radical>yoga</radical> <radical>correct</radical>ly, you will not only <kanji>prolong</kanji> your body but your life!
reading_mnemonic: You can <kanji>prolong</kanji> your life with correct yoga techniques, but unfortunately, if you ever stop doing yoga after you've started this prolonging process, it will just <reading>en</reading>d (えん) suddenly.
meaning_hint: Do you want to prolong your life? Then do yoga correctly!
reading_hint: Correct yoga will prolong your life but no yoga will end it. Do you think that's a fair trade?

meaning_mnemonic: Whenever all the <radical>feathers</radical> start <radical>stand</radical>ing, you know it's time for <kanji>the next</kanji> day. Once the day comes to its end, all feathers start standing up like clockwork.
reading_mnemonic: With <kanji>the next</kanji> day approaching and all feathers standing up, you get ready to drink your usual liter of <reading>yolk</reading> (よく). Yes, you heard that right.
meaning_hint: Imagine every single feather simultaneously standing up once the next day gets closer. It's like some kind of ritual that's repeated at the end of every single day. Must be stressful to be a feathered animal!
reading_hint: Imagine having to drink an entire liter of egg yolk at the end of each day. Just like the feathers have their ritual of standing up, you have your own ritual of downing a liter of egg yolk. Just don't ask questions.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>servant</radical> holding a <radical>gun</radical> sits on the <radical>ground</radical> to <radical>see</radical> if he can find something to <kanji>look at</kanji>. The servant is on watch duty and has the gun to protect the perimeter. He patrols the grounds to look at the distance to see if there are any trespassers.
reading_mnemonic: You start backing away, but then you realize that you are Macho Man <reading>Ran</reading>dy (らん) Savage. Randy Savage backs away from no fight! I mean, <kanji>look at</kanji> this guy – this servant guy. Take away the gun and he’s not much to look at. You can take him.
meaning_hint: Look at this servant. He’s doing a great job. Oops, looks like he sees you. Yup, he’s pointing his gun at you. Imagine staring down the barrel of his gun as you slowly back away.
reading_hint: Pretend you are Macho Man Randy Savage. Run at this servant guy, take his gun away, and beat him up. Ohhh yeaaahhh!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> will <radical>cross</radical> through your body and into the <radical>net</radical> of your <radical>clothes</radical> so that you'll be hit with <kanji>nostalgia</kanji> when you wear them again. Nostalgia for all the things that happened every single time you wore those clothes.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>nostalgia</kanji> ties <reading>knots</reading> (なつ) in your stomach and your chest. Even if the memories and feelings weren't particularly bad, there's something about the wistful feeling of nostalgia that knots up your insides.
meaning_hint: When you put on an old piece of clothing, a shirt maybe, or some pants, feel the nostalgia that was embedded in it and left there by your soul.
reading_hint: Feel these nostalgic knots inside you. Now tie knots in the clothes that make you feel nostalgic so you don't wear them again.

meaning_mnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical>, put them on this <radical>turtle shell</radical>, and <kanji>push</kanji> push push! Push the turtle right on out of there.
reading_mnemonic: You're trying to <kanji>push</kanji> this turtle out so you can use the shell for your new <reading>o</reading>bi (お). You keep seeing other people wearing turtle shell patterns on their obi, but you're going to go a step further. You're going to MAKE your obi out of actual turtle shells! You just have to push this darn turtle out first.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself using your fingers to push a turtle out of its shell. It really doesn't want to leave, so you have to push really hard.
reading_hint: Imagine pushing some turtle shells flat to make a beautiful obi. Wear it out, watch everyone gasp in awe. Oh... oh no! They're trying to push you out of your obi like you did the turtle. They're mad about your turtle crime!

meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>ground</radical> of the <radical>rice paddy</radical> is a <radical>treasure</radical>. As you pull it out of the mucky ground, you can feel your life force being<kanji>renew</kanji>ed.
reading_mnemonic: Just as you were getting accustomed to this life <kanji>renew</kanji>ing treasure, <reading>こう</reading>いち comes out of nowhere, stealing your treasure and running off with it.
meaning_hint: Seems like the treasure has some magical ability to renew the cells in your body. Really try to imagine this feeling as you pull the treasure out of the rice paddy’s ground. Perhaps it feels just as refreshing as getting a full night’s sleep.
reading_hint: All you could hear was a sly “yoink!”, and suddenly your life renewing treasure was gone. God knows what things こういち will get up to with this life renewing treasure in his possession (but it sure won’t be anything good).

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tree</radical> <radical>pirate</radical> jumps from a tree and attacks you by hitting you with a <kanji>pillow</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: As he hits you with his <kanji>pillow</kanji> he makes a demand: "Give me your <reading>Mac cooler</reading> (まくら). He wants the device that cools down your Mac computer.
meaning_hint: It hurts a little bit, but the fact that tree pirates use pillows as their weapons is a bit anticlimactic.
reading_hint: You give him your Mac cooler and he goes on his way. He's quite happy he can cool down his Mac, now. The trees are quite warm and he hears this will help to cool down his tree apples.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> knocks a <radical>cleat</radical> off a <radical>child</radical> and the shoe begins to <kanji>float</kanji> away in the water. This child was just out playing soccer, minding his own business, when the tsunami crashed in and knocked his cleats off his feet. Now the cleat is floating away with the tsunami.
reading_mnemonic: While you <kanji>float</kanji> in the water, an <reading>u</reading>ni (う) jabs into your foot. “<reading>Uwa</reading>aa~ (うわ)” you cry. That uni really hurts. They’re all spikey and painful and junk. “Uwaaaa~”
meaning_hint: Your cleat is floating away but you’re floating too. The water is really deep now, but you try and float towards your cleat to retrieve it. Imagine floating in this wet water reaching for your lost cleat.
reading_hint: Say “Uwaaa~” out loud a few times while you imagine an うに stabbing you as you float along with the tsunami water. What a day you’ve had.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> rips a <radical>flag</radical> off your house in the <radical>rain</radical>, causing a <kanji>leak</kanji> where it was ripped out. The rain and the tsunami combined just tore the flag right off your house. The force was so strong that it actually removed part of the wall, and now you have a leak in your home.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>leak</kanji> is getting worse and worse and so is the tsunami, when you see something outside. It’s a bunch of <reading>roe</reading> (ろう) floating in the waves. Using your quick thinking skills you scoop it up and shove it into the cracks where the water is leaking in. It works like a charm!
meaning_hint: Look at this leak in your house and see the rain water coming in. Hear it drip drip dripping down the wall.
reading_hint: Did you know that roe can plug any leak? It’s true. If you don’t mind wasting some, you should break a leak into your own house and then try to plug it back up again with some salmon roe.

meaning_mnemonic: You take some <radical>bamboo</radical> and <radical>attach</radical> it together into a <kanji>token</kanji> of appreciation for your friend. Your friend greatly appreciates this token of friendship.
reading_mnemonic: You hand over the <kanji>token</kanji> but your friend drops it and yells, "You <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ)! I'm allergic to bamboo!"
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to attach some bamboo together into a token for someone.
reading_hint: Imagine the embarrassment you feel, forgetting they were allergic to bamboo. Oh well, it’s the thought that counts. You’re still a fool, though.

meaning_mnemonic: You give a <radical>shellfish</radical> to a <radical>lifeguard</radical> to <kanji>purchase</kanji> their services. You think lifeguards are interested in normal money? Come on, they're obviously out there using shellfish for their purchases. It's only natural, given their seaside habitat.
reading_mnemonic: This time you <kanji>purchase</kanji> some <reading>こう</reading>いち goods. You know, since you're such a fan. How else can you show love in this world, if not through purchases? The lifeguard hands over a little haul of こういち plushies and figurines. Nice.
meaning_hint: Imagine the grooved surface of a shellfish shell as you press it into the hand of a lifeguard. Congrats, you've just completed a purchase. Uh… what'd you get?
reading_hint:
And it's totally because those こういち goods are so valuable that the lifeguard accepts shellfish for them. Has nothing to do with the fact he couldn't get real money for them, okay?

Hint: When you see the "lifeguard" radical, the reading is probably going to be こう.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>run</radical> every day and soon you <radical>become</radical> a human that can <kanji>go beyond</kanji> the normal speed limits of man. You run and you run and you run. This training allows you to become super human. You go beyond the limits of a normal human. You become a human who can run really fast.
reading_mnemonic: To solidify your ability to <kanji>go beyond</kanji> the abilities of other humans, you <reading>etch</reading> (えつ) sacred runes into your legs to preserve your running abilities. Etching these runes makes sure that your skills will never deteriorate.
meaning_hint: Run fast! Go beyond the limits imposed upon you by nature! Imagine yourself running faster than any human has ever run ever. Feel yourself cutting through the air, wind breaking across you as you break the sound barrier. Boom!
reading_hint: Etch these runes into your legs. Go ahead and run a finger over your legs and pretend to etch runes into them. These runes you etch will allow you to run fast forever.

meaning_mnemonic: When you <radical>eat</radical> you want to see the shape of your <radical>gun</radical> from under a <radical>towel</radical>, so you <kanji>decorate</kanji> your house with them.
reading_mnemonic: If you actually <kanji>decorate</kanji> your house like this it will give people quite a <reading>shock</reading> (しょく). In fact, they might be so shocked they run out of there screaming.
meaning_hint: Just picture it: an entire home decorated top to bottom with guns covered in towels, so only the outline shows. As your guests try to eat you say, "Guess what gun this is?! Haha, you got it wrong, pull the towel back."
reading_hint: Maybe you should be more conscious of other people when you decorate. Or at least, don't shock people into fainting or yelling or leaving. Guns do that, you know?

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> gets trapped in a small room with a <radical>stool</radical> and an <radical>insect</radical>, and together they create a very <kanji>boisterous</kanji> situation. The horse and the insect are both scrambling frantically to get out of the small room, knocking the stool around all over the place.
reading_mnemonic: At the height of their <kanji>boisterous</kanji> sounds, there's a sudden, awful CRONCH(!) and you see the <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) of the insect slowly rising out of the room, through the ceiling.
meaning_hint: Imagine the boisterous sounds coming from this small room. Hear the horse neigh and stomp his hooves, hear the insect buzzing loudly, and hear the wooden stool rattle around as the horse and insect try to escape the room.
reading_hint: The horse gets even more frightened now that it killed the insect and saw an actual soul float by its head and it grows even more boisterous.

meaning_mnemonic: You fly <radical>north</radical> to the <radical>moon</radical> and break your <kanji>back</kanji> when you finally land on it. You weren’t braced for impact so when you hit the moon, your back took all of the impact.
reading_mnemonic:
Your <kanji>back</kanji> hurts so much that the only way to properly explain the pain is in <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい) form. 

I flew to the moon.
It is there I broke my back.
My back hurts real bad.
meaning_hint: Imagine flying north to the moon. Feel the force of being rocketed through space on your journey. Now feel your spine telescope and your back break. It really hurts.
reading_hint: Say this haiku out loud. Memorize it and use this haiku to remember this kanji.

meaning_mnemonic: Point some <radical>fingers</radical> at the <radical>sun</radical> and put some others in your <radical>ear</radical> as you sit on a <radical>stool</radical> — now you can have your <kanji>photograph</kanji> taken. This is a glamour <kanji>photo</kanji>, so you have to be in one of these weird, artsy poses for it to come out juuust right.
reading_mnemonic: As you're getting the <kanji>photograph</kanji> taken, the photographer tells you to say <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ). He says it's like saying cheese but more effective. Satsuki!
meaning_hint: Which fingers do you point at the sun, and which do you put in your ear? Act it out, that'll help you remember. Now try to relax on the stool and smile for the camera. This is a professional photograph, so you better do what you're told!
reading_hint: Go ahead and say Satsuki out loud, all happy-like. Makes a pretty big smile, right? From now on you should always say Satsuki when you're being photographed.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>next</radical> <radical>plate</radical> is the one you will <kanji>steal</kanji>. This one would be too obvious, better wait for the next one.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>steal</kanji> this plate from a <reading>とう</reading>きょう restaurant, so after you steal it, picture yourself running through the streets of とうきょう as you lose your pursuers.
meaning_hint: Wait for this next plate. Here it is—now steal it! Imagine stealing this plate and making off with it.
reading_hint: Be careful not to drop that plate! Wouldn’t want to steal something just to break it. Imagine running through the streets of とうきょう. See all the sights and hear all the sounds. Just don’t get too distracted, you gotta get to a safe place and stash your stolen plate away.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>rocket</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> has several points which will <kanji>detach</kanji> during takeoff.
reading_mnemonic: When the rocket turkey parts <kanji>detach</kanji>, they fall to the ground onto a bed of <reading>ree</reading>ds (り).
meaning_hint: Imagine the rocket turkey taking off. Just like a regular rocket, as it goes up, used pieces of the turkey rocket detach and fall back to the ground.
reading_hint: The reeds catch on fire - probably best not to fire off turkey rockets so close to these fields of reeds. Imagine the launch site completely surrounded by reeds.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>bully</radical> take an <radical>insect</radical> and <kanji>dissolve</kanji> it in some acid. Bullies are messed up, mean people. They do awful things that normal kids and people wouldn't do... like dissolve a living creature.
reading_mnemonic: The only bullies heartless enough to <kanji>dissolve</kanji> insects like this are <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう). Do you really think an adult would melt a harmless critter? No. Only inexperienced youths could be so cruel.
meaning_hint: Actually... try to forget this one. Don't think about a bully dissolving things in acid. Think happy thoughts. Like... the bully getting dissolved instead and all the little insects coming back to life and being happy.
reading_hint: When you first read this and you pictured the bully dissolving the insect, you pictured them as a youth, right? See? It makes complete sense.

meaning_mnemonic: Bring <radical>thread</radical> to the <radical>library</radical> to <kanji>knit</kanji>. Libraries are quiet and generally pretty empty nowadays, so it's the perfect place to relax and do some knitting. Of course, if you find thread lying around in a library you know what to do now.
reading_mnemonic: You need to come to the library to <kanji>knit</kanji> because if you try to do it at home all your <reading>hen</reading>s (へん) will try to eat the thread. Hens love eating things that look like worms, like hay, string, and thread. So it's important to get far away from them before you start to knit.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself sitting on a rocking chair in the library knitting away. It's quiet and peaceful and the light is warming you just right. Ahh, bliss!
reading_hint: Imagine the granny or grampy you are (and must be to have hens and want to knit in peace in a library). You're tired of these hens eating your thread while you try to knit sweaters for all your grandbabies so you come to the library.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> growing out of a <radical>rice paddy</radical> into the shape of a <radical>cross</radical> are really <kanji>showy</kanji> <kanji>flower</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: These <kanji>flower</kanji>s are too <kanji>showy</kanji>. You hate them. You hate these showy flowers so much you run them over with your <reading>ca</reading>r (か)!
meaning_hint: Picture these showy flowers growing straight up and out of the middle of the rice paddy, into the shape of a giant cross.
reading_hint: Imagine how the car feels as you ram into the showy flowers. The petals explode and rain everywhere. You turn on your windshield wipers to push them away.

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>root</radical> where your <radical>fang</radical> <kanji>previously</kanji> was. Your fang isn't there anymore, you ripped it out.
reading_mnemonic: To remember the beautiful fangs you <kanji>previously</kanji> had, you keep all of the ones you pull out in a box you lock with a <reading>key</reading> (き).
meaning_hint: Look at the root where your fang previously was. Previously, when you were young, you had a lot of nice fangs but they're all rotting as you get older and you pull them out.
reading_hint: Previously you didn't have a key, but now you do to keep your old fangs safe.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>line up</radical> your body with the <radical>sun</radical> and everything looks <kanji>normal</kanji>.  The sun is lined up behind you, and you’re looking at all the things the sun has lit up. Everything looks normal.
reading_mnemonic: Soon you see something that doesn’t look so <kanji>normal</kanji>. It’s a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ), a jester. Dressed all in fool’s garb, this fool is dancing up towards you with a ridiculously unsettling grin on his face.
meaning_hint: Gaze out in a line from the sun as you observe the world. Everything is very normal looking and comforting.
reading_hint: Imagine this creepy looking fool as he approaches. He’s kind of freaking you out. Feel very uncomfortable as you continue looking at this fool.

meaning_mnemonic: You put a <radical>lid</radical> on a dish you're cooking, made up of the <radical>mouth</radical>s and <radical>forehead</radical>s of a <radical>pig</radical>. This is truly an <kanji>exceptional</kanji> dish you are cooking up.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Exceptional</kanji> pig mouth forehead soup is a favorite of Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち. You see him sniffing around your window, then he knocks and waves.
meaning_hint: Mix those pig mouths and foreheads in your pot. Think of how exceptional your soup looks.
reading_hint: "Can I have some of your exceptional soup?" Grandpa ごういち asks. "It smells so swell."

meaning_mnemonic: See that <radical>gold</radical> man <radical>oversee</radical>ing everyone? He's a <kanji>model</kanji> human. His goldenness is a sign of how exemplary he is, and he's overseeing all of us flawed, regular folks to try to help us get on his level.
reading_mnemonic: As you look closer at the golden <kanji>model</kanji> human, you realize he's actually Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! No wonder he's covered in gold and overseeing everyone — he's the greatest conqueror in history!
meaning_hint: Imagine this golden man standing on a raised platform, looking down at you judgmentally with his arms crossed. You mutter curses at him under your breath, but really you're just jealous you're not a model human like him.
reading_hint: Hold on a sec — didn't Genghis Khan kill a bunch of people and father like a thousand kids? He doesn't seem like such a model person to you, but, hey, you don't make the rules around here — Genghis Khan does.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>building</radical>s are at a <radical>surplus</radical>. Because of that, we will need to <kanji>exclude</kanji> some of them from our future plans. Also, we'll need to <kanji>remove</kanji> some buildings too.
reading_mnemonic: Working with you on this project to <kanji>exclude</kanji> buildings from the plan, as well as <kanji>remove</kanji> buildings that already exist is little <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ).
meaning_hint: Say you're looking at a map of the city, with all the buildings laid out on it. There are too many, so you start marking which ones to exclude from the building plans. You also remove some buildings from the map all together.
reading_hint: She's pointing out buildings to exclude / remove with great ferocity. "Remove this one. Exclude this one. Oh, and remove this one too."

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Wolverine</radical> is doing a <radical>construction</radical> job and using his <radical>mouth</radical> to take <radical>measurement</radical>s, so he knows how many folks he needs to <kanji>inquire</kanji> with about helping him. Using the mouth is a strange way to take measurements, but that's just how Wolverine does things. Once he measures it all out, he'll know how many construction workers he needs to inquire with about working for him.
reading_mnemonic: Another thing Wolverine has to <kanji>inquire</kanji> about is <reading>jean</reading>s (じん). He wants to make sure everyone on the construction site has good-fitting jeans to work in, so he inquires with them about their jean measurements.
meaning_hint: Imagine Wolverine going around, measuring the construction site with his mouth. Look at him with his mouth wide open, wandering around the site deep in thought. What a weirdo.
reading_hint: Imagine Wolverine handing you some new jeans at the construction site. Um, they're kind of all ripped up? Turns out that's just the style he likes. You'll be so fashionable now! Just like good old '90s Wolvie.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>poop</radical> twice on a sleeping <radical>drunkard</radical> <radical>person</radical> and you find yourself wondering <kanji>how many</kanji> more times you can poop on them and wonder <kanji>how much</kanji> they can take before they realize what’s going on.
reading_mnemonic: As you continue thinking about <kanji>how much</kanji> poop this drunkard can take and <kanji>how many</kanji> more poops you can drop on him, he begins to wake up. Quickly, you <reading>eke</reading> (いく) out one last poop before you scurry away. You eke that poop out just in time to make your escape.
meaning_hint: You’ve already pooped twice on this drunkard, how many more times can you do it? How much do you think this person can take? Just imagine yourself doing this.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself as you eke out that last poop. Really concentrate on eking it out so you can get away in time. Flex your butt muscles for added effect.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>canopy</radical> <radical>guy</radical> is a guy who stands in the <kanji>corridor</kanji> trying to sell people canopies.
reading_mnemonic: You take the <kanji>corridor</kanji>, but this time the canopy guy is gone. In his place is a new salesman. This one is trying to sell you <reading>roe</reading> (ろう).
meaning_hint: You walk through this corridor every day, and the canopy guy always accosts you with canopy sales. You wish there was another corridor you could take, but it's the only way to get where you want to go.
reading_hint: The roe salesman in the corridor is worse than the canopy salesman. They're both insistent, but the roe salesman smells bad.

meaning_mnemonic: Wrap your <radical>fingers</radical> around this <radical>broom</radical>, because it's time to <kanji>sweep</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Sweep</kanji>ing is good for the <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Every sweep of the broom makes your soul a little more pure. Cleanliness is next to godliness, after all.
meaning_hint: Keep your fingers tightly wrapped around the broom — if you drop it mid-sweep, all that dirt and dust will go flying everywhere and you'll have to start over from scratch.
reading_hint: Feel your soul getting more and more purified as you sweep every inch of your house. Your soul's getting squeaky-clean, and it honestly feels amazing.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> came in and buried your <radical>flag</radical> so now you have to use a <radical>spoon</radical> and dig it out of the <kanji>mud</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You may know of Dora the Explorer. But have you heard about her dirty, <kanji>mud</kanji>dy brother, <reading>Doro</reading> (どろ)? They're like a team. And because of all the exploring he does, Doro is always covered in mud.
meaning_hint: Imagine using a spoon to dig your flag outta all this mud. So much mud! Feel its cold, soiled wetness on your hands as you dig away.
reading_hint: Imagine them working together — Dora and Doro, the Muddy Explorers! Who knows why she's able to stay clean, while Doro gets so muddy…

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> in <radical>spring</radical> that's nice and <radical>dry</radical> will make a good <kanji>pole</kanji> or <kanji>rod</kanji>. Break it out of the ground and give it a swing to see what a nice, sturdy <kanji>stick</kanji> it is!
reading_mnemonic: After much deliberation, you decide to use this <kanji>pole</kanji> to go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). It's a pretty long pole, so you can just stand at the top of the lane and directly poke the bowling pins with it.
meaning_hint: Use this pole to hang your towels or curtains. Ooh, use it to pole vault!
reading_hint: Imagine all the irritated glares you get as you use your pole to cheat your way through several games of bowling. That's against the rules, buddy!

meaning_mnemonic: If you can write an interesting <radical>poem</radical> about <radical>winter</radical> while riding on a <radical>horse</radical>, I’ll be pretty <kanji>surprised</kanji>. It’s hard enough to write a poem as it is, let alone an interesting one. And then you’re riding on a horse and the poem has to be about winter? Heck, I’d be surprised if you didn’t fall off the horse and injure yourself.
reading_mnemonic: You might also be <kanji>surprised</kanji> to learn that you are to ride this horse all across <reading>きょう</reading>と. That gives you a time limit. You have until you traverse きょうと to write this poem about winter.
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to write a poem while riding a horse. It’d be really hard to do. I bet you’d be surprised if you managed to do it as well.
reading_hint: Imagine riding through きょうと on a horse. As if that wasn’t difficult enough for poem writing, now you have all the sights and sounds of きょうと to distract you as well.

meaning_mnemonic: You use your <radical>mouth</radical> when you see a <radical>Chinese</radical> person and you <kanji>sigh</kanji>. You sigh so loud that it’s practically a roar coming out of your mouth. Chinese people and Chinese things make you sigh because it reminds you of when you stayed in China and it brings up memories. The sigh is a sigh of nostalgia.
reading_mnemonic: Your favorite Chinese thing to do is <reading>tan</reading> (たん). You can wander around without your shirt on and tan all day. No one will <kanji>sigh</kanji> or scoff at you, it’s perfectly acceptable. The Chinese just love to get their tan on.
meaning_hint: Let out a long sigh as you recall memories from China and all the Chinese things you saw and did. Oh, sigh.
reading_hint: Sigh again as you remember how nice it was to tan everywhere. Picture yourself wandering around some Chinese streets with your shirt off as you soak in the rays and get a tan.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>warehouse</radical> radical and the <kanji>warehouse</kanji> kanji are the same! You can call it a <kanji>storehouse</kanji> too.
reading_mnemonic: You have a <kanji>warehouse</kanji> to store all of your <reading>sou</reading>ls (そう). When people or animals you know pass away, you take their souls and store them in your dry warehouse for safekeeping. It's a public service, really.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Imagine if you kept a bunch of souls in a warehouse. This way they can't haunt anyone. And they can't get lost out in the spirit world. The warehouse is best for them.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>child</radical> shares his <radical>lineage</radical> with a <kanji>grandchild</kanji>. The child passes his lineage down to his child and then that child’s child is the grandchild and between them all a lineage is shared.
reading_mnemonic: The <reading>son</reading> (そん) of a son is a <kanji>grandchild</kanji>. In this case, a grandson. Easy.
meaning_hint: Look at this family tree. See the child, and then the grandchild. Trace the lineage with your finger as you observe.
reading_hint: Just remember that a son is all you really want. You need a son to carry on the family name and the lineage. So make sure that your child and your grandchild are both sons.

meaning_mnemonic: Birds use <radical>grass</radical> and <radical>fruit</radical> to make their <kanji>nest</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: The bottom of the <kanji>nest</kanji> also seems to be padded with someone's <reading>su</reading>it (す). Did... did this bird attack a human for this?!
meaning_hint: Picture a cute little nest with a family of birds in it. It looks like it was woven together with bits of grass and strawberries, so it smells delicious too.
reading_hint: Picture the nest, made of grass, fruit, and the suit of the angry salaryman who's shouting below the tree, and missing the top half of his suit.

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>mountain</radical> coming out of the <radical>ground</radical> that you want to climb, but your <radical>forehead</radical> needs a <radical>towel</radical> for all your sweat. You don't have a towel, so you take off your <kanji>belt</kanji> and tie it around your forehead.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>belt</kanji> isn't absorbent enough, so you wrap it in your <reading>tie</reading> (たい) and then tie the whole thing around your forehead again.
meaning_hint: The belt is easy to tighten and it catches all the sweat before it can fall into your eyes. It isn't quite as absorbent as a towel though.
reading_hint: It works! Your belt tied in a tie is the perfect invention!

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> around a <radical>tombstone</radical> to measure its <kanji>diameter</kanji>. You’re doing this to each tombstone, and if you loiter around long enough, you’ll soon know the diameter of every tombstone.
reading_mnemonic: You need to know the <kanji>diameter</kanji> of each tombstone so you can make <reading>ca</reading>kes (けい) for each one. But the cakes can't be wider than the tombstones, or they'll flop off onto the ground.
meaning_hint: Imagine loitering around a tombstone. You're leaning on it. You're staring at nothing. Oh, and you're also measuring its diameter.
reading_hint: These carefully measured tombstone-diameter cakes are your special way of saying thanks to the ghosts for letting you loiter around their graves.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>request</radical> that <radical>winter</radical> <kanji>rescue</kanji> you from the warmth of the other seasons.
reading_mnemonic: You get a note in the mail from winter! This is it, you're going to be rescued! You open the note and it says, "I have <kanji>rescue</kanji>d you with this <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう)." A cucumber falls out of the note.
meaning_hint: It's so hot. You hate it. You really hope winter will get your request and come early to rescue you.
reading_hint: This cucumber is supposed to be cool and refreshing enough to rescue you from the heat. I guess it was too much to expect winter to come early just for you. At least cucumbers are yummy.

meaning_mnemonic: If you play <radical>blackjack</radical> on the <radical>moon</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical>, your cards will <kanji>scatter</kanji> everywhere. The cards scatter because the gravity is so weak on the moon, so the blackjack game is prone to disaster.
reading_mnemonic: You are playing blackjack with <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん). Santa-san is doing his best to use his Christmas magic to prevent the <kanji>scatter</kanji> of cards, but it’s not working out so well. Good effort, Santa-san.
meaning_hint: Imagine playing blackjack on the moon where even the slightest move causes the cards to scatter about. Watch them float along. It’s very frustrating to play this way.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself next to Santa-san as he tries to prevent the cards from scattering. Look at the expression of concentration on Santa-san’s face as he tries to prevent the scatter.

meaning_mnemonic: If you grind <radical>rice</radical>, every <radical>part</radical> of it, eventually it will become a fine <kanji>powder</kanji>. You can then use this rice powder as a part to create some other dish. Some kind of cake, perhaps.
reading_mnemonic: Actually, grinding the rice down to a <kanji>powder</kanji> is super <reading>fun</reading> (ふん). It’s so fun you don’t want to stop! AH HAHAHAHA! RICE POWDER! SO FUN!
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself grinding this rice into a powder. Hear the scrape of the rice on stone as you grind it down to the finest powder.
reading_hint: Okay, we got a little carried away there. Just keep in mind how fun this is. Make sure you say fun with a little bit of a Scottish accent. You know what they say, Scots have more fun!

meaning_mnemonic: Under the <radical>moon</radical> while you sit on a <radical>cliff</radical>, you inject a syringe of <radical>tofu</radical> right into your <kanji>vein</kanji>. Ahh, tofu. You're addicted to it. You sneak out to the cliff at night to get your tofu fix, injecting it straight into your vein.
reading_mnemonic: Eventually, your friends and family find you at the cliff, tripping on tofu. Each and every <kanji>vein</kanji> on your body is engorged, pulsating with the effects of tofu. Your friends try and take you home. "You cyan't <reading>myake</reading> (みゃく) me!" you slur, suffering from the effects of the tofu. "None of you cyan myake me go home! You cyan’t myake me! Nyah!"
meaning_hint: Feel the tofu running through your veins. Feel the tofu goodness spreading all over your body. It's so intense you can even taste a little tofu in your mouth.
reading_hint: Say this out loud, it'll help you remember. Really focus on the "myake" be sure to say it like you're all bugged out from tofu overdose.

meaning_mnemonic: You want some <radical>flowers</radical> so you take your <radical>cleat</radical> to the side of a <radical>tree</radical> hoping they'll fall down, but you get a <kanji>vegetable</kanji> instead. What? You kick the tree again and another, different vegetable falls out.
reading_mnemonic: You feed these <kanji>vegetable</kanji>s (that you didn't even want) to your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). It only needs a little though, since its stomach is so thin, so you just make it hold the rest.
meaning_hint: Now you have two vegetables and no flowers. Do vegetables even grow on trees?
reading_hint: Picture your cyborg nibbling on the vegetables you cleat-kicked out of this tree. You give all of the things you don't like to your cyborg nowadays. Which is probably why there are so many weird things all over it...

meaning_mnemonic: You want to <radical>change</radical> your <radical>shellfish</radical> into <kanji>freight</kanji> so you can transport it. That means packing them up into giant freight containers.
reading_mnemonic: To move this <kanji>freight</kanji>, you need a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). You strap all of the freight containers to the top of your car, stacked nice and high and precarious, so you can move it.
meaning_hint: Imagine changing your shellfish from loose, wet form, to solid freight form. It's actually a lot of hard work.
reading_hint: Picture all of this freight stacked on top of your car. As you drive the car, every turn you make feels slow and heavy from the freight weight.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>building</radical> made from a <radical>womb</radical> must be placed upon the <kanji>land</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>land</kanji> around your womb building will, unfortunately, <reading>reek</reading> (りく) though.
meaning_hint: Why must it be placed upon the land? If it were put in water, it would dissolve too quickly. So, you put your womb building on the land, instead.
reading_hint: It's made of biological materials, so of course it will reek.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> of (odd-looking) <radical>lion</radical>s puts a <radical>drop</radical> of magic liquid on his face to look like a <radical>person</radical> who <kanji>resemble</kanji>s someone else. The leader of lions is going incognito, so he needs to resemble a person who is not himself. He takes this drop of powerful face paint and transforms.
reading_mnemonic:
Now he <kanji>resemble</kanji>s an even greater leader, and all the lions get down on one <reading>knee</reading> (に) to pay their respects.

(Note that we recently changed this kanji's main reading from ね to に, because ね is an irregular reading.)
meaning_hint: Watch this leader as the transformation takes place. Now he doesn’t resemble anything like the leader he used to be. He doesn’t look like the same person. Watch him morph in your head.
reading_hint: See all the odd-looking lions respectfully getting down on one knee? They look even odder now.

meaning_mnemonic: You have some <radical>dirt</radical> next to a <radical>pool</radical> that has an extra <radical>drop</radical> of water in it because you need them to be <kanji>equal</kanji>. If there's too much dirt outside the pool and not enough water inside, it's gonna look weird. We can't have that.
reading_mnemonic: You spend a few hours making sure the water-to-dirt ratio is perfectly <kanji>equal</kanji>, not for yourself, but because the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England told you to. You may agree that equality is important, but it's the King's responsibility to keep things that way. Which is why he ordered you, small peon, to do the equalizing work for him.
meaning_hint: Imagine seeing a pool that isn't equal. It's like a tiny kiddy pool with an inch of water. And it's surrounded by mounds of dirt. No no no, this won't do—add more water to make them equal.
reading_hint: Okay, so the existence of a king kind of makes things not, well, equal, for all people. But at least aesthetically everything will be!

meaning_mnemonic: Inside your <radical>greenhouse</radical> there's a mound of <radical>dirt</radical> which appears to be someone's <kanji>grave</kanji>. When you dig into it, you discover that it's an entire <kanji>tomb</kanji>, with doors and passages and everything!
reading_mnemonic: You pry open the <kanji>grave</kanji> inside the inner chamber of the <kanji>tomb</kanji>, and are shocked to find the mummified body of <reading>Bo</reading>bo (ぼ) the Clown staring back at you! How terrifying!
meaning_hint: Why would someone put a grave in a greenhouse? Is this one of those "green burials" everyone's talking about these days? Having the whole tomb complex seems a tad counterproductive, though…
reading_hint: You watch in horror as Bobo raises his arms and slowly rises from his grave. "At last, I have awoken from my slumber… Prepare yourselves, mortals — Bobo's taking reservations for birthday parties again!"

meaning_mnemonic: On your <radical>roof</radical> is a <radical>scarecrow</radical> because you're <kanji>rich</kanji>. You want to scare away all the poor, non-rich people so you put a scarecrow on your roof.
reading_mnemonic: You wake up the next morning and all of your <kanji>rich</kanji>es have been stolen! You call the police and they call you a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ)! Pardon me?! "You basically put an advertisement on your roof telling everyone you're rich. Of course you got robbed, you fool."
meaning_hint: Imagine looking up at the roof at this scarecrow. There's a sign around its neck that says, "Rich people welcome." Ah, you're safe. Time to go to bed!
reading_hint: You poor sad, no longer rich fool.

meaning_mnemonic: You're <radical>loiter</radical>ing, staring down at the <radical>cross</radical> that got caught in the <radical>net</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical>, because it's trying to teach you to act with <kanji>virtue</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: As you try to decide if you want to live a life of <kanji>virtue</kanji> the ghost of <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu appears before you. He points to your heart and the cross, then points to his own and you see a glowing cross in his own.
meaning_hint: People who keep the cross in their hearts are supposed to be full of virtue, always doing the right thing, always helping others. But you aren't sure if you want to be virtuous, it's a lot of responsibility. So you look down at this symbolic cross and loiter instead of making a decision.
reading_hint: I don't know about you, but I'm pretty sure Tokugawa Ieyasu is telling you to choose a life of virtue.

meaning_mnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> to your <radical>forehead</radical> and stand your <radical>legs</radical> high up in a <radical>tree</radical> if you want to <kanji>look for</kanji> something. After all, shielding your eyes and getting up high is the best way to look for something, isn’t it?
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>look for</kanji> it for so long that you can't help getting a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). I mean, you're up some tree, shielding your eyes from the sun as you look for that thing, and it's been hours, days? Of course you're going to get a tan.
meaning_hint: Imagine it's like a quest from a book or game. What are you looking for? Maybe a powerful sword, or a lost princess. Picture it in your mind, this thing you're looking for.
reading_hint: Feel the sun's rays on your skin as you search and search and search. Watch as your skin gets darker, crispier, and — hmm — starts smelling like cooked meat. Now that's a good tan.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> sticks his <radical>toe</radical> into a <radical>shellfish</radical>. You ask him why and he says he wants to <kanji>spy</kanji> on it.
reading_mnemonic: You and the leader review the <kanji>spy</kanji> footage and find a single <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tot hidden within the shellfish. Together you uncover a huge smuggling ring. Clams, oysters, mussels, you name it! Shellfish of all kinds are using their bodies to sneak tater tots to the undersea world.
meaning_hint: There’s a hidden camera on his toe and the leader wants to have plenty of secret spy footage to review later.
reading_hint: If it wasn't for your toe spy network, these egregious tater tot crimes would have never reached the light of day.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>thread</radical> is <radical>odd</radical>, but that's what makes it <kanji>beautiful</kanji>. It's a little frayed, it's a little miscolored, but that's what makes it appealing to you.
reading_mnemonic: You take this <kanji>beautiful</kanji> thread and lock it up with a <reading>key</reading> (き). You want to keep it all to yourself, you want its beauty to be for your eyes only!
meaning_hint: If something is a bit odd, it's usually beautiful in its own way, just like this beautiful thread.
reading_hint: To remember that there's a beautiful thread hidden away, you tie a not-as-beautiful thread to the end of the key.

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>canopy</radical> comes <radical>beforehand</radical>, it's a <kanji>preface</kanji> for the rest of the canopy.
reading_mnemonic: You read the <kanji>preface</kanji>. It just goes on and on and on about <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ), a short lady the author loves very much.
meaning_hint: Although not necessarily a book preface, you can imagine a book made out of canvas pages. The canvas pages that came beforehand (i.e., in the beginning, before the book started) is the preface.
reading_hint: Actually imagine reading through the preface of your favorite book. Except, there's a preface that just goes into great detail about Jo-Anne and doesn't seem related at all to the book.

meaning_mnemonic: In its <radical>scooter</radical>, a <radical>cat pirate</radical> scoots up to <kanji>welcome</kanji> you!
reading_mnemonic: The cat pirate then launches into a speech about how they <kanji>welcome</kanji> all kinds of people. Especially <reading>gay</reading> (げい) people. He puts a certain emphasis on gay, so you figure that the cat pirate either thinks you're gay or wants you to be.
meaning_hint: "Nya! Welcome to my ship, grab a scooter and scoot on up here, we're about to start the welcome ceremonya!"
reading_hint:
You're really happy this cat pirate welcomes gay people aboard. Whether that's because you're gay or just because you're a baseline decent human being is up to you.

Also if you aren't the kind of person who would welcome gay people onto your cat pirate ship, you should just leave nyaow.

meaning_mnemonic: If you look inside a <radical>samurai</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical>, you'll discover their true <kanji>intention</kanji>s and <kanji>ambition</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: You try to read the <kanji>intention</kanji>s of the samurai in front of you and they're dark, evil, scary. Oh no! These are the intentions of the <reading>shee</reading>p (し) samurai!
meaning_hint: If you're facing down a samurai, it's pretty important for you to be able to understand their intentions. Not all samurai are good, you know.
reading_hint: The sheep samurai only has violent intentions for other samurai and sheep. Its heart is dark and surprisingly easy to read.

meaning_mnemonic: If your <radical>mouth</radical> is <radical>big</radical> but your <radical>heart</radical> is in the right place, people will recognize your <kanji>kindness</kanji>. Even if you blabber on with your big mouth, as long as everyone knows that your heart is filled with kindness, they will love and accept you.
reading_mnemonic: To further show your <kanji>kindness</kanji>, you chop up some <reading>on</reading>ions (おん) for a friend who always cries when they chop onions. The onions make you cry too, so it's a nice act of kindness that you're toughing it out for your pal.
meaning_hint: Share your kindness with the world. Use your big mouth to let people know what you're doing. "I'M TAKING OUT THE TRASH AND NOBODY EVEN ASKED ME!" Y'know, that sort of thing. Go ahead and say that out loud a few times.
reading_hint: Imagine chopping up these onions. Smell the onion smell in the air as your eyes begin to sting from the onions. Your friend had better appreciate this kindness.

meaning_mnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical> to this <radical>cleat</radical> to dig out bits of <radical>tree</radical> and <kanji>gather</kanji> them. Taking your fingers to this cleat is the only way to gather the tree bits stuck in it. Gather them in a big pile… as evidence to examine later.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>gather</kanji> them all together and realize there are <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい) parts mixed in with the tree bits. Uh oh, it's as you feared. Your cyborg definitely got into trouble somewhere around here and you walked all over it with your cleats.
meaning_hint: Picture your fingers really getting in there to gather the tree pieces from out of this cleat. Some of the tree bits are stabbing under your fingernails, but you have to keep gathering them, don't stop now!
reading_hint: Gather all of these cyborg bits so you can put the cyborg back together later.

meaning_mnemonic: Under a <radical>tree</radical> sitting on the <radical>grass</radical> is a <radical>woman</radical> who loves <kanji>sakura</kanji>, or <kanji>cherry tree</kanji>, if you’re an English speaker. This woman really likes to just chill out in the grass under these cherry trees and watch the sakura blossoms. It’s very pretty.
reading_mnemonic: How do you say <kanji>cherry tree</kanji> in Japanese? <reading>Sakura</reading> (さくら). <kanji>Sakura</kanji> is Japanese for cherry tree. I’m sure you’ve probably heard this before, so it shouldn’t be too hard to remember.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself sitting under the tree in the grass with the woman. Smell the cherries and see the sakura blossoms falling down all around you. How whimsical.
reading_hint: Sakura means cherry tree and sakura is the way to pronounce this kanji. Easy!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>eternity</radical> radical and the <kanji>eternity</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: How long is an <kanji>eternity</kanji>? Just look at this little <reading>a</reading>corn (えい) and ask. The acorn starts small but it grows into a huge tree and can live for all eternity by dropping more acorns to create more trees. Its acorn line will live on forever!
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: It's the miracle of eternal life!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> that hits at <radical>night</radical> leaves even more <kanji>fluid</kanji> than normal. It's dark at night, so the sun isn't there to help evaporate all that tsunami <kanji>liquid</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>fluid</kanji> stays in the <reading>駅</reading> (えき) longer than anywhere else. All of those tsunami liquids pour deep inside the 駅 late at night and the sun still can't reach them to help evaporate them during the day. It's a real *night*mare.
meaning_hint: Picture the dark fluids from the tsunami flowing by your window at night. You look out and the liquid is shining in the moonlight. It looks like an entire sea is out there!
reading_hint: Picture the 駅 filled to the brim with fluids and liquids. Maybe if you swim, you'll be able to catch your train.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>eye</radical>'s <radical>root</radical> is the <kanji>eyeball</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You know who has some pretty good <kanji>eyeball</kanji>s? <reading>Gan</reading>dhi (がん). Gandhi used his eyes to see the oppression around him and decided to do something about it. But it's all thanks to his eyeballs.
meaning_hint: Go ahead and feel your eyeballs moving around in your head. Your eyes aren't just the part on the surface, they're the whole balls in there, attached at the root.
reading_hint: Look at Gandhi’s eyeball with your eyeball. It's pretty remarkable.

meaning_mnemonic: You come across a <radical>spirit</radical> wearing a <radical>top hat</radical> and it claims to be your <kanji>ancestor</kanji>. Well of course, if you remember correctly, all of your ancestors wore top hats when they were alive. This spirit must be your long lost, great great... uh... somebody.
reading_mnemonic: You ask your <kanji>ancestor</kanji> what it wants and it points to the <reading>so</reading>ap (そ) by the sink. Soap? It nods at you. I guess your ancestor is here to see the wonders of soap.
meaning_hint: Imagine coming across your long dead top hatted ancestor in spirit form. What would you do? What would you say?
reading_hint: Your ancestor didn't come here to see you, it just wanted to learn about soap. Something that they didn't have back when it was alive. That's actually kinda sad... and stinky. Your ancestor needs to use this soap, if anything.

meaning_mnemonic: Tie a <radical>thread</radical> to yourself whenever you take the <radical>blame</radical> for someone. This marks your <kanji>achievement</kanji>. Your achievement in blame-taking, I guess. Isn't it a great achievement to selflessly shield others from blame? Of course it is! So these little blame-threads are symbols of your achievement.
