reading_mnemonic: But your latest <kanji>achievement</kanji> is covering yourself in these threads and everyone thinking it's <reading>sexy</reading> (せき). The newspapers and fashion mags can't get enough of your sexy style. See? If you're a good person, people will think your achievements are sexy.
meaning_hint: Pretty soon your fingers, toes, and whole body are covered in threads, for all your innumerable achievements. Think back on those achievements, on all those times you took the blame for someone. You're just so, so selfless. What a good person you are. Ahh, it feels good to be good.
reading_hint: Imagine setting this sexy thread-based fashion trend. You're named the sexiest person alive and everyone dresses like spools of thread to copy you. But the achievements to match? Well, only you have those.

meaning_mnemonic: There’s a <radical>comb</radical> and what looks like the <radical>same</radical> <radical>comb</radical>, and you found them on the <radical>ground</radical> next to some <radical>fins</radical> you showed <kanji>interest</kanji> in. At first, the shiny fins on the ground caught your interest, but once you got closer to the fins you also noticed a pair of combs that are interesting too.
reading_mnemonic: Since the combs <kanji>interest</kanji> you more than the fins, you examine the combs more closely. Taking interest in where they were made, you discover they were made in <reading>きょう</reading>と. Dang, きょうと sure does make some nice combs.
meaning_hint: Pick up these fins and the two combs. Feel their weight in each hand as you examine them with interest. Smell the fish smell and feel the teeth on the combs.
reading_hint: You've always had an interest in きょうと, so it makes sense that you'd be interested in things from きょうと too.

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to <radical>go</radical> to <radical>Korea</radical> you'll need a good <kanji>defense</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You need a good <kanji>defense</kanji> to go to Korea, so you fill your pockets with <reading>a</reading>corns (えい). When you get to Korea you plant them all around yourself. In no time you'll have a strong tree defense, keeping you safe.
meaning_hint: Things in Korea haven't been on the safest side lately, so make sure you have some ways to defend yourself before you go.
reading_hint: Okay, so maybe this defense strategy will take a while, but you aren't rich and acorns are on the ground for free. Don't judge!

meaning_mnemonic: If your <radical>outfit</radical> gets sucked into a <radical>black hole</radical>, you'll need to get a <kanji>duplicate</kanji> version of the same outfit, 'cause you're not getting the original back. Hopefully you had <kanji>multiple</kanji>s…
reading_mnemonic: As you're shopping for <kanji>duplicate</kanji> outfits, some guy runs up and starts screaming "<reading>f***</reading> (ふく) you!" at you. "F*** you, man! You're out here buying <kanji>multiple</kanji> outfits while kids in other countries are starving!"
meaning_hint: Always pick up a few duplicate outfits if you're planning on venturing out to a black hole.
reading_hint: In response, you shout "F*** you too, bro! I need these duplicate outfits for my black hole mission!" For the next few minutes, the two of you just scream "f*** you" at each other multiple times, over and over.

meaning_mnemonic: You count <radical>nine</radical> of them in the <radical>tree</radical>, each <radical>turkey</radical> holding something <kanji>random</kanji> or <kanji>miscellaneous</kanji>. There are nine turkeys in the tree and they’re all holding random, miscellaneous things. How odd.
reading_mnemonic: As if things couldn’t get any more <kanji>random</kanji>, <reading>Zazu</reading> (ざつ) from the Lion King flies into the tree and starts asking the nine turkeys <kanji>miscellaneous</kanji> trivia. Zazu always did have a thirst for knowledge, but this is kind of strange.
meaning_hint: Look at the turkeys in the tree. There are nine of them and they are all gobbling up a storm. Hear them gobble as you imagine what random, miscellaneous things they are holding.
reading_hint: Look at Zazu asking the turkeys random questions about miscellaneous trivia. You’ve seen the Lion King, right? Zazu should be pretty easy to visualize here.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>husband</radical> and his <radical>husband</radical> find a <radical>shellfish</radical> and <kanji>agree</kanji> to share it.
reading_mnemonic: Since the husbands were able to <kanji>agree</kanji>, <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん) believes they’re both good husbands and belong on the good list. Therefore, Santa-san gives them each lots and lots of shellfish for Christmas.
meaning_hint: Picture the husbands pulling a different side of the shellfish, it pops in two, and they each get a shell. They smile and high five.
reading_hint: Imagine Santa-san flying over the husbands in his sleigh, raining shellfish down upon them from the sky. It warms the heart, wouldn’t you agree?

meaning_mnemonic: Take some <radical>alcohol</radical> and put a <radical>spider</radical> in it to make <kanji>acid</kanji>. Spiders have a little acid in them already, but if you want something really potent, smash one up in some whiskey to make a deadly acid that'll rip through anything.
reading_mnemonic: You're trying to find a container for this <kanji>acid</kanji> when <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん) bursts through your wall. He frowns down at you. You're on the naughty list now. You killed spiders AND made dangerous acid? Ooooh you're in trouble.
meaning_hint: Imagine crushing up some spiders into alcohol. Watch it turn green. It's going to eat through the beaker! Quick, do something!
reading_hint: It's Christmas morning. Instead of gifts, Santa-san just throws a piece of charcoal at your face. Feel the charcoal smash into your face and explode into acid, burning you. Santa-san runs away laughing. Ho ho ho!

meaning_mnemonic: To use your <radical>gold</radical> at the <radical>bar</radical> you need to turn it into a <kanji>coin</kanji>. Well, more than one coin, probably. But you know for sure that you can make at least one coin out of the gold you have.
reading_mnemonic: You use the <kanji>coin</kanji> to pay a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) at the bar. The centaur is the bartender and he makes special drinks for those who pay in gold coins. Good for you!
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself strenuously pushing a lever to flatten your gold and punch a coin out it. You sweat as you hurry to make enough coins so you can go to the bar before it closes.
reading_hint: Imagine handing this centaur bartender your gold coins. Hear him thank you in his booming voice and make you a mysterious gold-colored drink. Look in wonder at how the centaur has managed to fit in the narrow space behind the bar counter.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>eat</radical> part of a <radical>director</radical> to <kanji>domesticate</kanji> him. This director has been going around kicking cameramen and punching actors and it’s really ruining his reputation. You need to domesticate him. The only way to do this is to shock him into submission. His ego is so big from two blockbusters that you have to eat part of him to get him to calm down.
reading_mnemonic: While you did succeed in your attempt to <kanji>domesticate</kanji> the director, he is now bleeding profusely from the bite wound. All over the script! You gotta get your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) and drive him to the hospital like right away. Get to the car! Hurry!
meaning_hint: Which part of the director did you choose to eat in order to domesticate him? Think about eating that part of the director and imagine its taste in your mouth as you chew. Eww.
reading_hint: You’re proud that you managed to domesticate the director, but now you feel kind of bad for having injured him. Okay, you really just feel bad because he’s probably going to sue you and he definitely has a lot of money. Oh, and he’s bleeding all over your car.

meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>gun</radical> it's <radical>easy</radical> to <kanji>wound</kanji> people. When you have a gun and a lot of responsibility it's easy to get carried away and mess up, wounding the people you were supposed to be protecting. You can even wound yourself.
reading_mnemonic: Which leader wounded the Japanese people? The <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). The Shougun is a military leader. The Shougun even has gun in the name. And they wounded hundreds of thousands of samurai and soldiers.
meaning_hint: Picture how easy it would be for the leader of your country to wound someone if they had a gun. Scary, right?
reading_hint: Picture the Shougun you've slowly grown to know over the last thirty-ish levels. But he's holding a gun wounding everyone around him. He's screaming and shooting and wounding everyone in sight. Even his mom, even his dog, even his farmers.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> and his <radical>older brother</radical> join a <kanji>party</kanji>. They can raid and pillage better in a <kanji>group</kanji> like this, so it makes sense that the Viking and his older brother would join a group with other Vikings.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>party</kanji> has decided to raid <reading>とう</reading>きょう. It’s a large undertaking, but they have a large group of Vikings on the job. とうきょう doesn’t stand a chance.
meaning_hint: Look at this big group that contains the Viking and his older brother. Hear them shouting as a group and imagine their Viking battle cry.
reading_hint: Imagine this group of Vikings rampaging through とうきょう. See all the Japanese people running away and screaming as the group of Vikings comes busting in, swinging their axes and shouting loudly.

meaning_mnemonic: Two <radical>pool</radical>s, both altered and not quite like a pool you’ve seen before are put together and flipped around – this is an <kanji>egg</kanji>. One pool is the egg white, the other pool is the egg yolk. They’re different, but similar.
reading_mnemonic: You remember <reading>Tamago</reading>tchi (たまご), right? Well, Tamagotchi is an <kanji>egg</kanji>, and the toy looks like an egg, so it’s pretty easy to remember that tamago means egg.
meaning_hint: Separate the two pools from one another. Now the pools look like the kanji. The kanji is one white pool and one yellow pool. Neat, huh?
reading_hint: Just think Tamagotchi whenever you see this kanji. Tamagotchi = tamago = egg. Easy!

meaning_mnemonic: If you see a <radical>grass</radical>y <radical>cliff</radical> with an <radical>elf</radical> under it in <radical>winter</radical>, it's because their parents are so <kanji>strict</kanji>. When elf kids misbehave they're supposed to go stand under grassy cliffs to think about what they've done.
reading_mnemonic: One of the <kanji>strict</kanji> parents of this elf kid is... <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん)?! Looks like he had another illegitimate kid... this time with an elf.
meaning_hint: Only really, really strict parents would make their kids do that in the middle of winter! It's cold!
reading_hint: Oh Genji, if only you were as strict about using protection as you were on your elf child.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> have nothing to do when you're at your <radical>cottage</radical> except <kanji>throw away</kanji> things. Your cottage is for vacation. And vacation means being as lazy as possible. But you certainly don't want to live in filth, so you have to throw away your trash at least.
reading_mnemonic: The number one thing you <kanji>throw away</kanji> while in your cottage is your <reading>su</reading>it (す). Suits just don't fit the cottage vibe, man.
meaning_hint: Imagine sitting in your cottage. You throw away a banana peel. You throw away some used suntan lotion. You throw away all your cares in the world.
reading_hint: Just throw away all your suits when you want to relax. Don't worry about needing one someday, throw it awaaaay!

meaning_mnemonic: If you get on your <radical>scooter</radical> and <radical>enter</radical> somewhere, you’re going <kanji>into</kanji> that place.
reading_mnemonic: Once you’ve gone <kanji>into</kanji> this place, what do you see? Your <reading>子</reading> (こ)!
meaning_hint: Really focus on going "into" wherever this is. Into the place you go. Right into it.
reading_hint: How did your 子 get into here? Your 子 really gets into everywhere. They get into all kinds of places, and they get into all kinds of trouble!

meaning_mnemonic: Standing on your <radical>roof</radical>, you're <radical>certain</radical> that the nearby <radical>mountain</radical> is shrouded in <kanji>secrecy</kanji>. But you can't do much about it, stuck on your roof as you are. So much secrecy, but so far away.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>secrecy</kanji> is so secret that the people who know about it wear magical <reading>mitts</reading> (みつ) of secrecy that make it so we can't tell anyone, no matter what.
meaning_hint: Really feel the frustration of wanting to uncover all the secrecy around this mountain but being stuck on your stupid roof.
reading_hint: You can imagine trying to break one of the mitts-wearing members of this group, but they can't tell you anything because of their magical mitts.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hit not one, but <radical>TWO</radical> <radical>prison</radical>s, making them extremely <kanji>dirty</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: What's the most <kanji>dirty</kanji> are the prisons' <reading>o</reading>bi (お) collections. You can hear the prisoners crying, "Oh, not the obi. Our obi are all dirty, oh no!"
meaning_hint: Imagine observing the two prisons being hit by a giant wave of muddy water, making them completely dirty. How disgusting!
reading_hint: The obi were the only nice thing the prisoners had left, and now they're all dirty. Even as you leave the prisons, you can still hear their echoing cries of "Oh, not our obi!"

meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>valley</radical> you feel a <radical>lack</radical> of <kanji>want</kanji>. You want for nothing in this valley. It is the valley of plenty.
reading_mnemonic: Once you get to the valley, you <kanji>want</kanji> some protein. Egg <reading>yolk</reading> (よく) falls from the sky! It’s a miracle! You wanted protein, and egg yolk was presented. It falls right into your open mouth.
meaning_hint: Think of how much you want to be in this valley. They have everything in there. You lack nothing. You want for nothing. Man, you wanna be in that valley so badly.
reading_hint: This was the yolk you wanted. Feel it slosh into your mouth from the sky. Taste the yolk on your tongue before swallowing it. Mmm, egg yolk.

meaning_mnemonic: You're in the <radical>sun</radical> playing <radical>football</radical> and you get very <kanji>warm</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: It's <kanji>warm</kanji> like this because it's <reading>dawn</reading> (だん). That's when the sun comes up.
meaning_hint: Feel how warm the sun is, as you try to throw and catch the football.
reading_hint: Dang, you knew you should have played night football so it wouldn't get so warm. Now the dawn sun is making you uncomfortable under all those layers of football protection.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> combines with a <radical>table</radical> to make a <kanji>desk</kanji>. When you think about it, a desk is just a table with more stuff. To make this more stuff, like drawers and such, you need more wood, and wood comes from trees. Add materials from the tree to the table and you have a desk.
reading_mnemonic:
When you finish the <kanji>desk</kanji> you turn to your friend and say, “<reading>It’s cool, eh</reading> (つくえ)?” Your friend looks at you and says, “S’coo’ eh? What?” “This desk, it’s cool. It’s cool, eh?” “Aw, yeah man. S’coo’ eh.”

Another way to remember this reading is to think about where you find lots of desks. That's right, in a <reading>school</reading> (つくえ).
meaning_hint: Chop this tree into pieces to transform this table into a desk. Hear the sounds of construction in your head as you make this desk with your bare hands.
reading_hint: Just imagine someone slurring “It’s cool, eh?” While you think about how cool this desk really is. Run your hands over the finished project as you say it.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>grain</radical> is <radical>certain</radical> to keep a <kanji>secret</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: If someone finds out your <kanji>secret</kanji> (not from the grain though, it will certainly keep it) then your entire face will fill with <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ).
meaning_hint: Imagine telling a piece of grain your deepest, darkest secret.
reading_hint: This works both ways too. If someone tells you a secret, that same heat fills you up. Now whether this heat is from embarrassment or the rush of getting to be part of a secret is up to you to figure out.

meaning_mnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> a <radical>shrimp</radical> who is bad with <kanji>translation</kanji> has no <kanji>reason</kanji> to live. Most shrimp make a living with their translation skills. They are the translators of the ocean. If they don’t know how to say something in all the languages of the sea, they have no reason to live. They might as well be dead.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>translation</kanji> this shrimp failed to make was for the word “<reading>yak</reading>” (やく). The shrimp found no <kanji>reason</kanji> to translate yak because there are no yak in the sea and then the shrimp just started questioning everything in his life and fell apart.
meaning_hint: Imagine this one shrimp who is bad at translation. He’s trying to find a reason to live, but he’s having trouble. Look at him all alone, being shunned by the other shrimp.
reading_hint: The translation of the word yak was the reason this shrimp fell into despair and gave up the translation profession. Now everyone hates him. See this poor shrimp being harassed by others while they shout at him, “Yak! Yak! Yak!”

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> runs into <radical>nine</radical> <radical>tree</radical>s and produces a <kanji>dye</kanji> the color of the forest. The tsunami water mixes with each tree, nine of them actually, and the resulting liquid is a forest green dye.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>dye</kanji> continues to pass through the forest and hits a group of <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん). Now each centaur is dyed with the forest green dye. They don’t look happy.
meaning_hint: Imagine this happening in front of you. See the tsunami turn from the color of water to the color of a forest. The dye washes over you and now you’re forest green as well.
reading_hint: Hear each centaur shouting about how he hates that dye for changing his color. They look kind of funny, so you laugh at them a bit, but not too loud – they might hear you and get even more angry.

meaning_mnemonic: You line up stalks of <radical>bamboo</radical> with an equal <radical>interval</radical> between each one. This pattern creates a sense of <kanji>simplicity</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: That kind of <kanji>simplicity</kanji> is what Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) always demanded. Everything he decided was based on simplicity.
meaning_hint: Bamboo, interval, bamboo, interval, bamboo… Almost like a binary rhythm: 1, 0, 1, 0, 1. Now that's simplicity. It doesn't get simpler than that!
reading_hint: Simplicity is how he took over the world. Attack or don't attack? Boom, simple. If he hadn't stuck to simplicity, he wouldn't have been able to do what he did. Complex thoughts just get in the way of expansionist ambitions, you know?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>gate</radical> to <radical>talent</radical> is now <kanji>closed</kanji>. In fact, the gate to talent is always closed. It's not like anyone can just waltz in and get themselves some talent. You have to work for it! You have to work hard to get through that closed gate to talent!
reading_mnemonic: Outside the talent gate that's always <kanji>closed</kanji>, <reading>hay</reading> (へい) is piled up to make beds for the people who are still trying to get in. Who knows how long they'll be there outside the closed gate, sleeping in the hay, but they're all working hard to become talented.
meaning_hint: After your hard work, hear the gate close behind you with a creak. You're on the inside now, you're one of the talented ones. And your talents have changed you, made you different from the others — much like the talent radical in this kanji is a little different from its usual shape!
reading_hint: Picture a bunch of people outside the closed gates, sleeping in piles of hay. You were there too once. You know how itchy that hay is, and how cold it can be at night. But it was all worth it.

meaning_mnemonic: To <radical>say</radical> what's in a <radical>samurai</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical> you start a <kanji>magazine</kanji>. You go around writing about and interviewing all the samurai so you can share what they say to everyone who wants to read!
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, your <kanji>magazine</kanji> helps the <reading>shee</reading>p (し) samurai find other samurai... and kill them. None of the samurai want to share their feelings with your magazine anymore, oh no!
meaning_hint: Why hadn't anyone thought of making a samurai magazine before?!
reading_hint: The sheep samurai is ruining your magazine! Maybe you should start a new magazine called, "All the places the sheep samurai will be, so stay away!" to make up for all the damage you've caused.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>hole</radical> leading to the <radical>private</radical> <radical>heart</radical> of your house is the <kanji>window</kanji>. Or maybe it's just the window to your soul, and not your house. Like the poetry people say.
reading_mnemonic: You realize that a ton of people are looking into your <kanji>window</kanji> and seeing all your private things. This makes you <reading>mad</reading> (まど)! What right do they have to know your private thoughts and desires?!
meaning_hint: Imagine someone looking into a hole that shows your private heart. That's a window. A window you probably should have kept closed. Now everyone is looking in.
reading_hint: Look out your window back at the faces. Try to express how mad you are at them with your face through the glass. Make as many mad faces that you can think of.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>Not</radical> <radical>Mouth</radical> says “<kanji>no</kanji>”. What else would you expect from something named the “Not Mouth”? The Not Mouth will always say “no”, regardless of the question.
reading_mnemonic: You ask the Not Mouth if it is afraid of <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). The Not Mouth says, “<kanji>no</kanji>”. So you set the Not Mouth on fire. The Not Mouth then screams, “NONONONONONONONONONOOOOOO~!” Looks like someone can’t handle the heat.
meaning_hint: Imagine encountering this Not Mouth. Ask it a question. It answers, “No”. Imagine doing this over and over until you believe that the Not Mouth always says “no”.
reading_hint: Listen as the Not Mouth continues to scream in bloodcurdling agony. Hear the flames flicker as they lick across the Not Mouth’s being. Seems as though the heat was too much for the Not Mouth.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>bamboo</radical> absorbed the <radical>moon</radical>'s <radical>power</radical>, and you want that power in your body, so you surgically insert them into your body to replace your <kanji>muscle</kanji>s and <kanji>tendon</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: Your special <kanji>muscle</kanji>s and <kanji>tendon</kanji>s have become famous and the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England has requested that you appear in his court! It looks like he wants you to teach his barber surgeons how you got your new muscles.
meaning_hint: Picture your new bamboo muscles glowing under your skin with the moon's power. You flex and feel the power coursing through you!
reading_hint: Imagine standing before the King, explaining in detail how you cut yourself open and replaced your weak human meat with the special muscles you have now. He's staring intently at you, but more so at your shining body than your mouth as you talk.

meaning_mnemonic: This kanji comes straight from the <radical>droopy</radical> radical. What do droopy things do? They <kanji>droop</kanji>. Oh, and sometimes they <kanji>dangle</kanji> too.
reading_mnemonic: You know what's better when it <kanji>droop</kanji>s a little? A <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい). Those swings with the droopy rubber seats are so comfortable to sit on and <kanji>dangle</kanji> your legs off of — way better than the rigid wooden ones.
meaning_hint: Don't let your spirits droop — kanji can be tough to learn, but you're doing great!
reading_hint: Imagine setting your bum in a droopy rubber swing seat. Feel it droop down a little lower as you put your weight into it and let your legs dangle. Ah, such comfort.

meaning_mnemonic: Under his <radical>roof</radical>, the <radical>king</radical> stares into a <radical>drop</radical> of water containing his greatest <kanji>treasure</kanji>. Just kidding, the king has way more treasure than that. The drop of water is just reflecting what's around him: mounds and mounds of treasure, stacked all the way up to the roof.
reading_mnemonic: All of this <kanji>treasure</kanji> is kept in the king's <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). He can't let other people see how much treasure he has, or they'll get mad. Or worse, ask him to share some with his people. Yuck. The treasure stays at home.
meaning_hint: Picture the treasure reflected in the drop of water. It's marvelous!
reading_hint: Picture his home from the outside. It looks like a normal, small castle. But then you walk in and it's a treasure trove of... well, treasure!

meaning_mnemonic: Someone climbs onto the <radical>roof</radical> from the <radical>ground</radical> when <radical>dawn</radical> comes to <kanji>proclaim</kanji> that a new day has begun! They proclaim this at the top of their lungs!
reading_mnemonic: The thing that climbs up on the roof to <kanji>proclaim</kanji> that the new day is here is a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). Ohoho, did you think I was going to say it was a rooster? Wrong! Centaurs are taller than humans and roosters, so when they climb onto the roof with their horse legs they can see dawn starting before anyone else.
meaning_hint: Picture a shadowy shape on top of the roof, proclaiming to the entire town that the new day is here! It's come! And it's also time to get up.
reading_hint: Picture that shadow on the roof, proclaiming it's a new day, but this time make sure you picture the outline of this centaur. He has a human top half and a horse bottom half. And he's yelling down at you, "HEY THE SUN IS UP IT'S DAWN GET OUT OF BED!"

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>horns</radical> of the creatures who drink <radical>alcohol</radical> have a <radical>measurement</radical> so large that they are <kanji>revered</kanji> the world over. The horns are revered both for their size and potency. The horns can be used in medicine, so they are very valuable, those alcoholic monster horns.
reading_mnemonic: The reason you are interested in these <kanji>revered</kanji> horns so much is that your <reading>son</reading> (そん) is sick and you need one of these revered horns to make a medicine for your son. Only with the revered power of the horns will you be able to save your son’s life.
meaning_hint: Look at one of these revered horns. Pick it up and feel its weight. It measures very large. Your arms are getting tired from the weight.
reading_hint: Good thing you already have one of these revered horns. Now imagine yourself grinding it up into a powder to mix into a potion for your son to drink. Hear the horn grinding away as you physically break the horn up into millions of tiny pieces.

meaning_mnemonic: Those that are in the <radical>middle</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> are the ones you have the most <kanji>loyalty</kanji> towards. The middle of your heart is the most sacred, special place for people, so it makes sense that those you keep in the middle of your heart are those you are most loyal to.
reading_mnemonic: You pictured <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう), didn’t you? No? Well you should have. Chewbacca deserves everyone’s <kanji>loyalty</kanji>. Chewbacca is super loyal to you, so he deserves your loyalty back.
meaning_hint: Who do you feel the most loyalty towards? Imagine them in the middle of your heart. Feel them in your chest. Imagine them, literally inside the middle of your heart.
reading_hint: Chewbacca is very loyal. Focus on your loyalty to Chewbacca. Picture him right in the middle of your heart and hear his Chewbacca yell. Also keep in mind that this kanji shares its reading with 中 which is also included in this kanji.

meaning_mnemonic: To make your <radical>fingers</radical> <radical>wide</radical> you <kanji>extend</kanji> them.
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>extend</kanji> your fingers a <reading>cock</reading> (かく) comes running by and runs straight into them. Bu-COCK!
meaning_hint: Extend your fingers right now and see how wide you can make them.
reading_hint: You poked the cock right in the eye with your extended fingers. Maybe you shouldn't be doing that in public. Innocent cocks could get hurt.

meaning_mnemonic:
If someone has their <radical>fingers</radical> in your <radical>syrup</radical>, it's just another way of saying they <kanji>manipulate</kanji>d you. Cause only slimy people manipulate other people, so their fingers are slimy and sticky because they're covered in your syrup from mucking up your life.

This also has another meaning: <kanji>chastity</kanji>. Just imagine if someone wanted you to keep your chastity and they called s.e.x. when someone "puts their fingers in your syrup." That would be pretty effective, right? Cause it's like, super gross.
reading_mnemonic: If you want to <kanji>manipulate</kanji> someone else it's going to have a permanent effect on your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Being manipulative isn't cool. And your soul will suffer forever for it.
meaning_hint: "Ah golly gee wiz, he sure got his fingers all up in my syrup again, dagnabbit." You know, this saying we say all the time when someone manipulates us into doing something.
reading_hint: What happens to your soul, you ask? It gets covered in syrup. That's probably where the syrup fingers phrase came from in the first place.

meaning_mnemonic: Writing a <radical>poem</radical> about <radical>winter</radical> is the only way to get <kanji>respect</kanji> from other poets.
reading_mnemonic: Once you have their <kanji>respect</kanji> you get <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)!
meaning_hint: Even better, write a poem about winter IN winter. Then they'll really respect you.
reading_hint: The cake has the word RESPECT in fancy, cursive letters. Imagine eating that cake and taste the respect of your poet peers. Mm mm!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>greenhouse</radical> is already really good when it comes to a plant's livelihood. But add the <radical>sun</radical> into that equation and you have something really great for the plants. Their <kanji>livelihood</kanji> will be awesome, now!
reading_mnemonic: From out of nowhere, <reading>Bo</reading>bo (ぼ) the notorious clown thief starts picking the plants, ending their <kanji>livelihood</kanji>. Bobo needs the flowers for his clown acts, so he uses the livelihood of the flowers to support his own livelihood.
meaning_hint: Just feel happy that your plants have such a nice livelihood now that the sun is out. They look so healthy and fresh!
reading_hint: Look at Bobo do what he needs to do to maintain his own livelihood. Hear the flowers screaming as they are plucked from their homes.

meaning_mnemonic: On a <radical>cliff</radical>, a <radical>fire</radical> burns to <kanji>ashes</kanji>. The ashes then blow off the cliff, and follow the wind wherever it may go.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>ashes</kanji> inspire you to write a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい). Using the ashes you write a haiku on a rock for everyone to enjoy.
meaning_hint: See the light from the fire as the ashes fly off the cliff. The wind carries some of the ashes over to you. Feel the ashes as they strike your face and wipe the black ash with your hands.
reading_hint: Ashes on my face. Falling from the cliff of fire. Gives my life meaning. Or just come up with your own haiku. Whatever works for you.

meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>receive</radical> something edible, arrange it in a <radical>circle</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> it. That way, it’ll <kanji>ripen</kanji> more quickly.
reading_mnemonic: Once everything you received has <kanji>ripen</kanji>ed, you kick back near your <reading>juke</reading>box (じゅく) and eat it. The jukebox sets the mood, making the ripened treat feels twice as sweet.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself receiving something, arranging it in a circle in the pan, and leaving it to boil. See how quickly that stuff ripens? Now it’s all ripe and yummy.
reading_hint: Imagine the jukebox playing music to accompany your ripened treat. Ahh, jukebox music and ripened goodies. Can life get any sweeter?

meaning_mnemonic: Put a <radical>rice paddy</radical> <radical>together</radical> with other paddies and you'll see how much they <kanji>differ</kanji>. When you're driving or taking a train through the countryside it may seem like all of those rice paddies blurring together are all the same, but they can differ quite a bit!
reading_mnemonic: To see how rice paddies <kanji>differ</kanji>, you need an <reading>ea</reading>gle's (い) eye view. It can be hard to see from the ground, so soar up into the sky like an eagle and look down at the rice paddies together.
meaning_hint: Try to picture how each rice paddy differs from the next. Can you think of any differences in the ones you've seen?
reading_hint: Aha! As an eagle you can easily see that all of the rice paddies have differing colors, shapes, and (if you fly low) smells. They differ a lot!

meaning_mnemonic: Social commentary aside, the <radical>white</radical> <radical>king</radical> is the king that wears all white. Traditionally, this is someone better than a king. The <kanji>emperor</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: Who is the <kanji>emperor</kanji>? It is <reading>こう</reading>いち. こういち the emperor stands over his subjects.
meaning_hint: See the emperor, garbed all in white. There are other kings, but the emperor's white clothing shows his status above the rest.
reading_hint:
How did こういち become emperor?! His first decree as emperor is that everybody must learn kanji now.

Note: from now on, Emperor こういち will be a recurring character for the reading mnemonic of こう.

meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>become</radical> a <radical>plate</radical>, I will <kanji>pile</kanji> food up upon you. With such a huge pile, everyone will think you're <kanji>prosperous</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You're so <kanji>prosperous</kanji> you use your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) to cut the food pile and shove the bits into your plate mouth. In fact, your entire family is using your special saber to cut and eat off of you. A normal knife wouldn't be able to get through all this food!
meaning_hint: If you have piles of something, you're prosperous. If you become a plate, that's the best thing that can happen to you.
reading_hint: You know a family is seriously prosperous when they don't use forks, they use a magical saber to cut their food. Wowee wow wow.

meaning_mnemonic: If you grind a <radical>stone</radical> down to more than just a <radical>few</radical> pieces, it will become <kanji>sand</kanji>. Stones that get broken up enough will become sand, and you pick up a few grains in your hand.
reading_mnemonic: After breaking up your stone into <kanji>sand</kanji>, you fetch your special sand <reading>sa</reading>w (さ). A few bits are still too chunky, so you'll have to use a sand saw now.
meaning_hint: Feel these few grains of sand between your fingertips. Imagine the stones that they originally came from. Nature is crazy.
reading_hint: You may wonder what a sand saw is. Well, it's a saw for cutting sand into even finer little pieces. How does it work? It's a mystery!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits the <radical>greenhouse</radical>, destroying the last remaining plant life in the area. The tsunami recedes, and there is nothing but sand, and the lands become a <kanji>desert</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: See the sun come up over the <kanji>desert</kanji>, then go <reading>back</reading> (ばく) down. Up, back down, back up, back down. You stand here for days watching because you're so depressed. Stop being so <kanji>vague</kanji> in your approach, sun! You’re weirding everyone out!
meaning_hint: The greenhouse was your only hope in turning the desert into a green forest. Now it's all washed away by salt water, meaning nothing will grow anyways. Feel disappointed about this.
reading_hint: Up, back down, back up, back down. Move your head up and down to keep up with the sun’s movements. Will it ever rise like normal?

meaning_mnemonic: Bring some <radical>rice</radical> under a <radical>canopy</radical> and stuff it in <radical>Wolverine</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical>. Make him chew it a bit, scrape it out, and voila — you've made <kanji>sugar</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: You learned how to make this kind of <kanji>sugar</kanji> in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. You found a special とうきょう sugar sensei and they introduced you to all the secret methods you know now.
meaning_hint: Taste that sweet, sweet sugar. Not many people know, but this is how it's made. The canopy is needed for temperature control, and the wolverine… well, it's got special enzymes in its mouth or something.
reading_hint: As you eat the sugar, you picture とうきょう. The place where you fell in love with these sugar-making techniques.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> you have <radical>inside</radical> is your whole <kanji>supply</kanji>. When you're outside sewing and you run out, you just go inside, look through your supply, and pick a new one.
reading_mnemonic: You have this <kanji>supply</kanji> because <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう) had a prediction, saying you would. And that if you didn't keep a good supply of thread inside, you'd run out.
meaning_hint: Go inside your house or shed, it doesn't matter where, and look happily at your thread supply. You have thread in all sizes, shapes, and especially colors.
reading_hint: Nostradamus says you should have a large supply of everything you use regularly. Thread, water, canned foods. You can't see the future, but he can, and if he's telling you to stock up on your inside supplies I think you should listen.

meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>moon</radical> and a <radical>city</radical> inside your <kanji>lung</kanji>. Inside of your lung there is a city and above that city lies a moon. It’s a pretty sight, but also very strange. Moons and cities don’t usually reside inside lungs.
reading_mnemonic: Having a moon and city inside your <kanji>lung</kanji> inspires you to write a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい). Perhaps the city and moon are just metaphorical. Either way, a haiku is the best way to go about things here.
meaning_hint: Imagine having a moon and a city inside your lung. Feel their presence as you breathe in and breathe out. Also, 月 often refers to internal organs as well, so remember that when you see this kanji.
reading_hint:
The city and moon
Inside my lung, forgotten.
Where is the answer?

Or just think of your own haiku. Be creative!

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to give <radical>flowers</radical> to <radical>someone</radical>, give them to an <kanji>author</kanji>. Authors really love flowers, because they help them think of flowery language they can use to sound smart in their books.
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, the <kanji>author</kanji> is allergic to touching flowers, so they hold them with a pair of <reading>cho</reading>psticks (ちょ). They hold them real close to their face with the chopsticks, so they can still use them to think of fancy, author-ly words.
meaning_hint: Imagine a time when a book you were reading used flowery language and you couldn't understand. Imagine the annoyance with the author for being so overwrought and flowery.
reading_hint: Picture the author trying to use chopsticks to hold some little flowers. It looks like they're having a hard time, but people will do anything to be a great author, I suppose.

meaning_mnemonic: If you take the <radical>flowers</radical> off some <radical>coral</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> them, <kanji>steam</kanji> comes out.
reading_mnemonic: As you watch the <kanji>steam</kanji> coming off the coral flowers, someone appears behind you. It’s <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big farmhand. "Are you steaming coral?" he asks. "Smelled it a mile away. I love steaming me some coral.”
meaning_hint: Steam comes out of the coral flowers because they hold a lot of water. They're flowers, but they're coral flowers, so they came from the ocean. Of course there's water in them, and if you boil that water steam will come out.
reading_hint: It's weird that Joe is so sensitive to the smell of steaming coral. He's lived as a farmhand nowhere near the sea all his life... at least that's what he told you. And now he's in your house breathing in the steam really deeply with his nose practically in the pot.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>storehouse</radical> radical and the <kanji>storehouse</kanji> kanji meanings are the same. If you know the radical, you know the kanji!
reading_mnemonic: You have a <kanji>storehouse</kanji>. Inside is where you keep all your <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう).
meaning_hint: Your brain is like a storehouse, you know? Hopefully that's where you kept your storehouse knowledge.
reading_hint: Be really proud of your storehouse. You've collected a lot of zombies and put them in there, where they'll keep fresh a long time!

meaning_mnemonic: If you see <radical>icicle</radical>s hanging off of a <radical>samurai</radical>'s <radical>clothes</radical>, don't worry, those are just part of his winter <kanji>attire</kanji>. Samurai have to travel and fight in all sorts of conditions, so it only makes sense that they have attire for every season.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Attire</kanji> like this is important because it reflects your <reading>soul</reading> (そう). The samurai is wearing clothing with icicles because that's how his heart feels too: frozen over because he has to fight in this horrible winter weather.
meaning_hint: Picture a samurai in his winter attire. His helmet is white and fuzzy and he has decorative icicles hanging down off of his armor.
reading_hint: Picture the kind of attire you'd wear as a samurai to reflect your soul. Mine would be bright pink and covered in horses and bananas and daisies to show that I have a happy, kind soul and that I don't want to be killed by anyone else.

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>lid</radical> on your <radical>village</radical>, so you <radical>kick</radical> it over on its <kanji>backside</kanji> to see what's on its <kanji>underside</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: It turns out your village's own <kanji>backside</kanji> is covered in <reading>ura</reading>nium (うら). Eureka! You're all gonna be rich!
meaning_hint: You've been curious about what's on the lid (and your village's) backside for years, and you finally can't take it anymore so you kick the lid as hard as you can!
reading_hint: Wait, isn't uranium bad for people? This explains why all of the villagers' backsides are covered in weird glowing, green lumps.

meaning_mnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> <radical>someone</radical> with <kanji>various</kanji> talents is important. Various talents can include speaking various languages so if you are someone who knows various languages you can say things that various peoples will comprehend.
reading_mnemonic: You’re so good at speaking <kanji>various</kanji> languages that you decide to put on a <reading>sho</reading>w (しょ). At the show, you say a lot of things in various languages and everyone is impressed.
meaning_hint: You are someone who can say things in various languages. Speak some of these various languages now. You are someone who is good at saying things. Nice job.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself at this show. The spotlight is on and all eyes are watching. You feel kind of nervous, but you speak the various languages with ease.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> who is <radical>king</radical> of every <radical>shellfish</radical> sets the <kanji>rent</kanji> for everyone. If you rent a home in King Shellfish's kingdom, the rent will be set by the great shellfish leader himself.
reading_mnemonic: If you don't have enough shellfish to pay your <kanji>rent</kanji>, King Shellfish will punch you in the <reading>chin</reading> (ちん).
meaning_hint: King Shellfish loves when people rent from him. Imagine him rolling in all of the rent money (shellfish) he collected from all the people who rent from him. Hear his shell clattering as he frolics on all the dead shells from the shellfish.
reading_hint: Feel your entire body shaking, but especially your chin, as you tell him you're two shellfish short on rent this month. The last thing you see is his big fist coming right for your chin.

meaning_mnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> accidentally firing a <radical>catapult</radical> is an unforgivable <kanji>mistake</kanji>. They say you shot the catapult at your own castle. Even though it was an accident, it’s still a mistake you will never be forgiven for.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>mistake</kanji> alerts <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご), who thinks it's town destroying time. He barrels over the hills, sees where you mistakenly catapulted your own people, and proceeds to stomp on the rest of it for you.
meaning_hint: Imagine shooting the catapult on your own people by mistake. Hear the catapult whoosh as your heart drops into your stomach. Oh, what a terrible mistake you’ve just made.
reading_hint: Picture Godzilla jumping happily on the castle and surrounding town you hit by mistake. This is probably the worst mistake you've ever made.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>moon</radical> <radical>storehouse</radical> is another storehouse you own. Remember how your regular storehouse holds zombies (from the kanji 蔵?). Your moon storehouse is on the moon, and it is filled with <kanji>internal organs</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Where did you get all these <kanji>internal organ</kanji>s from? You actually harvested them from the <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう) from your other storehouse.
meaning_hint: Your moon storehouse has internal organs because 1) you have to keep them far away from the zombies or they'll go berserk, and 2) you think it's funny to see them bounce around in low gravity. Boing, boing, boing, haha look at that internal organ.
reading_hint: Basically, you store zombies in your regular storehouse, harvest their organs, then launch them into space where they are put into a storehouse on the moon where the zombies will never be able to find them again.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>valuable</radical> radical and the <kanji>valuable</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: Most <kanji>valuable</kanji> things require a <reading>key</reading> (き) to be accessed. I mean, you've gotta keep your valuable stuff safe, right? Best to keep it under lock and key.
meaning_hint: The most valuable kanji are the ones you don't have to learn from scratch.
reading_hint: Just be careful not to lose track of the key. Otherwise, your valuable stuff is going to be safe from everyone… including you.

meaning_mnemonic: Inside the <radical>building</radical> is the <radical>Winter</radical> <radical>Cow</radical>. You need to see it, so you go inside. It is deep down in there, and you'll have to <kanji>descend</kanji> to reach it.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>descend</kanji> to the bottom and there it is, the Winter Cow. You walk up to the cow, and it swings its head back to see you. Instead of a cow's face, it's <reading>こう</reading>いち's face.
meaning_hint: Focus on descending the stairs. There are many signs that say "To Winter Cow: descend" with an arrow pointing down. "I guess I'll have to descend," you say.
reading_hint: "Hello, thank you for descending all the way down here to see me," says the Winter Cow with こういち's face. "You thought you could use the Winter Cow to stop me, but as you can see, I got here first."

meaning_mnemonic: Into a <radical>well</radical>, a <radical>drop</radical> of rice falls from your <kanji>rice bowl</kanji>. You were eating out of your rice bowl on the side of a well, and some rice fell out of your rice bowl and this drop of rice proceeded to fall all the way down.
reading_mnemonic: Just then, the <reading>don</reading> (どん) of your local mafia shows up, steals your <kanji>rice bowl</kanji>, and shoves you into the well. You’d gotten on the don’s bad side, so he came to teach you a lesson. The don steals your rice bowl and shoves you into the well.
meaning_hint: You lost a drop of rice from your rice bowl. Feel sorrow for the lost rice as you watch it fall down the well and hear it splat into the water below.
reading_hint: Hear the don shout, “See ya, sucker!” as he pushes you into the well. You’ve just lost a drop of rice, now your rice bowl, and now you’re all wet in a well. Worst day ever.

meaning_mnemonic: If someone fills your <radical>mouth</radical> with <radical>dirt</radical>, you're going to <kanji>throw up</kanji>. That's just a fact.
reading_mnemonic: When you <kanji>throw up</kanji> it sounds like you're shouting, "<reading>HA</reading> (は)!" Then all the dirt-puke comes out in a wave. It's less of a laugh ha and more of an oh no I'm throwing up ha.
meaning_hint: That's pretty much what throwing up feels like too, right? Like a bunch of dirt coming out of your mouth. Barf.
reading_hint: Imagine the sound you made the last time you threw up. It sounded like you were saying HA from the bottom of your tummy.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>woman</radical> sitting on that <radical>stool</radical> is actually a <kanji>dude</kanji>. Oh oops! You thought he was a woman because of his long hair and the way he was sitting. Maybe you shouldn't be judging people based on their looks!
reading_mnemonic: You only found out this <kanji>dude</kanji> wasn't a woman when a <reading>do</reading>g (ど) ran up and the dude stood up. He started rubbing the dog's face saying, "Who's a good boy? Who's a good boy? Not you! Me! I'm the best boy of them all. Because I am a dude, the best of all boys! HAHAHAHA!"
meaning_hint: Dude, I can't believe you called that dude a woman. What's wrong with you?!
reading_hint: Look at the poor dog. He's not a dude and now he doesn't think he's a good boy, all because of this other dude. Was he sitting on the stool this whole time, waiting to make this dog feel bad? I guess you'll never know...

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>samurai</radical> handling a <radical>jackhammer</radical>, cleaning <radical>brush</radical>es (in their 肀 form), and carrying <radical>water</radical> for someone?! All that should tell you that he's not just working, he's been made into a <kanji>slave</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You don't like seeing <kanji>slave</kanji>s. As you walk by, you hand this slave a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun so he can free himself.
meaning_hint: Traditionally, samurai don't work with jackhammers or do menial tasks like cleaning brushes and hauling water. This samurai is doing these things for someone else, meaning he's somebody's slave.
reading_hint: The samurai can use the ray gun to shoot his keeper and get away. Imagine watching him holding up the master and getting away. A ray gun is a truly powerful weapon in the hands of a slave.

meaning_mnemonic: If you see some <radical>flowers</radical> that are <radical>dry</radical>, pull them up and you'll find a <kanji>potato</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Potato</kanji>es are totally <reading>emo</reading> (いも) because of the way they dry out flowers. Emo people and potatoes both like to dry out flowers.
meaning_hint: Potatoes grow when flowers dry out. All the nutrients are going to the potato under the ground, so the flowers up top get sucked dry.
reading_hint: Picture an emo person sitting alone in a dark room, staring at a potato. "You and I are the same."

meaning_mnemonic: A magic <radical>thread</radical> fell from the <radical>roof</radical> onto the <radical>leader</radical> of a <radical>hundred</radical> people, causing him to <kanji>shrink</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: As the leader <kanji>shrink</kanji>s, the hundred people start screaming and running around the entire place. As a result, everything is <reading>shook</reading> (しゅく) up. Especially the poor, shrunken leader, who gets knocked around the most.
meaning_hint: Picture one hundred people under the same roof watching in horror as their leader suddenly shrinks to nothingness before them.
reading_hint: The leader gets shook so badly, he can't even call out to his people. They're going to trample his tiny, shrinking body!

meaning_mnemonic: If it's made of <radical>thread</radical>, your <radical>barracks</radical> will be <kanji>pure</kanji>. If you build it out of clay or bricks or wood, it will inevitably have some dirt in it. Build it out of thread to keep it pure of... well, impurities.
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, this <kanji>pure</kanji> thread barracks can only be used in <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). Any other time of year there will be snow and rain and mud that will get tracked into the pure barracks and ruin them. But June is the cleanest, most pure month, so you can use it then.
meaning_hint: Imagine a barracks made of pure thread. No brick or clay barracks can compete with yours, as long as you make it out of pure, unadulterated thread.
reading_hint: Okay, so maybe building a pure barracks that you can only use in June was a bad idea, but it's there now and I don't want you getting it dirty, so just use it when I say so, okay?

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>thread</radical> that wants to <radical>loiter</radical> from some <radical>horns</radical> and you would be <radical>correct</radical> to assume that it would hang <kanji>vertical</kanji>. The thread wants to loiter and hang from one of the horns. When things hang from things, they usually hang vertical, so that’s what this thread is going to do.
reading_mnemonic: For some reason, Channing <reading>Tat</reading>um (たて) is strangely attracted to <kanji>vertical</kanji> pieces of hanging thread. Channing Tatum starts batting at the vertical thread like a cat.
meaning_hint: Look at this vertical thread as it hangs from the horns. Take hold of it and pull on the vertical thread. Feel it in your hand.
reading_hint: Look at Channing Tatum playing with this thread. He’s like a cat playing with a piece of yarn. Look at Mr. Tatum bouncing around batting at the thread like some silly feline.

meaning_mnemonic: There's some <radical>rice</radical> in the shape of a <radical>nine</radical> on a <radical>cross</radical> and this is <kanji>stylish</kanji>. The rice grains are arranged to form the number nine. This number nine is prominently displayed on the cross, and it's stylish to carry it around and be seen with it.
reading_mnemonic: It's also <kanji>stylish</kanji> to <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい) the cross around while you wait for things. You're waiting at a bus stop so you swing the cross around violently. You swing it so violently that the rice comes flying off!
meaning_hint: Imagine hefting this cross with the rice in the shape of a nine on it. You don't feel stylish, but society tells you that you are. You're stylish.
reading_hint: See the rice fly through the air from you swinging the cross. Hear it as it clatters to the ground. This cross just isn't as stylish anymore now.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>ear</radical> and the <radical>mouth</radical> of a <radical>king</radical> are <kanji>holy</kanji>. The king has holy hearing and holy eating abilities. This king is divine.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>holy</kanji> king has heard of you and your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) that glows with pure, blue light. Specifically, the holy king wants to use it on his food!
meaning_hint: See the holy ear and mouth of this king. They glisten and glitter and people write hymnals about their holy prowess. Imagine a song playing as you witness their holy majesty.
reading_hint: The holy king heard you turned into a prosperous plate and used the saber to cut your food and now he wants to try it too. As he holds your saber, a holy circle forms around it, making it sparkle and shimmer! Your saber is holy now too!!

meaning_mnemonic: Seeing a <radical>stone</radical> and some <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> next to a piece of <radical>poop</radical> and another piece of <radical>poop</radical>, you try and pick each of them up with a <kanji>magnet</kanji>. The magnet fails to pick up either piece of poop. The magnet doesn’t pick up the horns either. But wait! The magnet picks up the stone!
reading_mnemonic: You’re pretty excited that you lifted something up with your <kanji>magnet</kanji>. Science is cool! But then you hear someone laughing behind you. It’s <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ)! Jesus was using his magic to levitate the stone all along, it wasn’t your magnet. Darn it Jesus! You’ve fooled me for the last time!
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself trying to pick each object up off the ground with your magnet. Hear the clink of the stone against metal as the magnet lifts it off the ground. Must be some iron in it or something.
reading_hint: Imagine Jesus snickering behind you as the stone falls to the ground once again. Imagine running after Jesus so you can catch him and give him a piece of your mind. He’s always playing tricks on you.

meaning_mnemonic: You use <radical>thread</radical> in your <radical>construction</radical> project to hold everything together. The color of the thread is a <kanji>deep red</kanji>, and it gives everything a <kanji>crimson</kanji> look.
reading_mnemonic: You're done, and the whole building is a <kanji>deep red</kanji> <kanji>crimson</kanji> color (it takes a lot of thread to hold a whole building in place). It's time to show the client, Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち, the finished product.
meaning_hint: Because thread isn't that strong, you have to use A LOT (to hold things like beams in place). So, the deep red or crimson thread actually stands out a lot in this construction project.
reading_hint:
こういち walks up with a big smile on his face. "I love the color crimson!" he exclaims. "It's the same deep red color as my enemies."

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading.

meaning_mnemonic: You need to take a <radical>body</radical> <radical>measurement</radical> before you can <kanji>shoot</kanji>. Whether it's for a photo shoot, you're gonna shoot them with a gun, or if you're about to shoot them full of radiation for X-rays, you need the right measurements of that body first.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>shoot</kanji> is being done by a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ). Not only is he doing the body measurements for the shoot, but he's also going to be shooting you with a shot so you don't get the flu.
meaning_hint: Imagine someone measuring your body before they shoot you. You hope it's the good kind of shoot and that you're not going to die.
reading_hint: Shamans are just a different kind of doctor. And this shot is going to save you from getting the flu, which could kill you. Make sure you always go to the local shaman and schedule a shooting.

meaning_mnemonic: In a <radical>greenhouse</radical>, a <radical>towel</radical> becomes a <kanji>curtain</kanji> as soon as you hang it. Greenhouses are made of windows, it's all glass, there are no walls. So when you put a towel in there it turns into a curtain. It has no choice but to cover a window, after all.
reading_mnemonic: You're hanging these towel <kanji>curtain</kanji>s because you need a quiet place to eat a Big <reading>Mac</reading> (まく). But you're a little ashamed. Big Macs aren't really good for you, but this stupid greenhouse has too many windows and everyone can see you stuff your face. So that's where the towel curtains come in.
meaning_hint: Imagine hanging towels over all the windows in the greenhouse. They're really fuzzy, thick curtains now.
reading_hint: Imagine hiding in a greenhouse blocked off with towel curtains, stuffing your face with Big Macs. No one can shame you in here. You can eat as many as you want. And hey, you can use the curtains as napkins too.

meaning_mnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical> and <radical>dry</radical> them. Then <radical>dry</radical> them again. This is what you do when you are in  <kanji>worship</kanji>. Once you go into your place of worship, and again before you leave. You have really sweaty fingers, and you can't worship anyone or anything if you have wet hands. So you dry them!
reading_mnemonic: Once you're dry and in wherever your <kanji>worship</kanji> is, you begin with a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい). Really all places of worship have their own version of the haiku, and heck, the 5-7-5 rule is just a guideline, so everyone is doing it pretty close to right anyway.
meaning_hint: Picture your sweaty, sweaty fingers. Dry them off before you enter a place of worship! No god wants to deal with your weird, damp hands.
reading_hint:
Imagine being in your place of worship. Close your eyes and say a haiku:

So I won't offend
Lord Crabigator-sama
I dry my hands now.

meaning_mnemonic: You want some <radical>flowers</radical> to put on the <radical>canopy</radical> above your <radical>helicopter</radical> to keep the <radical>bird</radical>s away, so you ask people what they would <kanji>recommend</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You ask for a <kanji>recommend</kanji>ation from the <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) that owns the local flower shop. Now he's walking around your helicopter canopy and staring at the birds, nodding.
meaning_hint: You've bought flowers before, but you really don't know which ones would work best to stop birds from landing on the canopy and pooping on your helicopter.
reading_hint: I hope the centaur recommends a cheap flower... but with your luck flowers that keep birds away will cost a fortune.

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>fingers</radical> on a <radical>turkey</radical> are pointing right at you, you can <kanji>infer</kanji> that it wants revenge. This turkey knows you killed his brother for dinner last night, and now it's going to make you pay. You infer that this turkey is going to take its fingers and wrap them around your neck.
reading_mnemonic: You were able to <kanji>infer</kanji> the turkey's plan, so now it's your turn. You look around and see the only way out... is to <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい) to safety! How did I infer that, dear Watson? Why, isn't it obvious! There are vines here. Turkeys can't fly. So I shall swing my way to freedom!
meaning_hint: Imagine a turkey is pointing at you with its fingers. The gears in your brain begin to slowly turn. From looking at the turkey to its fingers and back to the turkey, you can infer that something bad is probably about to happen to you.
reading_hint: Picture yourself as Sherlock Holmes, you inferred your way to freedom, swinging away on a conveniently-placed vine.

meaning_mnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical>, step onto the <radical>forehead</radical> of your <radical>car</radical>, and <kanji>brandish</kanji> them in the air. Wave them around for everyone to see.
reading_mnemonic: What are you <kanji>brandish</kanji>ing in your fingers? Your car's <reading>key</reading> (き)! You want every driver and every car to know that you aren't going to be driving ever again!
meaning_hint: Imagine standing on top of your car, brandishing your fingers in the air while all of the cars stuck behind you honk angrily.
reading_hint: You brandish the key in the air and then throw it into the woods. No more cars for you! Everyone should follow your example! Brandish your car keys in the air and throw them away!

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>tsunami</radical> coming, so you put your <radical>fins</radical> on, open your <radical>mouth</radical> wide, and try to <kanji>run alongside</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: You're able to <kanji>run alongside</kanji> the tsunami for about ten seconds before everything <reading>en</reading>ds (えん).
meaning_hint: Your mouth is open so you can scream, because this is probably a bad idea.
reading_hint: Okay, so maybe trying to run alongside a tsunami with just some fins and a mouth full of screams and terror wasn't the best idea. But at least everything ended while you did... uh... something unique?

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> was the <radical>original</radical> event to mark the <kanji>origin</kanji> of Japan. A big tsunami came and uncovered the islands of Japan. It's the origin of all life, as far as Japanese people are concerned.
reading_mnemonic: This tsunami was also the <kanji>origin</kanji> of <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん), from "The Tale of Genji." Just think, without this origin, Japan wouldn't exist and neither would the first Japanese heartthrob, Genji!
meaning_hint: Picture the original tsunami used in the origin story of Japan (never mind that we just made this up). It was huge. Bigger than anything you've ever seen. And without it you wouldn't have your precious anime and manga.
reading_hint: The origin of Genji was the origin of all Japan: a big ol' tsunami.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tiger</radical> kills a <radical>pig</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> in this new <kanji>drama</kanji> about the animal kingdom! The tiger and pig used to be friends when they were younger, but now they're bitter enemies and the tiger just stabbed the pig right in the chest with a knife.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>drama</kanji> is being put on by <reading>Gecki</reading> (げき), your childhood pet gecko. Gecki is playing all the parts, because as true drama fans know, one-gecko-shows are the highest form of art.
meaning_hint: Picture this scene from the drama as dramatically as you can. The pig stares into the tiger's eyes, "Tiger, you wouldn't–uh!" The pig's eyes slowly lower to see the knife sticking into its gut, the tiger's hand still holding it. "This is what you get, you pig," the tiger spits back, twisting.
reading_hint: Picture Gecki acting out the part of the tiger and the pig in this drama. It's so intense, you're moved to tears. Bravo Gecki, bravo!

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Boot</radical>s give you the <radical>power</radical> to <kanji>work</kanji>! That's why so many professions require you to have work boots.
reading_mnemonic: You need boots to give you the power to <kanji>work</kanji> for the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. Really, all work is for the King, since everything is for him. But even he wears boots. And they give him the power to uh... king over things.
meaning_hint: Imagine putting your boots on and suddenly being filled with the power and energy to work hard and efficiently.
reading_hint: Picture yourself in work boots. Now picture the King in work boots. Yours are worn and beaten up and his are clean and look brand new. But at least you both have them, which means you both work at least a little!

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>arrow</radical> is what this <radical>turkey</radical> <radical>lack</radical>s. For a turkey, that is a <kanji>delight</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Delight</kanji> is what Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) felt when he saw his turkey friend, sans an arrow.
meaning_hint: A turkey that lacks an arrow is a delighted turkey.
reading_hint: He loves his turkey friend, and every time he sees it he squeals with delight.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>child</radical> will ask if they want <radical>two</radical> <radical>water</radical>s because they need a grown-up's <kanji>consent</kanji>. Children aren't allowed to have whatever they want, they need to make sure it's okay and get consent first.
reading_mnemonic: "Of course my child, you have my <kanji>consent</kanji>," comes the voice of the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). The Shougun is very pleased by this child, because he expects everyone to ask for consent around him.
meaning_hint: Picture the child finishing their first water. They look at a big stack of bottled waters, but they know better than to just take two more, they need consent first. "Excuse me, sir, could I please have two more waters?" the child asks.
reading_hint: You need to make sure to get express consent from the Shougun before you do most things. Especially if it's something like taking more than you've been given.

meaning_mnemonic: Raise your <radical>fingers</radical> to another <radical>member</radical> and you'll receive a <kanji>loss</kanji> in return. You should never <kanji>hurt</kanji> a member of your team, group, or society. It's just wrong. And you'll incur a loss as punishment for your bad deeds.
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately for you, payment is in the form of the <kanji>loss</kanji> of your <reading>son</reading> (そん). The loss of any family member is horrible, but this was your son. He was your favorite child. You loved him. But that's the loss you must bear for raising your fingers to a team member.
meaning_hint: Imagine raising your fingers to slap, hurt, or point angrily at a member of your own team. How dare you! I hope you're ready for some deep, personal loss.
reading_hint: Picture your son. Your future son, maybe even your animal son, if you don't want to have real kids. Now imagine the loss of that son. Nooooo!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> <radical>branch</radical> is exactly what it sounds like: the <kanji>branch</kanji> of a tree.
reading_mnemonic: You're smelling and feeling the  <kanji>branch</kanji> when, suddenly, it starts screaming at you: "<reading>Eh, da</reading>d?" (えだ). The branch won't leave you alone. It follows you around screaming "Eh, dad?", thinking you're its long-lost tree branch father. Poor thing.
meaning_hint: Picture a tree branch in your hands. Feel it. Smell it. It smells and feels like a tree.
reading_hint: Picture the branch suddenly crying out, "Eh, Dad?" in a squeaky voice. You'd better find its real dad, otherwise this branch is never going to leave you alone.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>claw</radical> radical and the <kanji>claw</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Ravishing!
reading_mnemonic: You sink your <kanji>claw</kanji>s into anything your two <reading>目</reading> (つめ) see, because you're such a fierce predator. If your two 目 can see it, your claws will get to it. Your claws are basically an extension of your two eyes, that's how skilled you are.
meaning_hint: Lucky for you, this kanji has already etched its claw marks into your memory!
reading_hint: Imagine your claws darting out, almost automatically, as soon as your two 目 have seen your prey. Imagine catching your dinner that way.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>beans</radical> radical and the <kanji>beans</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
reading_mnemonic: These <kanji>beans</kanji> are from <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Everybody knows とうきょう has the best beans. What?! You didn’t know that? Tokyo’s beans are the best beans!
meaning_hint: *toots*
reading_hint: Imagine eating these beans from とうきょう. They’re the best beans you’ve ever had. Even if you don’t like beans, you love とうきょう beans. Mmm… beans, beans, とうきょう beans.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>slice</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> is called a <kanji>carve</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You work up a sweat trying to <kanji>carve</kanji> meat and wood all day, so you grab yourself a nice <reading>Coke</reading> (こく). You like Coke, and it goes well with meat and sawdust.
meaning_hint: You carve meat, you carve wood, all with a slicing motion with a knife.
reading_hint: Imagine washing down all your hard carving work with an ice cold Coke.

meaning_mnemonic: Set up your <radical>canopy</radical>, <radical>attach</radical> some <radical>meat</radical> to it, and the meat will eventually <kanji>rot</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You let meat <kanji>rot</kanji>. You're a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). That's really all I can say at this point.
meaning_hint: Imagine the smell of rotten meat hanging from your canopy. Now no one wants to come near here.
reading_hint: What, did you think attaching this meat to the canopy would make it turn into jerky or something? Fool...

meaning_mnemonic: Next to the <radical>scooter</radical>, there's a <radical>flag</radical> warning you that there are <radical>sheep</radical> around here, so you should go <kanji>slow</kanji>. That means you might be <kanji>late</kanji>, but at least the sheep will be okay!
reading_mnemonic: You follow the flag, but the sheep refuse to <kanji>slow</kanji> down so you have to get creative. You place <reading>chee</reading>se (ち) every ten feet or so, that way the sheep slow down to bend down on their scooters and take a bite.
meaning_hint: It's also a warning to the sheep that if they want to ride scooters they should also slow down, because as we all know, sheep are speed demons.
reading_hint: Picture all of the sheep finally going slowly because there is yummy cheese all over the road.

meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>circumference</radical> around someone's <radical>hair</radical>, as in, you can see a circle drawn around the top of their head. As you examine it closer, and pull at it a bit, you realize that someone has <kanji>carve</kanji>d the hair off someone's head!
reading_mnemonic: When you think of all the people who could have done this... all the people who could have <kanji>carve</kanji>d out someone's hair, only one person comes to mind, and that is Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう). She's the only person evil enough to do something like this.
meaning_hint: Their hair has literally been carved out of their head, and you discovered it. Of course, the nastiness, the smell, and the feel should be applied to your imagination to make the mnemonic stronger.
reading_hint: You can also imagine Mrs. Chou doing the act of carving someone's hair out, but that may just be too much.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> will pick up a bunch of <radical>shellfish</radical> and dump them on your shore, so you take a <radical>knife</radical> and <kanji>measure</kanji> them.
reading_mnemonic: All of the shellfish you <kanji>measure</kanji> that are too small go into your <reading>sock</reading> (そく). Once the sock is full, you swing it around your head and lob it back into the ocean.
meaning_hint: You can't eat shellfish that are too small, so you place your knife along them to measure them. If they're big enough, you stick the knife in and eat them raw.
reading_hint: If you measure one of these shellfish and it's too small, stuff it right into your sock. Then once the sock measures about 12 shellfish long, toss it back to sea!

meaning_mnemonic: If you scrape a sharp <radical>stone</radical> against your <radical>skin</radical>, it will <kanji>tear</kanji>. The sharp stone tears your skin. It hurts!
reading_mnemonic: The moment your skin <kanji>tear</kanji>s you can't help but yelp "<reading>Ha</reading> (は)!" It sounds like a laugh, but it's more like a pained bark.
meaning_hint: Imagine this sharp stone. Feel its weight in your hand. Now drag the sharp stone against your skin. Feel your skin as it begins to tear. The pain is agonizing.
reading_hint: You make this noise when you tear other things with the sharp stone too, but now it's more of a HA! TAKE THAT! Feel the same pain I feel!

meaning_mnemonic: The kanji and the radical that look like this are exactly the same. They are both <kanji>cottage</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You got this <kanji>cottage</kanji> from a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ). In fact, everyone you know got their cottage from a shaman. One day you're just walking in the woods, and the next thing you know, a shaman is giving you the keys to their old cottage.
meaning_hint: As long as you know the radical cottage, you know the kanji cottage.
reading_hint: I guess shamans are moving out a lot around here. Why don't you go look for a cottage you can buy from a shaman! I bet you'll get a pretty good, totally not cursed, deal!

meaning_mnemonic: Try to <radical>say</radical> something to a <radical>lifeguard</radical> and you'll just end up getting a <kanji>lecture</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>lecture</kanji> loving lifeguard is none other than <reading>こう</reading>いち! I mean, look how long winded the stuff on this site is? Can you imagine him trying to give you a talk about proper swimming etiquette?
meaning_hint: A lifeguard's favorite part of the job is telling people what to do, where to swim, when to get out of the water, and they love saying it in a bunch of words.
reading_hint:
Close your eyes and picture こういち lecturing you. Ugh, it's the worst!

Hint: when you see the "lifeguard" radical, the reading is probably going to be こう.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical>, once stopped by a <radical>belt</radical>, will begin to <kanji>stagnate</kanji>. This belt is a belt of water resistance, so the belt stops the tsunami. The belt has caused the tsunami to stagnate.
reading_mnemonic: While you watch the tsunami <kanji>stagnate</kanji>, you get ready for work. While you were watching this whole thing happening, you were tying your <reading>tie</reading> (たい). Once the tsunami stagnated, you were all done tying your tie. And since the tsunami is no longer a threat, you’re ready to go to work.
meaning_hint: Watch this belt as it stagnated the tsunami. See the belt glow as its enchantment activates, immediately stagnating the tsunami water.
reading_hint: Look in the mirror at you in your tie. Put the finishing touches on your outfit and imagine going out into the world with your tie on, no longer afraid of any tsunamis. They’re all stagnant now thanks to that magical belt.

meaning_mnemonic: You create a <radical>thread</radical> with someone by <radical>call</radical>ing out to them to <kanji>introduce</kanji> yourself.
reading_mnemonic: You want to <kanji>introduce</kanji> yourself to the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) but no matter how much you call out, he won't come close enough for you to tie your introduction thread to him. You call louder and louder, "SHOUGUN LET ME INTRODUCE MYSELF!"
meaning_hint: You literally call out, and when they turn around, tie a thread to them, so you'll never forget that you introduced yourselves to each other.
reading_hint: Finally the Shougun sighs and turns toward you. You call out your name and get the thread around him. Uh oh. He doesn't look happy.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>jammed in</radical> radical and the <kanji>jammed in</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: Look down! You're <kanji>jammed in</kanji>to something. You can't move your feet. What is this?! Oh, your body's been jammed in a <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい).
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Picture yourself jammed into a kayak. Your pants are bunching and sticking out the sides and top. You really don't fit in here.

meaning_mnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>oneself</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>oneself</kanji> as well.
