You keep digging until you realize that these are a part of some big <kanji>ruins</kanji>. They're the ruins of tiger and cactus statues. It looks like whoever used to live here worshiped tigers and cacti. Or maybe they ate them. But now their whole civilization is just ruins.
reading_mnemonic: Well, as it turns out, these are the <kanji>ruins</kanji> of Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ), the city that existed before the modern day one. And it looks like the citizens of Little Kyoto faced their end a long time ago.
meaning_hint: Imagine looking through these ruins. Picture the broken tiger and cactus statues. Try to imagine the people who used to live here before it turned to ruins.
reading_hint: Picture the ruins of Little Kyoto. Try to imagine what it was like before the whole place fell apart. Maybe they would have lived on if they hadn't been doing things with sharp cacti and bitey tigers.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>boat</radical> that <radical>oversee</radical>s other boats, land, and people is usually a <kanji>warship</kanji>. They're the big boats that oversee everything around them. And if anyone steps out of line, a warship can take care of it.
reading_mnemonic: This huge <kanji>warship</kanji> is run by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん), the famous warlord. Really, what did you expect? Who else is strong enough to captain a warship but him?
meaning_hint: Picture a huge warship overseeing everything around it. It's big and kinda scary.
reading_hint: Picture Genghis Khan aboard a monstrous warship. He's carefully watching the sea and land that he's conquered.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> carries two <radical>husband</radical>s up into the <radical>sun</radical>, where they're going to <kanji>conceal</kanji> something.
reading_mnemonic: The husbands are going to <kanji>conceal</kanji> their pet <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) in the sun. The local government isn't too pleased that they have a pet centaur, so they have to conceal it in the sun so it doesn't get confiscated.
meaning_hint: Imagine witnessing this tsunami taking the two husbands up into the sun. Hear the water rushing. That tsunami is massive. What are they going to conceal in the sun?
reading_hint: Just make sure to remember the husbands are riding that tsunami alongside their centaur, heading towards the sun. They need to conceal the centaur to keep it safe.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>gladiator</radical> turns his <radical>hand</radical> into a <kanji>fist</kanji>. A fist for punching other gladiators.
reading_mnemonic: The gladiator's <kanji>fist</kanji> is now aimed at <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai. Little does the gladiator know, Ken knows the ways of the samurai and the gladiator. He slowly raises his own hands and turns them into fists.
meaning_hint: Picture the gladiator squeezing his hand into a tight fist, raising it up and yelling.
reading_hint: Ken and the gladiator clash! Fists fly! You've never seen hand-to-hand combat like this! You can hardly keep up!!

meaning_mnemonic: You climb up a <radical>mountain</radical> and a <radical>cliff</radical> in search of <radical>fire</radical>, but all you find is <kanji>charcoal</kanji>. You can make fire with the charcoal, but you were hoping to find fire itself. All that climbing for nothing.
reading_mnemonic: After crushing the <kanji>charcoal</kanji>, you decide to take advantage of your current location and lie out in the sun for a while to get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). Instead of getting a tan though, you catch on fire. Oops.
meaning_hint: You’re angry that you could only find charcoal so you take the charcoal in your hands and you crush it atop the cliff on the mountain. Feel the charcoal breaking in your hands as the wind carries the charcoal dust off from the top of the cliff on the mountain.
reading_hint: Imagine lying out in the sun next to the crushed charcoal, thinking you’re going to get tan. You’re very surprised when you actually catch on fire. Hot hot hot!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>fire</radical> in the <radical>rice paddy</radical> burns down the <kanji>field</kanji>. The rice paddy fire spreads out from the paddy and consumes the field next to it. This rice paddy fire has ravaged your entire farm!
reading_mnemonic: Your rice paddy farm and <kanji>field</kanji> are on a cay. A cay is a small, sandy island, and since your farm is on fire, this cay has turned into a hot cay. Or as Mario would say, that’s one-a <reading>hot-a cay</reading>! (はたけ) Mama mia!
meaning_hint: Look at this fire burning the rice paddy and the surrounding field. Smell burnt rice in the air as you see the flames dance before your eyes.
reading_hint:
Imagine running around this hot cay as you try to put out the fire in your field. The fire is ravaging the entire hot cay, so it takes you a while to put it out. Man those flames are hot.

Also, this kanji can have the reading はた for the kun'yomi as well. If you learn the longer version (はたけ) it will be easier to just shave off a kana to remember the shorter version.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>wrap</radical> radical and the <kanji>wrap</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: Think about being <kanji>wrap</kanji>ped up, nice and warm and safe in your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Home is where you wrap yourself in a blanket, in the warmth of the fireplace, in the arms of those you love.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Home is where the wrapped heart is.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>clothes</radical> radical and the <kanji>clothes</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Surprising!
reading_mnemonic: An <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) sees a pile of your <kanji>clothes</kanji> and lays an <reading>e</reading>gg (え) in them. The eagle’s egg needs a soft warm place to incubate, and your pile of clothes is the perfect place.
reading_hint: Look at the eagle and her egg sitting atop your clothes. Every time you get close the eagle starts to screech at you fiercely. Screee! Screee!

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>leader</radical> has <radical>two</radical> things that remind him of his <kanji>humanity</kanji>: his shoes, and his hat. These two things remind the leader of his humanity because humans are the only ones who wear shoes and hats.
reading_mnemonic: Another thing that reminds you and the leader about your <kanji>humanity</kanji> is wearing <reading>jean</reading>s (じん). Jeans are the go-to legwear for many people, and no one but humans wear them. Jeans are another great symbol of humanity.
meaning_hint: Reflect upon your own humanity. What does it mean to be human? Is it wearing shoes and hats? Probably.
reading_hint: Are you wearing jeans right now? If so, rub them with your hands and say aloud “Oh, the humanity!” Or just imagine wearing jeans if you aren’t actually wearing them.

meaning_mnemonic: Aside from <radical>gold</radical> there are a <radical>wide</radical> variety of other <kanji>mineral</kanji>s as well.
reading_mnemonic: One person is trying to control all the <kanji>minerals</kanji> of the world. That person is Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち.
meaning_hint: Think of the wide range of minerals there are that aren't gold. There are a lot.
reading_hint: Emperor こういち is getting all the minerals, and the 鋼, so he can take over all neighboring nations and force them into a subjugation of efficient kanji learning.

meaning_mnemonic: Dig up a <radical>mole</radical> and you <kanji>attain</kanji> a mole status. You're always trying to attain a new level for yourself and now you get to be on the mole level!
reading_mnemonic: Mole isn't enough, you want to <kanji>attain</kanji> <reading>shee</reading>p (し) status next. Everyone knows it goes human > mole> sheep. Now you need to stand in the field with the mole and get a sheep to come over.
meaning_hint: It's actually pretty easy to attain mole status. Just stand in a field. Wait for the ground to move under you and shove your hand in the earth. Blam! An achievement pops up above your head and you're there.
reading_hint: If you can attain two animal levels in one day you'll be a legend on the leaderboards.

meaning_mnemonic: Something you <radical>say</radical> to someone you <radical>become</radical> attached to should be said with <kanji>sincerity</kanji>. You need to speak with sincerity when you say things to someone who will eventually become your significant other, for example.
reading_mnemonic: When you say something with complete, unabashed <kanji>sincerity</kanji> your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) starts to pulse with the beating of your heart. It shows the person you want to become close to that you truly are sincere!
meaning_hint: Imagine saying something with sincerity to someone you want to become important to you. Say something like, “I love you” out loud. Say it with the utmost sincerity.
reading_hint: Say, "I love you," again and watch your saber beating in time with your heart. If you weren't sincere it would fade and grow dull.

meaning_mnemonic: Everyone has a <radical>poop</radical> <radical>guy</radical> from their <kanji>hometown</kanji>. You know. That poop guy. And every single time you try to visit your hometown, poop guy is there making everyone uncomfortable and reminding you why you left in the first place.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>hometown</kanji> is in <reading>きょう</reading>と which, unfortunately, means your poop guy is in きょうと too. Now every time you go back home he's there stinking everything up.
meaning_hint: Picture the poop guy from your hometown. Picture him really vividly. The awkward, rude things he does. The way he smells. The way he makes you never want to come back.
reading_hint: Imagine all the wonderful things that exist in your hometown of きょうと. All the things you love to do and see. And now picture poop guy. Ruining it all with his poop hands.

meaning_mnemonic: There is a tiny <radical>leader</radical> on <radical>Wolverine</radical>'s <radical>forehead</radical> sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> and commanding his troops to <kanji>invade</kanji> Wolverine's brain. These tiny soldiers are going to invade Wolverine's body in an attempt to destroy him. The leader himself isn't going to invade Wolverine's brain, he's just going to sit on the stool and supervise.
reading_mnemonic: Soon they realize that they cannot <kanji>invade</kanji> Wolverine through his head so they travel down to his leg and try to enter through his <reading>shin</reading> (しん). The little soldiers take a big knife and slice open Wolverine’s shin in order to invade his body.
meaning_hint: Imagine the little leader sitting on Wolverine’s forehead as he shouts “Invade! Invade invade! Invade his brain and destroy the Wolverine!”
reading_hint: See the little soldiers slicing open Wolverine’s shin as Wolverine grunts in pain. Getting your leg cut open is not a comfortable thing, even for someone as manly as Wolverine.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> gets a nice uniform for the <radical>sake</radical> of not wanting to look like a <kanji>fake</kanji>. The leader is a fake, he just doesn’t want to look like one. He’s not really a leader, but he thinks he should get an official-looking uniform for the sake of not looking like a fake.
reading_mnemonic: Well, everyone found out he was a <kanji>fake</kanji> anyway, and now you're all going to use a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ) to cut off his fake head.
meaning_hint: Look at this fake leader. See him scramble into a uniform. Does he look like a real leader? Or can you tell that he is a fake?
reading_hint: Watch as the guillotine comes down. You close your eyes for a second, and when you open them you see that the leader's body was a fake! Wait, where did he go?!

meaning_mnemonic: Even though you’ve <radical>old</radical> <radical>legs</radical>, you <kanji>overcome</kanji> your disability. These old legs of yours don’t work very well, but you overcome the odds and you run a marathon!
reading_mnemonic: When you pass the finish line and finally <kanji>overcome</kanji> the limits of your old legs, someone hands you an ice cold <reading>Coke</reading> (こく) as a reward. Mmm, such a refreshing Coke.
meaning_hint: Imagine running a marathon with your old legs to prove you can overcome anything. Feel your old legs cramping up as you try to overcome the marathon and finish the race.
reading_hint: Filled with the jubilation of achievement from overcoming all odds, imagine the flavor of this cold Coke in your mouth. Fizzy and Coke flavored, it runs down your throat and into your tummy. Yum.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>mole</radical> has a <radical>knife</radical> ready for your <kanji>arrival</kanji>. You get out of your airplane, you step on the ground and... ouch! You stepped right on the knife, and there's the mole, sticking it out of the ground. "I checked the schedule for your arrival!" it squeaks happily, and disappears.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>arrival</kanji> to <reading>とう</reading>きょう is now ruined. And your next stop is now going to be an arrival at a とうきょう hospital to get this mole knife out.
meaning_hint: Welp, your arrival didn't go smoothly. You have a knife in your foot. I'm not sure what you did to make that mole hate you, but you'll have to be more careful next time.
reading_hint: This certainly wasn't the type of arrival you were expecting when you bought your tickets to とうきょう.

meaning_mnemonic: If you have one <radical>stool</radical> and another <radical>stool</radical>, you have a <kanji>pair</kanji> of stools. Look at this pair of stools. Look at it.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>pair</kanji> of stools shares a <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Even if you tried to separate them, they'd eventually find their way back together.
meaning_hint: Imagine being in front of this pair of stools. Picture yourself sitting on one stool, getting up, then sitting on the other. Repeat this process.
reading_hint: It's the same with pairs of socks and pairs of people. Sometimes what looks like two things is really a perfect pair, with one soul. Meant to be together forever.

meaning_mnemonic: Wrap your <radical>fingers</radical> around this <radical>axe</radical> and put it in your <radical>mouth</radical> to express your <kanji>philosophy</kanji>. Your philosophy of… putting axes in your mouth. Everyone else seems to think it's a stupid philosophy, but by doing this you're showing them that it's not!
reading_mnemonic: Your axe-in-mouth <kanji>philosophy</kanji> means you're always tasting <reading>鉄</reading> (てつ). They say blood tastes like copper. Well, weapons (and your philosophy) taste like iron.
meaning_hint: Imagine expressing your philosophy, your deepest thoughts and beliefs, by putting an axe in your mouth. Is it some kind of metaphor for how powerful and sharp your philosophical thinking is?
reading_hint: Your philosophy and your way of life means everything tastes like 鉄 now, and you think everyone should be that way.

meaning_mnemonic: Under the <radical>cross</radical> is a <radical>mouth</radical> and another <radical>mouth</radical> wearing special <radical>clothes</radical> because they're in <kanji>mourning</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: As the <kanji>mourning</kanji> continues, the <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) of the dead person rises out of the cross. The soul looks around at all of the mourners and nods. Yes, your mourning has pleased it.
meaning_hint: Picture the cross looming overhead, people's mouths open so they can sob loudly, and everyone wearing the same color clothes (just picture whatever color works for your particular culture/religion combo).
reading_hint: If you aren't mourning enough, when the soul comes out of the person's body, it will get really mad. "What, I'm not worth a few tears? Really?" And then the soul will haunt you. So you better mourn like you mean it.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>servant</radical> secures a <radical>stool</radical> in the <radical>dirt</radical> so that it is a <kanji>solid</kanji> sitting device. If you just throw a stool on the ground it will flop over easily. But if you have a servant to dig it into the dirt a bit, it becomes nice and solid and safe for sitting!
reading_mnemonic: You need this stool to be extra <kanji>solid</kanji> because you're using it to test your new <reading>cata</reading>pult (かた). If it isn't solid, when you launch it and yourself in the catapult, the stool might fall over, killing you both. And you don't want that, now do you?
meaning_hint: Picture two stools in your mind. One is solid, you want to sit on it. The other looks like the wind will knock it over. Don't sit on that!
reading_hint: You better hope that catapult is solid too. If it isn't the whole thing will come apart when you try to launch yourself into the air.

meaning_mnemonic: Under the <radical>canopy</radical>, you use a <radical>tree</radical> for your <kanji>floor</kanji>. It also becomes your <kanji>bed</kanji>, because hey, what is a floor but the original bed?
reading_mnemonic: It's great having a <kanji>floor</kanji> or <kanji>bed</kanji>, but whenever you use it you say <reading>yucka</reading> (ゆか) because it just gets so dirty. That's the one flaw of using a floor as a bed. In fact, it's the dirtiest floor you've ever seen, because it's covered in <reading>yucca</reading> (ゆか) leaves. You're sleeping on a yucca floor. Ew!
meaning_hint: You lay it down, cut it into slats, and voila! A floor! And a bed!
reading_hint: You can imagine how comfortable it is to sleep on a floor of yucca leaves, but at the same time how horrible the smell must be when they start to rot.

meaning_mnemonic: Someone's <radical>fingers</radical> touch your <radical>tongue</radical> and <kanji>fasten</kanji> it together. You can't talk. All you can do is make sad tongue-less sounds as you try to figure out who would fasten your tongue like this.
reading_mnemonic: Only one creature could use its fingers to <kanji>fasten</kanji> your tongue like this: <reading>cats</reading> (かつ). Cats are notorious for fastening tongues so that people can't speak. Haven't you ever heard the phrase, "Cat got your tongue?"
meaning_hint: Try to fasten your own tongue with your fingers right now. You probably can't do it, right? That's because your fingers are too big and your tongue is too slippery.
reading_hint: In this case, many cats fastened your tongue. You should probably be nicer to cats from now on so this doesn't happen again.

meaning_mnemonic: Using a <radical>bow</radical>, shoot a <radical>melon</radical> and it will fly in a steep <kanji>arc</kanji>. Melons are too heavy to fly straight, after all.
reading_mnemonic: Shooting melons in an <kanji>arc</kanji> is something little <reading>子</reading>'s (こ) love to do. It also helps that when they come down from the arc they smash all over the place.
meaning_hint: Imagine shooting a really heavy melon out of a bow. Watch it make an arc through the air. Trace the arc with your finger.
reading_hint: Imagine being a 子 and watching melons fly in arcs all over. Imagine a little 子 version of yourself giggling and clapping your hands with absolute glee.

meaning_mnemonic: When your <radical>fingers</radical> move like that, you know it's an <radical>omen</radical> of a <kanji>challenge</kanji> that will face you soon in the future.
reading_mnemonic: In your finger, you see a <kanji>challenge</kanji>. And... if you look closely, you can see the fast movements of your fingers forming the outline of Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading>'s (ちょう) face. Oh no, the challenge is going to be Mrs. Chou!
meaning_hint: What kind of challenge is it? If you look closely at your fingers sometimes you can figure out the challenge that is to come based on their movements.
reading_hint: Whenever you see an omen (radical), there's a good chance that Mrs. Chou will be a part of it. This is not a good sign.

meaning_mnemonic: You see <radical>fingers</radical> on a <radical>flag</radical> by the <radical>exit</radical>, indicating that you should <kanji>dig</kanji>. The fingers on the flag point downward, just by the exit, as if to say "dig here!" There must be something under here. That's what you think to yourself as you start to dig with your fingers.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>dig</kanji> until you finally hit something. It's a bunch of buried old <reading>coots</reading> (くつ). They're in an underground room for old coots. One yells up at you, "Whatcha doing to our roof, ya whipper snapper?! Stop digging in our lawn!"
meaning_hint: Imagine digging under the flag by the exit of your building with your bare fingers. The dirt is getting under your fingernails as you dig. It's getting on your knees. It's everywhere.
reading_hint: You carefully un-dig the old coots and leave them to their old grumpy coot hole... basement... secret clubhouse? Whatever.

meaning_mnemonic: If you have <radical>fingers</radical> it's <radical>easy</radical> to <kanji>hoist</kanji> things above your head. If you don't have fingers, well, it's not so easy.
reading_mnemonic: Today, you have to <kanji>hoist</kanji> so much stuff. "<reading>Ah</reading> (あ)!" you say, "So much hoisting to do." Better get to it!
meaning_hint: Hoist the sails! Hoist the anchor! Hoist a baby! All of these situations are easier if you have a ton of working fingers. (The more the better.)
reading_hint: Imagine hoisting things over and over again. Each time you spot a new thing that needs hoisting, you say "Ah! Something else to hoist!"

meaning_mnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> in a <radical>shop</radical> to <kanji>grip</kanji> what you want to buy.
reading_mnemonic: You have a really strong <kanji>grip</kanji> thanks to <reading>acu</reading>puncture (あく)! All your fingers are primed for buying things. You can grip anything at a shop—pineapples, tires, even live turtles. All thanks to some well-placed needles.
meaning_hint: Imagine picking up a few different things in a shop. If something fits nicely in your grip: buy it. If it's too small or too big, put it back.
reading_hint: Acupuncture is great for strengthening your grip strength. If you do intense shopping (like I do) you should definitely consider it!

meaning_mnemonic: With your <radical>fingers</radical>, you grab a <radical>satellite</radical> and <kanji>shake</kanji> it! You're an astronaut and it's your job. Don't worry, the satellite won't break or anything, it's strong enough for space travel. Just do as you're told and shake the sucker.
reading_mnemonic: You need to <kanji>shake</kanji> it because this satellite is full of <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). You only like yogurt that's been well shaken. And who can resist space yogurt? Huh? Anyone?
meaning_hint: Imagine being in space and shaking a satellite with your fingers.
reading_hint: Imagine shaking yogurt in space. Now imagine eating yogurt as it continues to shake (in space still)!

meaning_mnemonic: Use <radical>simultaneous</radical> <radical>jackhammer</radical>s to perform the <kanji>purification</kanji> ritual. Two jackhammers running perfectly in sync will purify your body, mind, and soul! (And the ground around you that's being jackhammered.)
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>purification</kanji> ritual is supposed to help you forget your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). It's been a while, but you can't seem to get it out of your head.
meaning_hint: Close your eyes and feel the purification begin. Try not to let the loud jackhammering sound bother you, just focus on the simultaneous vibrations cleansing you inside and out.
reading_hint: Hopefully this purification (or just the two jackhammers shaking your brain up like pudding) will get the memories of your cyborg to go away. You can't keep picturing it all the time like this.

meaning_mnemonic: To your <radical>car</radical> you take an <radical>axe</radical>. The <radical>sun</radical> has broken your car for good this time (you think). You're so angry at your car. You think this is permanent. Turns out, though, that it's not permanent. It's just like that <kanji>temporarily</kanji>. You did make it permanent by taking an axe to your car, though.
reading_mnemonic: You were driving around in <reading>Zan</reading>zibar (ざん) when this happened, too, so now you're stuck in Zanzibar, a foreign place, and you have no idea what to do next.
meaning_hint: Feel sad that you were the one to destroy your car for good, even though the sun messed it up temporarily.
reading_hint: You should probably be worried about this situation.

meaning_mnemonic: You hit a <radical>tree</radical> with an <radical>axe</radical> so you can do some <kanji>analysis</kanji> on it. To do the analysis on the tree, a cross section needs to be obtained, so you must chop it in half with your axe.
reading_mnemonic: The person who is doing this <kanji>analysis</kanji> is very <reading>sexy</reading> (せき). Who is this sexy person? Why it’s you of course, you sexy beast. I love watching you do analysis on trees. It’s so sexy.
meaning_hint: Imagine taking this axe in your hands and chopping away at this tree to do your analysis. Hear the chop chop chop as wood chips fly into your face.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself being sexy and doing tree analysis. What sort of sexy analysis moves can you think of? Oh yeah, so sexy.

meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>tree</radical> <radical>district</radical> of town you can buy a lot of tree products, including doors. With those doors, though, you always need <kanji>hinge</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: You buy an awesome <kanji>hinge</kanji> and try it out. Instead of working properly, it just falls apart and the door falls on you, crushing your lower body. You yell out (and you have to yell) "I'm GOING TO <reading>SUUUUEEE</reading> (すう) YOU!"
meaning_hint: Don't forget to buy a hinge. Your door purchase will <kanji>hinge</kanji> on your hinges.
reading_hint: Really lengthen out that "sue" because it's a long "suu" for the reading and that will help.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>car</radical> works for one <radical>reason</radical>: its wheels can spin on an <kanji>axis</kanji>. Yep, that lateral axis is definitely a car's most <kanji>pivotal part</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Your friend is always bragging about his car's <kanji>axis</kanji>. He's all like, "My car's wheels move on more than one axis!" And you sarcastically reply: "<reading>Gee, coo</reading>l" (じく).
meaning_hint: Imagine the frustration of trying to drive a car without any axis (or axles) for its wheels to spin on. That thing isn't going anywhere. It's missing its pivotal part!
reading_hint: The pivotal part your friend leaves out is that those multi-axis wheels are why his car is always crashing. Gee, cool axis control, man.

meaning_mnemonic: You walk up to a <radical>tree</radical> and put <radical>dynamite</radical> on it. Then you set it off. BOOM. It explodes, but doesn't do what you'd expect. Instead of blowing the tree up, it blasts a very beautiful <kanji>pattern</kanji> into the side of it as well as the ground around it.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>pattern</kanji> is so beautiful that you want to put it on your <reading>gara</reading>ge (がら). So, you do.
meaning_hint: Think about what pattern it might be. Is it a checkered pattern? Something more complicated and beautiful? Whatever it is, think of something that really impresses you.
reading_hint: Imagine painting your garage with the pattern you imagined earlier.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> that is <radical>white</radical> is the most scary tsunami of all. It doesn't come in and destroy everything. No, it comes in, goes inside your door, then stays at your place <kanji>overnight</kanji>, without even asking!
reading_mnemonic: Your cat walks up to the white tsunami that's staying <kanji>overnight</kanji> and decides it doesn't like it, so it <reading>hack</reading>s (はく) up a hairball on it. Even that doesn't get the tsunami to leave.
meaning_hint: Imagine the white tsunami knocking on your door, walking into your house, and then just laying down on your bed. You try to tell it to leave, but it's a tsunami, and it says it will be staying overnight thank you very much.
reading_hint: See the hairball slowly sinking into the tsunami. The cat hacks up another one.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> breaks your <radical>bone</radical>s, not from hitting you, but from making you <kanji>slide</kanji> and <kanji>slip</kanji> into stuff. Hard to keep your footing in a tsunami, you know? You're slipping and sliding all over the place.
reading_mnemonic: As you continue to <kanji>slide</kanji> around you see a bunch of stray <reading>cats</reading> (かつ) slipping and sliding too. They're yowling and sliding right toward you. Uh oh…
meaning_hint: Imagine being knocked off your feet by a tsunami, then sliding into a wall or something and breaking a bone or two. You try to get up, but you slip again, breaking a few more bones in the process.
reading_hint: Picture these stray cats trying to right themselves on the wet ground and slipping again each time. They're doing that thing cats do when they touch water, twisting their bodies around and trying to cling to you. Great, now you've got a bunch of slippery cats perched atop your broken body.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> crashes into the <radical>sun</radical> (breaking it in half) and creating a different <radical>wing</radical> of the tsunami all together. This wing crashes down and creates a <kanji>lagoon</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>lagoon</kanji> was created on purpose by a really angry dude and his <reading>cata</reading>pult (かた). He wanted a nice place to lounge that's tropical, but not too bright. So he came up with a plan: use a giant catapult to send a tsunami into the sun!
meaning_hint: The lagoon is beautiful! The water is hot (from hitting the sun) but it isn't so bright that you'll get a sunburn.
reading_hint: Now picture it all in slow motion. The catapult tosses the tsunami into the air, it smashes the sun, breaks off into a wing of nice, hot water, and finally splashes back to the earth into a beautiful, secluded lagoon.

meaning_mnemonic: You take a <radical>turkey</radical> and try to <radical>boil</radical> it but you <kanji>char</kanji> it instead. That's what happens when you throw meat into a pot on high with no water in there.
reading_mnemonic: You're staring sadly at the <kanji>char</kanji>red turkey when you suddenly sense the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) looming behind you. You were supposed to be cooking this turkey for him and now he looks really, really mad.
meaning_hint: You should know better than to try to boil something without water! Now what are you going to do with this raw, but charred turkey?!
reading_hint: The Shougun reaches down and bites a charred hunk off the turkey. He yells and spits it out. Why did he do that?! You have no idea but it looked nasty.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>bamboo</radical> can be whittled into an <kanji>example</kanji> of anything. See, here's a <radical>car</radical>. Here's a <radical>snake</radical>. I can make so many examples just with bamboo.
reading_mnemonic: One last <kanji>example</kanji> for you. Now I'm going to take this bamboo and whittle it into an example of <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo!
meaning_hint: It's amazing how fast this guy can whittle!
reading_hint: Han Solo's bamboo example comes to life and runs off.

meaning_mnemonic: Take this <radical>thread</radical> and look at this <radical>part</radical>. Now look at this part! Now look at this part. You don't know where to look! Haha, I have <kanji>distract</kanji>ed you!
reading_mnemonic: Despite how distracted you are, that was indeed a lot of <reading>fun</reading> (ふん).
meaning_hint: You don't know what part of the thread to look at. What a distraction.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself really enjoying looking at parts of the thread. You don't know why it's so fun, but being derpy is exciting, I guess.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Thread</radical> and <radical>vines</radical> alone are too weak to hold anything up, but if you <kanji>twist together</kanji> both of them, you can make something much stronger.
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>twist together</kanji> the thread and vines, you suddenly feel a lump. You look down and see a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) twisted into it.
meaning_hint: As you twist together endless strands of thread and vines, feel how tough and sturdy your makeshift rope is beginning to get. You could swing through the jungle like Tarzan on this thing.
reading_hint: It turns out those vines you've been twisting together are cucumber vines, so if you need a break to rest your hands, you can chow down on a healthy and refreshing cucumber while you recover.

meaning_mnemonic: If you take a <radical>thread</radical> and wrap up a bunch of it, so much it becomes a <radical>hill</radical> of thread, you're going to have a lot of thread. You can wrap some of that thread around other thread to create a much thicker thread. This thread is a <kanji>rope</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Congratulations, now you have a perfect rope for <reading>tuna</reading> (つな) fishing. Tunas are massive, so you need a strong rope to reel one in.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself twisting this hill of thread up to make thicker ropes. They're strong!
reading_hint: Imagine yourself going tuna fishing on a small boat with nothing but one long rope. This is how tuna fishing is done where you're from, so you're sticking to tradition.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>thread</radical>-covered <radical>hill</radical>, but it's all lumpy and weird. You approach it to take a closer look, and you see it's actually a huge <kanji>net</kanji> with a bunch of stuff caught in it: fish, cats, tree branches. All this random stuff is what's making the net look so lumpy!
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>net</kanji> is creating a serious obstacle to you <reading>mow</reading>ing (もう) your lawn. The net has way too much stuff in it for you to be able to drag it away, so for now you're just going to have to mow around it.
meaning_hint: You'll need to differentiate this from 綱, which uses the same radicals (though 綱 has a real hill radical). Just remember that the "hill" in this one is all weird and lumpy because it's a net with a bunch of stuff caught in it.
reading_hint: Imagine the awkwardness of trying to mow the grass around this big, lumpy, writhing net full of loudly meowing cats, flopping fish, and goodness knows what else.

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>moon</radical> is <radical>dry</radical>, it's good for your <kanji>liver</kanji>! Haha, get it? Because there's no moonshine to ruin your liver. Don't drink, kids.
reading_mnemonic: As you pat your <kanji>liver</kanji> suddenly a strange feeling bubbles up and the ghost of Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) comes out! Right out of your liver.
meaning_hint: Imagine holding an empty bottle up to the moon and seeing it's completely dry. You pat your belly above where your liver is and say, "I guess you're safe tonight, liver."
reading_hint: Genghis Khan glares down at you, "You inherited my liver, and that means no moonshine for you. Protect your liver and don't drink, like me, Genghis Khan."

meaning_mnemonic: There are <radical>flowers</radical> and <radical>hills</radical> in your front <kanji>lawn</kanji>. Your lawn is pretty big and it is covered with both flowers and hills. It’s very pretty.
reading_mnemonic: You are the proud owner of a <reading>Shiba</reading> (しば) Inu, y’know, the Japanese dog? Well, this Shiba Inu of yours loves to run around in your <kanji>lawn</kanji> and through the flowers and over the hills. This is the perfect lawn for your Shiba.
meaning_hint: Imagine walking through your lawn and smelling all the flowers as you travel over the rolling hills. What a delightful lawn this is.
reading_hint: Look at your little Shiba running through the lawn all happy and elated. Hear him barking as he zooms up and down each hill.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> find their <radical>death</radical> in a <radical>river</radical> out in the <kanji>wild</kanji>. Flowers live on water, but this river drowns the flowers to death. The wild is a dangerous place.
reading_mnemonic: <reading>Ah</reading> (あ), the flowers in the <kanji>wild</kanji> have been claimed <reading>à la</reading> (あら) the great flood. Ah, you don’t think so? Well I do.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself out in the wild wilderness as you hear the flowers screaming until death takes them. The river is raging, there’s nothing you can do to help the flowers. The wild has claimed them.
reading_hint: Imagine this scene again, only imagine it like the wild flowers are experiencing their own great flood. Ah, those flowers are drowning to death à la everything that died in the Biblical flood.

meaning_mnemonic: You keep your <radical>substitute</radical> <radical>clothes</radical> in a <kanji>sack</kanji>. The clothes in the sack are just substitute clothes, so they only come out in emergencies.
reading_mnemonic: You need your substitute clothes so you take out your substitute clothes <kanji>sack</kanji>. You open it up to grab your substitute clothes, only to find out that there is a ton of salmon roe inside your sack. Oh man, you forgot all about putting that in there. You yell: <reading>F**K ROE</reading> (ふくろ) because that roe destroyed all your clothes and made it nasty.
meaning_hint: Look at this sack of substitute clothes. Imagine it filled with all the clothes you hardly ever wear. What kind of clothes would be inside this sack?
reading_hint: Imagine how nasty it is in there. Also imagine how smelly it is. Feel angry about putting roe in your sack. Why would you even do that? Why would the roe do this to you, as well.

meaning_mnemonic: Who will <radical>say</radical> <radical>turkey</radical>? Who? <kanji>WHO</kanji>???
reading_mnemonic: Then, someone says turkey. "<kanji>Who</kanji> said turkey? You? How <reading>dare</reading> (だれ) you."
meaning_hint: Say "who" over and over again, demanding someone says "turkey," for some reason.
reading_hint: You're so mad at that person even though you demanded someone say turkey.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical>'s <radical>hat</radical>, not to mention the <radical>hair</radical> stuck to said hat, is incredibly <kanji>rare</kanji>. If you have your hands on either of these things, you'll be a rich duck.
reading_mnemonic: Then you look closer at the hat. It's about to get even <kanji>rare</kanji>r. Attached to the hair is the king's <reading>chin</reading> (ちん).
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself holding a king's hat covered in his hair. Who will you sell it to? This is such a rare item!
reading_hint: Not sure how it got there, but you'll make a pretty penny from this. The king only had one chin, so that's about as <kanji>rare</kanji> as they come.

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>yakuza</radical> takes a <radical>knife</radical> to <radical>clothes</radical>, it will of course cause a <kanji>split</kanji> or <kanji>tear</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: "I want to <kanji>split</kanji> and <kanji>tear</kanji> that." You agree, so you say <reading>Let's</reading> (れつ) do it.
meaning_hint: It doesn't matter if it's a yakuza member or not. Any knife will cause a split or tear in clothes.
reading_hint: Think of a thing you want to tear with your yakuza buddy. You're into it, so you agree and say "Ret's do it!"

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Imperial</radical> <radical>clothes</radical> allow you to perform an ancient <kanji>attack</kanji> that cannot be repelled.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>attack</kanji>'s power is concentrated in the <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう) in this imperial outfit. You attack with your feet, kicking the enemy with your old, fancy shoes.
meaning_hint: IMPERIAL ATTACK, GO! Imagine what this imperial attack might look like and imagine yourself in some swanky imperial clothes while you perform it.
reading_hint: Picture the shoes as you attack, they glow—full of attack power! These +4 attack shoes are really special.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>construction</radical> project made from <radical>shellfish</radical> will act as a <kanji>tribute</kanji> to the Crabigator.
reading_mnemonic: This great <kanji>tribute</kanji> is being commissioned by Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち.
meaning_hint: The Crabigator is a sea creature (maybe?), so making a tribute from shellfish makes some sense.
reading_hint:
In his power, こういち has made a new religion around the Crabigator, to put the fear of kanji learning into his subjects.

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading.

meaning_mnemonic: You have to <radical>run</radical>, put your <radical>ear</radical> on the <radical>stool</radical>, then run back. That's the <kanji>charm</kanji> of this game.
reading_mnemonic: The added <kanji>charm</kanji> of this game is that there's this big <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ) at the end. It's so charming because you love chutes.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself playing this game and basking in the charm of tapping your ear on the stool.
reading_hint: As you watch, you really see the charm of this game. Some people are throwing the stool down the chute, some people are sliding down it on their ears...how charming.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>foot</radical> on a <radical>giant</radical> is a pretty long <kanji>distance</kanji> for a normal person to walk. Giants have big feet and can travel large <kanji>distance</kanji>s very quickly.
reading_mnemonic: Going to Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ) is a short <kanji>distance</kanji> for a giant but a long <kanji>distance</kanji> for you.
meaning_hint: Compare the distance you can travel with one step compared to a giant. It's a considerable difference in distance.
reading_hint: Imagine walking the distance between where you are and the (possibly made up) Little Kyoto. Think about the distance.

meaning_mnemonic: People had to use their <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>plow</radical>s <radical>long ago</radical> to put their name in a <kanji>register</kanji>. This is because there was no paper yet, so they would hitch a cow to their bamboo plow, and dig their names into the dirt to create all kinds of registers.
reading_mnemonic: If your name is in a <kanji>register</kanji>, people will find you very <reading>sexy</reading> (せき). This was true long ago too. There's nothing sexier than someone whose existence is accounted for in official documentation.
meaning_hint: School registers, family registers, member registers — you name it. Picture the fields and hillsides back then, full of names scraped into the dirt.
reading_hint: No wonder people went to the trouble of plowing their names into the dirt back then. They understood the sex appeal of registers.

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>rain</radical> touches your <radical>foot</radical> and gives it a little rain <radical>kiss</radical>, you know that part of your foot is <kanji>expose</kanji>d to the elements. You can also see some <kanji>dew</kanji> forming on your exposed foot. So that's what rain kisses are!
reading_mnemonic: You run along the <reading>ro</reading>ad (ろ), trying to get out of the rain, but the road keeps going and going, with walls on both sides.
meaning_hint: Ugh, you shouldn't have bought these open toed shoes, at least not during rainy season!
reading_hint: You wonder how long this road will go. With exposed toes, your feet are going to get all wet with dew if you can't get off this blasted road.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>cow</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical> doesn't like to leave the barn to go to the <kanji>pasture</kanji>. It has to, though, because a cow's gotta eat.
reading_mnemonic: ぼく is one of the common ways to say "I" in Japanese (which you should remember!). In this case, you're a boy... a cowboy, and you say, "<reading>ぼく</reading>will take these cows out to pasture!"
meaning_hint: It's cold outside (it's winter after all). Imagine you having to herd all the cows out to pasture even though they don't want to leave the warm barn.
reading_hint: Actually say that phrase out loud while thinking about bringing your cows to pasture.

meaning_mnemonic: You take out a <radical>flag</radical>, lay a <radical>towel</radical> below it, and use your <radical>knife</radical> to carve the pattern through them both. Voila! This is exactly how old <kanji>printing</kanji> presses used to work.
reading_mnemonic: As you get your flag ready for <kanji>printing</kanji> you look over and see <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ), who's already finished a nice pattern on her towel. She's a little young to be using a knife, but her print came out nice.
meaning_hint: The old way of printing involved taking the pattern you want (the flag), pressing it against the place you want to print it (the towel), then just slicing that sucker on there (with the knife).
reading_hint: You know her pretty well at this point, so try to imagine what kind of printing Satsuki would make. Picture her holding it up to show you.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>good</radical> <radical>moon</radical> is a <kanji>bright</kanji> moon. Not just in terms of light, but in mood as well. Like, gee wiz, what a bright, happy moon.
reading_mnemonic: It's so <kanji>bright</kanji> that you can see all the <reading>roe</reading> (ろう) in the sea glowing and reflecting the light like stars. And the tiny baby fishies inside the roe are smiling brightly back up at the moon!
meaning_hint: Picture the good, good bright moon. Shining and shimmering and smiling down at us on Earth.
reading_hint: Picture this scene like it's a Disney cartoon. The good moon is smiling brightly down at the roe which are smiling right back. They might even start singing a song!

meaning_mnemonic: A small <radical>tsunami</radical> comes in the <radical>morning</radical> with the <radical>moon</radical>. That just means the ocean gets bigger when the moon's out, and that my friends is simply the <kanji>tide</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You go check out the <kanji>tide</kanji>. The water is coming in a little stronger, and something washes up on the shore. It's a body! You roll the body over and it is Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう)!
meaning_hint: The moon does affect the tides a bit, so you can imagine the tsunami as the water getting bigger whenever the moon is out. That's just the tide.
reading_hint: She wakes up and says, "If only you came at low tide then I would never have been saved!" She jumps up and runs off, yelling something about destroying the world.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>root</radical> of a <radical>stamp</radical> is to get an <kanji>instant</kanji> picture down. Back in the olden days, people had to make seals and draw out things by hand, so stamps came about so you can—BAM—just stamp it and have what you want in an instant.
reading_mnemonic: Think about the thing you want to make <kanji>instant</kanji>. I'd want instant <reading>sock</reading>s (そく)! Don't waste time searching for a matching pair or pulling them on. Just take out a pair of instant socks, add water, and the socks are already on your feet. In an instant!
meaning_hint: Lots of things have roots from wanting to have something in an instant. Just think about instant ramen. The root of that was to get ramen real, real fast. Similar to stamps, ya know?
reading_hint: Imagine using instant socks. How do they feel? Soggy, but hey, it was fast!

meaning_mnemonic:
Look at the <radical>dirt</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> at <radical>dawn</radical> and you'll be able to see all the <kanji>hedge</kanji>s growing in the area really well. At any other time of day, hedges tend to blend into their surroundings. But at dawn, the shadows the sun makes really helps them stand out.

This hedge also works as a <kanji>fence</kanji>. It borders an area and you can't walk through it. A natural fence!
reading_mnemonic: You're looking intently at one particular <kanji>hedge</kanji> because yesterday you dropped your <reading>ca</reading>r <reading>key</reading>s (かき) behind it. Now that you know where the hedge is you're going to need to search it to find your car keys.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself leaning over a balcony at dawn, looking at all the hedges appear and their shadows stretch over the ground.
reading_hint: Imagine rooting around under some hedges, desperately trying to find your car keys. Hedges are prickly and there's spiders in here! If only you didn't need a car in the first place!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>canine</radical> and a <radical>woman</radical> stand before you, radiating <kanji>dignity</kanji> and <kanji>might</kanji>. Who is this majestic pair?
reading_mnemonic: Just when you thought this woman couldn't emit any more <kanji>dignity</kanji>, an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) swoops down and perches on her shoulder. The huge canine behind her already gave her a dignified aura, but this eagle has made her look positively awe-inspiring.
meaning_hint: Try to imagine who this woman might be. A world leader? A powerful CEO? Whatever the case, she's got an aura of dignity — and that giant canine behind her only makes her look even more mighty.
reading_hint: Imagine trying to crack a joke in front of this mighty woman, only to be silenced by the dignified stare of her eagle. You just can't make lighthearted banter with someone who's got an eagle on their shoulder.

meaning_mnemonic: You take two clumps of <radical>dirt</radical> to make a <radical>measurement</radical>, using them to <kanji>seal</kanji> each side of your measuring tape, so it stays on the ground. This is the way people used to <kanji>seal in</kanji> things back in the day, back when we didn't have fancy things like tape and glue.
reading_mnemonic:
You can also <kanji>seal</kanji> <reading>foo</reading>d (ふう) in dirt to make it last longer. That's how jerky came about, you know. Seal some meat underground and in a few months you'll have some tasty dried meats to chew.

By the way, this has another reading you should probably know. It's ほう. Most sealing is done in the <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう), you know? So just think about eating food in your own home!
meaning_hint: Picture sealing other things with dirt. Like the bricks in your wall. Or the hole in your floor. Dirt can seal in anything.
reading_hint: Dig up your sealed food and eat it in the comfort of your own home. Mm, delicious!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>bamboo</radical> stalk looks the <radical>same</radical> as a big ol' <kanji>cylinder</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Picture a <kanji>cylinder</kanji> shaped <reading>とう</reading>きょう. All the buildings are cylinders. The city itself is cylinder shaped too. Even... gasp! The people of とうきょう!
meaning_hint: Compare the two things in your mind and make them the same.
reading_hint: とうきょう is a really weird city now that they passed the cylinder law. Imagine it so deeply that it weirds you out.

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>axe</radical> is sticking in the <radical>ground</radical> on top of a <radical>mountain</radical>. It's sticking into the very <kanji>peak</kanji>. Because why would you bother to stick your axe anywhere else on the mountain to prove you climbed it?
reading_mnemonic: You can't help but <reading>gawk</reading> (がく) at the <kanji>peak</kanji>. Somehow it looks even higher now.
meaning_hint: Picture that axe, jammed into the mountain's <kanji>peak</kanji>. The person who did that must have been really cool to get all the way up there.
reading_hint: Go ahead, imagine this peak with an axe in it and actually gawk a bit at how far you have to go to get that high.

meaning_mnemonic: We put up a <radical>flag</radical> to remember where we tried to use a <radical>jackhammer</radical> to take a <radical>measurement</radical> of your friend's <radical>heart</radical>. Granted, it was a terrible idea, but hopefully that flag of remembrance is some kind of <kanji>consolation</kanji>?
reading_mnemonic: Even though you weren't happy with the flag, you were even more frustrated to watch an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) swoop down and grab the flag and fly away with it.
meaning_hint: Obviously it's not good enough consolation. Be furious about this.
reading_hint: Now you have absolutely nothing. Not even a consolation flag.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>badger</radical>, at the <radical>root</radical> of its <radical>heart</radical>, is a <kanji>courteous</kanji> creature.
reading_mnemonic: Badgers are especially <kanji>courteous</kanji> to <reading>con</reading>dors (こん). Condors live in the trees while badgers live in burrows under those trees. When humans tear down the condor's homes, the badgers who live below them always let them stay over until they get back on their feet.
meaning_hint: They may seem gruff and rude at first, but they'll still let you in their home in the middle of winter unannounced and offer you tea, a warm fire, and a place to sleep.
reading_hint: Picture a courteous badger taking care of an injured, and now homeless, condor. It's fluffing the condor's pillows, bringing it soup, and patting it on the shoulder saying, "There, there."

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>loiter</radical>ing <radical>mountain</radical> <radical>king</radical> who hangs around during <radical>winter</radical> to provide an indication (this kanji uses the "indication" kanji as its top 2/3rds) of things to come now looks into your <radical>heart</radical>, and only sees bad things. So, he <kanji>chastise</kanji>s you about it.
reading_mnemonic: Why is he chastising you, really, though? He foretold Mrs. Chou coming back in a previous mnemonic, but you saved Mrs. Chou on the beach (once again, in a previous mnemonic), so you could have prevented her second coming. He is <kanji>chastis</kanji>ing you for saving Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).
meaning_hint: "What's in your heart is no good," he chastises you.
reading_hint: In the 潮 kanji you saved Mrs. Chou from dying, so he is chastising you for it.

meaning_mnemonic: Below the <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s is a <radical>hand</radical>. The hand is just there to <kanji>chafe</kanji> against the trees.
reading_mnemonic: As the hand <kanji>chafe</kanji>s on the trees, it says <reading>ma</reading>ma (ま), mama.
meaning_hint: There's a hand just chafing against some trees. That's... interesting.
reading_hint: This is the hand of a child looking for its mama. And, it chafes against trees to try and find the warm, soft touch of its mama. Instead, it only finds rough bark, so it continues its chafing.

meaning_mnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> to hold the <radical>roof</radical> of the <radical>festival</radical> up, but it <kanji>rub</kanji>s against them. The roof is heavy, and you have to walk to keep up, so the roof is rubbing against them really, really badly.
reading_mnemonic: Just when you thought the <kanji>rub</kanji>bing couldn't get any worse, <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) appears and hops up onto the roof. AAGH! It hurts!
meaning_hint: Have you ever been to a Japanese festival? There's always one of those portable shrines that are basically just roofs people hold up. That's what you're trying to hold with your fingers as the whole thing rubs against them.
reading_hint: Picture Satsuki sitting on the roof as you carry it through the festival. She may be a little girl, but her extra weight makes the rubbing so much worse — you can really feel the skin chafing.

meaning_mnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> in somebody's <radical>business</radical> by <kanji>slap</kanji>ping them! This method also works if someone is getting too involved in your business. Slaps are basically your way of doing business with your fingers.
reading_mnemonic: ぼく is one way to say "I" in Japanese, usually used by dudes. But you're going to use it now when you <kanji>slap</kanji> this guy. Who's in everybody's business, slapping them? <reading>ぼく</reading>.
meaning_hint: Imagine giving someone a good, hard slap in the face. You're all up in their business now.
reading_hint: Say that out loud, using ぼく. You're the boss, the boss of slap business.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>simultaneous</radical> radical and the <kanji>simultaneous</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: What <kanji>simultaneously</kanji> protects and kills? Your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい).
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: It's also simultaneously here and there at the same time. It moves really fast. And simultaneously glowing blue and white. Your saber is a lot of things all at once!

meaning_mnemonic: Take your <radical>spoon</radical> to the <radical>sun</radical> to be able to take a bite. It's <kanji>delicious</kanji>, you hear. That's the <kanji>point</kanji> of the spoon.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>delicious</kanji> taste is... familiar somehow. You take a few more bites and then scream. It tastes like <reading>shee</reading>p (し)!
meaning_hint: Taste that delicious sun. It doesn't even burn!
reading_hint: You had your eyes closed because the sun is so bright. When you open them you realize the delicious sun you were eating is really just a giant, really bright, cooked sheep. You should probably point this out to NASA or something.

meaning_mnemonic: You've been ordered to <radical>spear</radical> this <radical>tree</radical> but you decide to be really <kanji>gentle</kanji> instead.
reading_mnemonic: You're being really <kanji>gentle</kanji> to the tree when a <reading>jewel</reading> (じゅう) falls out of its branches and hits you on the head.
meaning_hint: Imagine gently poking the tree with your spear. You glance around nervously, hoping no one notices how easy on it you're being.
reading_hint: The jewel has a little note on it, "Thank you for being gentle." Did... did the tree just reward you for being gentle?!

meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>pirate</radical> they are going to <kanji>sink</kanji> no matter how good of a pirate captain they are.
reading_mnemonic: As the tsunami comes to <kanji>sink</kanji> the pirate, the pirate holds his <reading>chin</reading> (ちん) up high. He will die with dignity.
meaning_hint: Tsunamis are big. Pirates are small. Simple as that.
reading_hint: Imagine this dignified chin-holding-up-high pirate. Nod your head, because you know he got what he deserves.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> can't resist the <radical>call</radical> of a <kanji>bog</kanji>. Whenever a tsunami is headed toward land, a bog will always call out to it, "Hey tsunami, don't you want to hit me with your water? Come this way~"
reading_mnemonic: As you listen to the <kanji>bog</kanji>'s call you realize it looks just like the <reading>Numa</reading> (ぬま) Numa guy in your mind. You open your eyes and it's just a bog. You close your eyes, and there he is. The Numa Numa guy.
meaning_hint: Close your eyes and hear the bog's tempting call.
reading_hint: So that's what happened to him! If you're a youngun, just do a little Google and you'll be pretty pleased, I'm sure.

meaning_mnemonic: The sound of <radical>spring</radical> <radical>water</radical> always puts you at <kanji>peace</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You want to feel this <kanji>peace</kanji> all the time, not just in spring, so you dip your <reading>tie</reading> (たい) in the spring water. Now your tie is making the peaceful dripping sounds you love.
meaning_hint: Everything from winter is melting and trickling and thawing. All the dripping sounds fill you with a peace you can barely describe.
reading_hint: Imagine carefully dipping all of your ties in the peaceful spring waters and hearing that sweet sound you love at all times.

meaning_mnemonic: You're struck by a <radical>tsunami</radical>, mauled by a <radical>canine</radical>, and then set on <radical>fire</radical>. It seems the universe is trying to <kanji>destroy</kanji> you.
reading_mnemonic: Actually, the universe wants to <kanji>destroy</kanji> you because you're a <reading>Mets</reading> (めつ) fan. The universe despises the Mets — why do you think they haven't won a World Series since 1986?
meaning_hint: A tsunami, a canine attack, and a fire disaster make for quite a triple-whammy. What'd you do to make the universe want to destroy you so badly?
reading_hint: Imagine pulling on your Mets jersey only to suddenly receive a tsunami warning on your phone. Then, you hear a canine growling and catch a whiff of something burning nearby. Uh-oh, the universe is trying to destroy you again!

meaning_mnemonic: A (fresh water) <radical>tsunami</radical>. Some <radical>horns</radical>. The <radical>ground</radical>. A bunch of <radical>poop</radical>. Combine these together and let them decompose a while. You'll have yourself some very <kanji>nourishing</kanji> soil.
reading_mnemonic: This is the kind of <kanji>nourishing</kanji> soil that <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) would make if he was into making soil, probably.
meaning_hint: The stuff in horns is great for fertilizer. So is poop. So is water (as long as it's fresh water). So nourishing!
reading_hint: Imagine him making soil made out of those things. Jesus approves of this nourishing soil.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>fire</radical> <radical>door</radical> is a door that leads to something full of fire. That thing is a <kanji>furnace</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You're using the <kanji>furnace</kanji> to melt tar to put on the <reading>ro</reading>ad (ろ).
meaning_hint: Open the fire door and feel the heat of the <kanji>furnace</kanji>. Hot!
reading_hint: Smell the tar baking from the furnace. Feel the heat from the furnace. Fix the road, while you're at it.

meaning_mnemonic: Two <radical>king</radical>s are here right <radical>now</radical>! We need to entertain them otherwise who knows what they'll do. Get out the <kanji>harp</kanji>, or the <kanji>zither</kanji>, or something.
reading_mnemonic:
You may already know that a Japanese <kanji>harp</kanji> is called a こと, but here's a mnemonic to help you if you don't:

When playing your <kanji>harp</kanji>, you always wear your <reading>coat</reading> (こと).
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself playing a harp or a zither, even if you don't know how. Entertain these kings to the best of your ability, right now. That's why you're doing it and not someone who knows what they're doing. They want it now.
reading_hint: Your harp playing is pretty bad, so you put your coat on just in case you need to leave in a hurry to escape an angry audience.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>measurement</radical> radical and the <kanji>measurement</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.
reading_mnemonic: "Calculate your enemy's position with accurate <kanji>measurement</kanji>," is more or less what <reading>Sun</reading> (すん) Tzu said, I think. The guy wrote The Art of War, you know. When Sun says measurements are important, you don't question him.
meaning_hint: If you make sure to know the radicals, you'll also make sure to know the kanji too, automagically.
reading_hint: Imagine Mr. Sun measuring distances and rations and whatnot just before going to war — maps, compasses, all kinds of measurement devices piled on his war table. Also, don't forget that in Chinese Sun is pronounced more like "soon."

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>dragon</radical> radical is the same as this kanji, which is <kanji>dragon</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Imagine a <kanji>dragon</kanji>. You are talking to it, which is already pretty strange, but as you're talking, he turns into the Street Fighter character <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう).
meaning_hint: Not to <kanji>dragon</kanji> with it, but make sure you know your radicals so you know your kanji!
reading_hint: Then imagine after his transformation he attacks you using his Street Fighter moves. "Hadouken!"

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>thread</radical> is all that's keeping <radical>Wolverine</radical> and a <radical>pig</radical> from falling off the <kanji>edge</kanji> of a cliff. Wolverine and this pig had no <kanji>relation</kanji> prior to this, but after such a harrowing experience they're sure to form a deep bond.
reading_mnemonic: Just as it seems like they'll be able to climb back from the <kanji>edge</kanji>, the thread snaps, and Wolverine and his new pig friend plunge to a tragic <reading>en</reading>d (えん). What a shame — they had just begun to form such a friendly <kanji>relation</kanji>, too…
meaning_hint: Picture Wolverine and a pig hanging precariously over the edge of this cliff. What a crazy way to form a relation! It'll all be for naught if the thread snaps, though…
reading_hint: Moral of the story: stay away from edges, because they might spell the end for you and all your earthly relations.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>feather</radical> is on a <radical>rice paddy</radical>, and they are attached <radical>together</radical>. You put a feather on a rice paddy to create a giant airplane. Flawed, sure. But, this rice paddy + feather is going to be one <kanji>wing</kanji> of your amazing aircraft.
reading_mnemonic: To make the feather and rice paddy stick together you need some kind of sticking agent. This time we find the original bird that the feather came from and grab its eggs. We break the eggs open and use the <reading>yolk</reading> (よく). It's pretty sticky, but still probably not aircraft safe.
meaning_hint: Imagine a <kanji>wing</kanji> that is made of a rice paddy and a feather put together. Doesn't make sense, looks ugly, and probably will not fly. Imagine it anyways. It is your wing.
reading_hint: Imagine using the yolks to stick everything together. It's all over your hands, and things are pretty nasty.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>good luck</radical> radical and the <kanji>good luck</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: For <kanji>good luck</kanji>, you keep special <reading>kits</reading> (きつ) in your <reading>kitch</reading>en (きち). These kitchen kits contain good luck charms.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Your good luck kitchen kits have charms that say things like, "good food," "good company," and "good luck not cutting off your fingers with kitchen knives."

meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>blade</radical> and the kanji <kanji>blade</kanji> are exactly the same.
reading_mnemonic: When you stab your <kanji>blade</kanji> into an enemy you shout "<reading>Ha</reading> (は)!" It's just the normal "thrusting with blade" sound.
meaning_hint: Please, just know your radicals. Please!
reading_hint: Ha, HA, HaaAA! Those are the blade sounds we all know and love.

meaning_mnemonic: If someone puts a <radical>blade</radical> to your <radical>heart</radical>, you're going to have a rough time. They leave it there, for a long time. They aren't going to cut you, but you can't move. You have to <kanji>endure</kanji> the pain and <kanji>conceal</kanji> your discomfort with the situation.
reading_mnemonic: Learning to <kanji>endure</kanji> and <kanji>conceal</kanji> is all part of your <reading>nin</reading>ja (にん) training. You must learn to stay totally still, without breathing — you have to be able to endure this if you want to conceal yourself.
meaning_hint: To endure something is to conceal how it makes you feel, after all. Now imagine there's a blade pressed close to your heart. Try to endure the pain and conceal your emotions, or your heart might be cut.
reading_hint: No one knows better than a ninja how enduring and concealing are closely related. Go ahead, hold your breath and try walking soundlessly around your house, concealing your footsteps. Not easy to endure, is it?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tree</radical> has an <radical>omen</radical> growing from it, one single <kanji>peach</kanji> that is the size of a house. Inside is a child who will save the village from demons.
reading_mnemonic: If <reading>momo</reading>taro (もも) wasn't enough, think about the <kanji>peach</kanji> growing larger and larger. You want it to grow bigger, so you yell at it: "<reading>MO</reading>RE! <reading>MO</reading>RE!" (もも).
meaning_hint: Of course, this tree omen of a <kanji>peach</kanji> refers to momotaro, the little boy who was born from a peach (you should look it up if you don't know this children's story).
reading_hint: It grows bigger everytime you say "MORE MORE" twice. If you say it just once, it stays the same.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>spicy</radical> radical and <kanji>spicy</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Hot!
reading_mnemonic: When you eat <kanji>spicy</kanji> food, it gives a <reading>から</reading>て chop to your mouth. That's where that spicy feeling comes from!
reading_hint: Imagine a bunch of little black belt spice molecules からて punching, kicking and chopping all the way through your mouth and into your throat and nasal passages. Oww, it's pure agony!

meaning_mnemonic: No one can <radical>say</radical> what a <radical>scooter</radical> weighs in grains of <radical>rice</radical> — it's a <kanji>mystery</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: To get to the bottom of this mystery, you use a nozzle (なぞ). You pass rice through the nozzle one grain at a time, so you can figure out precisely how many grains of rice are equal to the weight of a scooter.
meaning_hint: Imagine lifting a scooter, hefting it, trying to guess its weight in grains of rice. At least 100 grains, I'd say. But no one can say, really. It's a total mystery.
reading_hint: Imagine watching this little nozzle squeeze out the rice, grain by grain, onto a scale. And imagine the boredom you'd feel watching that… But yes, you really can resolve any rice-based measurement mysteries thanks to this nozzle.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>temple</radical> isn't a monk like you'd expect. It's a <kanji>samurai</kanji>, who protects the monks in return for prayers that keep him safe.
reading_mnemonic: The reading is <reading>samurai</reading>, which should be easy enough to remember.
meaning_hint: You walk up to a temple and you're surprised to find that there's a samurai guarding it. Talk to him for a while so he can tell you why he has been doing this job. He's quite a nice guy, actually.
reading_hint: If you know the meaning you know the reading as well! That doesn't happen too often with kanji, so consider yourself lucky!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of a <radical>big</radical> <radical>rice paddy</radical> is standing in the middle of said rice paddy with an <radical>umbrella</radical>. Who is this wacky leader? Why, it is <kanji>I</kanji>. It is <kanji>me</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Why am <kanji>I</kanji> standing in the middle of this rice paddy with an umbrella? I'm digging for <reading>ore</reading> (おれ).
meaning_hint: Imagine that this leader is you. Be rough about it, because 俺 is the "rougher" version of "I." You should only use it in casual situations or if you're a badass.
reading_hint: More accurately, I'm standing here with an umbrella so I don't get wet. My minions are doing the ore digging, but I am watching to make sure they don't slack off.

meaning_mnemonic: From my <radical>mouth</radical> I have told you <radical>seven</radical> times not to do that. Now I have to <kanji>scold</kanji> you.
reading_mnemonic: What were you doing wrong that caused me to <kanji>scold</kanji> you? You were feeding the <reading>鹿</reading> (しか). If you feed them they'll never be able to feed themselves!
meaning_hint: SEVEN TIMES! You'd think you'd learn.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself feeding the deer, then getting scolded (or you doing the scolding to someone else feeding the deer, whichever works best).

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> loves her <radical>catapult</radical> for some good ol' fashioned <kanji>recreation</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Her favorite thing to shoot at with her catapult for <kanji>recreation</kanji> is <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご).
meaning_hint: You ask the woman what she does for fun... for recreation. She says, she goes out and shoots her catapult.
reading_hint: Get out of here, Godzilla! I'm gonna shoot you.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>ladle</radical> radical and the <kanji>ladle</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: You use a <kanji>ladle</kanji> to distribute soup. But as you're ladling you find something odd floating in the soup. It's... a <reading>toe</reading> (と)! You scream, dropping the ladle and the toe on the ground.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Your mom comes rushing in and smacks you on the head. "Fool!" she says, "This is toe soup, what did you think you'd ladle up?!" Oops.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>slide</radical> to get off this <radical>jet</radical> is a beautiful, brilliant <kanji>vermilion</kanji> color.
reading_mnemonic: You jump onto the <kanji>vermilion</kanji> slide. It's actually more of a steep <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ). And... oh... urp... it's making you sick.
meaning_hint: Vermilion is like an orange red, which is perfect for visibility purposes.
reading_hint: The vermilion chute spits you out and you spit out your lunch. And it looks vermilion. Uh oh. Hospital. Please.

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>axe</radical> is stuck into the <radical>ground</radical> on a <kanji>hill</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You reach the top of the <kanji>hill</kanji> and realize the entire hill is covered in <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう). The axe has a little note on it that says "Cucumber Hill Axe - Please Use."
meaning_hint: Imagine climbing up the hill. The sun is coming up behind the axe,  silhouetting the whole thing very nicely.
reading_hint: You take the axe out of the hill and cut off some cucumber to eat. Mm mm!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>grain</radical> you cut off a <radical>tree</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> is a <kanji>pear</kanji>. You've found some particularly big, sturdy ones, which is why you need the help of your knife. These pears are sure to be super juicy and delicious, but you need some extra tools to get them off the tree.
reading_mnemonic: As you cut the <kanji>pear</kanji>s down, you can't help but <reading>gnash</reading> (なし) your teeth. You want to eat these pears right now. You gnash and grind and wait though, because you want to have them all down before you start the feast.
meaning_hint: Imagine looking up at a tree full of thick-stemmed, jumbo-size pears. You pull out your knife and get ready to hack them all down, one by one.
reading_hint: Imagine a beautiful, ripe pear in your mind. Now gnash your teeth. Mmm. You want to eat a pear right now, don't you?

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> of <radical>business</radical>? Ha! That's <kanji>me</kanji>! That's <kanji>I</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: You use the word <kanji>I</kanji> a lot. You say it so much, you just start referring to yourself with the french <reading>beaucoup</reading> (ぼく) for a lot. Because you love yourself a lot.
meaning_hint: This version of <kanji>I</kanji> is more boyish. It's a sort of young man/boy way of saying "I," though you'll hear women use it too sometimes (it's a teensy bit tomboyish though).
reading_hint: I am beaucoup I am. (Is that a pun? Did I do it right?) I dunno. Just say ぼく a lot and try to connect all of your approximate knowledges of languages together. Merci ぼく from ぼく.

meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You look up through the cloth, and see a hairless cat. This <kanji>small animal</kanji> is hairless, but most surprisingly is covered in <reading>hickie</reading>s (ひき).
meaning_hint: You can only see the legs, though, because a cloth is covering the cage. What kind of small animal is it? Try to look.
reading_hint: Who is giving this cat all those hickies? Pretty gross, if you ask me.

meaning_mnemonic: You have <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>vines</radical>, so you <kanji>shout</kanji>! It's hard for you to talk normally, and even harder for people to understand you unless you shout.
reading_mnemonic: You need to <kanji>shout</kanji> so that people can understand you through the mouth vines. You shout, "<reading>きょう</reading>と!" because you hear there is a temple there for people with your condition.
meaning_hint: You were born with mouth vines (a rare condition where your mouth is always full of vines), so you've been shouting all your life. And yet people still can't hear you. It's very frustrating.
reading_hint: Imagine the word きょうと not coming out clearly because of your mouth vines. People can only hear the きょう part, so they don't know where you want to go. SHOUT LOUDER! きょうと！

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>gold</radical> <radical>pool</radical> is where you're going to go <kanji>fishing</kanji>. Because, if the pool is gold, hopefully the fish are too.
reading_mnemonic: You caught a couple of gold fish. <reading>Two</reading> (つ) gold fish, actually!
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself fishing in a gold pool, catching golden fish. What a great <kanji>fishing</kanji> spot this is.
reading_hint: Count your golden fish. They're so nice! They're also worth their weight in gold, because, well, they are gold.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>long</radical> <radical>hair</radical> is your <radical>friend</radical>. It's your <kanji>hair</kanji> and you're so proud of it.
reading_mnemonic: Who gave you this hair? Probably the great <reading>神</reading> (かみ) of hair. You deserve this great hair.
meaning_hint: Just feel your hair, it's such a good friend, and you wish it was longer.
reading_hint: Pretend to pray to the great hair 神. What is his reply? Does he give you more hair?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>mountain</radical> <radical>wind</radical> coming down from the mountains is very fierce. It is so fierce it's a lot like a <kanji>storm</kanji>. Well, it is a storm, really.
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>storm</kanji> is nothing to scoff at. If you get hit by the storm and get all wet and nasty and electrocuted, you're bound to get <reading>a rash</reading> (あらし).
meaning_hint: If you think about it makes sense. Those mountain winds that come down from the mountain are cold and windy and nasty. When they mix with warmer air down below, it creates a nasty <kanji>storm</kanji>.
reading_hint: The rash comes from being wet and not drying off when you get home. Your skin gets irritated by this moisture.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>bamboo</radical> thing you put on your head to <radical>stand</radical> taller would probably be a hat. A bamboo hat is probably one of those hats you think of when you think of rice farmers, a <kanji>conical hat</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>conical hat</kanji> is kind of like an umbrella on your head. What's the reading for "umbrella" in Japanese? Why, it's <reading>kasa</reading> (かさ)!
meaning_hint: You know the ones I'm talking about? They're cone-shaped, sort of, and they go on your head, keeping the sun off your face.
reading_hint: Think about a bamboo umbrella on your head. They're pretty similar in shape, after all.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> goes through a little <radical>door</radical>, and plops down a <radical>big</radical> drop on your face. This tsunami is your tears inside your eye area. The door is your tear duct. The big drop is the <kanji>teardrop</kanji> coming down your eye.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>teardrop</kanji>s are like little waves. What are they? They're <reading>波だ</reading> (なみだ).
meaning_hint: Imagine a teardrop going through this process to get out of your eye, though in a more miniature teardrop size.
