reading_hint: Imagine if a Shogun and a Viking had actually met in history (never mind that the dates don't overlap). You can definitely picture the quiet, refined Shogun putting his palm over the mouth of some noisy Viking during a tough diplomatic meeting.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits your <radical>mohawk</radical> and it begins to <kanji>drip</kanji>. The water from the tsunami continues to drip from your mohawk long after the tsunami has passed. There’s a lot of water in your hair now, so it’s probably going to drip for a while.
reading_mnemonic: Luckily, you’re a self-proclaimed <reading>techie</reading> (てき), so you develop a device to stop the <kanji>drip</kanji> drip dripping. This techie device of yours is like a hair dryer helmet. You put it over your mohawk, it does its thing, and your hair is dry! Being a techie sure does have its benefits.
meaning_hint: Imagine having all this tsunami water in your hair. Hear it drip from your mohawk. Drip… drip… drip…
reading_hint: Feel the weight of this dryer helmet on your head as you listen to it whirr away. The dripping has finally stopped! Success!

meaning_mnemonic: Without a <radical>fire</radical>, you will <radical>lack</radical> the ability to <kanji>cook</kanji> anything. You really need a fire to cook properly. Having a fire will make sure any cooking endeavors you attempt will not fail.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>cook</kanji> your meal evenly by <reading>swi</reading>nging (すい) it back and forth over the fire. You swing it left, you swing it right. You swing it to, you swing it fro. All this swinging ensures a properly cooked meal.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself as you cook with a fire very confidently. Knowing you cannot fail, feel the heat and hear the crackling embers of the fire. Smell the delicious smell of whatever you are cooking as well.
reading_hint: Imagine swinging this meal over the fire. Feel the flames licking at your fingertips as you cook this meal by swinging it back and forth over the fire.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>big</radical> <radical>treasure</radical> <radical>treasure</radical> <radical>treasure</radical> <radical>treasure</radical> is <kanji>refreshing</kanji>! Sorry, I can’t say treasure treasure treasure treasure without saying it four times. See? I did it again. Anyway, this big treasure treasure treasure treasure is really refreshing. It’s been a long time since you found a treasure treasure treasure treasure, so finding this big one is really refreshing.
reading_mnemonic: You’d had a <reading>sour</reading> (さわ) taste in your mouth for not being able to find any treasure treasure treasure treasure for such a long time, but now that you found this big one, the sour taste is totally gone and it’s sooooo <kanji>refreshing</kanji>!
meaning_hint: Imagine finding this big treasure treasure treasure treasure and being really refreshed and relieved and happy. This treasure treasure treasure treasure is so big, you’ll be set for life!
reading_hint: Imagine the most sour taste you could ever imagine. So super-duper sour! Now imagine that sour taste instantly melting away once you discover this big refreshing treasure treasure treasure treasure. Jackpot!

meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>rice paddy</radical> there is a <radical>forehead</radical> with a <radical>top hat</radical>. It sits atop an area of <kanji>tatami mat</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: Who made this creepy <kanji>tatami mat</kanji> altar? It was <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the increasingly concerning farmhand who lives in your village.
meaning_hint: The meaning you're trying to learn is tatami mat, so focus on the beautiful tatami mats that are strangely in the middle of this rice paddy. On top is a forehead with a top hat. It almost looks like the tatami mats are an altar for this gruesome scene.
reading_hint: You saw Joe setting up the tatami mats a day earlier, but you thought he was just making an area for workers in the field to rest in. Does the forehead with a top hat have anything to do with Joe? What sort of ominous sign could this be?

meaning_mnemonic: Rub <radical>charcoal</radical> under your <radical>eye</radical>s if you want to see <kanji>clear</kanji>ly. Sports players also rub charcoal under their eyes to see nice and clear in the bright sun.
reading_mnemonic: You have to have <kanji>clear</kanji> vision because you're about to go out in your <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat. The light off the water shines right into your eyes and you need the charcoal to clear that up for you. Otherwise you'll end up crashing into the shore or going over a waterfall to your death.
meaning_hint: You can even rub charcoal in your eyes if you really want to clear them up, but you should probably only do this under the supervision of your eye doctor.
reading_hint: Imagine how dangerous it would be not to have clear vision while you row around in a row boat. You might even smack yourself in the face with your own oars.

meaning_mnemonic: You give <radical>bamboo</radical> to <radical>someone</radical> and they make them into <kanji>chopsticks</kanji>. They are very talented at turning bamboo into bamboo chopsticks. This someone is an expert at their profession.
reading_mnemonic: Now use those <kanji>chopsticks</kanji> to eat some delicious <reading>hash</reading> (はし) browns! That's what chopsticks were made for. That's what they're always made for.
meaning_hint: Watch this person take ordinary bamboo and transform it into chopsticks right before your eyes. Hear the whooshing and whittling sounds as they form the bamboo into chopsticks with expert speed and precision.
reading_hint: Imagine trying to pick up hash browns with your chopsticks. They would be really hard to eat if you didn't have these handy eating tools!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> is the <radical>same</radical> as your <kanji>torso</kanji>. You must have a really huge torso for it to be the same as the moon.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>torso</kanji> got so big because of all the <reading>dough</reading>nuts (どう) you ate. You ate so many doughnuts that your torso got to be the size of the moon and then you floated away into space with your own gravitational pull and everything. Doughnuts are dangerous things.
meaning_hint: Imagine if your torso was as large as the moon. You’re a gigantic human. Imagine floating around in space like this. Wooooaaaahhh.
reading_hint: Imagine how many doughnuts you would have to eat to get your torso this size. Now imagine eating all of those doughnuts. I hope you like doughnuts, because you’re going to be eating a lot of them.

meaning_mnemonic: There are <radical>flowers</radical> growing around a <radical>heart</radical> with a <kanji>wick</kanji> sticking out of it. The heart is a sacrifice to the old gods and the life energy bleeds from the burning wick to feed the flowers. The wick burns, releasing the power of the heart which spreads into the ground around the heart causing the flowers to grow.
reading_mnemonic: Sometimes the <kanji>wick</kanji> burning the heart isn’t enough for the flowers to grow properly and they get thirsty for the blood from your <reading>shin</reading> (しん). So, you slice open your shin with a sacrificial knife and drip your blood over the thirsty demon flowers.
meaning_hint: See this morbid scene. As the wick burns you can smell the smell of burning organs. The flowers are pretty though.
reading_hint: Imagine slicing your shin with a knife, causing blood to drip on the flowers around the heart with a wick in it. Your shin really hurts from the injury, but the flowers are rejoicing as they’re showered in blood.

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>insect</radical> specializing in <radical>construction</radical> builds a <kanji>rainbow</kanji>. Did you ever wonder where rainbows come from? Construction insects build them. Construction insects build rainbows.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>rainbow</kanji> building insect is so <reading>needy</reading> (にじ). He is needy because he needs everyone to look at his rainbow and praise him for what he’s done. “Look at my rainbow! Look at how good it is and I am! I’m so needy! Needy for praise and recognition!”
meaning_hint: Imagine this insect with a construction hat on, building a rainbow. He works very fast. The resulting rainbow is beautiful!
reading_hint: Imagine this construction insect being all whiny and needy for attention and stuff. It’s pathetic. Hear him whine and whine because of how needy he is.

meaning_mnemonic: Take your <radical>towel</radical> and make it <radical>long</radical>. It has to be longer than that! For this will be your <kanji>notebook</kanji>, which you will write all your notes in.
reading_mnemonic: Inside your long towel <kanji>notebook</kanji> you just write the same name over and over again: "Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading>" (ちょう).
meaning_hint: You are making the towel really long, because it's white and it might be good for writing in. It's going to become your notebook.
reading_hint: Something bad is going to happen, and you need to warn everybody. It's her, it's her, it's her. Mrs. Chou, Mrs. Chou, Mrs. Chou!

meaning_mnemonic: The type of <radical>insect</radical> that ruins your <radical>writing</radical> is the <kanji>mosquito</kanji>. You're trying to write, when you hear that horrible high-pitched mosquito noise in your ear. You jerk your head and swat and it's gone. But when you look down, there's lines all over what you were writing.
reading_mnemonic: That <kanji>mosquito</kanji> noise sounds like it's driving a tiny <reading>ca</reading>r (か) in your ear. How are you supposed to write with a bug driving around going bzzz bzzz like a revving car?
meaning_hint: Imagine sitting at your desk writing when a mosquito starts bzzzzing in your ear. You can't ignore it, it's gonna bite you. So you flail around helplessly.
reading_hint: You can't. You can't write like this. You need to kill the mosquito and its little car too.

meaning_mnemonic: There is an <radical>insect</radical> on the <radical>roof</radical> in a <radical>spoon</radical>. It's there for the eating, and it's how you plan to attract a <kanji>snake</kanji> to your roof.
reading_mnemonic: Why don't you just bring a <kanji>snake</kanji> up to the roof yourself? Because snakes are <reading>heavy</reading> (へび).
meaning_hint: Snakes love insects, especially when they're ready to eat on a spoon. But, snakes don't usually go on top of the roof, so that's why you have to tempt one with an insect in a spoon.
reading_hint: Especially big snakes. They're really, really hebi.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>shellfish</radical> are worth a <radical>fortune</radical> because you decided to <kanji>paste</kanji> your fortune to the shellfish.
reading_mnemonic: You're a little crazy, which is why you decided to use <kanji>paste</kanji> to paste your entire fortune to these shellfish, and as you paste each one you yell out, "<reading>Ha</reading> (は)!" Paste.
meaning_hint: Picture all of your riches, your money, your jewels. They're all pasted directly onto all of your shellfish!
reading_hint: "Ha ha!" Paste paste. "Ha ha ha!!" PASTE PASTE PASTE.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>landslide</radical> <radical>measurement</radical> <kanji>humiliate</kanji>s you. You told everyone you couldn't come to work, that your house had been destroyed and your car was carried away, but then scientists measured the landslide, and it only went a few feet.
reading_mnemonic: Your friends and coworkers <kanji>humiliate</kanji> you more by making <reading>joke</reading>s (じょく). "Uh oh, I heard it might rain later, I hope you don't drown in the inch of water that's coming!"
meaning_hint: Honestly, that's what you get for lying. But it doesn't make it any less humiliating even if you deserve it.
reading_hint: Imagine all of the cruel jokes people say to humiliate you. Feel your face burn and your stomach flip.

meaning_mnemonic: Melt down this <radical>gold</radical> <radical>book</radical> and recast it as a <kanji>flower pot</kanji>. A solid gold book is too heavy to hold and too shiny to read, but a solid gold flower pot would make a fancy new addition to your garden.
reading_mnemonic: The gold <kanji>flower pot</kanji> has a <reading>hatch</reading> (はち) attached to it, which used to be the book's back cover. That's fine, you use the hatch as a lid for the flower pot!
meaning_hint: Imagine melting down your gold book. You're excited to have your new flower pot, but you're a little sad because this book was actually pretty interesting. Make a mental note to buy it in paperback later.
reading_hint: Okay, so this may not be the best flower pot. You usually don't need a hatch or this much gold coating. But that's how they used to do it in the old days, so that's good enough for you!

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>gate</radical> makes the <radical>sound</radical> of <kanji>darkness</kanji>. The gate looks like darkness, but it’s even more creepy that it sounds like darkness too.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to taste the <kanji>darkness</kanji> and discover that it is so <reading>yummy</reading> (やみ)! Who knew that darkness was so yummy? Mmm… this darkness tastes so yummy!
meaning_hint: Imagine this gate making an ominous sound. The sound of darkness. Look deep into the darkness of the gate as you hear the sound it makes. Ouummmmm…
reading_hint: Take the darkness in your hands. It’s like really dark cotton candy. Imagine eating it and how yummy it is. Keep hearing the sound it’s making as you eat. Yummy!

meaning_mnemonic: In this <radical>building</radical> there's a <radical>small</radical> <radical>sun</radical> that's <radical>small</radical>. They keep this small, small sun inside of a <kanji>crevice</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: They keep the sun in the <kanji>crevice</kanji> because this is a place where people <reading>ski</reading> (すき).
meaning_hint: You wouldn't want to keep a sun out in the open, even if it's small (and small). You'd put it inside something, like a crevice. Imagine walking into the building and seeing the small sun right inside, in a crevice.
reading_hint: If the sun were out in the open, it'd melt all the snow, making it hard to ski here. So they hide the sun away in a crevice so people can ski.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Rain</radical> falls off a <radical>tree</radical> and lands in your <radical>eye</radical> where it turns to <kanji>frost</kanji>. It turns to frost because it’s super cold outside and your body is freezing. The rain that fell off the tree turns to frost as soon as it hits your eye.
reading_mnemonic: This whole description is pretty emo. You came up with this whole emo <kanji>frost</kanji> idea because you are a <reading>shemo</reading> (しも). An emo who goes by she pronouns.
meaning_hint: Feel the rain hit your eye and then crystalize into frost immediately. The rain in your eye is wet, and then very cold as it becomes frost.
reading_hint: Picture yourself walking proudly, casting your frost gaze at all who you pass. "Wow look at that frosty, cool shemo. She's sooooo cool!" you hear them whisper. (Haha cool, get it? Because you're frosty and also a little sad, which is totally in when you're a teen.)

meaning_mnemonic: You’re so hungry that you <radical>eat</radical> a <radical>table</radical> so you won’t <kanji>starve</kanji>. If you don’t eat this table, you will most definitely starve, so you eat the table to stay alive.
reading_mnemonic: After you finish the table, you still feel like you might <kanji>starve</kanji> so you decide to eat every <reading>key</reading> (き) off your key ring. One by one, you eat each and every key so you don’t starve.
meaning_hint: You don’t want to starve, do you? Feel your tummy gurgle in pain. You gotta eat this table. Taste the dry wood taste on your tongue as you eat the table and get splinters in your tongue and gums.
reading_hint: Imagine eating every key. Taste the cold metal on your tongue. After you swallow each key, you hear it clink against the other keys already in your tummy. Clink!

meaning_mnemonic: If you have nothing to <radical>eat</radical> but your <radical>ego</radical>, you'll <kanji>starve</kanji>. You can't eat it, and your ego is keeping you from accepting actual food or help from other people.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>starve</kanji> (thanks ego) and turn into a <reading>ga</reading>rgoyle (が). As your stomach aches, your skin turns gray and you slowly harden into a crouched position.
meaning_hint: You have to check your ego at the door, because you'll definitely starve otherwise.
reading_hint: Now your starved body has been moved to the top of a castle, where you scare away people and birds with your loud grumbling stomach and scary face.

meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>mysterious</radical> <radical>rice paddy</radical> there is some <kanji>livestock</kanji>. This livestock is also mysterious because no one knows where it came from. The mysterious livestock are only seen in the mysterious rice paddy. How mysterious indeed.
reading_mnemonic: Your favorite member of the mysterious <kanji>livestock</kanji> crew is a little <reading>chick</reading> (ちく). The chick is very cute and little. The chick is also very mysterious because it floats about without even flapping its little wings.
meaning_hint: Examine this mysterious livestock from a distance. Imagine them floating around the mysterious rice paddy like they aren’t even walking. Hear them moo and oink as well.
reading_hint: Look at this chick bobbling about in the air. It looks very ephemeral and other worldly. Still a very cute chick though. You want to pet it.

meaning_mnemonic: When you ride a <radical>scooter</radical>, you wear a <radical>cape</radical> and carry a <radical>cross</radical> in order to move at a <kanji>swift</kanji> speed. The cape gives you a swiftness buff, naturally, and the cross lets you pray to a divine power to help you move ever more swiftly down the road.
reading_mnemonic: You move at such <kanji>swift</kanji> speeds now, your <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) are ripped right off your body.
meaning_hint: Hear the cape flapping behind you wildly, and hear yourself murmuring your prayers for extra swiftness. You just have a need for speed, okay? And with any luck, the gods will grant you the gift… of swift…
reading_hint: Fortunately, the lack of jeans make you even more swift and aerodynamic. You also feel a swift and pleasant breeze on all your downstairs bits now…

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>horse</radical> may look <radical>odd</radical> but it’s actually perfect for <kanji>horse riding</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: Before <kanji>horse riding</kanji>, you’ll need the <reading>key</reading> (き) to access the stable and let out the odd horse you'll be riding.
meaning_hint: Look at this odd horse. It's a little odd-looking, but it turned its quirks into strengths and now it's an exceptional horse for horse riding!
reading_hint: Want to ride the odd horse? Then, you must have the key to open its stable!

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> are <radical>mysterious</radical>ly appearing in the <radical>rice paddy</radical>. No matter what you do to them, they continue to <kanji>amass</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: As the flowers <kanji>amass</kanji> it starts attracting wildlife. Soon thousands of baby <reading>chick</reading>s (ちく) are amassing in the rice paddy as well.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself spraying poison on these mysterious flowers, but they just double in size, and continue to amass.
reading_hint: Feel the wetness of the rice paddy under your feet and the fluffiness of the chicks pressing in on you. Watch the looming, mysterious flowers above you as the chicks smother you.

meaning_mnemonic: You see <radical>shrimp</radical> on <radical>ice</radical> at the market in the morning. "I'll get some on the way home" you think to yourself. Later that evening, you see no shrimp, just ice. The supply of shrimp has been <kanji>exhaust</kanji>ed. It's all gone! You never thought they'd <kanji>use up</kanji> their shrimp supply so quickly.
reading_mnemonic: How could the store <kanji>use up</kanji> its shrimp supply so quickly? Suddenly you notice the worker behind the fish counter has shrimp stuffed into their <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) . Aha! The culprit!
meaning_hint: Imagine the lack of shrimp on the ice. All you wanted was some delicious shrimp for dinner, but now it's all gone.
reading_hint: Imagine the worker's jeans and how gross they are with all those shrimp stuffed into them. Yuck!

meaning_mnemonic: You use a <radical>cleat</radical> to climb a <radical>tree</radical> and your <radical>hair</radical> gets the same <kanji>coloring</kanji> as the foliage because your hair is like a chameleon. The coloring of your hair changes to whatever color is around you, so you use your cleats to climb trees to make your hair turn green and brown, and in the fall, yellow and orange and red!
reading_mnemonic: This cool <kanji>coloring</kanji> can only happen because you turned yourself into a <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). Your hair and scalp are now made of high tech parts, not human ones.
meaning_hint: Imagine digging your cleats into the bark of the tree until you get high enough for your hair to start coloring itself tree colors. It might be a lot of work, but it's worth it.
reading_hint: You couldn't take the pain of losing your cyborg, so you've become one. Your robo-hair can change its coloring, but soon you'll be even more cyborg-y than before!

meaning_mnemonic: When your <radical>soul</radical> has an <radical>idea</radical> it's just a <kanji>recollection</kanji> of something you already know. Only your brain can have original ideas, so when your soul tries to come up with one, it's just a recollection of something you thought before now.
reading_mnemonic: Your soul is sending you a <kanji>recollection</kanji> right now of... your past life as a farmer in <reading>Ok</reading>lahoma (おく). You see yourself farming and sweating and living a wonderful life. Hmm, maybe you should move to Oklahoma and live the simple life again.
meaning_hint: When you have a recollection of some idea or thing in the past, it's just your soul saying, hey remember this? Let's not forget this.
reading_hint: Imagine recollecting a past life in Oklahoma. Life was simple and nice. You should consider taking this as a sign to move there again!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> hits your <radical>form</radical> and you begin to <kanji>melt</kanji>. You are a witch and water makes you melt. The tsunami is a lot of water, so once it touches your form, you begin to melt.
reading_mnemonic: The only way to reverse the <kanji>melt</kanji> process is to eat some <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). The yogurt counteracts the melting from the water and your form will become whole again. Quick, eat some yogurt!
meaning_hint: Feel the water from the tsunami crashing into your form. Feel the water on your body as your form begins to melt. I’m melting! I’m mellltiinnnggg!
reading_hint: Feel the melt begin to reverse as you eat your yogurt. Taste the yogurt on your tongue as your form begins to solidify once again.

meaning_mnemonic: You take a <radical>rake</radical> in for a <radical>measurement</radical> but it is <kanji>resistant</kanji>. The rake doesn’t want to have its measurements taken. The rake is resistant to have any measurement taken because it feels inferior to the other rakes and doesn’t want to be embarrassed if it ends up being smaller than the others.
reading_mnemonic: Because the rake is persistently <kanji>resistant</kanji>, you eventually tie it to the table with your neck <reading>tie</reading> (たい). The tie won’t hold for long, so you need to take each measurement quickly.
meaning_hint: Try and move this rake over to the measurement table. It is resistant so you have a hard time moving it. Imagine the rake struggling and resisting endlessly.
reading_hint: See yourself tying the tie around the rake and attaching it to the measurement table. The rake is still jostling around, but the tie keeps it from moving about too terribly much.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>foot</radical>, as your <radical>mama</radical> says, was given the <radical>task</radical> to <kanji>dance</kanji>. Shake those two foots your mama gave you and dance dance dance!
reading_mnemonic: You both <kanji>dance</kanji> so much that you develop an <reading>odo</reading>r (おど). Not just any odor, an odor particular to stinky dancing feet.
meaning_hint: Picture your mama asking you to dance. The two of you pick up and kick your feet to dance, spinning 'round and 'round.
reading_hint: Lean down and take a big whiff of your dancing feet. The odor is... HORRIBLE! AHGH!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>servant</radical> uses a <radical>stool</radical> to break open <radical>shellfish</radical>. This is really <kanji>clever</kanji>, because shucking shellfish is a lot of work. It's also a <kanji>wise</kanji> move, because it'll save him time that he can spend on all his other servant chores.
reading_mnemonic: "Yes, more <kanji>clever</kanji> than you!" he yells, tearing off his servant's disguise. "It is I, <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai. I won't let you eat these shellfish. I'm taking them back to the sea, where they belong!"
meaning_hint: "You sure are clever, man!" you shout encouragingly at your wise servant as he plops his stool down on another mound of shellfish. Dinner tonight is sure to be a seafood feast, and you can't wait!
reading_hint: Picture Ken standing there with his sword and crab-shell armor. Pretty clever of him to disguise himself as your servant for so many years just to save a few mollusks. It might have been wiser not to shuck so many of them first, though…

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>light</radical> shines on your <radical>forehead</radical> in your <radical>car</radical> with a great <kanji>radiance</kanji>. The car you are in is a convertible, so the light shines right onto your big bald forehead. The light has a pretty powerful radiance as it is, but the sheen from this light hitting your forehead is the most powerful radiance ever witnessed by man.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>radiance</kanji> is the <reading>key</reading> (き) to powering your car. Your car runs on reflected solar energy, and the radiance coming from your forehead is the most powerful source of radiant sunlight ever imagined. Your forehead is a perfect key for this car.
meaning_hint: Imagine this radiance emanating from your shiny dome. The radiance is blinding!
reading_hint: Point your shiny forehead, filled with radiance, at your car. Hear the engine start and jump to life as you power it with the reflected sunlight from your forehead.

meaning_mnemonic: There's <radical>power</radical>, <radical>power</radical>, and <radical>power</radical> stacked on top of the <radical>moon</radical>, <kanji>threaten</kanji>ing to bring it down!
reading_mnemonic: This extra power-filled moon is now trying to <kanji>threaten</kanji> the safety of <reading>きょう</reading>と. All of きょうと is shaking and getting dark as the moon comes closer and closer.
meaning_hint: The moon actually looks like it wants to threaten you now too. It seems to have grown a big, smiling, angry mouth and it's way closer to you than you've ever seen it. All that power is radiating out of it and down towards you, threateningly.
reading_hint: Picture the powerful moon, threatening to crush きょうと and all its residents. If only a small green boy could turn back time and save everyone from this powerful moon!

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s isn't actually a canopy of trees at all. When you look closely, you see these trees are actually giant stalks of <kanji>hemp</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Who planted all this <kanji>hemp</kanji>? Your <reading>ma</reading>ma (ま) did.
meaning_hint: Wow, these hemp plants are so big that they look like trees. But, despite their size they're still just hemp.
reading_hint: Your mama knew that you were going to like hemp in the future, so she planted these hemp plants for you. What a great mama.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Fire</radical> you use to light up the <radical>street</radical> needs to go in a <kanji>lamp</kanji>. Campfires, matches? No, none of those work. You need bright, safe lamps to keep the street nice at night.
reading_mnemonic: These street <kanji>lamp</kanji>s were first used in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. It was the most advanced city after all. And more people were out at night in とうきょう than anywhere else in Japan. So they needed lamps to light things up!
meaning_hint: Picture these lamps lining the street, with their little fires burning brightly. Nowadays we don't have real fire in them, we use electricity!
reading_hint: Picture とうきょう, not lit up by all of the electricity, signs, shops, and things that light up its streets today, but by old-fashioned fire lamps!

meaning_mnemonic: When a flower looks like a <radical>mouth</radical> with <radical>horns</radical> that reach up to <radical>heaven</radical>, you know it's in <kanji>blossom</kanji>. It's all opened up like a mouth, and the pistil and stamen are like horns reaching up to heaven for its sunlight and rain.
reading_mnemonic: If it's shaped like a mouth with horns poking out of it, you know it's going to be a tough little <kanji>blossom</kanji>. That's why you'll need a <reading>saw</reading> (さ) if you've got any hope of cutting it down for your blossom collection.
meaning_hint: Imagine studying this little blossom. Its mouth is open and nectar-filled, and out of it spring these horn-like things that reach up to heaven. It smells nice too. A perfect specimen for your blossom collection, maybe?
reading_hint: Picture those classic dainty little blossoms — buttercups, pansies, you name it. Now imagine their tougher cousins, blossoms so tough you'd need a saw to cut them down. If you can picture the latter, you should remember the さ reading.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>dirt</radical> needs a  <radical>clown</radical> in it if you want to <kanji>cultivate</kanji> good crops. Clowns act as a natural fertilizer, and this will make it super easy to cultivate any crops you could ever want in this dirt. Isn't nature amazing?
reading_mnemonic: Before you can start this whole <kanji>cultivation</kanji> process however, you have to say <reading>bye</reading> (ばい) to the clown. Say bye to him, then bash his head in, and drop him in the dirt.
meaning_hint: Imagine burying a dead clown in the dirt and then planting some seeds on top. It's worth it, you think, if it helps cultivate your crops.
reading_hint: Saying bye is polite. Never forget to say bye, even in the middle of a cold-blooded clown murder.

meaning_mnemonic: If you try to put your <radical>soul</radical> into <radical>every</radical>thing, you'll <kanji>regret</kanji> it. It's not so much that putting your soul into things is bad, but every single thing? You'll burn out immediately. You won't be able to be good at any of those things. And you'll regret all the time you wasted trying to be the best at everything.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>regret</kanji> is too strong for you to ignore, so you decide to drop everything but <reading>kay</reading>aking (かい). If there's one thing you refuse to regret, it's your bright orange kayak and all the friends you made at kayaking club.
meaning_hint: Imagine if you went full force into everything you've ever even thought of doing. Imagine how burnt out your soul would be. Imagine all the regret you'd have that you didn't just stick to one thing and do it well.
reading_hint: Throw everything you regret away. Only kayak from now on.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> has a lot of <radical>power</radical> that is <radical>power</radical> it gets from the <radical>power</radical> of every <kanji>armpit</kanji> of every human on earth! Why does your armpit smell? It’s because the moon is draining the power from your armpit. The moon saps power from your armpit and that power is sucked up into the sky where the moon collects the power for its own devices.
reading_mnemonic: This is a pretty <reading>wacky</reading> (わき) <kanji>armpit</kanji> image isn’t it? Seeing lines of power traveling from your armpit to the moon. Definitely wacky. Have you ever seen something this wacky in real life?
meaning_hint: Imagine an aura of stinky power traveling from your armpit to the moon. Feel the power leaving you as the moon saps your power through your armpits.
reading_hint: Imagine this whole scene again but keep in mind how wacky it all really is. The moon sapping power from you through your armpits? So wacky.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>scooter</radical> with <radical>horns</radical> with a <radical>pig</radical> riding on it will be able to <kanji>accomplish</kanji> anything.
reading_mnemonic: It was even able to successfully <kanji>accomplish</kanji> a feat that involved it jumping its scooter right into a <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい), swinging back and forth, then jumping off.
meaning_hint: Imagine the pig scooting around. It is able to accomplish anything you can imagine (so go nuts with your imagination).
reading_hint: Perhaps it even takes the swing all the way around in a full 360 degree rotation. This horned scooter riding pig can truly accomplish anything.

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>king</radical> gives you a <radical>knife</radical> you become part of his <kanji>squad</kanji>. Every king has a knife squad or two protecting him and making sure his subjects keep in line.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>squad</kanji> is led by <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo, so it's basically a 50/50 shot that the king is gonna die.
meaning_hint: Imagine if you were given a knife and allowed into the king's squad. Would you let the power corrupt you?
reading_hint: I'm not saying Han Solo is a bad squad leader, but his track record isn't the greatest. I mean, best case scenario the king gets stabbed in the arm and they manage to win.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>surplus</radical> will cover all the <radical>dirt</radical> with <kanji>paint</kanji>. The surplus of tsunamis will strike the paint factories first (everyone knows paint factories are built close to the ocean). Then the paint will flow over all the dirt and paint it a ton of rainbow colors.
reading_mnemonic: You touch the <kanji>paint</kanji> in the dirt with your <reading>toe</reading> (と) to see if it's still wet. It is. The paint is still very wet and now your toe looks like a tiny rainbow.
meaning_hint: Picture all of the different colors of paint being swept along by tsunami after tsunami, painting the dirt as it goes.
reading_hint: Imagine dipping each of your toes into the paint-dirt, one by one, to paint each of them a different color. Why are you doing this?

meaning_mnemonic: You have a <radical>surplus</radical> of <radical>ladle</radical>s laid out <kanji>diagonally</kanji> in front of you. Why are they diagonal? And why do you have so many ladles? Why the surplus? Hang on, we'll get there.
reading_mnemonic: In front of this <kanji>diagonal</kanji> pattern is a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ) who is currently staring at you. You slowly reach down and turn one of the ladles around, diagonally, and look back up. The shaman's eyes are wide, and now they're scribbling on in a notebook.
meaning_hint: Look at all these ladles lying diagonally in front of you. Pick them up. Feel the wood grain. Wonder why you have so many.
reading_hint: Uh oh, is this one of those weird tests where they find out if you're the chosen one or some old scholar reincarnated? Who knew laying ladles at diagonal angles could reveal this kinda stuff?!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>district</radical> is full of <radical>weapons</radical>, but everyone prefers to <kanji>punch</kanji> each other. When everyone's armed, things can escalate quickly, so it's better to stick to fists to be safe.
reading_mnemonic: The district residents <kanji>punch</kanji> each other in front of the <reading>王</reading> (おう). These punching matches are actually gladiatorial battles for the 王's entertainment.
meaning_hint: Picture two heavily armed residents of your district punching the living crap out of each other. They're both armed to the teeth with missile launchers, flamethrowers, katanas, lightsabers, etc., but neither one wants to be the first to draw a weapon and escalate the situation.
reading_hint: Imagine the heavily armed combatants from earlier slugging it out, but this time the 王 is watching them from a box seat above the action. He gleefully encourages them, a maniacal, bloodthirsty glint in his eye.

meaning_mnemonic: On a <radical>cliff</radical>, the <radical>pope</radical> is holding a <kanji>shield</kanji>. The pope is near the edge of the cliff and is using the shield to fend off demons. The demons want to shove the pope off the cliff, but the pope is using his holy shield of popery to fend them off.
reading_mnemonic: The light of the pope's <kanji>shield</kanji> begins to fade. The shield's holy power is waning! Just as the demons are about to push him off the cliff, the sun begins to rise. That's right! It's <reading>June</reading> (じゅん) and summer has just begun. The pope's shield reflects the power of June's bright sun into the eyes of the demons, casting them off the cliff behind him and back into hell!
meaning_hint: Imagine the shield glowing brightly as hymns play in the background. The demons try to get close to the pope to shove him off the cliff but the holy shield is too bright and popey.
reading_hint: This pope would have been in a lot of trouble if June's summer sun hadn't risen at just the right moment. His shield would have become useless and he might have been shoved off the cliff if it was a less sunny month.

meaning_mnemonic: You need to feed this <radical>grain</radical> to a <radical>flower</radical> wearing <radical>turkey</radical> on a <radical>stool</radical> so you start the <kanji>harvest</kanji>. Grains don't pick themselves, you have to harvest them.
reading_mnemonic: You're just starting to <kanji>harvest</kanji> the grains when an angry <reading>cock</reading> (かく) shows up! He's mad because these are his grains and you're about to harvest them and feed them to a turkey instead of him.
meaning_hint: Picture this fat turkey with flowers on its head, sitting on a stool. It's gobbling for grains and you need to be the one who harvests them.
reading_hint: Imagine an angry cock chasing you around as you try to harvest grain. He keeps pecking you, ouch!

meaning_mnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>towel</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>towel</kanji> as well.
reading_mnemonic: You know who loves <kanji>towel</kanji>s? The <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. He loves towels so much that he went around and put きん on every towel in the land, so you think of him whenever you use one.
meaning_hint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Go into your bathroom or your linen closet right now. Look at your towels. Look for the small きん hidden there.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>horse</radical> who can write a <radical>paragraph</radical> gets to be a <kanji>chess piece</kanji>. Only the most erudite of horses gets selected to be a chess piece. The knight’s chess piece is represented by a horse, and for a horse to earn this honor, they must first write a paragraph explaining why they deserve the honor.
reading_mnemonic: Upon being told that he will never be a <kanji>chess piece</kanji>, the horse slips into a <reading>coma</reading> (こま). The horse will never awaken from this coma as he knows he will never fulfill his dream of becoming a chess piece, so he has nothing to live for.
meaning_hint: Imagine a horse trying to write a paragraph about why it deserves to be a chess piece. See the horse struggle with his hooves to write out a paragraph as he neighs in nervous frustration.
reading_hint: See this horse in its coma. You feel bad that it will never be able to be a chess piece, but this is a bit dramatic.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>stop</radical> a <radical>spoon</radical> from moving by tying it with <radical>thread</radical> that's <kanji>purple</kanji>. The spoon wouldn't stop moving around, so you had to use your favorite purple thread to make it stop. It's the strongest thread you have.
reading_mnemonic: To remember this reading, think about how the village you previously went to was all <kanji>purple</kanji>. It was the "<reading>村先</reading>" (むらさき) that you went to.
meaning_hint: Imagine tying this spoon down with thread that's a color other than purple. Ah, the thread snaps. Darn, you better use your extra strong purple thread (the best color for strength!).
reading_hint: Imagine that everything is in purple in this village. Cars, houses, trees. Absolutely everything was purple.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> give you a good <radical>reason</radical> to <kanji>extract</kanji> things. You extract juice from fruits, oil from seeds, and coffee from beans. Why? Because you have fingers! That's your reason.
reading_mnemonic: Your magic fingers can even extract the essence out of living things, so you decide to <kanji>extract</kanji> some from <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう). You always thought a nice Chewbacca extract could take your cookies to the next level. It'll help offset the sweetness and make them extra chewy (or chewbacca-y!).
meaning_hint: Imagine having fingers that work like a magic wand of extraction. With a flick of your fingers, you could extract any radical out of a kanji too. How could these special fingers not be a reason for you to extract? With these fingers, you can't stop extracting!
reading_hint: Taste that chewy goodness in every bite. All you need is a dash of Chewbacca extract.

meaning_mnemonic: With your <radical>fingers</radical> under an <radical>axe</radical>, you <radical>say</radical> a <kanji>vow</kanji>. A vow is a sacred promise, so you have to be really serious when you say one. That's why you do it with an axe hovering above your fingers—it's a reminder that if you break this vow your fingers are gonna get chopped off.
reading_mnemonic: When you finish your <kanji>vow</kanji>, you take out your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) and cut the axe in half! You know you're never going to break your vow, and if you do, you'll use your own saber to cut off your fingers.
meaning_hint: Picture the axe hovering above your fingers. Now say the vow. But don't lie. The axe knows when you're lying.
reading_hint: Imagine what it would be like to slice off your own fingers with the saber you've carried for so long. You better not break that vow.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> connects to <radical>five</radical> different <radical>mouths</radical>, allowing you to <kanji>realize</kanji> something. You can realize profound truths about the world with these five mouths speaking to your soul.
reading_mnemonic: Right now those five mouths are helping you to <kanji>realize</kanji> something about <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご). First you hear Godzilla, then you see him, then you smell him, then you touch him. Finally you taste Godzilla. Now you've realized exactly what kind of thing he is. You truly know Godzilla.
meaning_hint: Maybe the five mouths are your five senses, "speaking" information to your soul so you can realize and perceive things about the world. Whoa, dude…
reading_hint: You now realize why Godzilla destroys all those buildings: he's warning the Japanese people about the use of new technology and warfare and experimentation. He just wants everyone to realize what the consequences of these new things could be!

meaning_mnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> and a <radical>stone</radical> to begin <kanji>cultivation</kanji> of the land. You use your fingers to till the soil, the stone to flatten the ground, and you carefully poke the seeds into the dirt and then use the stone to smash them further down.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Cultivation</kanji> is suddenly halted because a <reading>tack</reading> (たく) sticks into your finger. You pull the tack out, throw it away and start cultivation again when... ouch! There's another tack. This field is full of tacks!
meaning_hint: Imagine being in a field, using your fingers and a stone to cultivate the whole thing, by hand.
reading_hint: Now cultivation is going to involve getting all the tacks out of here, preferably with the stone and not your fingers, so they don't get stuck anymore.

meaning_mnemonic: You run your <radical>fingers</radical> along the words of a  <radical>paragraph</radical>. You are learning about the law because you plan to <kanji>arrest</kanji> somebody.
reading_mnemonic: Who do you want to <kanji>arrest</kanji>? You want to arrest <reading>こう</reading>いち. Even though he's started wars, created a religion, and trapped a child in a cavity, it's going to be tricky to arrest him because he's the emperor now.
meaning_hint: Imagine reading the paragraph very slowly. You find your answer. It looks like you'll be able to arrest that person...
reading_hint: Still, according to this paragraph you're running your fingers through, you can do it. You can arrest こういち.

meaning_mnemonic: There’s a <radical>stone</radical>, a <radical>tree</radical>, another <radical>tree</radical>, and a <radical>coat rack</radical>. That stone and two trees will make the perfect <kanji>foundation</kanji>, or <kanji>cornerstone</kanji>, for a house to put that coat rack in! This stone is flat and heavy and both of the trees are flexible and durable.
reading_mnemonic: After you finish the <kanji>foundation</kanji>, the only step that's left is to wash the entire thing with <reading>so</reading>ap (そ).
meaning_hint: Imagine making a foundation with the stone and the two trees so you’ll have a place for your coat rack. Feel the wood grain and cold stone against your skin as you move them around to form the foundation.
reading_hint: Imagine getting on your hands and knees, rubbing a bar of soap all over the foundation you built. The soap gets into all the nooks and crannies in the wood and stone. Now if a coat falls off the coat rack onto the floor, you just add water and poof! Clean!

meaning_mnemonic: On the <radical>roof</radical> there's some type of <radical>turkey</radical> <radical>bird</radical>, but thinner and longer. You look closer and realize it's a <kanji>crane</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>crane</kanji> leans down toward you from its roof perch and squawks, "<reading>Too ru</reading>de (つる)!" You're shocked! You didn't know the crane could speak English, but even worse, it knew you called it a turkey bird.
meaning_hint: Picture the beautiful black and white crane dancing gracefully on the roof. Now that you know what it is, you feel a little bad about calling it a turkey bird.
reading_hint: The crane keeps saying you're too rude whenever other people come close so they know what kind of person you are. Yikes, you really shouldn't have gotten on this crane's bad side.

meaning_mnemonic: Take your greatest <radical>treasure</radical>, hold a <radical>knife</radical> nice and close to it, and <kanji>prune</kanji> away all the weak and dead bits.
reading_mnemonic: Currently, you want to <kanji>prune</kanji> your greatest treasure: your <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Your car is made of wood, and it keeps trying to grow new buds and branches, so you have to prune them off.
meaning_hint: All treasures need to be taken care of, no matter what they are. Prune away anything that isn't necessary.
reading_hint: You want your car to be small and sleek, not a giant truck, so you have to carefully prune it when it grows too much.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>hill</radical> <radical>knife</radical> is the most <kanji>sturdy</kanji> knife you can find.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>sturdy</kanji> (but strangely shaped) knife is used by Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち.
meaning_hint: The reason hill knives are so sturdy is because they are shaped like hills. Very wide, very thick, not so long. So, they don't break and are more sturdy.
reading_hint: Imagine Grandpa ごういち rolling the knife back and forth over various things to cut them. "I love my sturdy hill knife," he says.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> of that <radical>turkey</radical> is <kanji>solely</kanji> his. It’s very unique, so that mouth could belong to no other turkey but him. That mouth is solely his.
reading_mnemonic: You traveled a long way to see this turkey and the mouth that is <kanji>solely</kanji> his but you missed your exit on the highway and you have to hang a <reading>U-ey</reading> (ゆい). You know what hang a U-ey means, right? It’s making a U-turn. Well, you have to hang a U-ey on the highway so you can get off at the right spot to see this turkey and the mouth that is solely his.
meaning_hint: Imagine what makes this turkey’s mouth so unique. This beak is solely his. It belongs to no other. This turkey is the only turkey with a mouth like his.
reading_hint: Imagine hanging a U-ey on the highway. Hear your tires screech as you turn around and get off where you’re supposed to.

meaning_mnemonic: On the <radical>dirt</radical> there is a <radical>lid</radical> trying to cover the number of <radical>times</radical> the <radical>dawn</radical> has risen here. You walk over and pull the lid off and the whole thing transforms into a <kanji>podium</kanji>. A podium for the dawn to tell you how many times it's risen over this dirt over and over again. It just keeps going and you realize the lid was here because this dang podium can't seem to keep a lid on it! (Get it? Figure of speech!)
reading_mnemonic: You listen to what the <kanji>podium</kanji> has to say for so long you finally can't help but ask, "Who are you?" "I'm the <reading>dawn</reading> (だん), of course!" And this is my podium where I spread the good news about the dawn!
meaning_hint: This kanji also just... really looks like a podium right? With a guy wearing a graduation cap, pointing toward you, in the crowd.
reading_hint: I guess that makes sense. This podium is by the dawn for the dawn.

meaning_mnemonic: You give a <radical>flag</radical> and a <radical>spoon</radical> to a <kanji>nun</kanji> as an offering. You give her this flag and this spoon because they are very precious to you and you want to show the nun how serious you are about repenting for your evil deeds.
reading_mnemonic: You’re getting down on one <reading>knee</reading> (に) as you present the flag and the spoon to the <kanji>nun</kanji>. The nun accepts your offer and takes your flag and spoon from you. Your soul is saved!
meaning_hint: Imagine handing the nun your country’s flag. And also a spoon that is very valuable to you. Imagine getting down on one knee as you do so.
reading_hint: Feel your knee digging into the cold floor as you present your offerings to the nun. You feel slightly sad as she takes your precious belongings from you.

meaning_mnemonic: Bite a <radical>tree</radical>'s <radical>root</radical> with your <radical>fang</radical> to get an <kanji>approximation</kanji> on its age. It won't be exact, this isn't scientific or anything, but it will be pretty close.
reading_mnemonic: As you're recording your <kanji>approximation</kanji> down,  somebody comes up and shouts, “Hey <reading>guy</reading> (がい)! I’ll give you $10,000 for that tree there, guy!” This guy must be crazy. That’s much more than your approximation of the value of this thing. But you sell it to the guy anyway.
meaning_hint: Once you have an approximation on the age, you'll be able to come up with an approximation on how much it's worth too.
reading_hint: The guy is trying to look at your approximation now, while calling YOU "guy" over and over, and you just want him to buy it and leave you alone!

meaning_mnemonic: In the event of a <radical>tsunami</radical>, <radical>Wolverine</radical>, your <radical>forehead</radical>, and a <radical>stool</radical> will all be <kanji>immersed</kanji> in it. You will become immersed in the tsunami after it hits because tsunamis are gigantic and bring a lot of water. Now you and your forehead are immersed in the tsunami along with Wolverine and a stool.
reading_mnemonic: Still <kanji>immersed</kanji>, the stool floats over and hits you in the <reading>shin</reading> (しん). The tsunami gave the stool a good push and while it was fully immersed, it ran into your shin. It really hurts.
meaning_hint: Feel the water all around you as you become immersed in this tsunami. See Wolverine trying to swim his way to the top as you watch the stool floating about in the water.
reading_hint: Hear the stool crack against your shin and feel the pain erupt through your leg. You try to scream but you’re under water so you just get a big mouthful of tsunami.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> and a couple <radical>fire</radical>s run into each other. They cancel each other out completely, and the remainder left behind is a <kanji>faint</kanji> remnant of what was once there.
reading_mnemonic: Just like the tsunami and fire canceling each other out, leaving a <kanji>faint</kanji> remainder of what was, your <reading>tan</reading> (たん) is extremely faint as well.
meaning_hint: You go to see if you can find any remainder of the fire + tsunami battle. You can, but it's so faint.
reading_hint: Look at your skin. It has such a faint tint.

meaning_mnemonic: If <radical>someone</radical> wants to <radical>boil</radical>, you let them <kanji>boil</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: Welp, the pot is at a rolling <kanji>boil</kanji> and your <reading>knee</reading> (に) is in there. The person wasn't saying they wanted to boil, they said they wanted to boil someone. And that meant you. Well, your knee anyway.
meaning_hint: There's someone in your face yelling about how they want to boil! You yell back, "Okay, if you want to boil, just boil!"
reading_hint: Once your knee is done boiling, the person kneels down in front of you and presents it on a platter, mocking you, since you can't kneel anymore. You should pay more attention to what's going on from now on or you could find yourself in... HOT WATER. Ohohoho.

meaning_mnemonic:
If a <radical>helicopter</radical> <radical>loiter</radical>s near a <radical>black hole</radical> then it's going to <kanji>capsize</kanji>. I mean, yeah, it's not a boat, but just look, that's what's happening! That helicopter is going down!

If you want to save it you better <kanji>cover</kanji> up the black hole! The only way to save it from capsizing is to cover it right up!
reading_mnemonic: You're doing your best to <kanji>cover</kanji> the black hole to stop the helicopter from <kanji>capsiz</kanji>ing but it's not working! “<reading>F**k</reading> you (ふく) black hole!" you shout. You just want to cover it up! You just want to do the right thing!
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to cover up a black hole so that the helicopter doesn't capsize. It's one or the other, dude. Cover or capsize. You decide.
reading_hint: Man, f**k you you, black hole. F**k you you. Making that helicopter capsize and then refusing to be covered?

meaning_mnemonic: Watch what you <radical>say</radical> to what appears to be a <radical>sweet</radical> <radical>tree</radical>, because that tree will 100% <kanji>conspire</kanji> against you.
reading_mnemonic: Now you're getting it! That tree is trying to <kanji>conspire</kanji> against you and take you down. Take your <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball and bowl it and all its friends down!
meaning_hint: Trees may look all sweet and innocent, like they aren't listening to every word you say, but it's a front! They're conspiring against you!! They'll blackmail you and take everything!!!
reading_hint:
Watch as the conspiratorial tree and all it's gossiping friends are bowled over by your bowling ball. Phew. You're safe from the trees.

For now.

meaning_mnemonic: The only type of <radical>can</radical> you'll be <radical>building</radical> in <radical>prison</radical> is one made of <kanji>pottery</kanji>. Do you honestly think they would let you have anything made of metal or plastic? No, too dangerous. You have to make all of your cans and plates and underpants the old-fashioned pottery way.
reading_mnemonic: You get so good at <kanji>pottery</kanji> that the guards start to notice and offer to sell what you make in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. とうきょう is a big pottery town, after all. You'll make a fortune! Well... the guards will at least.
meaning_hint: Picture the first piece of pottery you ever made. Feel it in your hands. At least you've learned something new while you've been stuck in prison.
reading_hint: Selling your prison pottery in とうきょう might even help you get out faster. Yippee!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>building</radical> that has a <radical>bully</radical> will <kanji>isolate</kanji> itself. Buildings have feelings like people, you know.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>isolate</kanji>d building only has one companion, and it's the <reading>cock</reading> (かく) that lives inside of it.
meaning_hint: Picture a single building, isolated from the rest of the city and those mean, bully buildings that live there.
reading_hint: Picture the sun rising behind an isolated building. A cock lets out a cry from the roof. It's a sad and lonely scene.

meaning_mnemonic: If someone <radical>loiter</radical>s it's <radical>correct</radical> to <kanji>subjugate</kanji> them.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>subjugate</kanji> these loiterers with your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい)! It's the correct thing to do, after all, and you were given this saber all those levels ago because you did the correct thing.
meaning_hint: You can't just let people do whatever they want. If it isn't correct to loiter here, then it IS correct to subjugate them, and force them to do what you want. You control them now. And they're gonna get it!
reading_hint: As you swing your saber around, subjugating the loiterers, and making them do as you say, you remind yourself that this is correct. And if it's correct, you have to use your saber to enforce it.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Compare</radical> a <radical>building</radical> to some <radical>dirt</radical> and you'll see the difference between you and His and Her <kanji>Highness</kanji>. You live outside and sleep on the dirt. But His and Her Highness both live in a huge building.
reading_mnemonic: I bet His <kanji>Highness</kanji> has never had to sleep in <reading>hay</reading> (へい) either, like you do every night. Hay is what you use as a pillow, mattress, blanket. Basically hay and dirt are all you have. Unlike His Highness.
meaning_hint: Picture Her Highness, high up in her building. Now compare her home and life to yours, you dirt person.
reading_hint: Picture His and Her Highness coming out of their huge building and walking by you in your hay pile. "My goodness, Highness, have you ever seen such a dirty, fluffy creature?" "My no, dear, let's move quickly to remove it from our sight!"

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of this <radical>valley</radical> is very <kanji>vulgar</kanji>. The name of the valley is Vulgar Valley, so it makes sense for its leader to be very vulgar.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>vulgar</kanji> leader leads his Boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang through Vulgar Valley, patrolling the area and spreading vulgarity as he goes.
meaning_hint: Imagine meeting the leader of Vulgar Valley. Imagine all the vulgar things he says to prove he is worthy of being the leader of Vulgar Valley. He is so very, very vulgar.
reading_hint: Hear the motorcycles rev and purr as they carouse through the Vulgar Valley. Hear the leader screaming vulgar things as he and his Bosozoku bike gang ride all around.

meaning_mnemonic: You take a <radical>stool</radical>, another <radical>stool</radical>, and yet another <radical>stool</radical> and stack them on top of a <radical>tree</radical> to make a <kanji>mulberry</kanji> bush. This is how mulberry bushes are made. You take three stools and you put them atop a tree.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>mulberry</kanji> tree then produces a <reading>qua</reading>ntity (くわ) of mulberries. Yum.
meaning_hint: Imagine putting three stools on top of a tree and watching it transform into a mulberry. The science of nature!
reading_hint: Imagine the huge quantity of mulberries growing from your stool tree. Imagine picking them all. What an enormous quantity of mulberries you now have.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> at the <radical>gate</radical> of the <radical>king</radical> makes sure all of his plants get <kanji>watered</kanji>. The gate prevents the tsunami from damaging anything inside the king’s home, and the plants around the gate get watered by the water of the tsunami.
reading_mnemonic: These plants are getting <kanji>watered</kanji> in <reading>June</reading> (じゅん), the month they need watering the most. June is a hot month, so it’s good that this tsunami came and got the king’s plants watered when it did.
meaning_hint: Hear the tsunami crash into the gate as you see the water slosh around and make sure that all of the king’s plants get good and watered.
reading_hint: Again, watch and hear the tsunami crashing into the gate and getting the plants watered. This time, remember that the month is June and it is very hot and sticky outside. You’re sweating and very hot.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> takes his <radical>slide</radical> into his private <radical>jet</radical> and is given a <kanji>pearl</kanji> as a gift, by you! He's letting you ride in his jet today, and you were told the king should be presented with a pearl as soon as he boards.
reading_mnemonic: The king takes the <kanji>pearl</kanji> and tosses it into a <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ) next to the slide he took to get into the jet. You watch as the pearl goes down, down, down until it lands into a huge vat of pearls.
meaning_hint: Imagine giving this gorgeous white pearl to the king after he slides down his slide and boards the jet. See his king face light up as he sees the pretty pearl.
reading_hint: Oh. I guess the king didn't need your pearl, since he has a pearl chute to get them off his jet before takeoff. You feel silly. And you kind of want to jump into the pearl chute yourself.

meaning_mnemonic: You take a <radical>lid</radical>, and aiming it at the <radical>sun</radical> you <radical>kick</radical> it into the air and eventually it begins to <kanji>decline</kanji> and crashes into the sun, causing it to supernova. Imagine kicking this lid so hard that it flies into space. See it fly up to the peak of its arch, then watch as it begins the decline until it finally collides with the sun.
reading_mnemonic: As the lid begins to <kanji>decline</kanji> in the sky, you realize what’s going to happen so you attempt to <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい) to safety. Swing on some vines in the direction opposite of the sun. You know that once that lid finishes its decline and collides with the sun, there’s gonna be trouble.
meaning_hint: As you watch the lid decline, keep in mind that the sun radical has its middle line extending outside the box on both sides. This is because the sun is going supernova. Pew!
reading_hint: Imagine swinging on these vines as you hear the sun explode and go supernova behind you. Swing faster! Swing faster!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>commander</radical> who's <radical>big</radical> will use his size to <kanji>encourage</kanji> his troops.
reading_mnemonic: He <kanji>encourage</kanji>s the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) too! You've never seen the Shougun express any emotion other than intense indifference or intense dislike.
meaning_hint: "Look at me! You can be a big commander someday too!" he shouts, encouraging everyone.
reading_hint: Picture the encouraged Shougun: his eyes are shining with tears, his heart is pounding, he's been encouraged to be better!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>few</radical> members of society without <radical>power</radical> suffer from severe <kanji>inferiority</kanji> complexes. Most people in this world have lots of power, so the few that do not feel quite inferior. It is this feeling of inferiority that fuels their inferiority complexes.
reading_mnemonic: Since you suffer from such extreme feelings of <kanji>inferiority</kanji>, when someone offers you and your friends the chance to gain power, you yell “<reading>Let’s</reading> (れつ) go!” without a second thought. “Let’s go, guys! Come on! Power awaits us!”
meaning_hint: Imagine you are one of the few without power. Feel the inferiority crushing down on you. It feels terrible and there is nothing you can do about it.
reading_hint: Let’s go, let’s go! Time’s a wastin’! I’m tired of this inferiority and want to gain some power!

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>crab trap</radical> has the <radical>power</radical> of <kanji>intuition</kanji>. By the power of intuition, this crab trap can trap like, every crab. Through intuition, it knows where the crabs will go and how they will react, so it is much easier to trap them.
reading_mnemonic: This crab trap's <kanji>intuition</kanji> powers were extracted from Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) himself. And as we all know by now, Genghis Khan was the absolute master of intuition, he always knew what his enemies and his allies were about to do.
meaning_hint: Look at this crab trap and see it visibly glowing with intuition. Its powers of intuition are so strong that you can actually see it shimmering with intuition as it expertly traps all those crabs.
reading_hint: Genghis Khan probably has the strongest intuition of anyone in history. This crab trap is getting close though.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>woman</radical> one has to <radical>oneself</radical> is one's <kanji>royal consort</kanji>… You know, assuming one is a member of the royalty. This is a woman who's tied the knot with some royal person and been made into a <kanji>princess</kanji> or <kanji>queen</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You can tell someone's a <kanji>royal consort</kanji> by the <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ) she radiates. Royal consorts are all smoking hot… literally, because they have to wear bulky, elaborate dresses that were designed several centuries ago. Those things trap heat like nobody's business.
meaning_hint: Even if you're royalty, you can't expect to have a woman all to yourself without popping the question. Put a ring on it and make that lady your royal consort!
reading_hint: Imagine standing next to a royal consort. She and her fancy, voluminous 17th-century dress are radiating so much heat that even you're starting to sweat.

meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>height</radical> radical and the <kanji>height</kanji> kanji are the same!

That said, there's a secondary meaning to this kanji of <kanji>ten feet</kanji>. So, when you think of height, think of that height as being ten feet tall.
reading_mnemonic: When you think of <kanji>height</kanji>, think of our huge farmhand friend, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう). He's a big guy, and he has a lot of height to him. In fact, he's <kanji>ten feet</kanji> tall.
meaning_hint: If you paid attention in radicals school, you'll be able to learn this kanji meaning easily.
reading_hint: Joe really is a big guy. Look up at him, and try to measure him, although ten feet might be a little high for you to reach.

meaning_mnemonic: At the top of the <radical>mountain</radical> is a <radical>signpost</radical> to tell you you've reached the <kanji>summit</kanji>. Literally every mountain that humans have reached the top of has one of these summit signs.
reading_mnemonic: At the <kanji>summit</kanji>, you decide to build your new <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). It's been destroyed so many times, and you're sick of it. Maybe if you build you home at the summit of this mountain it will be safer.
meaning_hint: If that signpost wasn't there how would you know you reached the summit? Like, if you were on top of Everest and there was a bad storm... you'd just topple off the other side. Those summit signposts are important!
reading_hint: Picture your beautiful summit home. You know, George Washington had one of these too. So. That's cool, I guess.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>construction</radical> of an under<radical>ground</radical> <radical>prison</radical> will be done by someone who is <kanji>adept</kanji> at it.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Adept</kanji> at constructing underground prisons, you've caught the eye of Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち, the royal underground prison enthusiast.
meaning_hint: And that adept person is you! You're an expert when it comes to constructing underground prisons, so you've been hired for this job. Good luck!
reading_hint:
With a dignified nod, Emperor こういち entrusts you with the grand project of constructing an underground prison, confident that the adept builder will make prisoners feel right at home in their new subterranean digs.

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>fang</radical> on a <radical>building</radical> shows that it's <kanji>wicked</kanji>. Something wicked probably lives in the building too.
reading_mnemonic: You peek inside the <kanji>wicked</kanji> building and a wicked <reading>ja</reading>guar (じゃ) suddenly pounces at you! You let out a sigh of relief when it's stopped by the window, but then... the window opens?! What a wicked building! It's letting the jaguar get you!
meaning_hint: Not like, wicked cool, but like wicked evil. I see you, New Englanders.
reading_hint: Imagine being chased around the outside of this wicked fanged building as the wicked fanged jaguar tries to eat you. Ahhh!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> that is <radical>fat</radical> is a <kanji>burdensome</kanji> animal.
reading_mnemonic: But you have to be nice to the <kanji>burdensome</kanji> horse because this horse is your <reading>da</reading>d (だ).
meaning_hint: A fat horse. So burdensome. Not to mention the burden on the horse on top of the fatness.
reading_hint: Go visit your dad the burdensome horse because you have to visit your dad. It's weird because you're a human and he's a horse.

meaning_mnemonic: Under a <radical>canopy</radical>, <radical>Wolverine</radical> opens his <radical>mouth</radical> and says, "<kanji>China</kanji>!" Why would he say China? It's all very <kanji>sudden</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>sudden</kanji> urge to shout "<kanji>China</kanji>" also came about because Wolverine woke up in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Outside of his pagoda-shaped canopy, Wolverine noticed the kanji and assumed he was in China.
meaning_hint: Picture Wolverine shouting "China!" in his canopy all of a sudden. Okay, he only said "China" because someone asked him to name a country, and when he looked at his canopy, it reminded him of a pagoda.
reading_hint: In Wolverine's defense, he hasn't been learning Japanese like you have, so we can almost forgive him for his mishap. He could do better though. Pretty bad in this day and age to get China and Tokyo mixed up…

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>Yoga</radical> <radical>King</radical> presides over all <kanji>courts</kanji>: law, imperial, and so forth. In this case, you've met him in the court of law, where he is judge, jury, and executioner.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>court</kanji> of the Yoga King is against you. You have to turn this around, so you start pushing <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots into his tater-tot-hole shaped mouth.
meaning_hint: Picture him sitting above you in the judge's chair in court. He's about to make his judgement.
reading_hint: "I hereby sentence you to..." *ploop* "Ooh, this is really good. I hereby sentence..." *ploop* "Wow, are these tater tots? Court dismissed."

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> <radical>can</radical> be a <radical>tree</radical>, but it can also be a <radical>psychopath</radical>. The tree goes from being a regular tree to being a psychopath. Such mood swings are so rough. Especially when the tree slips to <kanji>gloom</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Then the tree switches from <kanji>gloom</kanji> to being super excited (these moodswings!). The super excited tree starts doing dubstep. <reading>Uts</reading> (うつ) uts uts uts uts ♬.
meaning_hint: Imagine all the mood swings of this tree. Psychopath tree! Also very gloomy sometimes.
reading_hint: Sing along with the moody tree. Gloom to dubstep. Such a big change.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>fish</radical> with two <radical>mouth</radical>s has the number <radical>two</radical> on it because it's in <radical>prison</radical> (that's its prison number). Who put the poor two-mouthed fish in prison? An <kanji>alligator</kanji>. Because alligators hate two-mouthed fish.
reading_mnemonic: The reading is the same as the わに in "WaniKani." Ever heard of it?
meaning_hint: How the alligator wishes it had a second mouth like the fish with two mouths, prisoner number 2! It believes that with another mouth, it would be the most talked-about predator, so it imprisons the two-mouthed fish out of jealousy.
reading_hint: So long as you've ever used WaniKani, you probably know the reading of the word alligator.

meaning_mnemonic: This is an animal with an <radical>angle</radical>d <radical>sword</radical> claw that's filled with <radical>cow</radical> meat but looks like an <radical>insect</radical>. What kind of animal is that? It's a <kanji>crab</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The reading is the same as the <reading>かに</reading> in WaniKani.
meaning_hint: If you think about the different parts you can see why this is a crab. A delicious, delicious crab.
reading_hint: If you've used WaniKani before, you probably know this reading, hopefully. All praise the great allicrab!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>tsunami</radical> uses its <radical>drop</radical>s to carry out its <radical>specialty</radical>, which is turning itself into <kanji>record book</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>record book</kanji> is a <reading>bo</reading>ok (ぼ), when it comes down to it.
meaning_hint: The bamboo tsunami hits the ground and drops of bamboo turn into sheets of paper. The sheets of paper combine to become record books that record what happened here, on this day.
reading_hint: Bamboo can also become <reading>bo</reading> staffs too, if they don't turn into record books.

meaning_mnemonic: You want to write a <radical>chapter</radical> about <radical>hair</radical> in your new book so you have to make sure it's really <kanji>clear</kanji>. Clear enough so that ANYbody can read it.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>clear</kanji> writing got the attention of the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) and now he's at your door once again. Although, this time he's angrier than usual. His hair also looks awful. He took your advice after reading the book and cut his entire ponytail off.
meaning_hint: Your writing is so clear and concise that just about everyone does read it. Well, everyone with hair that is.
reading_hint: Picture the Shougun angrily yelling at you about his hair disaster! The only good thing he can say about your book is that it gave clear directions.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> crashes into the <radical>sun</radical>, which is then caught in a <radical>net</radical> and tied to a <radical>stool</radical>! Wait a second... this doesn't sound realistic, it sounds really <kanji>comic</kanji>! That's because it's from that comic book about a pirate you're always reading!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Comic</kanji>s are <reading>man</reading>ga (まん) in Japan, whether they're comic or serious.
meaning_hint: Imagine reading this issue of your favorite stretchy pirate boy comic. Lumpy uses his long arms as a net, traps the sun and ties it to the stool right as the tsunami hits. What kind of crazy trouble is he getting into this time?!
reading_hint: You've known about Japanese comics or manga for a long time, and now you can read them too. Look how far you've come. Wow, what a journey. I'm just so proud of you, kiddo.

meaning_mnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> on the <radical>street</radical> needs to be <kanji>revise</kanji>d. Your words need to be polished, you can't publish improv. Revise your street word inspiration before you share it again.
reading_mnemonic: You want to <kanji>revise</kanji> the song you wrote about <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots at lunch today. But it needs a lot of revising.
meaning_hint: Think about all the stupid things you said on the streets today. Do you want those exact phrases, mistakes, and mouth sounds following you around forever? Revise it before you write it down!
reading_hint:
"Tater tots, tater tots, tater tots, man.
Bake me a tater tot as fast as you banana."

Well that's bad. Better revise this so it rhymes.

"Tater tots, tater tots, tater tots, man.
Bake me a tater tot as fast as you want."

Nope, still bad. Revise!

"Tater tots, tater tots, tater tots, man.
Bake me a tater tot as fast as you clam."

Yes. It's gold.

meaning_mnemonic: Watch what you <radical>say</radical> <radical>next</radical> from your <radical>mouth</radical> when someone comes to <kanji>consult</kanji> with you.
reading_mnemonic: After they <kanji>consult</kanji> with you, the person decides to buy a <reading>shee</reading>p (し).
meaning_hint: This person wants to consult you about something important, so whatever you say next from your mouth better be really smart-sounding and helpful. Make this person glad they consulted you.
reading_hint: They were on the fence about buying one, which is why they consulted with you. You think sheep are the ultimate safe-haven asset, so you told them not to be sheepish and advised them to invest in one.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>gold</radical> <radical>name</radical> on this piece of wood is the <kanji>inscription</kanji> that shows the artist's signature.
reading_mnemonic: The inscription is a name written on something, right? Remember the kanji for "name"? It is 名, and it shares the reading with this kanji as well, making both names <reading>めい</reading>.
meaning_hint: This inscription refers to a signature or something similar. This is an artisan's signature in the form of an inscription.
reading_hint: If you need to review 名 go ahead and do that. It will help you to remember this kanji.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> is making a <radical>dirt</radical> <radical>cage</radical> in the <radical>sun</radical>. She's using it to trap water, making this a dirt <kanji>dam</kanji>. If you think about it, dams are just dirt cages anyway.
reading_mnemonic: Something about the woman working hard to build this <kanji>dam</kanji> makes her super <reading>sexy</reading> (せき) to you. Maybe it's because the sun is so bright, making her shine, or maybe it's because hard work is attractive. Who knows.
meaning_hint: Picture the woman working tirelessly in the hot sun, moving and shaping the dirt around this river into a dam to make a pond.
reading_hint: If you wanna be sexy maybe you should build a dam too.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>correct</radical> way to block the <radical>sun</radical> with <radical>dirt</radical> is with an <kanji>embankment</kanji>. Build a huge, sloping embankment correctly and the sun won't be able to bother you ever again!
