meaning_mnemonic: Put a <radical>turkey</radical> on <radical>ice</radical> and it will only get <kanji>semi</kanji> frozen. You add more ice, and it stays the same. Only some of it will freeze. What the heck!
reading_mnemonic: Finally you realize it will only <kanji>semi</kanji> freeze because it's <reading>June</reading> (じゅん)! It's too hot out for your turkey to freeze all the way. The ice keeps melting off the top and semi thawing it!
meaning_hint: Imagine covering a turkey in ice and every time you check it, it's only semi frozen. It doesn't matter what you do, it just refuses to freeze all the way through.
reading_hint: The lesson is to never try to freeze something in June. If you do it will only semi freeze.

meaning_mnemonic: There's so much <radical>grain</radical> in your <radical>mouth</radical> you need to use a <radical>knife</radical> to remove it. Only when there is a <kanji>surplus</kanji> can you put that much grain in your mouth.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>surplus</kanji> is thanks to the hard work of folks like <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big farmhand. He worked hard to make sure there was a surplus of grain this year.
meaning_hint: Imagine seeing so much grain that you can stuff your mouth so full you can't swallow or spit it out. You have to use a knife to remove it. After you get all the grain out, comment on the surplus of grain.
reading_hint: Remember how Joe made the soil better? Perhaps this is some of that work paying off. Thanks to that and everything else, there is a great surplus.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>drunkard</radical> <radical>cross</radical>es <radical>thread</radical> across special <radical>spikes</radical> to separate the <kanji>fiber</kanji>s. The best way to separate the fibers of thread is to get a drunkard to do it. If you're too precise it will just snap. You gotta be loose with it! Just flop them onto the spikes and the fibers will come apart by themselves!
reading_mnemonic: You stare at the <kanji>fiber</kanji>s easily coming apart when you realize that this drunkard seems to have a few extra legs... and hair and a tail? This isn't just a drunk guy, it's a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). Maybe separating fibers is really a centaur trick, and that's why normal people can't do it...
meaning_hint: Imagine a drunkard crossing threads onto spikes and pulling the tiny fibers free. You don't think you could do that. Maybe if you got drunk too...
reading_hint: Watch the centaur cross the fibers and separate them with his spikes. How strange.

meaning_mnemonic: If you try to <radical>say</radical> something to the <radical>Death Star</radical>, it would only <kanji>admonish</kanji> you. It was made to destroy planets, after all, so I don't know why you'd expect it to have anything nice to say. It just admonishes you about everything you've ever done wrong until you can't stop crying.
reading_mnemonic: Right now you're being <kanji>admonish</kanji>ed about living in a <reading>yu</reading>rt (ゆ). The Death Star bellows, "What, am I too big and safe for you? I'm easy to move too, you know. You're a disgrace! You should be ashamed!"
meaning_hint: Imagine being admonished by the Death Star. It's hovering above you, giant and round, just yelling at you about everything you've ever messed up. It's really awful being yelled at. But the Death Star doing it is particularly scary and embarrassing.
reading_hint: Imagine sitting in your yurt, the home you love, but you can still hear the admonishments from the Death Star in your ears. You feel... bad.

meaning_mnemonic: If you get a <radical>big</radical> <radical>hair</radical> stuck in your <radical>private</radical> <radical>soul</radical> it will be a <kanji>disaster</kanji>! Have you ever gotten a hair in your eye or your mouth that you just couldn't get out? You cough, your eyes water, it's awful! You can't focus on anything until it's gone. Now imagine if a big 'ol hair got stuck in your own private soul!!! Disaster!!!
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>disaster</kanji> is so huge that even <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん) left his vacation in <reading>Zan</reading>zibar (ざん) to come tell you you're on the naughty list for being such a disaster person. WHAT?! Even you Santa-san?
meaning_hint: This disaster is ruining your life. You can't eat, sleep, all you can do is panic trying to get the hair out.
reading_hint: Santa-san has forsaken you. Your life is a wreck. This is a total disaster just because of one big hair.

meaning_mnemonic: Whether you're a <radical>tiger</radical> or <radical>Wolverine</radical>, the anti-claw regime will want to <kanji>oppress</kanji> you. Too afraid of any potential violent rebellion, this oppressive regime is going to imprison and declaw every single clawed being.
reading_mnemonic: Tigers and other clawed beings like Wolverine are so <kanji>oppress</kanji>ed, they're forced to cover their claws with <reading>Gak</reading> (ぎゃく). You know, that weird silly putty they made us play with as kids? Well, it's now an instrument of anti-Wolverine, anti-tiger oppression.
meaning_hint: Imagine being a tiger or Wolverine and being oppressed just because you have claws. Imagine being stuck in a cage. Not being allowed to vote. It's awful!
reading_hint: Imagine how silly it would look for Wolverine and these animals to have their claws covered in multicolored goo. Gak is such a humiliating way to oppress someone.

meaning_mnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> to take down the <radical>old</radical> <radical>flag</radical> and <kanji>install</kanji> a new one. Flags get old, just like computers and furniture and people. So when one gets too old, you have to get on up there and install a new one in its place.
reading_mnemonic: You've decided to <kanji>install</kanji> a <reading>su</reading>it (す) where the old flag used to be. I mean, a suit is probably more durable than a flag, am I right? This way you won't have to install flags as often.
meaning_hint: Imagine reaching out and grabbing onto the old flag with your fingers. Feel the dirty grime and little holes it's gotten. Now install something new!
reading_hint: Picture a newly installed suit flying in the air on a pole.

meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around a <radical>surplus</radical> of anything, you need to be <kanji>gentle</kanji> and touch everything <kanji>gently</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You have to be <kanji>gentle</kanji> and handle these things <kanji>gently</kanji> because they belong to <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ). You're loitering around her surplus of (whatever you decided to imagine).
meaning_hint: Unfortunately it's not specific as to what kind of surplus you're loitering around, so you'll need to imagine a surplus of something memorable to you. Then, just imagine loitering around there, being really gentle with everything.
reading_hint: Imagine Jo-Anne coming back to her surplus, then asking you if you were gentle with the goods. You were, you treated everything very gently.

meaning_mnemonic: In your <radical>fingers</radical> are <radical>flowers</radical> which really <radical>suit</radical> you. But you throw them to the people waving to you from below, as you <kanji>board</kanji> your ship. The flowers fall, unneeded. You're <kanji>embark</kanji>ing on a new life, a new journey. There will be more flowers where you're going.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>board</kanji> a ship to <reading>とう</reading>きょう, bound for your new life, of course!
meaning_hint: Picture yourself boarding the ship and letting the flowers slip from your fingers, into the crowd of people waving goodbye.
reading_hint: Where else would you go at this point? I mean, come on. You've made it 53 levels since you embarked on your kanji journey with us. We know you by now. You want to be in とうきょう.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> are growing out of the <radical>ground</radical> and over your pet <radical>pig</radical>'s <radical>forehead</radical> and it's causing the poor thing to live in total <kanji>darkness</kanji>. The flowers are hanging down over its eyes and won't let any light in at all. And some of the flowers seem to be growing directly out of the pig's forehead too. The pig has just been wandering and bumping into things all day because of it.
reading_mnemonic: To end the <kanji>darkness</kanji> you <reading>mow</reading> (もう) the flowers. You have to drive your lawnmower over its head too, because they're growing out of its forehead too. They'll grow back soon though and cover your pig's world in darkness once again and you'll have to do it all over again.
meaning_hint: Imagine being a pig with flowers hanging down off your forehead and into your eyes so that darkness was all you knew. It's very sad, actually.
reading_hint: Imagine mowing the darkness away. Be careful! If you mow the pig's eyes, the darkness will last forever!

meaning_mnemonic: If you're in Japan, the <radical>fish</radical> in your <radical>village</radical> are <kanji>carp</kanji>. You may know them as <kanji>koi</kanji>, too.
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>carp</kanji> is called a <reading>koi</reading> (こい) in Japanese. We've used that word before so I hope you know it by now. A koi is a koi, you know?
meaning_hint: Picture your village in Japan. There's a big, still pond full of bright and beautiful giant carp. You hear the village kids giggle as they feed them and their big round mouths pop out of the water.
reading_hint: Carp are koi and koi are carp. Blub blub.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>cross</radical> carrying <radical>drunkard</radical> wants you to go to a <radical>rice paddy</radical> <radical>together</radical> so you can <kanji>receive</kanji> something from them.
reading_mnemonic: Against your better judgement, you decided to go and <kanji>receive</kanji> the thing. You close your eyes, hold out your hands, and feel a silky, light <reading>tie</reading> (たい) being placed in them.
meaning_hint: You really don't want to receive whatever it is this drunkard wants to give you, and it's even sketchier that they want you to go to a rice paddy together to do it.
reading_hint: The tie looks harmless enough, but you still receive it skeptically. The drunkard is just holding up his cross and smiling at you. Hmm.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical>s a <radical>criminal</radical> wears will always be <kanji>scarlet</kanji>. Scarlet is the color of freshly spilt blood, which is why bad guys wear it so much.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>scarlet</kanji> color holds a lot of <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). Scarlet is the color of fire, and it has the heat of fire too.
meaning_hint: Picture a person wearing all scarlet all the time. Don't they give you a criminal-y vibe?
reading_hint: Picture a criminal, covered in this scarlet thread, heat radiating off of him.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> is rising and there is a <radical>net</radical> covering <radical>someone</radical>, who's being forced to watch as <kanji>dawn</kanji> appears. They've never seen dawn before, and they didn't want to, but you're making them.
reading_mnemonic: The someone who saw <kanji>dawn</kanji> for the first time goes on to become sumo wrestling champ <reading>Akebono</reading> Taro (あけぼの). He always gazes at the sunrise at dawn before he wrestles and it fills him with strength.
meaning_hint: Picture someone tied down in a net as dawn breaks.
reading_hint: Look at Akebono looking at you as you look at dawn breaking.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>old</radical> <radical>moon</radical> (the moon we had before the current moon) was actually just a huge <kanji>barbarian</kanji> floating in the sky.
reading_mnemonic: At least, this is the kind of story you told your <reading>子</reading> (こ) when they asked you where the moon came from.
meaning_hint: Just imagine a huge barbarian (whatever that means to you) floating in the sky, looking down. Some ancient people look up and wave at the old moon barbarian.
reading_hint: "You see, my child, the old moon was not like the current moon. It was a giant barbarian in the sky, who would eat children that asked too many questions."

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>melon</radical> radical and the <kanji>melon</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Wahoo!
reading_mnemonic: What's the best use for a <kanji>melon</kanji>? Not eating it, but running over it in your <reading>ca</reading>r (か)! Melons make a nice big splat sound when you drive over them, and that's what you plan to do to every melon you see.
meaning_hint: Make sure you remember this radical means melon and you'll remember the kanji meaning too!
reading_hint: Imagine hitting the gas pedal in your car as you head for a big melon. Watch it burst into a million pieces as your car makes contact, hurling melon bits all over your windshield. It's a melon firework!

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>drop</radical> of <radical>bear</radical> on this <radical>towel</radical>. A true <kanji>commander</kanji> can notice little things like this.
reading_mnemonic: You watch the <kanji>commander</kanji> in all his glory... going back and forth on a <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい).
meaning_hint: Imagine a commander carefully looking over everything. They not only notice a drop of something on a towel, but they're able to identify that the drop is a small piece of liquid bear.
reading_hint: "Weeee!" says the commander.

meaning_mnemonic: If you can find the <radical>door</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> shaped like a <radical>mouth</radical> it will <kanji>enlighten</kanji> you.
reading_mnemonic: The door <kanji>enlight</kanji>ened you by opening up and revealing the <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい) in its door-mouth. "Cake is the key to enlightenment, my child. Now eat it from my mouth."
meaning_hint: Imagine searching for this special door in the cold wind and snow of winter. The search for enlightenment always has something to do with suffering and searching on your own like this.
reading_hint: All you need to do to attain enlightenment is to eat this cake... out of this mouth. Eh. Maybe enlightenment isn't worth it.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> that are thought to be little <radical>tent</radical>s sent from <radical>heaven</radical> are <kanji>hollyhock</kanji>s. They're shaped almost like little teepees and would make the perfect tent for a faerie or a little yokai!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Hollyhock</kanji>s are mostly <reading>あおい</reading> (blue) because they copy the color of the heavens, where they came from.
meaning_hint: Picture a tiny yokai using a hollyhock as a tent. Aww cute!
reading_hint: Imagine yourself in a field of hollyhocks and the あおい of them match the あおい of the heavens! It's like you're standing on a giant flower filled mirror of the sky.

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>horse</radical> sees a <radical>spider</radical> it will start running at a <kanji>speed</kanji> no one ever thought a horse could go! Horses are super afraid of spiders, you see, so if they see one, they'll bolt and reach top speed! That's actually the only way to get a horse to run that fast.
reading_mnemonic: Once people realize that they can increase the <kanji>speed</kanji> of their horses, <reading>soon</reading> (しゅん) everyone will be taking spiders with them to scare their horses into running faster. I say soon because it hasn't quite caught on yet. But if you help to spread the word it can happen even sooner.
meaning_hint: Imagine the eyes of a horse seeing a spider and then running at top speed to get the heck away from it.
reading_hint: Soon the world will be filled with horses running at full speed all thanks to the spider increase of the 2000s! Soon we will be in the future of top speeds!

meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>say</radical> something the same way they say it in the <radical>capital</radical>, everyone in the whole country will be able to <kanji>comprehend</kanji> what you're trying to say. If you try to speak in your hometown's dialect no one will. But everyone can comprehend what you want if you use the capital's dialect to say it.
reading_mnemonic: Now that the townspeople finally <kanji>comprehend</kanji> what you were trying to say, they sell you the <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat you were trying to buy. You were using the wrong word, but りょう is what they needed to hear to understand you, and now you can row your way back home.
meaning_hint: Imagine the confused faces of the people in the countryside as you try to talk to them. Decide to say it the way it's said in the capital and see their faces light up as they comprehend what you're trying to say.
reading_hint: Picture yourself rowing away in your new row boat as the townspeople wave goodbye. They were much nicer once they comprehended what you were asking for. Maybe you'll row your way back here someday.

meaning_mnemonic: You cut the <radical>flowers</radical> off the <radical>grain</radical> with your <radical>knife</radical>. The flowers that fall off are <kanji>jasmine</kanji> flowers.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>jasmine</kanji> is growing out of everything, not just grain! It's also growing out of the <reading>ree</reading>ds (り)!
meaning_hint: It's weird that jasmine flowers are growing out of your grain, but that's why you're cutting them off.
reading_hint: You have a farm that grows grain and reeds. Jasmine flowers are growing out of both of those things, ruining your crops! Perhaps you should become a jasmine tea farmer instead...

meaning_mnemonic: If you travel to the <radical>fish</radical> <radical>capital</radical>, you'll find the leader of all fish, the mighty <kanji>whale</kanji>! Okay, so a whale isn't technically a fish, but that's why it makes such a good leader. You can usually only find whales hanging around near the capital because they really enjoy ruling over all the other fish. They are the biggest, after all.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>whale</kanji> who resides over the capital just happens to be <reading>gay</reading> (げい). You only know this because his husband is a man whale too. Actually, now that you think about it, all the whales you've ever met have been gay.
meaning_hint: Picture the underwater fish capital. Statues of whales line the fishy streets, and all of the fish bow down to the whale ruler's giant body.
reading_hint: Picture the gay whale ruler holding whale hands... fins? with his whale husband. They're so cute.

meaning_mnemonic: Clearing his <radical>flowers</radical> of <radical>icicle</radical>s, the <radical>samurai</radical> returns home to his <kanji>villa</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Once the samurai enters the <kanji>villa</kanji>, he lets his <reading>soul</reading> (そう) go. He's been fighting for long enough and this villa was always where he wanted to go.
meaning_hint: Picture the samurai's villa. It's in the countryside and covered in flowers and icicles. Even though it's spring, it's still cold, so all the new flowers are very cold.
reading_hint: Picture the samurai sitting in a chair in his beautiful villa and breathing his last breath. His soul slowly floats up and out of his still body and dances around the villa, free.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>drunkard</radical> gets his hands on a <radical>plow</radical> and starts <kanji>planting</kanji> right away. Drunk people are surprisingly good at planting, especially when they have machines to help them do it.
reading_mnemonic: He keeps <kanji>planting</kanji> and planting... a little too much. You walk over and realize this drunkard isn't just any guy... he's YOUR <reading>CY</reading>BORG (さい)!
meaning_hint: Picture a drunk guy laughing and plowing a field and planting seeds. He wouldn't do this if he was sober, but drunk people can't help themselves.
reading_hint: Oh my sweet cyborg child! Is this what they've done with you? You're just a drunkard who's only good for planting? Nooooooo!!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>mouth</radical> with a <radical>sword</radical> is threatening to cut off your <radical>fingers</radical> because they've <kanji>kidnap</kanji>ped you! You can't see anything about them, since they're all covered up. All you see is their angry mouth and the sword they keep putting near your fingers.
reading_mnemonic: You've been <kanji>kidnap</kanji>ped and your kidnapper has asked for one thing in exchange for your safe return: a <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい). Kayaks are expensive. And it's way easier for this dude to kidnap you and demand your family buy one and give it to them than for them to work for one.
meaning_hint: Imagine you've been kidnapped. And the person who kidnapped you is going to cut off some of your fingers and mail them to your parents if they don't pay the ransom soon enough. You don't want your fingers to be cut off with a sword! Help!
reading_hint: If your parents don't send in the kayak to your kidnapper soon, you're gonna lose some fingers. Come on guys, just get the kayak already!!!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>forehead</radical> is the <radical>origin</radical> for the <radical>measurement</radical> for your <kanji>crown</kanji>! If you measure somewhere else on your head for a crown it won't fit.
reading_mnemonic: You want your <kanji>crown</kanji> to be just like Genghis <reading>Khan</reading>'s (かん), which was more of a cap. He's your hero though, because he ruled over the most people, so you want yours to match his measurements.
meaning_hint: Imagine someone putting a tape measure around your forehead and taking careful measurements for your crown. They better not mess up, or you'll have them put to death!
reading_hint: Why don't you go look up what Genghis Khan's crown/cap looked like. Then try to imagine how it would look atop your head.

meaning_mnemonic: You're in a really important position, <radical>heavy</radical> with <radical>power</radical>, but you can't carry all of that around with you, so you <radical>boil</radical> it all down to just a few <kanji>merit</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: The only problem with consolidating your <kanji>merit</kanji>s like this is that rac<reading>coon</reading>s (くん) are always trying to steal them. Raccoons really like shiny, small things. Like, say, the physical representation of all your personal and professional merits.
meaning_hint: Most people in places heavy with power, like the scouts, boil down all of their responsibilities and accomplishments this way into merit badges.
reading_hint: This is why you never see a military officer or a girl scout leaving their merit badges out in the open. They pin them right onto their clothes. Otherwise the raccoons would come.

meaning_mnemonic: You took someone's <radical>alcohol</radical> and <radical>drop</radical> <radical>drop</radical> <radical>drop</radical>'d it all into the <radical>river</radical>. Now you have to <kanji>repay</kanji> them. Alcohol costs a lot of money, you know.
reading_mnemonic: In order to <kanji>repay</kanji> them for the alcohol you wasted, you offer to give them your <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). They don't want your shoes though, so you have to bring them to a store and watch them try on a bunch of different pairs so you can repay them that way.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself dropping the alcohol, drop by drop, into the river. Uh oh, the person just caught up to you, so you better repay them.
reading_hint: Imagine waiting as this person tries on like twenty different pairs of shoes. They're getting them for free! Why do they have to be picky?! Just let me repay you already!

meaning_mnemonic: You use <radical>thread</radical> and <radical>writing</radical> to sew our <kanji>family crest</kanji>. You've been sewing your family crest since you were a kid, you could do it today with your eyes closed.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>family crest</kanji> is of a <reading>mon</reading>ster! (もん) And the writing says "Our Family Monster."
meaning_hint: Imagine the thread slowly forming the writing that makes up your family crest. You're making a scarf with your crest on it. It's like a wizarding school house symbol.
reading_hint: What kind of monster is on your family crest? Picture it in your mind right now. Now tweet to us @wanikaniapp because I really want to know what you came up with!!

meaning_mnemonic: At <radical>noon</radical> you <radical>stop</radical> to buy <radical>stamp</radical>s because that's the only time they're being sold at <kanji>wholesale</kanji> price. Everyone's so busy eating and working and being at school, that they miss this awesome deal, but not you. Everyday at noon you stock up on wholesale stamps. Do you send a lot of mail? No, you probably just collect them. But they were cheap, so who cares!
reading_mnemonic: Oh, one more thing. Going at noon isn't enough, you have to say the password to get them at <kanji>wholesale</kanji> price too. The password is "<reading>Oh Roshi</reading>" (おろし). Okay, so maybe that's just the name of the guy there, but it always works when I want to get stamps wholesale so you should do it too!
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself stopping at the empty bank at noon, grabbing a handful of wholesale stamps. So many stamps. So few extra charges.
reading_hint: "Oh Roshi" you say carefully as you hand your stamps over at noon. The guy raises an eyebrow at you, but gives you them wholesale! It worked!

meaning_mnemonic: You see a bunch of <radical>tree</radical>s lined up outside of the <radical>gate</radical> to the <radical>east</radical> and realize they look like perfect <kanji>column</kanji>s. It seems like a building or a castle should be resting above them.
reading_mnemonic: Out of the tree <kanji>column</kanji>s bursts professional wrestler <reading>Ran</reading>dy (らん) Savage! "Oooh I'm talking about columns that I like mmmm oh yeaah. The Macho Man loves columns MMm OOHH YEAAH~!"
meaning_hint: Picture these column trees outside the gate to the east. You end up drawing them in the columns of your graph paper because they fit so well.
reading_hint: Now draw Randy Savage in the columns on your graph paper. He just seems to fit there now.

meaning_mnemonic: Jump on a <radical>scooter</radical> to <radical>excuse</radical> yourself from answering a question and you've successfully <kanji>deviate</kanji>d from that nosey person's plan! You've also been able to <kanji>elude</kanji> them pretty well.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>deviate</kanji> from the plan a little too well, and as you're scooting, what you've done <reading>eats</reading> (いつ) away at you. You feel guilty for eluding them...
meaning_hint: Imagine excusing yourself by scooting away on a scooter. If they want to get your answer, they'll have to chase you.
reading_hint: Welp, it's too late to turn back now. You've already deviated pretty far, and you're not even sure where you are now. You were too busy trying to elude that person that you didn't look where your scooter was taking you.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>triceratops</radical> with a <radical>mustache</radical> will go on talking forever. Every time you think he's done he says, "<kanji>Furthermore</kanji>..." and keeps going. Something about a mustache on a dinosaur makes him kind of a jerk, but he's held in such high <kanji>esteem</kanji> that no one ever interrupts him.
reading_mnemonic: Why don't you just interrupt this mustache triceratops while he's saying <kanji>furthermore</kanji>? Because he's the <reading>Shou</reading>gun's (しょう) pet. If you interrupt him you'll probably be put to death or something. Just let him keep going and hope he runs out of furthermores to talk about.
meaning_hint: Imagine this mustachioed triceratops. He probably has a monocle and a fedora too. Every time you think you can talk or leave he says, "Furthermore" again. Come on guy, how much do you have to say?
reading_hint: "Furthermore!" the mustachioed triceratops points at the Shougun, "He is the best Shougun we'll ever have. And he'll let me talk forever OH OH OH!"

meaning_mnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> comes up the <radical>spikes</radical> of <radical>geoduck</radical>s <kanji>appear</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You watch these spikes <kanji>appear</kanji> and suddenly <reading>Ken</reading> (けん)the samurai also appears next to you. "I DID IT!" he shouts, "I've learned how to appear out of thin air!"
meaning_hint: The spikes don't just come out of the geoduck, they literally appear out of thin air. They didn't even <kanji>exist</kanji> before the sun touched them.
reading_hint: "Thank you sweet geoduck, you have taught me the way of appearing with your spikes."

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Wolverine</radical> puts a <radical>stick</radical> between his <radical>legs</radical> and straight into some <radical>rice</radical>, all while wearing a <kanji>solemn</kanji> expression on his face. He's probably remembering his dad who died from getting his legs stuck in rice. Just leave the poor solemn creature alone.
reading_mnemonic: When you first saw how <kanji>solemn</kanji> Wolverine was after his dad died, it really <reading>shook</reading> (しゅく) you. Now you're used to always seeing him emotionally cold and distant. It might be time to shake that solemnity out of him though.
meaning_hint: Putting a stick in some rice is a ritual that Wolverine practices to mark solemn occasions. Shh, listen up! He's probably about to say something solemn and important about his dad.
reading_hint: Do you think you could shake the solemnness out of someone? Imagine being shooketh so hard, you stop feeling solemn. Wouldn't that be great?

meaning_mnemonic: If you give <radical>Splinter</radical> your <radical>heart</radical> you'll feel pretty <kanji>foolish</kanji> afterwards. His heart belongs to the art of ninjutsu!
reading_mnemonic: Splinter tells you to your face how <kanji>foolish</kanji> you are and you feel your heart turn to <reading>goo</reading> (ぐ). Now you can't even give it to someone else. No one wants goo.
meaning_hint: You were foolish to give your heart away to Splinter. He didn't want it. Also he's a rat, what's wrong with you?
reading_hint: What will you do with your foolish goo heart now?

meaning_mnemonic: Who goes into their <radical>yurt</radical> to <radical>boil</radical> things? The <kanji>common</kanji> people! <kanji>All sorts</kanji> of people do it, it's really common. You don't boil things outside, do you?
reading_mnemonic: Here, the <kanji>common</kanji> people all do this, so just let me <reading>show</reading> (しょ) you how. First you go into your yurt, and… Hey, pay attention and watch what I'm showing you!
meaning_hint: Wait, you're telling me you didn't know it's common to boil things in yurts? Excuse me?
reading_hint: Okay, now that I've shown you what to do, you can fit in with the common people here.

meaning_mnemonic: Whenever you set <radical>foot</radical> in a <radical>bar</radical>, there's always something new to <kanji>practice</kanji>. If you usually drink a lot, you might want to practice moderation for example. Or perhaps you simply want to brush up on your social skills. Either way, plenty of opportunities for practice.
reading_mnemonic: This type of <kanji>practice</kanji> is especially hard for you because you're a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). You only have hooves for feet, and balancing on a bar is going to be a huge hurdle. But your teacher promised if you practiced enough you could make it, and here you are! With your hoofed foot on the bar!
meaning_hint: Are you the kind to practice flirting with the bartender, or are you more the kind to engage in a bit of small talk with the other patrons? Maybe you're just trying to practice patience while the bartender is talking instead of getting your order ready.
reading_hint: Picture your centaur foot on the bar as you practice to become a gymnast. It's going to take years and years of this kind of practice, but you're sure you'll make it all the way up there some day! Never give up!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>Mouth</radical> <radical>King</radical>, who is just one big mouth, has one skill, and that is to <kanji>present</kanji>. That big mouth is always presenting something.
reading_mnemonic: "I will <kanji>present</kanji> to you a presentation about mouths, but I require energy. Please fill my giant mouth body with delicious <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots.
meaning_hint: Imagine the Mouth King giving a presentation about the importance of mouths. His subjects all flatter him by saying, "Please, please, present to us about mouths again! We truuuuuuly love it!"
reading_hint: You pour a bucket of tater tots into his mouth body and he continues his presentation about mouths. "You see, mouths are just orifices..."

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>coat rack</radical> will turn into a <radical>bundle</radical> of sticks if you <kanji>neglect</kanji> it. When you neglect a coat rack, the weight from the coats will pull all of the arms off, leaving it as a <kanji>sparse</kanji> stick.
reading_mnemonic: You go up to the <kanji>neglect</kanji>ed coat rack and try to make things right by cleaning it with <reading>so</reading>ap (そ). But no matter how much you scrub, it won't get clean, the arms won't go back on.
meaning_hint: Picture this sparse, neglected coat rack. All of its arms and the coats that were on it have fallen off and are in a big pile on the ground.
reading_hint: If you neglect something for too long, not even a good soap scrub will bring it back. That goes for humans too. Take care of yourselves. Use soap.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>sick</radical>ness that hits you like an <radical>arrow</radical> will do its gross work <kanji>rapidly</kanji>. It's not a good thing, you're gonna get really sick really fast.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>rapid</kanji> sickness makes your head pound like there's a <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ) yapping and barking nonstop. And it's making you sh*t a lot.
meaning_hint: Picture this rapid sickness spreading through your body like a cartoon arrow ricocheting through your body. Yuck!
reading_hint: Imagine getting sick this rapidly. All of a sudden, there's a shih tzu barking in your head, it hurts! And... oh no, where's the toilet?!

meaning_mnemonic: Whatever you <radical>say</radical> through a <radical>satellite</radical> turns into <kanji>Noh chanting</kanji>. It gets all drawn out and distorted from going through the satellite, which makes it SOUND like Noh chanting at least.
reading_mnemonic: While you're fake <kanji>Noh chanting</kanji> you have to pause every few minutes to take a bite of <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Yogurt is good for the throat! At least you think it is. It's not like you're actually chanting, the satellite is doing all the real work.
meaning_hint: Say whatever you want and send it up to the satellite. Now imagine listening to it when it comes back and hearing beautiful, deep Noh chanting back!
reading_hint: Watch closely the next time you go to a Noh play. See the man Noh chanting? Watch close enough and you'll see him eat a scoop of yogurt every once in a while.

meaning_mnemonic: You cast the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical> in <radical>gold</radical> and attach it to a stick to turn it into a <kanji>sickle</kanji>! It's really big and bulky, but when you swing it fast enough your new sickle cuts lots of wheat! Just don't tell anyone what you did with the famous Mona Lisa painting and you should be able to get away with this!
reading_mnemonic: Now that you've turned the Mona Lisa into a <kanji>sickle</kanji>, you have to change the little description under its name in the Louvre. You draw a giant <reading>comma</reading> (かま) next to "Mona Lisa" and write "THE SICKLE." Then you shove it back on the wall as best you can.
meaning_hint: Imagine wielding your gold Mona Lisa sickle. It's sooo heavy, but it's really shiny and it cuts grass and wheat pretty well!
reading_hint: Hear all the guests say, "Mona Lisa... comma the sickle? WHAT'S HAPPENED HERE?!" As panic ensues. You like your addition and you're very proud.

meaning_mnemonic: If you <radical>announce</radical> that <radical>alcohol</radical> is illegal you're just being <kanji>cruel</kanji>. Honestly, saying no one can have any alcohol at all is totally <kanji>unjust</kanji>. They tried that in the 1920s and it didn't work then either, so cut it out! Let people drink what they want to drink!
reading_mnemonic: After this <kanji>cruel</kanji> announcement is made everyone will turn to the next best thing after alcohol. <reading>Coke</reading> (こく)! It's not a replacement, but if this unjust law won't change, then coke sales are going to go through the roof.
meaning_hint: Imagine if your country's leader announced that there would be no more alcohol. People would be in the streets in seconds calling them cruel and unjust!
reading_hint: Imagine drinking coke while complaining how unjust it is that you can't add rum to it.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>surplus</radical> <radical>stool</radical>, please <kanji>describe</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>describe</kanji> the features of the stool to <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ).
meaning_hint: You have a surplus (extra) stool, and someone wants it. But, they need to know more about it, so you describe it in great detail. Imagine yourself doing just that. Every screw and nubbin is described.
reading_hint: Jo-Anne is really excited about this stool. It's the perfect height for her (remember, she's on the shorter side to distinguish じょ from じょう). She pays attention to you describing the stool while sitting on it and spinning around. Have her ask for you to describe it more.

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to wear a <radical>top hat</radical> you <kanji>also</kanji> have to wear a suit to go with it. You can't just wear a top hat, that just doesn't look right. When you think top hat, think about what you also need with it.
reading_mnemonic: You have a top hat and all the clothes that go with it but you <kanji>also</kanji> need a nice <reading>ca</reading>r (か) too! You can't just get all dressed up and then have no way to get anywhere to show it off.
meaning_hint: Picture all the things you also need. A suit, a tie, a monocle, some shiny shoes. There's also ... something... else. What is it?
reading_hint: Think about all the things you also need when you wear a top hat. The car is probably the most important addition. Otherwise no one can see the top hat in the first place!

meaning_mnemonic: You're <radical>sick</radical> because you put an <radical>arrow</radical> in your <radical>mouth</radical> and tried to eat it. To be honest, that's just really <kanji>stupid</kanji>. You have to be stupid to try to eat an arrow. You deserve to be sick.
reading_mnemonic: The only way to cure this serious case of <kanji>stupid</kanji> is to make you eat <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). Not only will it fix your sick stomach by cushioning all the arrows that you shoved in your stupid mouth, but it should send smart cheese waves to your brain and cure you of that stupid all together.
meaning_hint: How stupid do you have to be to put arrows into your mouth until it makes you sick? Pretty darn stupid, it seems.
reading_hint: Imagine if everyone knew that cheese cures the stupid out of people. Then maybe we would have more cheese and less stupid in the world!

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>mouth</radical> coming out of the top of a <radical>tree</radical> so you go into <kanji>shock</kanji>. Your brain just can't comprehend what's in front of you.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>shock</kanji> you've suffered results in you needing to stay <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう) from now on. You don't get to travel, explore, or even get groceries. You're homebound from now on, buddy.
meaning_hint: A tree... with... a... mouth? No, it can't be... I... a-. Your face warps into a permanent state of shock.
reading_hint: I'd be shocked if you ever left your home again, honestly. But hey, there's always anime.

meaning_mnemonic: After your <radical>wedding</radical> you have a baby and it uses its <radical>mouth</radical> to <kanji>suckle</kanji> from your post-wedding boobs. Yes, babies <kanji>nurse</kanji> from breasts. Get over it, kids.
reading_mnemonic: The baby wants to <kanji>nurse</kanji> all day and the only thing you can seem to do at the same time is <reading>hoe</reading> (ほ) your garden. You hold the baby to your breast with one arm and hoe little rows with the other. It's actually a great workout and a nice way to spend time!
meaning_hint: You waited until your wedding to have your baby and all it seems to do with its mouth is suckle. It doesn't cry, which is nice, but it doesn't laugh or talk either.
reading_hint: This hoe has really helped you get through nursing. You think you'll keep this up even after your baby is weaned! Hoeing the garden is your new favorite hobby!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> managed to find the right <radical>direction</radical> home using marks in the <radical>dirt</radical> in the middle of <radical>winter</radical>. This makes you so <kanji>proud</kanji>! You're proud because this resourceful leader is YOUR resourceful leader.
reading_mnemonic: This leader you're so <kanji>proud</kanji> of is none other than Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち. He's been getting a bit slow over the years, and you really didn't think he'd make it back. But, by gum, he did it.
meaning_hint: Imagine how proud you and your whole country feel as your leader comes trudging in through the snow. He made it home. You're (sniff) so (sob) proud!
reading_hint: Let's be honest here, Grandpa ごういち isn't getting any younger. You really should be proud of him for surviving this long at all.

meaning_mnemonic: When you leave <radical>flowers</radical> on a <radical>tombstone</radical> they look good at first, but after a few days or some harsh weather, there's nothing left but <kanji>stem</kanji>s. That's why you have to replace flowers on tombstones all the time. If you don't it will look like you only left the stems there, and that's pretty disrespectful.
reading_mnemonic: You have an idea that can make these tombstones look better than just having leftover <kanji>stem</kanji>s all over them. You brought <reading>cookie</reading>s (くき)! Cookies don't fall apart in the wind, and everyone loves cookies, alive or dead. You replace the stems with the cookies and everything looks a lot nicer.
meaning_hint: Imagine being in a graveyard looking at all the tombstones and seeing nothing but stems on all of them. All the pretty flowery parts have died and blown away.
reading_hint: Look at all the cookie covered tombstones. Now your bag is full of stems, but you can use them to make more cookies to bring back next time. Don't worry, nobody eats them. Stem cookies are probably poisonous.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>building</radical> with a <radical>lip ring</radical> and it's just so cute you have to <kanji>flatter</kanji> it. "That lip ring looks wonderful on you, Buildingさん," you say.
reading_mnemonic: The building is <kanji>flatter</kanji>ed and you hear it shout, "<reading>Ah</reading> (あ)!" and blush. The entire building turns red.
meaning_hint: Flattering buildings is just as nice as flattering people, as long as they're okay with it.
reading_hint: The "ah" the building made was actually quite loud, and since you've never heard a building speak before, it scared you pretty bad. But at least you know the building was flattered by your compliment (I think.)

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> who usually hits you with a <radical>stick</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> finds it in his <radical>heart</radical> to give you some <kanji>leisure</kanji> time.
reading_mnemonic: You're just about to start your <kanji>leisure</kanji> time when a group of <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) appears with their own sticks. You look back for the leader, and he's wearing a bathrobe, sunglasses, and is drinking some hot cocoa. Wait... this is leisure time for... HIM?! OH NO!
meaning_hint: You fall to your knees, "Oh thank you for not hitting me with your stick and letting me relax!" You haven't had a moment of leisure in ages!
reading_hint: As the leader leisurely watches, the youths smack you with their sticks. What a cruel world! "Enjoy your leisure time," the leader yells over, "the youths hit much softer than I do. Have fun!"

meaning_mnemonic: What does a <radical>tree</radical> make that you'd want to put in your <radical>mouth</radical>? An <kanji>apricot</kanji>! Apricots are also about the size of an open mouth, so they kind of look like mouths hanging down under the trees. You can bite them right off the tree with your own mouth!
reading_mnemonic: After you put the <kanji>apricot</kanji> in your mouth you realize that this tastes really familiar, but not pleasant. It tastes just like the <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん) from the <reading>zoo</reading> (ず) smell. How did that happen? You don't like apricots anymore.
meaning_hint: Picture the mouth sized apricots hanging down off the tree. Imagine using your own mouth to bite them down. Just don't swallow the pit!
reading_hint: Imagine trying to eat the bite sized apricot and spitting it out after the taste of anchovies from the zoo fill you up.

meaning_mnemonic: What kind of <radical>flowers</radical> grow in the <radical>west</radical>? <kanji>Rose madder</kanji> flowers, of course. Don't know what those are? That's okay. Just know their roots are used to make dyes of a <kanji>deep red</kanji> color. Red like the rose in their name, but madder, wilder, deeper… And they grow in the west so they can take in those deep red colors of the setting sun.
reading_mnemonic: As you smash the roots of the <kanji>rose madder</kanji> and stain your fingers a <kanji>deep red</kanji>, a little girl comes up to you and points. She says, "<reading>赤ね</reading> (あかね)?" (That's red, right?) You look down at the rose madder roots and your fingers, and nod. She's right, it is red. Deep red.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself sitting in the west as the red sun sets, smashing those little rose madder roots into a paste to make your deep red dyes. Your fingers turn red from the rose madder too.
reading_hint: Hear the little girl say あかね while pointing at the rose madder and your deep red fingers. She's right. And the way she says it is so darn cute.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Dry</radical> an already <radical>dry</radical> <radical>tree</radical> to create a <kanji>bookmark</kanji>. The more you dry the tree, the flatter it becomes, making it the perfect way to mark your spot in a book without needing to fold a page or write on it.
reading_mnemonic: What are you bookmarking anyway? Look down and see yourself putting one of the tree <kanji>bookmark</kanji>s on a page in your notebook that says "<reading>She owe</reading>s me <reading>ree</reading>ds (しおり)." Oh yeah, you had to make this bookmark in the first place because your friend took your reed bookmarks and didn't give them back.
meaning_hint: To be fair, books are just dry trees in a way, but bookmarks are actually dry, dry trees that have flattened out and turned into chunks.
reading_hint: What does she owe you? Open the page to your bookmark and see she owes reeds!

meaning_mnemonic: When you meet the <radical>leader</radical> <radical>dog</radical> you have to <kanji>bow</kanji> to show your respect. Dogs are pretty great, so the leader of all dogs must be the greatest! You should bow really deeply to this one.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>bow</kanji> as low as you can, but you lose your balance and fall over. "<reading>F**k</reading> you (ふく)!" the leader dog barks. Oh no. You messed up and now the leader dog is super mad. Saying f**k you is a little harsh for falling over though, you think.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself bowing to the leader dog. Leader dog is so cool!
reading_hint: Bow even deeper as the dog leader keeps barking what sounds like f**k you. You're pretty sure that's what he's saying at least... but maybe... maybe it's not.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>reality</radical> of <radical>gold</radical> is that it has the power to <kanji>tranquilize</kanji> anyone who gets their hands on it. Gold may seem cool to have in movies and songs, but in reality, it has the power to mess you up!
reading_mnemonic: You've been <kanji>tranquilize</kanji>d and now drool is running down your <reading>chin</reading> (ちん). It's actually really gross. But you don't care, look at all this gold! No one will care about your chin when you're rich!
meaning_hint: Imagine being tranquilized by the sweet caress of gold coins. You can't even move. You're in a daze or something.
reading_hint: If you let gold tranquilize you, you'll drool all over your own chin. Maybe the chins of other people too, who knows?

meaning_mnemonic: When <radical>spring</radical> is <radical>dry</radical> you need to <kanji>dedicate</kanji> your time and effort somewhere else. You can't plant flowers or vegetables, it's too dry. You can't play outside, your skin will flake. So you need to dedicate yourself to something inside.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to <kanji>dedicate</kanji> yourself to your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). You don't want to be outside in the dry spring air, so you focus on home activities. You can clean the kitchen, build a shelf, or dedicate your time to your home internet system.
meaning_hint: You know how you get. When you can't go outside, you dedicate yourself full force into something else. I mean, you made it to level 55, right? That's some serious dedication. And it probably started because of one particularly dry spring.
reading_hint:
Dedicate yourself to doing things in your home! Haha, who are we kidding, you already did that.

We love you, nerd. ♡

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>hundred</radical> <radical>forehead</radical>s lower themselves to their <radical>heart</radical>s in <radical>winter</radical> in <kanji>grief</kanji>. There were once many of their kind, the forehead peoples, but now there are only a hundred left after this harsh winter took the rest of them. Their grief is immense.
reading_mnemonic: Their <kanji>grief</kanji> intensifies because all of the foreheads they lost belonged to <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう). Dead youths everywhere. Now there's no one left to repopulate the world.
meaning_hint: Imagine if winter was so brutal that there were only one hundred human foreheads left. You lost all of your friends and family. Feel that grief.
reading_hint: Grief for youths is so painful. No one should have to bury their forehead child.

meaning_mnemonic: You decide to carve a <radical>tree</radical> into a giant replica of your big <radical>toe</radical> because you think it will be really <kanji>simple</kanji>. Once you're done though, it looks like a really <kanji>crude</kanji> attempt at a fat, lumpy blob. Maybe carving isn't as simple as you thought.
reading_mnemonic: You're admiring your <kanji>simple</kanji> looking toe tree when a lumberjack runs up and asks who created it. <reading>ぼく</reading>, you say. As in "I" said it, in a manly dude, way. He doesn't seem to believe you, so you say it a few more times. ぼく ぼく ぼく!
meaning_hint: Imagine carving a really simple, crude toe from a tree. It's your first attempt, so I guess you should have known it wouldn't be great.
reading_hint: Who carved this simple toe? ぼく!

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>tree</radical> growing on a <radical>cliff</radical> and it has <radical>ten thousand</radical> <kanji>horse chestnut</kanji>s growing on it. These trees are rare, only grow on cliffs, and they always have exactly ten thousand horse chestnuts on each one.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to take a bite of a <kanji>horse chestnut</kanji> because it looks yummy, and there are ten thousand, so it's not like anyone will notice. You crack it open and take a nice big bite to find out that it tastes like <reading>toe chee</reading>se (とち)! UHG! It's the worst thing you've ever tasted. Like hot, moldy toe cheese! Ewwww!
meaning_hint: Picture this tree, weighed down by it's own horse chestnuts, leaning over the edge of the cliff.
reading_hint: Horse chestnuts taste like nasty toe cheese. Imagine what having one in your mouth would be like.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> <radical>long ago</radical> was very <kanji>frugal</kanji>, which is why you get to have too much in this life!
reading_mnemonic: You know what being <kanji>frugal</kanji> is? <reading>Sexy</reading> (せき). You save money, you use less resources, that's the sexiest thing I can think of.
meaning_hint: Thank you soul, for being frugal so that I can have so much now.
reading_hint: If you want your spouse or S.O. to think you're more sexy, just be more frugal. They'll definitely appreciate it and see you in a different light.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> gets double the <radical>dirt</radical> to build on if he's the most <kanji>excellent</kanji> and most <kanji>skilled</kanji> of all the leaders. You don't think dirt is so great? Well what if I took the dirt you own away from you? Your house is on that dirt. It's mine now. Get outta here.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>excellent</kanji> leader is going to use all this dirt to store her excellent <reading>ca</reading>rs (か)! Excellent people have to have cars in equal or greater than excellent-ness than themselves.
meaning_hint: Imagine being so excellent at leading, that you get double the dirt that everyone else gets.
reading_hint: Imagine your own car. Is it as excellent as you?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>soul</radical> in your <radical>toe</radical> leaves <radical>early</radical>. You <kanji>grieve</kanji> and <kanji>mourn</kanji> for your now-dead toe.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to <kanji>grieve</kanji> in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Your toe always wanted to walk in とうきょう, and now you can at least fulfill its dying wish.
meaning_hint: Look at your toe. It has no soul. It's dark and shriveled, like a mummy's toe. Grieve and watch your tears drip onto it.
reading_hint: Walk through the streets of とうきょう grieving, with your dead toe flopping onto the pavement.

meaning_mnemonic: You want to <radical>slice</radical> what you <radical>say</radical> in half so that people focus on <kanji>the above</kanji> text first. The above is what you want them to see! So slice what you say!
reading_mnemonic: As you focus on <kanji>the above</kanji> words you realize a <reading>guy</reading> (がい) is trying to push the rest of the things you're saying back up with the rest of them. No! The above text is all that matters! Go away guy!
meaning_hint: Imagine actually slicing the words you say in the air. The above section is what you want people to pay attention to, so you slice a big gap between the above and the below words.
reading_hint: That guy doesn't care if the above is all he's supposed to hear. That guy sucks.

meaning_mnemonic: You need to <radical>run</radical> on your <radical>toe</radical>s if you want to <kanji>proceed</kanji> down this road. If you can't run on your toes, well then you're out of luck, and you won't be able to <kanji>move on</kanji> at all. I didn't make this rule, I just enforce it. Now run!
reading_mnemonic: If you find that you can't <kanji>proceed</kanji>, you're a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). Everyone can run on their toes. Don't you know that? No? Then you're an even bigger fool, now get outta here!
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to proceed down the road by running on your toes. No other part of your foot can touch the ground!
reading_hint: Imagine being called a fool when you can't proceed along the road because running on your toes ends up being super hard to do.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>bone</radical>s <radical>have</radical> a <radical>scooter</radical> that moves through the <kanji>bone marrow</kanji> inside them.
reading_mnemonic: If you put your ear really close to a bone with <kanji>marrow</kanji> still in it, you'll hear the scooters going <reading>zuwee</reading> (ずい) as they move the blood around inside there.
meaning_hint: If you think about it, bone marrow is really just a road for tiny blood scooters.
reading_hint: That's how doctors can tell if there's something wrong with your bone marrow. If they can't hear the zuuwee of the scooters, you're gonna die soon. I mean, maybe, I'm not a doctor.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> is <radical>stand</radical>ing funny, facing a <radical>direction</radical> <kanji>nearby</kanji>. He can't go far because there's obviously something wrong with his feet. He's stuck nearby, very close to your side.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to bring the leader to a <kanji>nearby</kanji> <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) alley. It's really close and you can stay right by his side as he throws bowling balls and gets his anger out.
meaning_hint: Picture the leader very close to your side. He's looking off into the distance, a distance he'll never get to because all he can do is stand there with his messed up feet.
reading_hint: Picture the leader bowling nearby. You keep him close by, you never know when he'll fall over and need your help.

meaning_mnemonic: You start growing <radical>thread</radical> in your <radical>rice paddy</radical> to <kanji>accumulate</kanji> more. You didn't want to have to buy any, so you're just trying to grow thread with your rice. That seems like a good way to accumulate more to you.
reading_mnemonic: Sadly, this is a terrible way to <kanji>accumulate</kanji> anything thread related and you ended up <reading>rui</reading>ning (るい) your rice paddy too. I guess if you want to accumulate thread this isn't the way to do it.
meaning_hint: Just imagine how much thread you'll accumulate if you can just grow it along with your rice in the rice paddy! So much thread!
reading_hint: Your attempts to accumulate more of something by cheating have brought ruin to everything else you were doing. Nice job, dummy.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sick</radical> <radical>Death Star</radical> needs its <radical>heart</radical> <kanji>cure</kanji>d. If it doesn't get <kanji>healing</kanji> soon, it will die.
reading_mnemonic: You realized that the only way to <kanji>cure</kanji> the Death Star is to turn it back into a <reading>yu</reading>rt (ゆ). Then it won't be able to do any more harm, and its outside will reflect its pleasant self on the inside!
meaning_hint: Maybe the Death Star wouldn't be sick if it wasn't so mean to you, yelling at you for living in a yurt, when it was a yurt on the inside the whole time. Wait! I know the cure!
reading_hint: Hmm, but does this type of yurt healing technology exist?! You'll have to find out to cure it!!

meaning_mnemonic: You just <radical>receive</radical>d a <radical>building</radical>, so you want to surround it with an <kanji>enclosure</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>enclosure</kanji> felt a  bit quiet and lonely, so you decided to get yourself a pet <reading>cock</reading> (かく).
meaning_hint: Imagine the building you just received. You want to protect it, so you get started on that enclosure. Imagine building your enclosure with your bare hands.
reading_hint: Your pet cock is the perfect addition to your new enclosure. Just look at it, pecking happily between the enclosure and that building you received.

meaning_mnemonic: When you go to the pool or lake to swim, you'll see a <radical>flag</radical> stuck somewhere in the <radical>water</radical>. That flag is there so you know it's okay to <kanji>pee</kanji> in that area of the pool. Everyone knows <kanji>urine</kanji> dilutes in water, and if everyone just pees in one place, it won't be gross. So go pee over by that flag when you have to go. Don't bother getting out of the water.
reading_mnemonic: Right as you start to <kanji>pee</kanji>, you hear the life guard start to yell, "<reading>NYOOOOOO</reading>! (にょう)" and he's pointing at you! You were just peeing by the pee flag! Why is he mad?
meaning_hint: Imagine swimming over to the flag that's sticking out of the water and peeing there. Yeah, everyone knows what you're doing over there, but it's better than getting out and walking to the bathroom right? Just pee in the water. Just do it.
reading_hint: Imagine your embarrassment when you find out that you aren't supposed to urinate in the water, not even by the pee flag, because a life guard yelled NYOOOOOO and pointed right at you while you did it.

meaning_mnemonic: Between the <radical>roof</radical> and the <radical>ground</radical> there are <radical>few</radical> <radical>shellfish</radical>. This is because we invited some <kanji>VIP</kanji> <kanji>guest</kanji>s into our home and they ate all our shellfish.
reading_mnemonic: These <kanji>VIP</kanji> <kanji>guest</kanji>s are part of a <reading>Hin</reading>du (ひん) envoy.
meaning_hint: The VIPs came into our home (the place between our roof and the ground) where they consumed all our shellfish. Only the best for guests, I guess.
reading_hint: We are treating these VIP guests extra special because they represent the whole Hindu religion. They get shellfish because of course we weren't going to serve them beef!

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>tiger</radical> meets a <radical>man</radical>, it becomes the man's <kanji>captive</kanji>. Men can be pretty mean like that. They see a tiger and they want to put it in a cage, so the poor tiger becomes the man's captive.
reading_mnemonic: The man takes his new tiger <kanji>captive</kanji> to <reading>Rio</reading> (りょ). Why Rio de Janeiro? Turns out you're allowed to keep tigers captive down in Rio.
meaning_hint: Picture the sad tiger captive and the man laughing as he pokes at it. Tigers shouldn't be captives, but when men find them they always seem to do this anyway.
reading_hint: Now picture this tiger captive in Rio. He's stuck in a cage at home while the man yucks it up along Rio's beaches, bragging to all the locals about his captive tiger.

meaning_mnemonic: The only <radical>feeling</radical> a dead <radical>soul</radical> will have is <kanji>remorse</kanji>. Remorse for the people it left behind. <kanji>Regret</kanji>s for the things it did and didn't do.
reading_mnemonic: The soul with the most <kanji>regret</kanji>s is Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). Everyone knows to stay away from him in the spirit world because he's so sad. Not because of his own actions, but because of the state of his people now.
meaning_hint: If you died right now and left your soul behind, do you think you'd have regrets?
reading_hint: Picture Genghis Khan looking down at the state of his people, the Mongols, in the modern world. See the remorse in his soul's face. They should be leaders of the modern world. Not reduced to a people struggling in the background.

meaning_mnemonic: Go from a <radical>bow</radical> to a <radical>small</radical> <radical>gun</radical> and you're <kanji>increasing</kanji> in fire power! Even though the gun is small, it's still a step up from a bow.
reading_mnemonic: You keep trading in weapons to get <kanji>increasing</kanji>ly stronger gear, when you realize that at the top of the list is... a <reading>Ya</reading>nkee's (や) baseball bat? Huh. I would have gone for a tank or a bazooka. But I guess if this will increase your power level, you have to go for it.
meaning_hint: Imagine increasing your power with a new weapon. What would it be?
reading_hint: Imagine walking down a line of weapons, each one increasing in power from the one before it. Bow, small gun, bigger gun, tank, Yankee's bat. Yup, that sounds right.

meaning_mnemonic: If you find some <radical>rice</radical> in a <radical>top hat</radical> it's going to be really <kanji>coarse</kanji> and <kanji>rough</kanji>. Probably because it's not the kind that's meant to be eaten.
reading_mnemonic: You take the <kanji>coarse</kanji> rice and try to wash it with <reading>so</reading>ap (そ).
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to eat the rice you found in a top hat. It's so coarse it feels like it's scratching your teeth. There's probably some dirt in here too. This wasn't the best idea.
reading_hint: If you were trying to make this coarse rice taste like soap, you did it. But if you were trying to make it less coarse, well... sorry, but that's not how anything works.

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to <radical>loiter</radical> on a <radical>cliff</radical> with the <radical>pope</radical> you better be ready to move in perfect <kanji>circulation</kanji> with him. The pope is wearing really heavy robes and it's hot up here on this cliff, so you both need to walk in circles to keep cool.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>circulation</kanji> wouldn't have to even be happening if it wasn't <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). The cliffs are so hot in June, and in any other month the two of you would have just sat down and had a chat. But in June you have to do this weird circulation dance.
meaning_hint: Imagine this strange circulation of you and the pope up here on a cliff. It's not even like you two are up here for a reason, you're just loitering. But he doesn't want to be hot so he makes you circulate with him.
reading_hint: "This circulation is all June's fault," you mutter, walking in a circle with the pope to stay cool.

meaning_mnemonic: If you try to turn something into <radical>ice</radical> while having <radical>doubt</radical>s, it will just <kanji>congeal</kanji> instead. It will <kanji>freeze</kanji>, but it will get kinda yucky too. Your doubts will affect the ice-making process, so try not to be so worried and unsure next time you want normal ice.
reading_mnemonic: You always end up <kanji>congeal</kanji>ing your <reading>gyo</reading>za (ぎょう) this way. You want to <kanji>freeze</kanji> your leftover gyoza so you can enjoy it later, but you doubt it will turn out all right and it always congeals in a gross, half-frozen gyoza block. Yuck!
meaning_hint: Imagine looking over water in an ice cube tray. You aren't sure if they'll turn out alright, and you doubt whether ice is even a thing. As you think this, the water congeals and freezes into gross shapes.
reading_hint: Picture frozen, congealed gyoza. Do you want to eat that? I didn't think so.

meaning_mnemonic: If you put <radical>two</radical> <radical>two</radical> pound weights on a <radical>person</radical> on the <radical>moon</radical> they'll increase in <kanji>stature</kanji> when they come back down. Everything is lighter on the moon so the person won't even feel the weights. Then when they come back to earth they'll have grown a whole two inches in <kanji>height</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: Getting a taller <kanji>stature</kanji> will also make you seem super <reading>sexy</reading> (せき), both on the moon and back home on earth. If you stay on the moon long enough, you'll be the tallest person in the galaxy — and who wouldn't find that sexy?! Huh?
meaning_hint: Imagine how much height and stature you'd gain if you spent time lifting weights on the moon!
reading_hint: Imagine how sexy the height of a ten foot tall person would be right after they'd gotten back from weight training on the moon.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> is the reason why crops grow and people can get out of bed in the morning. But on planets with two <radical>sun</radical>s, they can get even more done and it makes them way more <kanji>prosperous</kanji> than us. They work all day, then as the first sun sets, the second one rises and they get a new burst of energy. The whole planet lives in a state of <kanji>prosperity</kanji> because of the two suns.
reading_mnemonic: The person on this planet with two suns who benefits from the prosperity the most is the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). Yes, this planet still has a shougun as their leader, but everything is going so well that they didn't see a need to change. The shogun will be in charge of this prosperous world forever. No one ever thinks of revolt when everyone has everything they could ever need.
meaning_hint: Picture the prosperous planet that has two suns. They get so much work done that no one is ever hungry or tired or without.
reading_hint: Picture the Shougun looking over the prosperous world, the two suns at his back.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>dawn</radical> radical and the <kanji>dawn</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Dawn</kanji> is <reading>dawn</reading> (だん)! It's also a great time to get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). The sun is bright at dawn, but not so much that it will send skin cancer rays into you.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Imagine getting a tan at dawn. Ahh. Relaxing and warm.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>soul</radical> of the <radical>Death Star</radical> is actually quite <kanji>pleasant</kanji>. And if you think about it, it's the people who control the Death Star that are evil and kill.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>pleasant</kanji> soul within the Death Star actually looks like a <reading>yu</reading>rt (ゆ), not a big angry, metal ball.
meaning_hint: Picture the Death Star in your mind. As you picture it, its pleasant, smiling soul floats up and out of it. "Oh hello, dear," it says happily, "Didn't see you there!"
reading_hint: Yurts are very pleasant structures. They're round and soft and moveable, just like the Death Star's soul.

meaning_mnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> in a <radical>jet</radical> if you want to <kanji>erase</kanji> them from existence. You aren't just chopping or burning them off. There's like, no trace of them at all. Your parents can't even remember if you were born with fingers or not. Jets are that powerful.
reading_mnemonic: You're almost done <kanji>eras</kanji>ing your fingers when the jet starts sucking all of you in. You would try to grab the closest thing to you, but your fingers don't exist anymore, so you hold onto the <reading>mats</reading> (まつ) at your feet with your toes. The mats are super strong (they're made for jets, after all) and you manage to save yourself!
meaning_hint: Imagine your fingers slowly getting closer and closer to the jet's engine. It feels like it's going to suck your whole body in. And you better watch out, because if it does you'll be erased from the planet. And probably from time itself.
reading_hint: Now you're so thankful to the mats. If they weren't there you might have erased your whole body! Thank you mats! I'll never walk on you without thanking you for your service again!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> has a <radical>hat</radical> that's made from a <radical>tree</radical> but the more you look at it, the more it looks like it's just a big <kanji>cork</kanji>. It's a big, wooden <kanji>plug</kanji> that he's just placed on his head. It can hardly be called a hat.
reading_mnemonic: It turned out this <kanji>cork</kanji> hat was given to him by a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). You look into it and it turned out it IS a really big cork that centaurs use on their big wine casks. But the king didn't understand, and thought it was a hat. The centaurs are probably still laughing.
meaning_hint: Picture how silly the king looks with this cork on his head. He kind of looks like a fat, plugged up bottle of wine when he yells now.
reading_hint: Picture the centaurs drinking and giggling, putting the cork from their wine on their head to imitate the stupid king.

meaning_mnemonic: How do you make a mnemonic for the word this? You don’t. But, we can try. You climb many <radical>hills</radical>. On top of one of the hills is a little <radical>drop</radical> of water. You pick it up and say “<kanji>THIS</kanji>… THIS IS WHAT I WAS LOOKING FOR!”
reading_mnemonic: You <reading>colle</reading>ct(これ) <kanji>this</kanji> very carefully. You’re going to add this to your drop collection.
meaning_hint: As you look at the drop of water, you note that you found the right thing. Written on it is the word “this."
reading_hint: You’re always collecting drops of water in the hills. It’s your hobby, in fact. But this one is the first one you’ve ever collected with “this” written on it. What a great addition to your drop collection.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>imperial</radical> radical and the <kanji>imperial</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Wahoo!
reading_mnemonic: Who is the coolest <kanji>imperial</kanji> you know? <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter. Wait what? You mean he FIGHTS imperials? Oh, okay. Looks like Ryu is the coolest imperial FIGHTER you know!
meaning_hint: Make sure you remember this radical means imperial and you'll remember the kanji meaning too!
reading_hint: Picture Ryu kicking the butts of imperial  scum like Bison and Darth Vader.

meaning_mnemonic: You got off your <radical>scooter</radical> to load some <radical>charcoal</radical> onto it, when it suddenly took off to a <kanji>distant</kanji> place! That's some powerful charcoal.
reading_mnemonic: You chase the scooter to that <kanji>distant</kanji> place, but then you see it heading for a lake. Oh good, it should be stopped by the water. But wait! In the distance you see your scooter drive onto a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat and... row itself through the water?! No!!
meaning_hint: Your scooter is charcoal powered, but it looks like you put too much in there and it drove off somewhere distant without you. If you look really carefully, you can just see it, a distant speck on the horizon.
reading_hint: You can still make out the distant shape of your scooter, now on a row boat, fading away and completely out of reach.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> of the <radical>flowers</radical> in the <radical>center</radical> (of England, if you remember) has a special <kanji>crystal</kanji>. The king crystal.
reading_mnemonic: The king thinks everyone wants his <kanji>crystal</kanji> so he hides it in the giant <reading>a</reading>corn (えい) in the center of the flowers in England. There's a tiny hole in the bottom of the acorn, and that's where this crystal stays hidden.
meaning_hint: Picture the king holding this crystal aloft, giggling as the sun shines through it and sparkles.
reading_hint: Imagine going to England, to the giant acorn in the center of the flowers, and searching for the king's crystal. If you find it, you can probably sell it for a fortune! Or just make a really sweet necklace.

meaning_mnemonic:
You're holding a <radical>sword</radical> in front of <radical>two</radical> <radical>building</radical>s. <kanji>What</kanji> will you do?

A. Stab the building on the left.

B. Stab the building on the right.

What do you choose?
reading_mnemonic:
A. <kanji>What</kanji> is, stab the building on the left? You stab the building on the left with your sword and what comes out is... a plate of delicious <reading>na</reading>chos (な)!

B. <kanji>What</kanji> is, stab the building on the right? You stab the building on the right with your sword and what comes out is... a plate of delicious <reading>na</reading>chos (な)!
meaning_hint: What? Do you think I'm gonna help you make this decision? No way, you need to dig deep and think about what you want to do.
reading_hint: What?! You got nachos either way. You should be happy, don't ask me any other questions.

meaning_mnemonic: You're tapping your <radical>fingers</radical> against a <radical>white</radical> wall when you realize the sound you're making is actually a sick <kanji>beat</kanji>. You keep going and start singing along. Boo boo boop. Boo do do do doop! Finger beats!
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>beat</kanji> is sick, but as you tap you realize you're <reading>hack</reading>ing (はく) up the wall! Slowly but surely, your fingers are hacking out little bits of the wall. And the beats don't sound as good until you move to a new, clean white area.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself in a completely white room. All you have is the white walls, your fingers, and your beats. When you stop all you're left with is white noise. Yuck! Keep those beats coming!
reading_hint: Imagine hacking the wall apart as you try to keep this beat going. The music is all you have, man! You can't lose the beat now!

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>animal</radical> with <radical>horns</radical> is drinking our <radical>alcohol</radical>? <kanji>Still</kanji>?
reading_mnemonic: The animal with horns that is <kanji>still</kanji> drinking your alcohol is no animal at all. It is actually one of the local <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) dressed up like a horned animal so they can drink as long as they want and you'll be too scared to shoo them away.
meaning_hint: You keep checking outside to see if they have stopped, but they are still drinking. They haven't stopped yet.
reading_hint: You keep checking, but that youth is still drinking. Of course, you don't know they are a youth, so it's quite impressive an animal can drink this much.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>roof</radical> is <radical>spicy</radical> because you're the <kanji>manager</kanji>. You're always testing new recipes in your house to share with everyone who works under you, but that makes all the spice stick to your roof (since it doesn't have good ventilation like a restaurant or a lab would).
reading_mnemonic: You've finally done it. You're now the <kanji>manager</kanji> of a bunch of spicy new <reading>cy</reading>borgs (さい). You've been toiling away for over a year, but you've finally replaced the cyborg you lost, and now you can manage them properly without anyone getting in the way.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself as the manager of a restaurant or a science lab. You have to test all of your managerial methods at home before you can bring them to work. And if you were to lick that roof of yours... it would be spicy from all of your experiments.
reading_hint: Unfortunately, you couldn't figure out how to make non-spicy cyborgs, but that's okay because you're going to be the best cyborg manager ever! No one will ever take them away from you again.

meaning_mnemonic: On the <radical>roof</radical> <radical>above</radical>, is a <radical>small</radical> <radical>stool</radical>. Anyone who sits in that stool will be <kanji>lonely</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You're so <kanji>lonely</kanji>, you make up an imaginary friend: <reading>Jack</reading> (じゃく). Jack doesn't get rid of the lonely feeling because he isn't real, but he is a distraction at least.
meaning_hint: Imagine sitting on this small stool, waaaaay up there above the roof. You can't see anyone and you can't talk to anyone. You feel this awful emptiness inside. That's what it's like to be lonely.
reading_hint: You're so busy talking to Jack, you don't hear the voices from below, "Aren't you lonely up there, miss? Do you want us to get a jack to get you down? Hello? I don't think she can hear us..."

meaning_mnemonic: You take some <radical>thread</radical>, put your <radical>scooter</radical> up to a <radical>signpost</radical>, and <kanji>sew</kanji> them together. You don't have a lock, so sewing your scooter up to the signpost to keep it safe is all you can think of.
reading_mnemonic: You usually only <kanji>sew</kanji> at <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう), so this is a big exception for you. You're usually worried that if you sew in public people will judge your stitches, so this is a big step for you!
meaning_hint: Imagine slowly sewing your scooter to the signpost, passing the thread back and forth through the signpost's metal holes and your scooter's handles.
reading_hint: Hopefully, if your scooter is still here later you'll be able to start sewing outside of your home more often from now on!

meaning_mnemonic: You've learned that you can <radical>catapult</radical> things to <kanji>give</kanji> them to other people! Don't catapult rocks at buildings and walls, catapult gifts to the people you love.
