reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Give</kanji> food to your new friend <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) with the catapult! You and Godzilla understand each other now. He's just hungry.
meaning_hint: If you want to give someone something, use a catapult!
reading_hint: You give Godzilla old used cars, chunks of old buildings, and sometimes hamburgers. Godzilla is so happy you're giving him food, he doesn't destroy anymore!

meaning_mnemonic: You go to buy a <radical>table</radical> but it looks like it has water damage from <radical>drop</radical>s of water hitting it. This makes it a pretty <kanji>mediocre</kanji> table. The water drops haven't really damaged it that much, it still stands and you can use it, but it's kinda ugly, so it's just mediocre.
reading_mnemonic: You weren't expecting to see such a <kanji>mediocre</kanji> table so you go up to the guy who's selling it and say, "Hey, I have a <reading>bone</reading> (ぼん) to pick with you!" But he doesn't think there is a bone to pick at all, he doesn't think the table is mediocre like you do.
meaning_hint: You didn't expect to see water drop damage on this table so now your mood is mediocre too.
reading_hint: Imagine having a bone to pick with someone because they're trying to sell you something mediocre and they just won't listen. Now you have a bone to pick with the person who recommended this guy to you!

meaning_mnemonic: When <radical>small</radical> <radical>drop</radical>s of water work <radical>together</radical> they gain <kanji>respect</kanji> for one another. If you end up working with someone long enough, and they end up being really great, you'll respect them too!
reading_mnemonic: If you <kanji>respect</kanji> someone enough you'll even take up their hobbies. The person you respect most loves the Yankees. You don't really feel the same, but you respect that about them so you chant, "<reading>Ooh Ya</reading>nkees, <reading>Ooh Ya</reading>nkees!" (うやうや) with them during each game.
meaning_hint: Imagine being a small drop of water and seeing other drops of water come to help you by working together. You'd really respect them, right?
reading_hint: Imagine respecting someone so much that you'll cheer ooh yankees over and over even though they're kind of a crappy team.

meaning_mnemonic: This was pure <radical>gold</radical> <radical>long ago</radical>, but now it's not. Someone got <kanji>confused</kanji> and <kanji>mixed</kanji> it with something else.
reading_mnemonic: It's <kanji>mixed</kanji> (and not pure anymore) because the <kanji>confused</kanji> person put it in that <reading>sack</reading> (さく) filled with various other things.
meaning_hint: You could swear this thing was gold a long time ago. Now it's a mix of different things.
reading_hint: Since it was in that sack for so long, it just ended up getting mixed with the other stuff in that sack. It was gold a long time ago, but now it's mixed with everything else.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>samurai</radical> hits the <radical>forehead</radical>s of <radical>grain</radical>s with his <radical>weapon</radical> so the <kanji>grain</kanji>s will pop off and into his mouth. He doesn't want to kill the plants, so he's just skimming the grain's foreheads so they'll fall off and he can eat them.
reading_mnemonic: Phew, all of this <kanji>grain</kanji> is making the samurai thirsty, so he reaches in his bag and pulls out an ice cold, refreshing <reading>Coke</reading> (こく).
meaning_hint: Picture the samurai taking out his weapon and spinning in a lightning fast circle. The forehead grains pop off and come raining into his open mouth.
reading_hint: These grains aren't even cooked, they're just hard and raw, so the Coke really helps wash them down.

meaning_mnemonic: The only <radical>building</radical> you'll get <radical>frostbite</radical> in is a <kanji>mausoleum</kanji>. Mausoleums and <kanji>tomb</kanji>s weren't made for living people, so they aren't insulated from the cold. No one cares if a dead body gets frostbite.
reading_mnemonic: You need to escape this <kanji>mausoleum</kanji> now but the only way out is on a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat over a creepy frozen pond. You've heard of these mausoleum row boats before, and you're pretty sure they're cursed.
meaning_hint: Unfortunately, you're realizing this because you thought a mausoleum would be a good hiding place from the winter cold. Spoilers, it's not.
reading_hint: Imagine taking your frostbitten fingers and trying to get away from the mausoleum in this row boat. It looks like it was made of bones and it is not very seaworthy. But you row with all your might, because you don't want to end up a permanent fixture in this tomb!

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Twenty</radical> <radical>penguin</radical>s together are always <kanji>evil</kanji>. Penguins are seen in groups a lot, sure, but did you know that any time there are exactly twenty penguins in a group they're plotting evil things? Evil things like global warming. And how to eat all the fish in the world so humans can't have any.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>evil</kanji> penguins can't let you tell the other human's what you've seen, so they murder you and hide your body in a <reading>hay</reading>stack (へい). Your family will never know what happened to you. The penguins will get away with their evil plans.
meaning_hint: Imagine walking around in the arctic when you stumble upon an evil group of penguins. They'd be cute if they weren't holding papers with "Kill All Humans" on them and tiny guns in their penguin hands.
reading_hint: Only someone or, in this case, something truly evil would kill someone and hide their remains under some hay. That's really disrespectful and, well, evil.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>boat</radical> that's <radical>white</radical> isn't just a boat, it's a <kanji>ship</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: A white-painted <kanji>ship</kanji> is a kind of <reading>hack</reading> (はく). It makes the ship more visible without costing much.
meaning_hint: Boats don't need to be a bright white color. But, ships do! They're dangerous and big, so they paint them nice and white.
reading_hint: You can also imagine hacking the ship's computer. "Look at me, look at me. You are the hacker now."

meaning_mnemonic: Put a <radical>hole</radical> in someone's <radical>body</radical> with a <radical>bow</radical> and they'll become <kanji>destitute</kanji>. You put a hole in them. They can't work. They can't take care of their house or their kids. They've lost it all.
reading_mnemonic: You see this <kanji>destitute</kanji> person and decide to give them a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう). They don't even remember that they're supposed to eat those, so they shove it in the hole you made with your bow.
meaning_hint: Picture a destitute person wandering in the streets with a hole in their body.
reading_hint: Picture a destitute person, sitting on the street, holding a cucumber. Putting the cucumber in their hole. Crying.

meaning_mnemonic: If you can <radical>guard</radical> your <radical>soul</radical> from bad things you'll always feel <kanji>delight</kanji> and <kanji>joy</kanji>. It's hard to guard your soul from everything though, so take all the joy you can, while you can.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>delight</kanji> you feel is so great that it becomes permanently <reading>etch</reading>ed (えつ) in your soul and even your face.
meaning_hint: Imagine guarding yourself from all the bad things in the world. No more politics, religion, internet trolls debating the two, it's all guarded from your precious soul. Ahhh, it's so joyous to be unburdened.
reading_hint: Picture joy etched on your own face. No one can get you down.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>specialty</radical> is <radical>drop</radical>ping people with <radical>thread</radical> by <kanji>bind</kanji>ing them! You can drop anyone as long as you have enough thread to restrain them. It doesn't matter how much they fight, you'll get them to drop!
reading_mnemonic: If you're going to be a weirdo who likes to <kanji>bind</kanji> people, at least make sure that they land on their <reading>back</reading>s (ばく) when they DO drop. Landing on your head or your boobs really hurts. So please at least try to make them land on their backs!
meaning_hint: Imagine if your specialty was dropping people by binding them with thread. You'd be such a weird person.
reading_hint: Imagine how you would bind someone so that when they drop they land on their back instead of somewhere more painful. How would you do that?

meaning_mnemonic: When you drive a <radical>car</radical>, everyone under your <radical>roof</radical> is putting their <radical>life</radical> in your hands, even if they don't say it with their <radical>mouth</radical>—it's still under your <kanji>control</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You have to maintain <kanji>control</kanji> of your car or you'll hit a bunch of <reading>cats</reading> (かつ). The roads are full of cats at all times. And if you lose control of your car for just a second, you'll hit at least two or three cats.
meaning_hint: It's really important to remember how many things are under your control when you operate a vehicle. It isn't just the people in your car either, it's everyone and everything in your way too.
reading_hint: If you don't want to think about hitting adorable cats with your car, you better maintain control. You're responsible!

meaning_mnemonic:
This <radical>bow</radical> is <radical>mysterious</radical> because it's strung with a single <kanji>chord</kanji>. I guess that makes the chord both a musical instrument <kanji>string</kanji> as well as a <kanji>bowstring</kanji>. Pluck the chord and it shoots an arrow while making a perfect G note. So mysterious!

Note: We're using "chord" here as the fancy name for a musical instrument string (like in "the chords of a harp" or "strike a chord"). This kanji covers both musical strings and bowstrings!
reading_mnemonic: Turns out this bow strung with a mysterious <kanji>chord</kanji> belongs to <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん). Of course! Only someone as elegant and dramatic as Genji would own a bow that plays music when you pluck its string.
meaning_hint: So this mysterious bow's string is for plucking a one-note tune or launching an attack. Either way, your enemies will be moved to tears.
reading_hint: Genji is a lover, not a fighter. Picture Genji, the most handsome and romantic man ever, serenading you with his one-note G-string bow. Swoon! Just hope he doesn't sink an arrow into you too.

meaning_mnemonic: Take a <radical>mole</radical> out of its <radical>hole</radical> and it will <kanji>suffocate</kanji>. They need to be in tight quarters, unlike us humans. So when you take one out of its nice, safe hole, it thinks it can't breathe and suffocates.
reading_mnemonic: As the mole <kanji>suffocate</kanji>s it <reading>cheats</reading> (ちつ) and shoves its head into your closed hand. You realize what it's doing and squeeze your hand tighter so it's more like a hole. You can feel the mole breathing again now.
meaning_hint: Picture a poor little mole suffocating in your hand after you took it out of its hole. Put it back right away! Don't kill that little mole!
reading_hint: Unfortunately, you can't use cheats on life, and the mole suddenly stiffens and suffocates to death anyway. You should feel bad about what you've done.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>together</radical> with another tsunami is going to cause a massive <kanji>flood</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: After the terrible <kanji>flood</kanji>, Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち comes to survey the destruction and offer hope to his people. But, instead he just tells the people that this flood happened because they didn't believe in the Crabigator's words, and because they weren't doing their kanji reviews.
meaning_hint: More than one tsunami will definitely result in a flood. Imagine the floody aftermath of more than one tsunami, arriving together.
reading_hint: "This flood was caused by you! It is because you did not believe in the holy Crabigator! It is because you didn't do your kanji reviews. You have sinned, sinned, sinned!" こういち says to the scared, cold, now homeless people.

meaning_mnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> in your <radical>ear</radical>s when there's an <radical>explosion</radical> <kanji>in addition</kanji> to all the other things you were told to do. You have to crouch under a desk, you have to stay away from windows, you have to close your eyes. But in addition to that, you need to protect your ears!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>In addition</kanji> to all the steps you have to take to protect yourself, you can't forget that ears come in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). You can't just stick one finger in one ear and have that be the end. You have a set of ears to worry about!
meaning_hint: Pretend you're in a class in the mid-20th century, watching a video on bomb explosion safety. In addition to putting your fingers in your ears, you have to do a bunch of other stuff. You can't just stop after the first step!
reading_hint: In addition to ears, you have a lot of sets on your body to worry about. Sets of eyes, nostrils, legs, you get the picture.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>eat</radical> and then <radical>wrap</radical> yourself into a ball because you're <kanji>bored</kanji>. You're also <kanji>sated</kanji>, because you ate just the right amount, but now you have nothing to do and it's soooo booooring.
reading_mnemonic: So now you're <kanji>bored</kanji> and stuck at <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). You aren't hungry. There's nothing on TV. And you don't want to have to leave your home to do something, even though that would probably solve your boredom problem.
meaning_hint: Haven't you ever been so bored that you just wrapped yourself up into a boredom ball?
reading_hint: Ehhhhhhgggg guys I'm stuck at home and I'm soooo bored. Heeelpp!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>thread</radical>s are <radical>humble</radical> and that makes you a perfect <kanji>gentleman</kanji>. You don't have to have fancy clothes to be a gentleman, just humble ones that don't stand out and show off too much. That shows how polite you are.
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, as a <kanji>gentleman</kanji> who dresses humbly, you're always getting kicked in the <reading>shin</reading>s (しん) by rude kids (and occasionally rude adults).
meaning_hint: Picture yourself as the perfect gentleman. You may not have a fancy top hat and tie, but you're humble about what you wear and you're kind to everyone.
reading_hint: But you're a gentleman, so you don't kick their shins back, you smile and hold in the pain. No matter how bad your shins hurt, you never fight back!

meaning_mnemonic: Under this <radical>canopy</radical> you have a <radical>brush</radical> (in its 肀 form) and a <radical>task</radical> to complete with it. This is totally <kanji>commonplace</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: This is so <kanji>commonplace</kanji> and familiar to you that you finish your task early and eat some <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう) while you wait for the guy to come back.
meaning_hint: This is a totally commonplace thing for almost every class or job you'll ever have. They sit you down under the canopy, put a brush in your hand, and say, "Here, complete this task. I'll be over there."
reading_hint: As you eat the yogurt you think about how commonplace yogurt is too. It's just a normal, plain, boring old food, found in practically everyone's fridge.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Stand</radical> staring up at the <radical>blue</radical> sky and you'll feel more <kanji>peaceful</kanji> than you ever have. Blue is a color that promotes peaceful thoughts and feelings. That's why all the social media sites are blue. To stop you from being a jerk.
reading_mnemonic: Now if someone asks you if the color blue makes you <kanji>peaceful</kanji> you should say "<reading>YAASS</reading> (やす)!" And you'll have us to thank for that! All those peaceful blue radicals. They're the easiest thing on the site, and if someone asks you if radicals make you feel peaceful say "YAASS" again!
meaning_hint: Imagine how peaceful staring at the blue ocean, the blue sky, or the blue tumblr makes you.
reading_hint: YAASS! Peaceful blue radicals standing in a row. What's that? YAASS you do want more radicals added to the site. Well okay then, if it will keep you at peace, sure!

meaning_mnemonic: If when you speak it sounds like the <radical>power</radical> of a <radical>drum</radical> is coming out of your <radical>mouth</radical>, then people will hold you in high <kanji>esteem</kanji>. Anyone who has a loud, booming, powerful voice will be held in high esteem in this day and age. Look at all those awesome musicians who have booming voices!
reading_mnemonic: You hold them in such high <kanji>esteem</kanji> that you give them your <reading>ca</reading>r (か). I ... I said you give them your car! Give them your car. Hey, hey!
meaning_hint: Imagine hearing someone speak and it's so powerful and loud that it sounds like they have a drum booming out of their mouth. You'll love them! You'll hold them in the highest esteem!
reading_hint: If you hold someone in high esteem you give me your- give them your car. Gimme!

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to fit your <radical>fingers</radical> into a <radical>key</radical> <radical>hole</radical>, you have to <kanji>squeeze</kanji> them in. Fingers aren't exactly supposed to fit in key holes... they're for keys, after all. But you want to squeeze your finger on in there anyway to unlock the door.
reading_mnemonic: Once you've <kanji>squeeze</kanji>d your finger in the key hole to unlock this door you squeeze all the money and jewels and treasures you can fit into your <reading>sack</reading> (さく). Your stealing sack.
meaning_hint: Imagine what it would be like to squeeze your fingers into a keyhole. Maybe try squeezing one finger really tight first. Did that hurt? Well it's going to be a lot worse in that key hole.
reading_hint: You have the ability to squeeze your fingers into locks to open them, so of course you're stealing. And you squeeze those stolen goodies into your sack so you can take them with you.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>insect</radical> that is amongst all the <radical>world</radical>'s <radical>tree</radical>s is the <kanji>butterfly</kanji>. They are always hanging around trees, those butterflies.
reading_mnemonic: As you look at a <kanji>butterfly</kanji>, fluttering around a tree, a hand reaches out from the tree and grabs it, then starts plucking the wings off. An evil laugh comes from inside the tree, and you know it's Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).
meaning_hint: Imagine any tree in the world with different kinds of butterflies fluttering around. Then go to another tree in the world that looks completely different. Once again, butterflies! They really are fluttering around every tree in the world.
reading_hint: She grabs another butterfly and starts pulling off wings again, though this time you can see her evil face looking at you while she does it. "I hate butterflies," she says. "They are always dying in my tea."

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>stone</radical> <radical>drop</radical>s from the sky into a <radical>rice paddy</radical> and then a <radical>cross</radical> <radical>drop</radical>s on top of it. They've combined to form a <kanji>tombstone</kanji>! Wait, does that mean a body fell from the sky first and you just didn't see it?
reading_mnemonic: Now you're curious about what's under there but when you try to touch the <kanji>tombstone</kanji> it's radiating with intense <reading>he</reading>at (ひ)! It must have come from outer space and this heat is from when it entered our atmosphere!
meaning_hint: Imagine seeing a stone and a cross drop from the sky onto each other to form a tombstone right in front of your eyes!
reading_hint: Can you feel the heat radiating off this space tombstone? Maybe there's a space skeleton sizzling under there too...

meaning_mnemonic: Who loves their <radical>flag</radical>, using <radical>jackhammer</radical>s, and precise <radical>measurement</radical>s? Your <kanji>military officer</kanji>! He's always saluting the flag, jackhammering near all the new recruits to test them, and checking the measurement between the floor and their pants. Who else could it be?
reading_mnemonic: That <kanji>military officer</kanji>'s favorite bird is an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い)! That should be obvious because the eagle is the bird of America! And that's the country he fights for!
meaning_hint: Picture a military officer doing all those things.
reading_hint: Picture the military officer saluting as an eagle flies overhead. Majestic.

meaning_mnemonic: You're trying to open the <radical>lid</radical> of a manhole that's covered in <radical>ice</radical> by <radical>jackhammer</radical>ing it a bunch of <radical>times</radical> but it's not working because it's just too <kanji>cold</kanji>. It's so cold that your jackhammer is starting to ice up too. But you need to get this lid open so you try time after time after time!
reading_mnemonic: Each time the jackhammer hits the freezing <kanji>cold</kanji> ice it makes a high pitched <reading>rin</reading>ging (りん) sound. It's actually really annoying to listen to but you really need to crack this ice open so you have to keep listening to it.
meaning_hint: Imagine if it was sooo cold that no matter how many times you tried to jackhammer the ice of the lid for this manhole, it wouldn't crack open.
reading_hint: If it wasn't so darn cold you wouldn't have to hear this horrible high pitched ringing sound because there wouldn't even be any ice to jackhammer through!

meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>cage</radical> with an <radical>axe</radical> in it above your house to show that you are an <kanji>artisan</kanji>. All of the artisans have to have this special sign somewhere near where they live to show they make things.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Artisan</kanji>s have to be marked this way because the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) says so. The Shougun doesn't want to have to put any effort into finding people to make things for him, so he created this new system.
meaning_hint: Imagine looking down your street and seeing caged axes above all of the artisans homes and shops. You want a new chair? You want some pants mended? Go to one of those signs and you'll find the right artisan.
reading_hint: Picture the Shougun marching angrily down the street. His shoe suddenly snaps in half. With just one glance he'll be able to find the shoe artisan and make them fix his shoe for free.

meaning_mnemonic: There is a <radical>scooter</radical> that is a <radical>satellite</radical>, but it is <kanji>far off</kanji> and <kanji>far away</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The scooter satellite is <kanji>far off</kanji>, but you can still hear it's horn go "<reading>har</reading> (はる) har har."
meaning_hint: Look up into the sky. That little moving light is the scooter satellite. It's just scooting along, far away from here.
reading_hint: The scooter satellite's only purpose is to make a "har har har" sound that is audible from far away. It's quite obnoxious.

meaning_mnemonic: You shoot an <radical>arrow</radical> at the <radical>mouth</radical> of the <radical>sun</radical> so that it will grant you a gift of <kanji>wisdom</kanji>. You heard about a Greek hero doing this and the god in the sun gave him the wisdom to see everything. You're hoping it will work for you too.
reading_mnemonic: You're standing around waiting for the <kanji>wisdom</kanji> to hit you when something else does. In the head. Ouch! You look down and it's a wheel of <reading>chee</reading>se (ち)! At first you're angry, until you realize that this must be a sign. Cheese IS wisdom. And the key to wisdom is eating a lot of cheese!
meaning_hint: Imagine shooting an arrow at the mouth of the sun and then waiting for the wisdom of the world to hit you. Did it work? Will you know when it works? Probably...
reading_hint: Picture yourself thanking the sun for teaching you that all wisdom comes from cheese. Now take a big bite!

meaning_mnemonic: If you could <radical>stop</radical> <radical>spoon</radical>ing water onto that <radical>tree</radical> we could use it for <kanji>brushwood</kanji>. But if you don't stop, then it will be too wet to burn at all! Stop already!
reading_mnemonic: You finally set the <kanji>brushwood</kanji> on fire and suddenly a <reading>Shiba</reading> (しば) Inu jumps out of the flames. So that's why they named this wood after them or... or vice versa?
meaning_hint: We need to use that brushwood to make a fire! So stop spooning water on it!
reading_hint: Shiba inu's come from setting brushwood on fire! That must have been how they got their name!

meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>shellfish</radical> becomes a <radical>drunkard</radical> and sees a <radical>cross</radical>, they'll become a <kanji>robber</kanji>. Because they'll steal the cross!
reading_mnemonic: Once the shellfish has a taste for being a <kanji>robber</kanji>, he'll join a Boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang so he can keep stealing more and more crosses. He'll drink more, steal more, and get to ride cool motorcycles.
meaning_hint: Some people make really bad choices when they're drunk, and in this case, when a shellfish gets drunk and sees something it wants it'll just rob people for it.
reading_hint: Picture a robber shellfish in a Bosozoku bike gang. Look at him the wrong way and he and his gang will steal all your stuff next time!

meaning_mnemonic: You take a <radical>drum</radical> and a <radical>branch</radical> and... well, what else would you do? You would <kanji>drum</kanji> or <kanji>beat</kanji> them together.
reading_mnemonic: Who loves to <kanji>drum</kanji> things together and make a huge racket? <reading>子</reading>s (こ), of course.
meaning_hint: A branch is just like a drumstick, so of course you're going to drum them together.
reading_hint: Kids can't get enough of it. Anything that makes a loud drumming noise or anything they can beat together is fun for them.

meaning_mnemonic: Move your <radical>coat rack</radical> in one <radical>direction</radical> by shooting it with a <radical>gun</radical>. Then you can keep shooting it to get a good <kanji>rotation</kanji> going. If you think there is a better way to make your coat rack make <kanji>revolution</kanji>s, you're probably wrong.
reading_mnemonic: As you watch your coat rack's <kanji>rotation</kanji>s a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) barges in and takes the gun from you, stops the rotating coat rack and leaves. How rude! I guess centaurs don't like when things rotate.
meaning_hint: Imagine getting your coat rack to make rotations in a circle just by shooting it with a gun!
reading_hint: Picture that centaurs angry face. Convince yourself it was the rotation it didn't like.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> is <radical>easy</radical> tonight, so you decide to cut it open. But, the moon wasn't the moon, it was your big, pale, round tummy. You just cut it open, and now you can see your <kanji>intestines</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: All over the world, people are cutting their bellies open, because they think its an easy moon (versus a normal moon, or hard moon, presumably). This is because they have fallen victim to a spell by Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).
meaning_hint: Imagine the moon, then imagine it cross-fading into your big, round tummy. Then, cut it open because you think the moon is easy to cut. And finally, see your intestines as clearly as you can.
reading_hint: This scene was in all the omen radicals you've been seeing about her. If only you could have stopped her, then none of this would have happened, right? Perhaps it was all destiny, you think, as your intestines fall from your stomach and Mrs. Chou cackles over your weakening body.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> melts into the <radical>ground</radical> after listening to loud <radical>music</radical> in the hot <radical>sun</radical>, so you have to find a nice big <kanji>tank</kanji> to scoop the melted tree into. The music made the tree vibrate at juuust the right frequency that it, combined with the hot sun, made the tree turn into a puddle. You can't just leave it being all blobby on the ground, so you find a tank for it.
reading_mnemonic: You're staring at the big <kanji>tank</kanji> when an eerie sound comes out, "Oooh I am the <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) of the tree! You've trapped me in this tank and now I'm going to haunt it!" You don't want to be haunted by a tank with a soul stuck in it.
meaning_hint: Imagine scooping a melted tree into a tank. It's a very strange sensation.
reading_hint:
"Um, okay?" you reply to the soul, and walk away from the tank. "W-wait! Tanks can't move? Come back, let me haunt you with my tree soul!"

It's a good thing there weren't wheels on that tank!

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>leader</radical> of your gang is a <radical>drunkard</radical> he'll try to have you <kanji>fell</kanji> trees thinking they're rival gang members. You all know they aren't but you can't argue with your leader, especially if he's drunk. Go ahead and <kanji>attack</kanji> the trees anyway.
reading_mnemonic: You and your gang head out to <kanji>fell</kanji> the trees, only to remember that all you have are <reading>bats</reading> (ばつ). Good luck trying to take down trees with nothing but bats!
meaning_hint: Imagine felling trees with a bunch of other guys, yelling things like, "This is what you get for stepping on my turf!" It's a little silly.
reading_hint: This is good news for the trees, but your drunken leader isn't going to be happy.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>s get <radical>blame</radical>d because they <kanji>pickle</kanji> everything in their path. That means that everything smells of pickles after a tsunami.
reading_mnemonic: Looking at all those <kanji>pickle</kanji>s, you bend down and pick up <reading>two</reading> (つ) of them. One in each hand. You look at the pickles in your hand and wonder whether to try one.
meaning_hint: Imagine a tsunami crashing over the land, being blamed by everyone. In its path are piles and piles of pickles, as far as the eye can see!
reading_hint: Picture the two pickles and imagine what kind of pickles they are.

meaning_mnemonic: The amount of <radical>dirt</radical> you need to get some <radical>peace</radical> is a <kanji>two mat area</kanji>. Two tatami mats, that is. Also called a <kanji>tsubo</kanji>, this is a pretty common measurement in Japan, so you should get really familiar with the amount of space that two tatami mats take up together.
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>two mat area</kanji> also happens to be big enough to hold <reading>two bo</reading>dies (つぼ). That's enough space to bring peace to two living, or dead, bodies!
meaning_hint: Picture how big a two mat area is. Lie down and feel the peace this much space brings to you.
reading_hint: Try to picture a two mat area with two bodies lying in it. Whether they're alive or dead is up to you.

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to turn your <radical>thread</radical> into a <radical>sweet</radical> color you just HAVE to go with <kanji>navy blue</kanji> or <kanji>dark blue</kanji>. That's the sweetest color in existence, everyone knows that. After all, blue raspberry flavor is dark blue and it's super sweet.
reading_mnemonic: Now that your threads are <kanji>navy</kanji> colored you need to be careful that <reading>con</reading>dors (こん) don't try to steal it for their nests. Condors love <kanji>dark blue</kanji> things and thread is perfect for a condor nest.
meaning_hint: Turn your thread a sweet navy color and people will be reminded of yummy blue raspberry. MmMmm.
reading_hint: Imagine a condor stealing your sweet navy thread right after you dyed it, flying off. Lame!

meaning_mnemonic: You have a <radical>net</radical> of <radical>thread</radical> for catching a <radical>turkey</radical>. But, it must be <kanji>spread out</kanji> and you must <kanji>arrange</kanji> it first.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>spread out</kanji> your net and <kanji>arrange</kanji> it just so. You expect it to catch a turkey, but instead you catch the ancient Egyptian god <reading>Ra</reading> (ら).
meaning_hint: The reason you'd use a net made of thread is because it's hard to see. But, it's only that way when you spread out the net. It's important to spread out and arrange your net otherwise the turkey will see it.
reading_hint: "Let me out of this net!" says Ra. "You arranged and spread it out just right so I couldn't see it!"

meaning_mnemonic: To the <radical>mountain</radical>s you took your <radical>horned</radical> <radical>husband</radical> for you two to get married. (Society wouldn't accept a horned husband!) To hide even more from the prying eyes of society, you take your horned husband into a <kanji>ravine</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>ravine</kanji> is still in <reading>きょう</reading>と though, because if you went outside the city your marriage wouldn't be legal. So you found a secluded, local ravine.
meaning_hint: Imagine walking up the mountain in your wedding clothes so you can get married without anyone trying to stop you. Then, because you're still worried, you go into a ravine where you are read your marriage vows.
reading_hint: This ravine runs right through the middle of きょうと, though most people don't notice it. That makes it the best place for a secret wedding to a horned freak-husband.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> says <radical>spring</radical> is looking <radical>dry</radical>, so you'll need your <kanji>salary</kanji> in advance.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>salary</kanji> is sent straight to your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう) instead of the bank. Okay, something is definitely fishy here.
meaning_hint: The leader shows you the number, it's all of your salary for spring. "This way, if things dry up worse, you'll be okay for a while," he says. This is awfully nice, but very suspicious at the same time.
reading_hint:
You're sitting at home, and there is a knock at the door. A man in a suit, hat, and dark sunglasses hands you a briefcase full of money. "Here's your spring salary. Enjoy the dry weather."

"T-thanks?"

Welp, have fun at home with your stacks of early salary cash.

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>alcohol</radical> <radical>nurse</radical> is someone who nurses alcohol. In other words, they <kanji>brew</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>brew</kanji> of alcohol is what <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう) loves most after a long day of working the fields.
meaning_hint: Imagine a nurse brewing alcohol with the same attention they would nurse a human patient. But this brew is much more important than any person's health.
reading_hint: Imagine what it must be like for Joe, out working on the fields all day long, working up a sweat. I’d say he’s earned a cold alcoholic brew after such a day. Cut the man some slack.

meaning_mnemonic: You give a <radical>bow</radical> and a <radical>stick</radical> to your friend's family. This is because your friend died, and he loved bows and sticks. You give this to his family, to give our <kanji>condolence</kanji>s.
reading_mnemonic: More than a <kanji>condolence</kanji>, you want to avenge your friend's death. He was killed by Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう), and she broke his stick and bow, which is why you got the family a new set.
meaning_hint: Imagine looking at the bow and stick as you slide them over to your deceased friend's family. This won't do enough, but it is a little condolence at least.
reading_hint: Imagine her saying, "My condolences," as Mrs. Chou does him in.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>nose</radical> isn't the first thing people see about your face, it's the <kanji>latter</kanji>. They see your eyes THEN your nose, then your mouth, in order going down. Just label them A <kanji>B</kanji> and C and you'll never forget!
reading_mnemonic: If you don't have any cereal you have to eat your <kanji>B</kanji> breakfast option. <reading>Oats</reading> (おつ). You don't hate oats, but they're definitely not your first choice.
meaning_hint: Imagine saying A B C as you look down someones face at their eyes, nose and mouth. Their nose is B!
reading_hint: Imagine running out of cereal so you have to eat your latter option of breakfast oats. They aren't awful but they aren't your A breakfast choice, that's for sure.

meaning_mnemonic: Riding a <radical>scooter</radical> to the <radical>library</radical> is a <kanji>universal</kanji> experience. People young and old have all, at some point, taken a scooter to the library, picked up a book, used the internet, sat in the comfy chairs, then left via that same scooter.
reading_mnemonic: Riding a scooter to the library is so <kanji>universal</kanji>, even <reading>hen</reading>s (へん) do it.
meaning_hint: Scooters and libraries are universally loved by all!
reading_hint: The next time you scoot to the library look around. There are little hen scooters and hens there with their chicks. They're reading them books about grains and how to hatch eggs. It's a universally adorable thing to see.

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>go</radical> to a <radical>big</radical> <radical>rice paddy</radical> and establish a <radical>prison</radical> right in the middle of it to restore the <kanji>equilibrium</kanji> of the area.
reading_mnemonic: To restore <kanji>equilibrium</kanji>, you throw all the <reading>こう</reading>いち clones into the prison.
meaning_hint: There was a lot of crime right here in this rice paddy, so you just built a big prison right there to lower the crime rate so this region can experience equilibrium once again.
reading_hint: You might remember from the kanji 耕 that there were こういち clones working in these rice paddy fields. Well, they were the ones disturbing the equilibrium. At least that's what Emperor こういち said. So by throwing them in a prison built right here, the area could once again achieve equilibrium.

meaning_mnemonic: "For the  <radical>samurai</radical>, it is a <radical>jackhammer</radical> that they will <radical>lack</radical>." That is the latest <kanji>article</kanji> of the law written about samurai, hundreds of years after katana were taken away. This article was written with great <kanji>sincerity</kanji> by the government.
reading_mnemonic: The one who wrote these <kanji>article</kanji>s with such <kanji>sincerity</kanji> was Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).
meaning_hint: By writing up new articles and laws they keep taking things away from samurai. First the katana, now jackhammers?
reading_hint: Genghis Khan knew that samurai were a threat to his kingdom, so he took away their secondary weapon, the jackhammer.

meaning_mnemonic: Put a <radical>guard</radical> at your <radical>gate</radical> to get a good <kanji>inspection</kanji> of your neighborhood going. The guard will stand there all day inspecting everything and then report it all back to you. You won't have to do any of the inspection yourself!
reading_mnemonic: His <kanji>inspection</kanji> is complete, but it looks like he's used an <reading>etch</reading> (えつ) a sketch to record all the results. That's not really helpful. If you shake it all the inspection results will disappear!
meaning_hint: Imagine the guard doing his inspection in front of the gate all day long. He should have a nice report for you once he's done.
reading_hint: If you hire a guard to stand at your gate and conduct an inspection of your neighborhood, make sure you tell him not to use an etch a sketch!

meaning_mnemonic: If you put your <radical>spoon</radical> in the <radical>sun</radical> while you're in <radical>prison</radical>, a <radical>mouth</radical> will appear to <kanji>scold</kanji> you. You can never see who the mouth belongs to, but considering you're in prison it's probably a guard. Either way, that person doesn't like what you're doing and they keep scolding you when you put your spoon out into the sun.
reading_mnemonic: One day you finally get fed up. You need to know who the mouth of the person who's <kanji>scold</kanji>ing you is. You stick your spoon out into the sun and the mouth appears, scolding you right on time. You reach out and grab them. But in your hand is a pile of <reading>cats</reading> (かつ).
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to reflect some precious sunlight into your prison room with a spoon. Now imagine a mouth appearing in the way of the sunlight and it scolds you.
reading_hint: WHAT KIND OF WORLD IS THIS THAT YOU'RE IN A PRISON WHERE CATS SCOLD YOU?!

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>elf</radical> who ventures outside in <radical>winter</radical> has a lot of <kanji>daring</kanji>. Elf ears are very, very delicate, and the winter cold can really mess them up. So if you see an elf adventuring in winter, know that that particular elf is a really daring dude.
reading_mnemonic: The amount of <kanji>daring</kanji> you'd need is about equal to the amount Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) had. And he was really daring!
meaning_hint: Imagine how much daring it would take to go outside in winter knowing you could be freezing your precious elf ears off.
reading_hint: Do you think you have the same amount of daring as an elf going out in winter and Genghis Khan? Tell the truth.

meaning_mnemonic: If you trap the <radical>moon</radical> in a <radical>greenhouse</radical> it will grow a creepy <kanji>membrane</kanji> around itself. The moon isn't used to the heat it would be exposed to in a greenhouse so that membrane is there to protect it. It's really gross looking though.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to open the greenhouse to check this moon <kanji>membrane</kanji> out but when you open the door the smell of big <reading>mac</reading>s (まく) hits you in the face. Eww. Why does this membrane smell like a big mac... or does that mean big macs smell like a festering moon?
meaning_hint: Imagine seeing the moon with a membrane sitting stuffed in a greenhouse. It looks like something out of the Alien movies and you're starting to think putting it in there wasn't a good idea.
reading_hint: Can you imagine if the moon had a membrane and it smelled like a big mac? How does that even happen? You should probably go ask that food chain what they use for their ingredients.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>death</radical> of your <radical>eye</radical>s means that you've gone <kanji>blind</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: When you're <kanji>blind</kanji> you probably shouldn't drive anymore because if you do you'll <reading>mow</reading> (もう) everyone over. Actually, don't mow your lawn either. Blind people probably shouldn't be driving anything.
meaning_hint: Imagine what it would be like to have one of your eyes die. You'll be blind in that eye forever!
reading_hint: Imagine a blind person mowing over everyone in their car as they drive on the sidewalk. Eeek. At least they don't have to see this mess.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>machine</radical> in your body that is powered by the <radical>moon</radical> is your <kanji>womb</kanji>, also called your <kanji>uterus</kanji>! Haven't you ever wondered why periods happen with the revolutions of the moon? That's because it powers your uterus and tells it what to do!
reading_mnemonic: It kind of sucks, but there is no way to sever this <kanji>uterus</kanji> - moon <reading>tie</reading> (たい). But if you find a way please tell me, I can't take this anymore.
meaning_hint: The next time you're cursing your uterus for making you bleed, curse the moon instead! It's the moon's fault you're in pain.
reading_hint: Picture an invisible tie tieing your uterus to the moon. Damn you moon, stop pulling, it hurts!

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Alcohol</radical> is a good <radical>teacher</radical> on the process of <kanji>fermentation</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Using <kanji>fermentation</kanji>, you are indeed making some alcohol. It will be drunk at a party put on by Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち.
meaning_hint: Because alcohol is so good and addicting, people will work extra hard to ferment it properly. This makes it a good teacher to learn about fermentation. People are motivated to figure it out!
reading_hint: You are fermenting thousands of bottles of alcohol for Emperor こういち's big party.

meaning_mnemonic: When <radical>building</radical>s <radical>have</radical> <radical>dirt</radical> piling up all around them, it's a sign of their <kanji>degradation</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: And who's leading this charge into <kanji>degradation</kanji>? Your <reading>da</reading>d (だ), of course.
meaning_hint: Picture a street where nothing is maintained. The buildings crumble in the dirt, but the people have lost the will to care. It's not just physical, it's spiritual degradation too…
reading_hint: Your dad isn't just part of the degradation — he's the ringleader. He's sitting in a broken lawn chair, surrounded by empty beer cans, takeout containers, and piles of dirt. He waves his hand, and more junk food wrappers fall around him, almost like he's commanding this lazy, degenerate lifestyle.

meaning_mnemonic: You're riding your <radical>scooter</radical> toward your <radical>yurt</radical> as fast as you can because someone is trying to get in there to <radical>boil</radical> your water and you want to <kanji>intercept</kanji> them.
reading_mnemonic: You manage to <kanji>intercept</kanji> the <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ) who was just about to open the door to your yurt, and instead you crash right into him.
meaning_hint: If you manage to intercept them before they can go in there to boil things, you'll be able to pick up all the clothes and comic books you left on the floor. You really don't want anyone to see those.
reading_hint: Phew, you intercepted the shaman in time (though she doesn't look very happy about being scooted into) and now you can clean up your yurt before they go in.

meaning_mnemonic: An altered <radical>bird</radical> is a <kanji>crow</kanji>! They look like birds but they're bigger and darker and smarter!
reading_mnemonic: If you can't tell just from looking at it that it's a <kanji>crow</kanji> and not a bird, walk up to it and <reading>caress</reading> (からす) it with your hand! If it caresses you back, it's a crow! If it freaks out and flies away or pecks your face, it's a normal bird. Crows are really smart and like affection. That's what makes them so cool!
meaning_hint: See that bird? Wait, that's not just a bird, it's a crow!
reading_hint: How many crows have you caressed today?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>convex</radical> radical and the <kanji>convex</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.
reading_mnemonic: Is this <kanji>convex</kanji>? It's <reading>totes</reading> (とつ) convex!
meaning_hint: Study your radicals and you'll read good.
reading_hint: Tote bags are totes not convex though. Don't mix them up.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>concave</radical> radical and the <kanji>concave</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: If you call someone <kanji>concave</kanji> the <reading>王</reading> (おう) will find out and he'll have his guards turn you concave.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Watch your mouth around the 王. Have you ever seen a concave person? Yeah that's because they didn't live through it. 王s aren't very nice, you know.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> that does well in the <radical>wind</radical> is a <kanji>maple</kanji> tree. Maple trees don't have fruit so the wind can't knock anything off of it. That's why they thrive well in windy places!
reading_mnemonic: When the wind goes through the <kanji>maple</kanji> tree you know it's <reading>kite day</reading> (かえで)!
meaning_hint: Picture a bunch of maple trees being blown by the wind. They don't seem upset, they actually seem really happy.
reading_hint: Imagine your kite in the maple tree.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>drunkard</radical> has <radical>dirt</radical> in his <radical>mouth</radical> as he tries to talk to you. You walk over and he spits a <kanji>question mark</kanji> into your hand. It's covered in dirt and spit. Um... does this mean he's confused? He probably doesn't even know where he is. But how did he make this question mark and why was it in his mouth?
reading_mnemonic: The drunkard takes the <kanji>question mark</kanji> back and presses it to his head. He's wearing a <reading>Ya</reading>nkee's (や) cap. Oh come on. "Do you just want to know the Yankee's score in the game?" The drunkard nods.
meaning_hint: Imagine having a drunkard spit a dirty question mark into your hand. You want to say, "Yes, I'm ??? too."
reading_hint: Picture yourself throwing the question mark at the drunkard who only cares about the Yankees. What a waste of time.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> grown in a <radical>warehouse</radical> will be very <kanji>pale</kanji>. They never see the sun, so they don't grow to have bright colors.
reading_mnemonic: When you grow <kanji>pale</kanji> flowers like this, they don't grow a <reading>soul</reading> (そう). Soul adds color to plants and flowers. That's probably why ghosts and dead bodies are pale too.
meaning_hint: Imagine walking into a warehouse and seeing hundreds of pale flowers growing in it. It's subtle, but beautiful.
reading_hint: Picture a bunch of pale, soulless flowers. You pick one up and it falls apart in your hands.

meaning_mnemonic:
The <radical>king</radical> will send his men with <radical>sword</radical>s into your <radical>private</radical> <radical>rice paddy</radical> if you come across <kanji>lapis lazuli</kanji>. Even though this is your private land, he wants that lapis lazuli to make some new crowns and thrones for himself. Plus he's the king, so he can do whatever he wants.

If you've never taken an art history class (or played Minecraft) you may never have come across lapis lazuli, but think of it as a pretty, blue precious stone that people really, really liked in the olden days.
reading_mnemonic: You run up and ask how this <kanji>lapis lazuli</kanji> thievery isn't illegal and one of the swordsmen just shouts, "<reading>Loo</reading>phole (る)!" What? What loophole? What law says that the king gets to steal your stuff?
meaning_hint: Watch the king's swords digging in your private rice paddy and digging up huge chunks of lapis lazuli. This isn't fair, that lapis should belong to you.
reading_hint: Imagine reading through a giant text called "The Law" and looking for this loophole the king's men claim to have about taking your lapis lazuli. You can't find it... but maybe if you search just a bit longer...

meaning_mnemonic: You have to put <radical>flowers</radical> in a <radical>bright</radical> place if you want them to <kanji>sprout</kanji>. If they're left in the shade they'll just rot.
reading_mnemonic: You want to grow these <kanji>sprout</kanji>s in your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう), not outside. So you need to find the windows in your house that are the brightest.
meaning_hint: Picture your flowers sprouting out of the ground in the bright spring sun.
reading_hint: Take a walk around your home right now. Can you find the best place to sprout flowers? Remember, it has to be nice and bright! Not dark and damp. Don't put them in your closet.

meaning_mnemonic: There are <radical>stone</radical>s that smell like <radical>trash</radical> in the <radical>river</radical>. You reach in and realize these rocks are made of <kanji>sulfur</kanji>. Even the flow of this water can't hide the stink of these sulfur rocks.
reading_mnemonic: You throw the <kanji>sulfur</kanji> rock as hard as you can, but it hits someone in the back of the head. Oh no, it's <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter. You try to apologize and explain that you just didn't want this stinky trash rock, I mean, sulfur rock near you. You didn't know he was there.
meaning_hint: If you haven't smelled sulfur before, it smells a bit like rotten eggs. Something that definitely belongs in the trash.
reading_hint: Ryu looks down at you, holding the sulfurous rock in one hand. Then he uppercuts you with it! Shoryuken!

meaning_mnemonic: If you spill something <radical>red</radical> on yourself in <radical>winter</radical> you should <kanji>pardon</kanji> yourself immediately and try to find something else to wear. It's very dangerous to wear red in winter, and even if it seems rude, make sure you pardon yourself right away and get that taken care of!
reading_mnemonic: If you don't <kanji>pardon</kanji> yourself fast enough the winter <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ) will burst in and cast a spell on you, taking away all the clothes that have red on them! Now you have to pardon yourself for being naked!
meaning_hint: Imagine spilling red wine or red paint down your shirt. Quickly pardon yourself and get changed now!
reading_hint: Imagine not pardoning yourself fast enough and having the winter shaman spirit away whatever you got red on. That's embarrassing.

meaning_mnemonic: You see <radical>seven</radical> <radical>sword</radical>s just sitting in a <radical>hole</radical> and so you decide to <kanji>steal</kanji> them. They were just left in that hole after all, it's not like anyone was using them. And even if they were, they'll never know you were the one to steal them, right?
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>steal</kanji> the swords and realize they aren't just seven random swords, they're from the famous sword <reading>sets</reading> (せつ) they sell online. This particular set is from the Seven Samurai movie. Yes! What a great steal!
meaning_hint: Imagine seeing seven swords just lying abandoned in a hole. Feel the urge to steal them all. Do it, do it!
reading_hint: Imagine your delight as you look up the different sword sets online and realize you happened to steal the best one!

meaning_mnemonic: Deep in your <radical>soul</radical>, you feel like you want to pull your <radical>fang</radical>s out by the <radical>root</radical>, but instead you let out a loud <kanji>sigh</kanji>. You're one of the only people you know that has fangs, and it weighs heavily on your soul.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>sigh</kanji> again and say, "I wish I was just a normal <reading>guy</reading> (がい)." I swear the rest of me is a guy like the rest of you. *sigh*
meaning_hint: Imagine all of the other people staring and pointing, the kids making fun of you, no one seeing you as equal. And just... sigh. Deep down, you want to get rid of your fangs, but you can't do it.
reading_hint: Now you're staring in the mirror at yourself. You sigh loudly and take out the pliers. You'll be a guy like everyone else. S-soon. As soon as you can muster up enough strength to stop sighing and rip these fangs out.

meaning_mnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> on your <radical>husband</radical> to <kanji>aid</kanji> him. There's something about a spouse's touch that just makes you feel better. Have you ever had someone rub your back while you were hurt or sick or sad? It's nice, right?
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, your husband wouldn't need your <kanji>aid</kanji> if he wasn't such a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). He walks into doors, he falls down the stairs, he touches the plate when the waiter says it's hot. And every single time your fool husband hurts himself, you aid him.
meaning_hint: Imagine rubbing your husband's back with your fingers to aid him. You want him to feel better. You want him to get that comforting feeling that this kind of touching gives.
reading_hint: That's true love, my friend.

meaning_mnemonic: Throw a <radical>drunkard</radical> in a cage with a <radical>tiger</radical> and some <radical>spikes</radical> and the tiger will have the time of its life <kanji>play</kanji>ing with them! Think of all the tiger play time possibilities!
reading_mnemonic: If you aren't the tiger, watching it <kanji>play</kanji> is a little like watching someone's head get cut off with a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). In fact, you don't want to watch it play at all anymore.
meaning_hint: If you were the tiger how would you play with the drunkard?
reading_hint: What would you rather watch? A guillotine at work or a tiger playing like this?

meaning_mnemonic: If you only let <radical>oneself</radical> (yourself) into your <radical>heart</radical>, you'll end up <kanji>mourning</kanji> all of the loves and friendships you could have had but didn't let in.
reading_mnemonic: While you're in <kanji>mourning</kanji>, you feel a sharp pain in your crying eyes. A <reading>key</reading> (き) comes sliding out with your tears. You look around wildly, but you're still alone. Is this... the key of mourning?
meaning_hint: Picture yourself all alone. You never let anyone into your heart but yourself, and now you're mourning the life and friends you could have had.
reading_hint: You try to push the key back into your body. Back into your eye, you even try to swallow it, but it comes back out. Finally you look down at your mourning heart and try the key there. It clicks. And opens.

meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> gets into a <radical>net</radical> that's im<radical>prison</radical>ing a bunch of <radical>insect</radical>s, the insects are going to get all <kanji>muddy</kanji>. The net gets dragged along with the tsunami water and the insects can't escape, so they just get covered in the yucky stuff.
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately for the insects, these <kanji>muddy</kanji> conditions have created the perfect habitat for <reading>duck</reading>s (だく). And muddy insects already imprisoned in a muddy net is a duck's favorite treat!
meaning_hint: Picture a net prison full of insects being dragged by a tsunami getting muddier and muddier. Poor buggles!
reading_hint: Picture a bunch of muddy ducks munching on some muddy trapped insects, in some muddy leftover tsunami water.

meaning_mnemonic: You're walking down the street when you see <radical>twenty</radical> <radical>big</radical> <radical>cross</radical>es lined up in front of you. Crosses creep you out and this is way too many for you to keep walking so you decide to <kanji>run</kanji> as fast as you can to get past them.
reading_mnemonic: You figure since you're already on the <kanji>run</kanji> that you might as well <reading>hone</reading> (ほん) your running skills while you make your way down this scary, cross lined street.
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to run down a street with twenty big crosses lining it. It's so scary that you keep running faster and faster as you go.
reading_hint: Imagine distracting yourself from the scary crosses by honing your running skills while you do it.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>triceratops</radical> standing in front of a full <radical>moon</radical> and you can't help but see a really strong <kanji>resemblance</kanji>. The triceratops has that round frill thing behind its horns and it looks just like the round full moon. Who knew that a triceratops would resemble the moon!
reading_mnemonic: The first person to see this <kanji>resemblance</kanji> was the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). That's why he decided to put a triceratops head with the moon behind it on his mon (family crest). Now he's known as the full moon dinosaur shogun, and he's very famous.
meaning_hint: Look at the triceratops. Now look at the moon. See the resemblance? Uncanny, don't you think?
reading_hint: Imagine being the Shougun and seeing the resemblance between a triceratops and the moon for the first time. You'd put that on all your stuff too, if you were him.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> is stuck in a <radical>prison</radical> under the <radical>ground</radical> and it's starting to <kanji>rot</kanji> and <kanji>decay</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You get close to the <kanji>rot</kanji>ting tree and see some rotten <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう) hanging from its branches. No!
meaning_hint: Trees can't live without sunlight, and it's too damp underground in a prison. The water gets all moldy and the tree will rot and die.
reading_hint: This poor cucumber tree, left in this ground prison to rot. There's nothing worse than eating a rotten cucumber.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>samurai</radical> puts his <radical>forehead</radical> on the <radical>table</radical> next to his <radical>weapon</radical>. He is just a <kanji>husk</kanji> of his former self. This <kanji>shell</kanji> of a samurai has nothing left.
reading_mnemonic: The only way to bring a <kanji>husk</kanji> of a samurai back to life is to have a <reading>cock</reading> (かく) crow right in his ear.
meaning_hint: He's just sitting there, he can't even pick up his weapon or raise his head anymore.
reading_hint: "Co co co co cooooock!" the cock screams, shocking the samurai back into his lifeless shell. You did it!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>receive</radical> radical and <kanji>receive</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.
reading_mnemonic: The best place to <kanji>receive</kanji> anything is in <reading>きょう</reading>と. The city is famous for the quality of everything they make, so if you’re in きょうと, keep your fingers crossed that you’ll receive something.
meaning_hint: Receive this nice, easy kanji with thanks!
reading_hint: Imagine being in きょうと and receiving something wonderful from a passerby in the street. What did you receive? How does it feel to receive something so great?

meaning_mnemonic: You have to reach a certain <radical>number</radical> of <radical>flowers</radical> and <radical>tsunami</radical>s hits to become a legitimate <kanji>fiefdom</kanji>. At least in the old days. If you don't meet one of these conditions, like if you get hit by tsunamis all the time but you only have two or three flowers that will grow, you can't be your own fiefdom.
reading_mnemonic: The most famous <kanji>fiefdom</kanji> checker is <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo. He takes his Millennium Falcon and swoops around checking for flowers and signs of past tsunamis and then he'll either say you can or cannot be your own fiefdom. Thanks Han Solo!
meaning_hint: Imagine being the person who has to check whether each fiefdom has enough flowers and tsunamis per year to keep their status.
reading_hint: Picture Han Solo flying around Japan checking to make sure all the fiefdoms are up to code.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>sweet</radical> <radical>woman</radical> sits under a <radical>tree</radical> and works as a <kanji>mediator</kanji>. If you're having a fight with someone, bring them to the sweet woman under the tree and she'll mediate for the two of you. Problem solved! Thanks sweet lady!
reading_mnemonic: You go to pay the woman for being your <kanji>mediator</kanji> for you two but she just says, "<reading>Bye</reading> ばい)." You try to hand her some money and she stares into your eyes. "Bye." Oh... okay, bye then.
meaning_hint: Imagine the sweet tree woman being a mediator for you and your arch rival. What she says fixes everything between you two!
reading_hint: Imagine her refusing to let you pay her for her mediator services and just saying "bye" over and over until you leave. Weird.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>husband</radical> was wearing his <radical>cleat</radical>s when he accidentally stepped on a <radical>bird</radical>. Luckily this particular bird is a <kanji>chicken</kanji>, and chickens are really durable!
reading_mnemonic: The only way to stop the <kanji>chicken</kanji> from putting on your husband's cleats and stepping on him to death in revenge is to feed it the <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい) you were making.
meaning_hint: Picture the sad, stepped on chicken. It's angrily clucking and chasing your husband around your yard for what he did.
reading_hint: This cake was for your husband, but now he gets nothing. The chicken gets to eat all of the cake in front of him instead.

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to take a <radical>flag</radical> out of the <radical>mouth</radical> of a <radical>mantis</radical> you have to make a formal <kanji>request</kanji> first. Mantises don't like to just give things away, and they're kind of mean so if you try to steal the flag it will probably bite you. So make a formal, polite request if you want it.
reading_mnemonic: After you <kanji>request</kanji> the mantis give you its flag it <reading>shock</reading>s (しょく) you by actually spitting it out of its mouth at your feet. You didn't think your request would work, or even if the mantis understood human speech, but it shocked you by doing exactly what you asked!
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to request a flag from a scary mantis that has it sitting in it's mouth. Will it give it to you? Will it bite you? You'll have to wait and see.
reading_hint: Imagine your shock at finding out the mantis not only understood your request, but agreed! This is great but also makes you wonder what other creatures can understand you...

meaning_mnemonic: When your <radical>scooter</radical> has a <radical>fault</radical>, you need to get an <kanji>alternate</kanji>. Hopefully your insurance will cover it too.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>alternate</kanji> scooter is made entirely of <reading>鉄</reading> (てつ). It's hard and cold and not very comfortable when you sit on it. That's why you chose to keep it as an alternate and not as your everyday scooter.
meaning_hint: You have a scooter that you drive every single day, but if you notice a fault, you need to send it in to get it fixed. That's when you break out your alternate scooter.
reading_hint: Imagine riding on your alternate 鉄 scooter. Feel the hard iron under your bum and the loud sounds it makes as you scoot down the street. I hope your normal scooter is fixed soon.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>tree</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> has been sitting directly on your <kanji>spine</kanji> for weeks straight. The pain is starting to become unbearable, and it seems like no one wants to help you.
reading_mnemonic: Just as your <kanji>spine</kanji> seems to be getting used to the pressure, the tree turkey unleashes a cry: "<reading>TWEE</reading>T (つい)!" To your horror, a flock of tree turkeys emerge from the trees.
meaning_hint: Tree turkeys are absolutely huge, and this exceptionally large one has been sitting right on top of your spine for what feels like ages. Imagine the pain of a huge turkey pressing down on your spine for weeks, perhaps months. How are you still holding on?!
reading_hint: You desperately try to escape from the weight of the tree turkey still sitting on top of your spine, but to no avail. The only thing you can see is dozens of tree turkeys approaching to plant themselves firmly on top of your spine, screaming "TWEET, TWEET".

meaning_mnemonic: Put <radical>thread</radical> in the <radical>mouth</radical> of a worm while the <radical>moon</radical> is out and it will poop out some <kanji>silk</kanji>. That's why they call them silkworms, didn't you know? But it has to be while the moon is out, otherwise normal thread will come right back out the other end of the worm. The moon is what makes the magic happen.
reading_mnemonic: Wearing <kanji>silk</kanji> is the <reading>key</reading> (き) to not being <reading>nude</reading> (ぬ). Wear it!
meaning_hint: Picture feeding some thread into the mouth of a worm as the light from the full moon shines in on you. See the silk come out of the worm's butt and into your hands.
reading_hint: Picture yourself wearing nothing but silk. Silk shirt, silk pants, even silk underwear. It feels great against the skin! And most importantly, you're not nude.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>building</radical> <radical>clown</radical> is the only clown allowed to <kanji>accompany</kanji> you. You hate all clowns, and so does the rest of your town. But this clown is really good at building buildings, and he's allowed to leave his part of town and accompany you while you fix buildings together.
reading_mnemonic: He can only <kanji>accompany</kanji> you for so long though and soon it's time to say <reading>bye</reading> (ばい) again.
meaning_hint: I know it's been a long time, but try not to mix this one up with 	部, if you can. Remember, the clown building is in another part of town and the clowns aren't supposed to leave. But the building clown is okay to accompany you through the rest of the city.
reading_hint: Wave bye to the clown, he'll accompany you on your building job tomorrow, but right now he has to go home and be with the rest of his clown people. He can't accompany you after dark.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>clown</radical> takes a <radical>knife</radical> and <kanji>divide</kanji>s himself. First he divides himself into two pieces, then he's in four pieces, then... oh why won't he stop?!
reading_mnemonic: The clown finally stops trying to <kanji>divide</kanji> himself, and you can't stand to look at all of him. You grab a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball and bowl all of his divided parts over!
meaning_hint: Instead of dying, the clown divides into slightly thinner clowns.
reading_hint: The many clowns crash to the floor as the bowling ball plows through their... his(?) divided bodies. You can't bear to look anymore.

meaning_mnemonic:
If you <radical>say</radical> the <radical>lineup</radical> of your family members that have lived on this earth under the <radical>sun</radical>, you're really explaining your <kanji>genealogy</kanji>.

This kanji has a second meaning too. It means score, as in a musical score or sheet music. So if your sheet music <radical>say</radical>s the <radical>line up</radical> of chords, especially <radical>sun</radical>nny notes, that's the <kanji>score</kanji> of a song. Those sunny notes are loved by your ancestors.
reading_mnemonic: If you can't tell the difference between your <kanji>genealogy</kanji> and a musical <kanji>score</kanji>, you're a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ) and I can't help you. You aren't a fool, are you? You know the difference.
meaning_hint: Say the line up of your family under the sun, then sing some sunny chords to match. You'll know your genealogy and a musical score!
reading_hint: Your family genealogy and a musical score aren't even close to being the same thing. Memorize these or everyone will think you're a fool.

meaning_mnemonic: You can only manage to avoid a <radical>tsunami</radical> by standing <radical>above</radical> it on a <radical>small</radical> <radical>stool</radical> if you are very <kanji>graceful</kanji>. If you aren't graceful enough the tsunami will knock you and the stool over and you'll be swept away like everyone else.
reading_mnemonic: No matter how <kanji>graceful</kanji> you are, the tsunami still <reading>shook</reading> (しゅく) the small stool. That's where your gracefulness came in. The stool shook, but you didn't. You balanced yourself and refused to go down.
meaning_hint: Imagine being graceful enough to balance on a small stool above a raging tsunami! Sooo cool!
reading_hint: It doesn't matter how badly the stool shook, you stayed graceful and didn't fall!

meaning_mnemonic: You hoist your <radical>towel</radical> above your <radical>table</radical>, <radical>drop</radical> into the water and <kanji>sail</kanji> away. You turned the towel into an actual sail and you're balancing on top of the table, which is now a boat.
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>sail</kanji> off into the ocean you hear a familiar voice shout out, "Ahoy!" <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo calls. He's also got a towel sail, but it's on the Millennium Falcon, which is half-submerged in the water.
meaning_hint: Imagine sailing out into the open sea, drops of water hitting your face, as the towel sail catches the air and carries you away.
reading_hint: You sail nervously past Han Solo who also seems to be sailing, though in worse shape than you. But he keeps throwing you finger guns so you figure it's best to just sail on and pretend this never happened.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>soul</radical> on a <radical>cross</radical> covered in <radical>flowers</radical> with <radical>shellfish</radical> at its feet and you suddenly <kanji>resent</kanji> them. It's not like you want to be the one up there on the cross, but so many people left flowers and shellfish for them. It should have been you...
reading_mnemonic: You realize you <kanji>resent</kanji> them because it looks like they're having <reading>fun</reading> (ふん). You aren't having fun, and you wanna be! How dare they have fun when you aren't!
meaning_hint: Picture the soul on the cross, flowers all around it and shellfish at its feet. Feel your resentment grow.
reading_hint:
You wanna have fun hanging out on a cross with flowers and all the shellfish you can eat. Your resentment grows and grows as you watch the soul having everything you suddenly want.

Fun fact: when you see 賁 in a kanji, the reading is probably going to be ふん.

meaning_mnemonic: Pour all of your <radical>alcohol</radical> in the <radical>pool</radical> to <kanji>serve</kanji> all your guests at once! They can all help themselves to as much or as little alcohol as they want! What a great idea!
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>serve</kanji> your guests (and yourself) you look over and see <reading>Shaq</reading> (しゃく) also trying to serve them. And by serving I mean getting served because he's throwing them into the pool.
meaning_hint: Imagine serving all of your guests their alcohol by dumping it all in the pool! Now jump in and drink all you want!
reading_hint: Picture Shaq serving your guests by tossing them into your alcohol filled pool. Thanks, Shaq... I guess.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Gambler</radical>s usually only see the <radical>sun</radical> at <kanji>dawn</kanji>. They gamble all day and through the night, and if they leave it's at the crack of dawn to get home, clean up, and then go back. I haven't known a gambler who's seen the sun ... well ever!
reading_mnemonic: If you're up at <kanji>dawn</kanji> you'll also see the <reading>あか</reading>(red) <reading>つき</reading>(moon). The あかつき only happens as the sun comes up at dawn so usually only gamblers see it. But if you get up early, you can see it too!
meaning_hint: Imagine being a gambler and seeing the bright dawn sun when you come out of the casino or pachinko parlor. It burns your eyes! Evil dawn, get back inside quick!
reading_hint: Imagine getting up at dawn and seeing the あかつき in the sky near the sun. It's so beautiful!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> does a <radical>dance</radical> on top of the <radical>tree</radical> to show his <kanji>greatness</kanji> to everyone around. If he dances all the way up there on top of the tree, then no one can deny his <kanji>excellence</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: If you don't recognize the leader's <kanji>greatness</kanji> he will throw <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ) all over you (it's a special mix of ketchup and catsup). And it's ketsup because it looks like blood. The blood he'll spill if you don't look up and clap at his great dancing.
meaning_hint: "Look upon your leader's greatness!" he shouts, doing a jig around on the branches.
reading_hint: "Oh leader, your greatness is so... great!" you shout up at him. He's holding the ketsup bottle over your head and staring into your eyes as he dances in the tree. He isn't sure if he wants to dump it on you yet. He isn't sure if you really recognize his excellence.

meaning_mnemonic: This is a <radical>gold</radical> <radical>roof</radical>, <radical>correct</radical>? Better put a <kanji>lock</kanji> on it, then.
reading_mnemonic: The person who put the <kanji>lock</kanji> on the gold roof was none other than... <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう)?!
meaning_hint: It's only natural that you'd want to put a lock on a gold roof so that nobody could steal it.
reading_hint: How did Joe, a simple farmhand, get hold of such a valuable gold roof? Either way, the lock he’s put on it is incredibly tough, so there’s no way you could make off with his gold roof anyway.

meaning_mnemonic: If you touch <radical>ice</radical> for too long you'll get <radical>frostbite</radical> and that's a really hard thing to <kanji>endure</kanji>. A lot of people can't, and die. Then again, you managed to endure touching ice long enough to get frostbite in the first place, so maybe this is baby stuff for you.
reading_mnemonic: The best people in the world at being able to <kanji>endure</kanji> the pain are the <reading>shino</reading>bi (しの) ninjas! They train by hanging out in frozen areas all the time and don't complain at all. Maybe if the pain gets to be too much you can go ask them for advice.
meaning_hint: Imagine having to endure the pain of frostbite because you were a jerk who put his hand on ice for a few hours.
reading_hint: Imagine if you were one of these cool Shinobi ninjas that could endure the extreme cold and pain of frostbite!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> <radical>rake</radical>d all the <radical>mountain</radical>s down into flat land so he could see everyone in the kingdom from his castle windows. You should share your <kanji>congratulations</kanji> with him. Now, quick tell him this was a great idea.
reading_mnemonic: The moment you say "<kanji>Congratulations</kanji>!" you've already started to <reading>miss</reading> (みず) those beautiful mountains. But you can't let missing them ruin your congratulations! Don't let the king know!
meaning_hint: Imagine saying "Congratulations!" to a king who just ordered his servants to rake down all the pretty mountains in his kingdom.
reading_hint: Imagine how hollow your congratulation sounds when you really miss those mountains. This king doesn't deserve your nice words.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>flowers</radical> that grow on your <radical>roof</radical> that look like a sleeping <radical>bear</radical> is <kanji>sedge</kanji>. Sedge grow all over the world!
reading_mnemonic: The next time you see a <kanji>sedge</kanji> bite into it. It tastes like <reading>suga</reading>r (すが)! Luckily, it don't taste like bear.
meaning_hint: You've probably seen them before, you just didn't know what they were called! Looks like a green sleeping bear bum, right?
reading_hint: Imagine biting into a sedge flower and tasting the yummy sugary taste. Mmmm.

meaning_mnemonic: As the <radical>tsunami</radical> hits your <radical>scooter</radical> and your <radical>car</radical>, it alters the wave, creating <kanji>ripples</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Ripples</kanji> come off of your scooter and car from when they were hit by a tsunami. More importantly, those things were <reading>ren</reading>tals (れん), so ripples or not, it doesn't matter if they were hit by a tsunami.
meaning_hint: They're hard to see because a tsunami is pretty big, but your scooter and car do create ripples in the water as it goes past.
reading_hint: As you watch the ripples come off your car and scooter, be sure to imagine the relief you'd feel if you knew they were rentals and not something owned by you.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> wants you to make a <radical>rocket</radical> that looks <kanji>glassy</kanji>. It won't be very stable, and it probably won't work, but making it glassy and pretty is the only way to make the king approve.
reading_mnemonic: You take the <kanji>glassy</kanji> rocket and try to stuff some thin <reading>reed</reading>s (り) inside to support it. But because it's clear and glassy the king sees them at the last second.
meaning_hint: Imagine a king who won't accept a rocket that isn't made of glassy material. He's not very smart...
