reading_hint: Picture the king admiring himself in the glassy surface and then his face contorts. He smashes his fist through the rocket and rips out the reeds. "REEDS AREN'T GLASSY!" he cries, and starts hitting you with them.

meaning_mnemonic: Going from a <radical>scooter</radical> to a <radical>helicopter</radical> that's <radical>big</radical> even for yourself (<radical>oneself</radical>), is a huge <kanji>transition</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You decide one <kanji>transition</kanji> isn't enough, so you transition to a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) too. Now you're a big half horse-half human creature and you'll fit in the big helicopter better.
meaning_hint: It's an upward transition, so that's nice, but it's still going to take you a long time to get used to using a big helicopter instead of a scooter.
reading_hint: A centaur is just a human halfway between transitioning into a horse, so it isn't even that hard to do!

meaning_mnemonic: You have to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to make an <radical>exit</radical> because you're so <kanji>clumsy</kanji>. You can't seem to get the door to open, your hands just slip around on the knob uselessly. Instead, you take your fingers and stick them into the door frame, pulling the whole thing off the hinges so you can leave.
reading_mnemonic: After you make your <kanji>clumsy</kanji> mistake you realize that these doors come in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). And now you have to try to open another door. And another door. So many sets of doors!
meaning_hint: Of course, you're too clumsy to do that right either, you accidentally threw the door backwards and hurt some people...
reading_hint: Imagine trying to use your clumsy fingers all over the sets of doors. You keep falling over and hurting yourself. Why is it so hard to leave?!

meaning_mnemonic: If there are <radical>mountain</radical>s <radical>above</radical> and <radical>below</radical> you, that means you're on a <kanji>mountain pass</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: It's only on this type of <kanji>mountain pass</kanji> that you'll be able to find the rare and adorable <reading>To</reading>kyo <reading>ge</reading>cko (とうげ). These tiny little critters can only be found on the ridges going above and below the mountains in Tokyo. There are like six mountains in Tokyo, so the next time you're scaling the ridges keep your eyes open for the Tokyo gecko!
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself walking along a mountain pass. Look up at the mountains above you, and down at the mountains below you.
reading_hint: Picture yourself hiking along a mountain pass in Tokyo and spotting a little Tokyo gecko run up and down on them. It's adorable and totally worth the climb.

meaning_mnemonic: If someone makes a <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>horse</radical>, you know it's <kanji>deliberate</kanji>. You can't accidentally make an entire horse out of bamboo.
reading_mnemonic: As you admire all the <kanji>deliberate</kanji> details in this bamboo horse, the ghost of <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu appears before you! "I deliberately made this horse to show you how important being deliberate is. You should be absolutely certain and decisive about your decisions."
meaning_hint: Someone sat down and deliberately planned the entire horse out, collected the materials, and made it by hand.
reading_hint: Tokugawa Ieyasu then poofs out of existence and so does the bamboo horse.

meaning_mnemonic: There were lots of things that were <radical>above</radical> you when you were <radical>small</radical> that you would need a <radical>stool</radical> to reach, so you just asked your <kanji>aunt</kanji> or <kanji>uncle</kanji> to get them for you.
reading_mnemonic: You ask your <kanji>aunt</kanji> and <kanji>uncle</kanji> to help you reach your special <reading>o</reading>bi (お) from high up in the closet.
meaning_hint: Your parents didn't want you having those things, which is why they put them up high, but your aunt and uncle would always spoil you and get them for you.
reading_hint: You aren't supposed to wear your obi unless it's a special occasion, but your aunt and uncle don't know that. Now you can run around with it on your head and your parents can't stop you by putting it up high!

meaning_mnemonic: If you can <radical>stop</radical> a <radical>turkey</radical> by putting a <radical>spoon</radical> in its face you'll know it's a <kanji>female</kanji> turkey. A male turkey will peck the spoon out of your hand and run away. But a female turkey will wonder why you're putting a spoon there, stop, and look up at you wondering if you're okay.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>female</kanji> turkey then runs around making a <reading>mess</reading> (めす) of things trying to figure out what's wrong with you. She kicks around her hay, water bowl, and scratches at your pants, making more mess marks on them. But you don't move and she gets even more worried.
meaning_hint: Picture the female turkey stopping when it sees you holding out a spoon. Why are you doing that human? it thinks. Are you sick?
reading_hint: Imagine watching a female turkey make a mess of her own cage because she thinks you need help. How sweet.

meaning_mnemonic: If there is <radical>dirt</radical> in a <radical>crab trap</radical>, it's a lot for the crabs to <kanji>endure</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to make the little crab that <kanji>endure</kanji>d being stuck in a dirty crab trap your pet, and put a <reading>colla</reading>r (こら) round its little neck.
meaning_hint: Imagine a little crab getting stuck in a crab trap with dirt and other crabs. Endure the horror as long as you can, little friend.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself putting a collar around the crab's neck, then adding a leash and taking your crab for a walk around your neighborhood.

meaning_mnemonic: We want you to say things with your <radical>mouth</radical> right <radical>now</radical> because it's <kanji>recital</kanji> time! Go, perform please. What do you mean you didn't know the recital was right now? Well, tough luck. Recite!
reading_mnemonic: Here, if you are too afraid to start the <kanji>recital</kanji> now drink this <reading>gin</reading> (ぎん) to get your courage up. But once you're done drinking it the recital starts!
meaning_hint: The recital is happening now. So open your mouth and start already!
reading_hint: Drink gin before all your recitals! It'll help!

meaning_mnemonic: Making a mnemonic for a <radical>crab trap</radical> is <kanji>VERY</kanji> hard. So hard that I couldn't come up with one. But it's very possible that one of you will!
reading_mnemonic: You know what else is <kanji>very</kanji> something? <reading>Jean</reading>s (じん). Jeans are very expensive. Jeans are very tight. Jeans are very popular. Jeans are very a lot of things.
meaning_hint: How hard is it to come up with a good mnemonic for a crab trap? Very hard!
reading_hint: How many sentences can you make with very and jeans? More than me I hope.

meaning_mnemonic: Under the <radical>mountain</radical>'s <radical>roof</radical> are people using <radical>jackhammer</radical>s to <kanji>worship</kanji> the gods. The noise from their jackhammering echos up through the mountain and up toward the sky where the gods live. As long as each person uses their own jackhammer, the sound will be loud enough to reach them.
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, the people who live around here can also hear you and the others <kanji>worship</kanji> in the mountain and they're going to <reading>sue</reading> (すう) you for the noise.
meaning_hint: Man your own jackhammer! (And worship the gods.)
reading_hint: You try to argue that you can't sue someone for the way they choose to worship, but they say they're all deaf now, all the animals have gone, and no one sleeps anymore because of the shaking. Yikes, they might have a case!

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>tree</radical> and your <radical>hat</radical> protects you from the <radical>water</radical>, but the hat itself is now covered in <kanji>lacquer</kanji>. You take it off and see that it's shiny and hard and protected.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to scoop up the rest of the <kanji>lacquer</kanji> water and use it on your furniture to protect it from your <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ). Now it can't bite or scratch or ruin any of your things anymore!
meaning_hint: Picture your hat and all of the other things the tsunami + tree water touched. It's all been hardened with a thick coating of the lacquer.
reading_hint: Watch as your shih tzu tries to bite your now lacquered chair. It gets its teeth around the leg, but it's hard as a rock. The shih tzu starts sneezing and spitting, letting go. Looks like it tastes bad too. Perfect!

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>mountain</radical> in the shape of a <radical>turtle shell</radical> sticking out into the ocean. It's a <kanji>cape</kanji>! No, not the kind you wear, the geographical kind. When you're on land, most capes look like turtle shell mountains sticking out into the sea. Do you know any capes like that near where you live?
reading_mnemonic: You decide to walk along this <kanji>cape</kanji> because it looks like the perfect place to get rid of your old <reading>me</reading>at <reading>sack</reading> (みさき). It's starting to smell, but the ocean breeze from the cape pulls the meat sack out of your hands and off into the sky (then probably into the sea too).
meaning_hint: Picture the turtle shell shaped cape. Feel the salty wind in your hair!
reading_hint: The next time you go to the cape, remember to bring your old, used meat sack. You can toss it right into the ocean and it won't bother you ever again! Just make sure no one sees you.

meaning_mnemonic: Move <radical>thread</radical> in the right <radical>direction</radical> and you'll end up <kanji>spinning</kanji> it into yarn. Then you can spin that yarn into a sweater or a scarf with your favorite half tofu half fugu mascot on it!
reading_mnemonic: While you're <kanji>spinning</kanji> you realize that you've always wanted to make your own <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball. And bowling ball cover. And bowling ball shoes.
meaning_hint: Imagine spinning thread into whatever your imagination wants.
reading_hint: Picture all of these thread-made bowling items. You're a really big bowling fan, huh?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>stone</radical> that looks like a <radical>turkey</radical> <radical>boil</radical>ing underwater is just a <kanji>reef</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>reef</kanji> is the <reading>Shou</reading>gun's (しょう) favorite place to be nowadays. He sits next to the water, and stares down into it for hours.
meaning_hint: It may seem like there are a bunch of turkeys under there, but it's just the shape the reef takes. Though I guess if you're looking from above the water it really does look like a bunch of turkeys freaking out.
reading_hint: You don't know if the Shougun likes the reef because he likes the beauty of the ocean, or if he's fantasizing about turkeys boiling. He's a... complex man.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>barracks</radical> radical and the <kanji>barracks</kanji> kanji are the same!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Barracks</kanji> are pretty big, so they weigh a <reading>ton</reading> (とん). And they probably take tons of materials to build.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
reading_hint: Imagine trying to lift some barracks. They weigh a ton! How are you supposed to do this? Why would you even try to lift something that obviously weighs a couple tons?!

meaning_mnemonic: You meet a <radical>woman</radical> whose <radical>mouth</radical> is <radical>big</radical> and you instantly know this is the person you want to <kanji>marry</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>marry</kanji> this woman right away and you both go straight to an <reading>inn</reading> (いん) together!
meaning_hint: When you think about the person you want to marry, picture their mouth wide in a big, beautiful smile, a wide-open laugh. That's the lady for you, alright!
reading_hint: When you get to the inn all you do is make her laugh and smile so you can bask in her bright, big, mouth smiles.

meaning_mnemonic: You try to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to cast <radical>doubt</radical> on people by using them to <kanji>imitate</kanji> each other. Right now you're using your fingers as finger puppets, imitating one of your friends to the others.
reading_mnemonic: Everyone thinks the friend you were trying to <kanji>imitate</kanji> is the imitation now and they have them in a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). You even doubt if they're an imitation or if your fingers are the imitation.
meaning_hint: Picture your angry friend standing up next to your fingers, shouting, "I'm the real me! That's just an imitation!" The rest of your friends look nervously at each other. They doubt this person is real now.
reading_hint: Okay, so that's a lie, Your fingers are definitely imitating them, but you don't want to end up in the guillotine so you keep your mouth shut.

meaning_mnemonic: If you see someone <radical>eye</radical>ing your <radical>womb</radical> you're probably going to be weirded out by how <kanji>friendly</kanji> they're acting. You only let people who are really, really close to you look at your womb. So if this guy is acting that friendly he should have introduced himself first.
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>friendly</kanji> guy comes up to you and start to introduce himself, "<reading>ぼく</reading>-" but you stop him. He's been getting way too friendly with your womb and you don't want to hear who he is. He can ぼく at someone else, you're out.
meaning_hint: Imagine having someone you aren't friendly with trying to have a look at your womb. No thank you.
reading_hint: Imagine only letting this guy start to introduce himself with ぼく before walking the heck outta there. Just because he was acting friendly doesn't mean he was actually your friend.

meaning_mnemonic: You see a <radical>gate</radical> with a <radical>tree</radical> growing through it and it reminds you that you need more <kanji>leisure</kanji> time. You walk up to the gate tree and curl up under its branches. No one will bother you because the tree is blocking the gate from being used.
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>leisure</kanji>ly nap, you hear a gruff voice, "Ah, you're done too, huh?" It's Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! He's spending his leisure time relaxing under this abandoned gate just like you.
meaning_hint: Imagine spending your leisure time curled up under a tree that's growing through a gate. It's so cozy and secluded!
reading_hint: But wait. Isn't Genghis Khan dead now? He winks at you, "Leisure time is for the dead." He smiles, and fades away, disappearing into the tree.

meaning_mnemonic: When under<radical>ground</radical> <radical>music</radical> makes it out into the light of the <radical>sun</radical> (the public), it finally becomes <kanji>official</kanji>. Music isn't really real unless people hear it outside of those secret underground concerts.
reading_mnemonic: Once the music is <kanji>official</kanji>, you'll feel it in your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). When you listen to it outside, under the bright sun, your soul gets to finally hear it too.
meaning_hint: Think of all that music you listened to underground but not in the light of the sun. None of that was official music. None of it!
reading_hint: The official music penetrates into your soul, instead of just your ears, and it's affecting everyone else's souls too! Oh no... wait that's too many people. This was your song! No, it's too popular now, stop your souls from liking it! Ahhh!

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to <radical>say</radical> how <radical>eternity</radical> sounds you have to <kanji>compose</kanji> a poem. Or an essay. Or a song. <kanji>Recite</kanji> those deep thoughts about eternity and impress everyone who hears it.
reading_mnemonic: You decide to <kanji>compose</kanji> a poem about an <reading>a</reading>corn (えい). The tiny acorn is the perfect vessel for you to say how eternity feels to you.
meaning_hint: Try to compose a poem about eternity right now. Just jot it down somewhere.
reading_hint:
Let me recite the acorn poem to you now:

Acorns start small
Grow up big and tall
Spread their baby acorns for all
Live on for all eternity

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>drop</radical> to the ground in a <radical>rice paddy</radical>, and then <radical>drop</radical> down in front of the <radical>cross</radical> you keep on you because you're trying to make yourself as <kanji>lowly</kanji> as you can in front of God. He said to lower yourself and this is the only way you can interpret that.
reading_mnemonic: The only problem about being <kanji>lowly</kanji> is the <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). Everyone always told you that heat rises, but not here. The rice paddy is incredibly hot... unless oh no. Is that the heat of hell?
meaning_hint: Imagine dropping down in a rice paddy, then again in front of your cross. Be as lowly as you can make yourself.
reading_hint: You tried to make yourself lowly in front of God but this heat is probably a sign that you didn't do it well enough.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Every</radical> <radical>leader</radical> will come to <kanji>despise</kanji> their followers. In the beginning everything is fine and dandy, but eventually the leader's followers will ask for more and more things, eventually ridiculous things, and the leader will really despise them for it.
reading_mnemonic: The more you <kanji>despise</kanji> them, the more <reading>boo</reading>ze (ぶ) you drink. This is not smart or healthy, but it's what this kind of hate does to people.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself as a leader who's come to despise every single one of their followers. Look down at them in disgust as they ask you to grant them all a pony.
reading_hint: This is not a healthy way to handle things! If you despise someone you should talk to them, don't turn to booze. Don't be like every leader that's come before you.

meaning_mnemonic: You pour molten <radical>gold</radical> down <radical>three</radical> <radical>slide</radical>s while taking <radical>measurement</radical>s in order to make a <kanji>cast</kanji> of something. Whatever it is you're casting, it requires a lot of gold (three slides' worth!) and some very precise measurements.
reading_mnemonic: At last, you reveal what you've <kanji>cast</kanji>: a gold statue of <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう). It's a life-size cast, which is why you needed so much gold and such exact measurements.
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself carefully pouring the gold down each of your three slides. Really be careful — the measurements need to be exact, or the cast won't come out right.
reading_hint: Picture your majestic Chewbacca cast. You place it outside your front door to guard the entrance to your house. Wraaaaaaarrrrrrr!

meaning_mnemonic: By putting <radical>flowers</radical> in a <radical>net</radical>, you manage to catch a <radical>canine</radical>! But this just makes everyone <kanji>scorn</kanji> you.
reading_mnemonic: You notice everyone's <kanji>scorn</kanji> and respond by saying: "I <reading>bets</reading> (べつ) you I can catch me another one. I bets you I can."
meaning_hint: Your scorn is well deserved — an upstanding member of society doesn't go around snatching up innocent flower-loving canines with a net. Go to the pet store and buy one like a normal person.
reading_hint: Mistakenly assuming everyone is scorning you for not having caught enough canines, you go out to snatch up some more. Uh oh — I bets everyone is really gonna scorn you now!

meaning_mnemonic: If you stay up so long that you see the <radical>moon</radical> go down and the <radical>dawn</radical> come, people will say you have serious <kanji>guts</kanji>. It takes guts to stay up that late. More specifically, it takes a powerful <kanji>gallbladder</kanji>. Your gallbladder is sensitive to sleep changes, you know, so you risk a lot by staying up like that.
reading_mnemonic: You stayed awake outside for so long, just to prove you had <kanji>guts</kanji> and powerful <kanji>gallbladder</kanji>, that you also got a sweet <reading>tan</reading> (たん) out of it!
meaning_hint: Imagine the guts you'd need to have to stay up so late that you see the moon and the sun switch places! And to risk gallbladder damage?! That takes guts!
reading_hint: Imagine the deep tan you'd get after staying awake outside for such a long time. Tanning is also bad for your gallbladder! Man, you've really got guts to be getting tans like that.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> that is <radical>good</radical>. This fabled creature has never been seen, but you <kanji>wander</kanji> the lands in search of it.
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>wander</kanji>, you come across a clue. It is fish <reading>roe</reading> (ろう).
meaning_hint: You wander everywhere, with no particular direction in mind.
reading_hint: Wherever the good tsunami goes, it leaves delicious fish roe behind. That's what makes it a good tsunami.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>spirit</radical> wearing two <radical>mustache</radical>s must be <kanji>evil</kanji>. Everyone knows a mustache makes you evil, but two? And on a spirit? Yup, definitely evil.
reading_mnemonic: You're watching the <kanji>evil</kanji> mustache spirit when it starts to float toward your <reading>ca</reading>r (か). And it's... hey! It's trashing your car! You knew this guy was evil!
meaning_hint: Look at the evil spirit with two mustaches on its spirit face. Imagine its face in detail, especially those mustaches.
reading_hint: Imagine running around, flailing your arms, trying to get the spirit to stop ruining your car. But your hands go right through him and his two mustaches. You can't stop his evil doings.

meaning_mnemonic: You can only give a <radical>kiss</radical> of <radical>alcohol</radical> to <kanji>dairy</kanji>. If you add anymore than a kiss the dairy will curdle and you won't be able to drink it anymore. Bleh.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Dairy</kanji> isn't kept with other drinks, it needs to be placed on a <reading>rack</reading> (らく). I hope you've been using your dairy rack to store your dairy. If you haven't you should get one right away!
meaning_hint: Imagine trying to give more than a kiss of alcohol to dairy and watch it bubble and curdle and get all gross and chunky.
reading_hint: Look at all your dairy products: milk, yogurt, cheese, all of it sitting nicely on your dairy rack.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> <radical>stand</radical>s in the <radical>village</radical> you <kanji>long for</kanji>. It's the village you're from, and no matter how far you go, no matter how old you get, your soul is still there, and you <kanji>yearn</kanji> for it.
reading_mnemonic: No matter how much you <kanji>long for</kanji> it, you can't go back because <reading>a cougar</reading> (あこが) is in the way. Whenever you try to return to your village to be reunited with the soul you yearn for, a cougar is back, and won't let you in.
meaning_hint: Have you ever been away from your village? Maybe you're far away right now, studying Japanese alone. Can't you feel your body longing for your soul back home?
reading_hint: You long for a cougar to leave you alone! You yearn for a cougar to bother someone else!

meaning_mnemonic: If a pilot manages to land under a <radical>canopy</radical> with a <radical>helicopter</radical> that's pierced with a sideways <radical>barb</radical> in the <radical>heart</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> you should <kanji>congratulate</kanji> them! People should always be congratulated for doing good things, and this is one of the best things you've ever heard of!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Congratulate</kanji> the pilot with <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)!
meaning_hint: Can you imagine how difficult landing a helicopter is in just one of these horrible conditions? Congratulate them!
reading_hint: Cake is always the appropriate celebratory food.

meaning_mnemonic:
The kanji and the radical that look like this are exactly the same. They are both <kanji>Asia</kanji>. 

This can also mean <kanji>sub-</kanji>. As in subtropics, subspecies, subhuman… That kind of sub-! When it's not in a place name, 亜 will usually be using its sub- meaning.
reading_mnemonic: What's the first sound in <kanji>Asia</kanji>? It's <reading>A</reading> (あ). Nice and easy!
meaning_hint: 亜 is associated with Asia because of its あ reading, but sometimes you might see it used as ateji for other place names as well. And don't forget that sub- meaning!
reading_hint: A is for Asia!

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>tsunami</radical> that's heading for your village is <radical>fat</radical> you should try to <kanji>select</kanji> a different one to hit instead. You don't want to select fat tsunamis because they do more damage.
reading_mnemonic: If you're having trouble <kanji>select</kanji>ing which one is the least fat just check to see which tsunamis are eating <reading>ta</reading>cos (た). Only fat tsunamis will be eating them, so just look for one without a taco.
meaning_hint: Imagine being at the tsunami store and selecting the least fat one there.
reading_hint: While you select the tsunami that's right for you. Keep an eye out for those fat taco-eating ones. And then stay away from them!

meaning_mnemonic: What kind of <radical>tree</radical> is <radical>spicy</radical>? A <kanji>Japanese birch</kanji>, or <kanji>birch</kanji>, obviously. There's nothing spicier than a Japanese birch!
reading_mnemonic: You can only cut down a <kanji>Japanese birch</kanji> using <reading>a zoo saw</reading> (あずさ) because a regular saw can't handle how spicy these trees are.
meaning_hint: Imagine licking the trunk of a Japanese birch tree and feeling the heat on your tongue.
reading_hint: Imagine using a zoo saw to cut down a Japanese birch tree. What's a zoo saw? It's a saw that's used in zoos, to build all the animal enclosures. And to cut down spicy trees.

meaning_mnemonic: People think <radical>tsunami</radical>s are only made of water but they have a <radical>few</radical> pieces of <kanji>sand</kanji> in there too. There's actually more sand in the ocean than most people think. And tsunamis can't just have water and sharks in them. A few grains of sand will always slip in.
reading_mnemonic: You already learnt the reading for 砂, the other kanji for <kanji>sand</kanji>. It should be easy to remember this one too, since it's also さ!
meaning_hint: Imagine how those few grains of sand feel to get swept up into a tsunami. They didn't ask for this. They just wanted to stay at the bottom of the sea.
reading_hint: As long as you remember 砂, you can remember さ.

meaning_mnemonic: If you ride a <radical>scooter</radical> with your <radical>fingers</radical> around an <radical>axe</radical> you're going to <kanji>die</kanji>. I mean, probably. There's a 99% chance you'll die.
reading_mnemonic:
You slip. The axe collides with your face. You <kanji>die</kanji>. But... wait! There's a bright, blue glow and suddenly you're un-dead. You watch in slow motion as your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) brings you back to life and takes the hit from the axe instead.

Wait, no!

It shatters.
meaning_hint: Picture someone else riding a scooter with an axe in their fingertips. That's so dangerous! One bump, one bad turn, and that axe slips and they'll die.
reading_hint: Your holy blue saber died for you. It saved you from certain death and now it died. After all you've been through together. All 60 levels. This is the end.

meaning_mnemonic: This <radical>cage</radical> is full of <radical>flowers</radical> on the <radical>right</radical> side to <kanji>hide</kanji> the fact that you're in here too.
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>hide</kanji> the ghost of <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu appears before you, inside the cage! His arms are crossed and he's shaking his head at you. "Do you think I managed to become the first shougun of Japan by hiding?"
meaning_hint: You don't really want to be in this cage, but there were some things you didn't want to do today, so you hid. Luckily these flowers block the right side of the cage and hide you completely.
reading_hint: "N-no?" you respond. "Wrong! I hid all the time. There's nothing wrong with hiding, you're doing a great job." he says, and disappears.

meaning_mnemonic: Under your own <radical>roof</radical>, a <radical>geoduck</radical> jumps out of the <radical>ground</radical> with a <radical>sword</radical> and stabs your husband/wife/marriage partner to death. Now you're a <kanji>widow</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You may be a <kanji>widow</kanji>, but that doesn't make you weak. You run outside, jump into your <reading>ca</reading>r (か), and run your entire house and the geoduck over.
meaning_hint: Imagine the geoduck laughing and screaming, "Now you're a widow! Now you're a widow!" flailing the sword around. It's awful.
reading_hint: It's just you and your car now, living the widow life, driving on into the sunset and forgetting your past.

meaning_mnemonic: You ate some <radical>grain</radical>s that made you so <radical>sick</radical> it feels like a <radical>knife</radical> is stabbing in your guts and all your poop is falling out. This is called <kanji>diarrhea</kanji>. Everyone's had diarrhea before, so you should know this feeling by now.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>diarrhea</kanji> is here. It's happening. And it smells so awful you can't breathe. You have to take some hollow <reading>reed</reading>s (り) and shove them up your nose and out the bathroom door so you can get some fresh air in you.
meaning_hint: Imagine back to the last time you ate something bad and it gave you diarrhea. It felt like someone was sticking you with a knife, didn't it?
reading_hint: You learned this reed technique from a group of ninjas who use them to breathe underwater, and you never thought you'd use them like this.

meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>dirt</radical>, you find a <radical>lid</radical> under the <radical>table</radical>. You lift it to reveal a deep <kanji>pit</kanji>. Why is there a <kanji>hole</kanji> under your table?
reading_mnemonic: When you reach the bottom of the <kanji>pit</kanji>, you hear someone scuffling around and the sounds of chains. You light a match and inch closer to see what it is. In the darkness, you see <reading>こう</reading>いち, starved and chained in a pit under your table.
meaning_hint: You pull away the lid and crawl inside. There is a ladder that takes you deep into the pit.
reading_hint: After you've nursed こういち back to health, he explains that he was put here by a clone こういち–the very same こういち who became emperor. He tells you he can think of around 61-70 different pain levels of revenge, but also that "it probably wouldn't be worth it, so let's just hang out for a while instead. Ooh, are those cookies?"

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> that you <radical>oversee</radical> are always <kanji>indigo</kanji>. You aren't sure why though...
reading_mnemonic: Wait, maybe these flowers are only <kanji>indigo</kanji> because there is something wrong with your <reading>eye</reading>s (あい)! Maybe your eyes are indigo and that's why it's all you see!
meaning_hint: Imagine if every flower you were supposed to oversee was indigo. Indigo everywhere!
reading_hint: Can you imagine having indigo-only seeing eyes? I can. Because of this awesome mnemonic.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Half</radical> of a <radical>rice paddy</radical> is always on the <kanji>shore</kanji>. Japan is surrounded by water, after all. So it only makes sense that half of every rice paddy is right next to the ocean shore, right?
reading_mnemonic: You decide to walk along this <kanji>shore</kanji> when you see <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo lounging in the sand. He's waving for you to join him! You get to lie on the shore with Han!!
meaning_hint: Picture a rice paddy. Half of it is trailing off into the shore. Hear the seagulls. Feel the ocean breeze!
reading_hint: Imagine lying on the shore with Han Solo. Enjoying a cool drink. Smelling the fragrance of the rice paddy behind you. Yeah, this is the life!

meaning_mnemonic: Use your <radical>mouth</radical> to sing and a <radical>shellfish</radical> to stroke your shamisen, and you'll be performing a <kanji>shamisen song</kanji> just like that! Yes, it's that easy. Just go find a shell, open your mouth, and you'll be singing one too in no time!
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>shamisen song</kanji> is just an <reading>歌</reading> (うた) but with a shamisen. They're still songs so they're said the exact same way!
meaning_hint: Imagine using your mouth to sing and a shell to play the shamisen to perform your shamisen song!
reading_hint: A shamisen song is a song, yay!

meaning_mnemonic: You put your <radical>fingers</radical> in the <radical>coffin</radical> to tickle the <radical>beggar</radical> inside. What you're doing is a new form of <kanji>torture</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The beggar you will coffin tickle <kanji>torture</kanji> is none other than old Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち. He is a frail old man, and doesn't like to be tickled.
meaning_hint: First, you put them in a coffin to make them think they're going to die. Then you tickle them with your fingers. There is no greater psychological + physical torture than this.
reading_hint: He keeps screaming: "STOP THIS TICKLE TORTURE. I CAN'T TAKE IT, JUST BURY ME."

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> swept away one of the <radical>cleat</radical>s your <radical>husband</radical> just bought and creates an entire <kanji>valley</kanji> with it.
reading_mnemonic: When the water clears, you and your husband go to the <kanji>valley</kanji> to look for the cleat and instead you find a bunch of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)! Maybe this is the tsunami's way of saying, "Thanks for letting me steal your cleat so I could make this valley shortcut."
meaning_hint: Watch as the tsunami rakes your husband's cleat across the mountain, carving a valley and an easier path for itself. Nature is brutal.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself eating cake with your husband in this newly formed valley. You both agree that this should be called Cake Valley!

meaning_mnemonic: Under this <radical>canopy</radical> is the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical>, being sold for cheap, what a great <kanji>bargain</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: This Mona Lisa is such a <kanji>bargain</kanji> because it's just a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん)!
meaning_hint: Look really closely at this Mona Lisa. Hmm, I don't remember her having a mustache, but I guess it is a little dark under this canopy.
reading_hint: No wonder this moustached Mona Lisa is such a bargain. You can really get anything as a rental these days, huh?

meaning_mnemonic: You <radical>say</radical> something to a <radical>boot</radical> when you want to be <kanji>humble</kanji>. You're down there because you're bowing in front of the person you're humbling yourself before. Because of that, your face is really close to their boots and you're speaking directly at them, instead of at the person's face, so it's more <kanji>discreet</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You're being <kanji>humble</kanji> like this because those boots belong to the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. You can't just say whatever you want at eye level to a king. He'll have your head chopped off for insolence, or worse!
meaning_hint: Picture yourself trying to be humble. You're down on the ground talking to a pair of boots. You don't dare raise your eyes past that.
reading_hint: Imagine saying something to the King. Get low down on the ground to be as humble as you can. Whisper into the King's boots. Lick them if you have to.

meaning_mnemonic: When you see a <radical>tsunami</radical> coming, <radical>courage</radical> will <kanji>well up</kanji> inside you, giving you the power to save everyone before you get washed away!
reading_mnemonic: Whom does that courage <kanji>well up</kanji> inside of? <reading>You</reading> (ゆう)! In fact, YOU feel like there is a never ending well of courage bursting out of you now. You are going to be a hero!
meaning_hint: Really feel the courage welling up inside of you in the face of this terrifying tsunami. Connect it to some physical feeling—perhaps you can feel it welling up in your chest.
reading_hint: Who wells up with courage? You do!

meaning_mnemonic: Give <radical>alcohol</radical> to a <radical>demon</radical> and you'll see how <kanji>ugly</kanji> it really is. Demons need to concentrate to keep their disguises going. Even if it looked pretty when you met it, like super handsome, give it a few drinks and its facade will slide off and reveal its super gross demon face.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>ugly</kanji> demon is drunk and taking off his <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう) and swinging them around his head. And, oh no, he's drinking more alcohol out of the shoes.
meaning_hint: Picture the drunk demon as its pretty face drips off revealing the ugly crap piled underneath.
reading_hint: The demon drinking alcohol out of its nasty shoes is the ugliest scene you've ever seen.

meaning_mnemonic: You see <radical>twenty</radical> <radical>slide</radical>s lined up like a <kanji>grid</kanji>. You don't know who put them here, but they almost look like the grid paper you do your maths homework in. But who would use a slide as grid paper?
reading_mnemonic: As you stare at the slide <kanji>grid</kanji> you see some people come out and overhear them talk about their mass <reading>maths</reading> (ます) tournament happening later today. Oohhh it IS a giant grid. And they're going to use it to do maths later!
meaning_hint: Picture twenty slides laid out in a grid system.
reading_hint: You can make a grid out of anything if you set your mind to it. And you can do math anywhere as long as you have some grid paper!

meaning_mnemonic: Put a <radical>yakuza</radical> member in a <radical>prison</radical> on the <radical>sun</radical> and he'll become a <kanji>martyr</kanji> to all the other yakuza still on earth. He died up there so the rest of them wouldn't have to. Because they definitely aren't getting caught now, if the penalty is a burning death in the new sun prison.
reading_mnemonic: The only reason he ended up a <kanji>martyr</kanji> in the first place is because they put him up there in <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). Everyone knows the sun is way too hot for humans in June. It's summer!
meaning_hint: Imagine all the yakuza coming out into the streets and holding up signs saying this guy was a martyr. A hero to all who do bad stuff in bad groups but don't wanna get caught.
reading_hint: Really, it's not the fault of the people who put him there that he's become a martyr. It's the month of June. PUNISH JUNE! PUT JUNE IN THE SUN PRISON! (Is what I assume those yakuza are shouting.)

meaning_mnemonic: Set <radical>fire</radical> to a <radical>geoduck</radical> if you want to <kanji>annoy</kanji> it. Fire won't kill a geoduck, but have you ever been on fire? It's not really pleasant. So you'll just annoy it pretty badly.
reading_mnemonic: You know who loves <kanji>annoying</kanji> geoducks more than anyone else in the universe? <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo! If you watch Star Wars closely enough you can see him shooting geoducks with his blaster and catching them on fire for fun.
meaning_hint: Imagine how annoyed you would be if someone set YOU on fire for no reason. The geoduck has every reason to find you annoying, dude.
reading_hint: Think how annoying it must be to be a geoduck when Han Solo comes around.

meaning_mnemonic: If you want to <radical>slice</radical> the <radical>power</radical> out of someone just <kanji>censure</kanji> them. Then they'll be cut down in front of everyone and they'll feel super embarrassed. It's the best way to slice them down without actually cutting them and getting arrested.
reading_mnemonic: Once you <kanji>censure</kanji> them and they lose all their power, they'll be just another <reading>guy</reading> (がい). A normal guy with everything lost to him. HAHAHA!
meaning_hint: Picture yourself as you slice the power out of that senator you hate by censuring them in front of the world!
reading_hint: Look at that guy now. What a loser.

meaning_mnemonic: You grab a <radical>tree</radical> to help you reach the <radical>bar</radical> out in the ocean, using it as a <kanji>jetty</kanji> leading straight to it. Now you can walk out on your makeshift <kanji>pier</kanji> and go get drinks without needing a boat or getting wet.
reading_mnemonic: You also made this <kanji>jetty</kanji> so it would be easier for <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん) to dock his sleigh. Oh, you didn't know? His sleigh is more of a speed boat now, and he wants a refreshing drink after delivering all those presents. You get extra presents for making him a jetty, so win-win.
meaning_hint: Imagine laying your tree into the water to make your own jetty extending out to the floating bar in the ocean. Innovative!
reading_hint: Picture Santa-san coming in from the ocean on his speedboat and docking on your jetty so he can go relax in the bar. Imagine the mountain of presents he lays on the jetty for you as thanks.

meaning_mnemonic: Have you heard the tale of the <radical>woman</radical> who fell in love with a <radical>coat rack</radical> during a full <radical>moon</radical> and made him her <kanji>groom</kanji>? It's a true story! She even had a wedding ceremony and had more coat racks sitting in the groom's side. The moon will make people do craaaazy things.
reading_mnemonic: Since their wedding happened under the full moon, the <kanji>groom</kanji> wore his special <reading>moon coa</reading>t (むこ) to the ceremony. This is the ideal coat for a groom to wear, since they can keep wearing it all through their honeymoon.
meaning_hint: Imagine what it would be like to fall in love with a coat rack and make it YOUR groom.
reading_hint: Try to imagine what this moon coat looks like. Does it glow in the dark like the moon shines at night? Or does it instead have a rugged texture like the moon's surface? Or maybe both?

meaning_mnemonic: You're walking in a <radical>greenhouse</radical> when you see a <radical>small</radical> <radical>drop</radical> of water fall and it makes you <kanji>yearn for</kanji> the person you <kanji>adore</kanji>. You haven't seen them in so long. But something about being in this greenhouse and seeing that tiny water droplet made your heart burst with longing.
reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>yearn for</kanji> that person from your past, you look back at the drop and see... <reading>Bo</reading>bo (ぼ) the clown?! Ugh. Apparently Bobo dropped that small drop of water.
meaning_hint: Imagine how it feels to yearn for someone else when you see the small water drop. Imagine how your heart feels.
reading_hint: Now you yearn for being somewhere Bobo is not.

meaning_mnemonic: You're in a <radical>net</radical> but you have the <radical>ability</radical> to <kanji>quit</kanji> playing along and <kanji>leave</kanji> at any time.
reading_mnemonic: You don't know if you want to <kanji>quit</kanji> this charade until you feel an uncomfortable <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ). You look down and the net you're in is being hung above a large fire.
meaning_hint: Your captors think they have you trapped, but you know you can leave whenever you feel like it.
reading_hint: As the heat spreads you decide to leave. You hop out of the net and watch the shock spread in your captors eyes.

meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>arrow </radical>is shot from <radical>heaven</radical> and hits right between your <radical>mouth</radical> and your <radical>mustache</radical> to <kanji>correct</kanji> your crooked smile. It also <kanji>straighten</kanji>s your teeth and the crooked cut of your mustache too. Who told you that looked good? Well, it doesn't matter. The heaven arrow corrected it for you.
reading_mnemonic: These heavenly <kanji>correct</kanji>ing arrows actually come from <reading>きょう</reading>と. They hate seeing people with crooked mustaches, so they shoot them out on the regular. It just looks like they're coming from heaven because they fall out of the sky.
meaning_hint: An arrow from heaven will correct all your problems!
reading_hint: If you want someone to correct your teeth or mustache, just wander around きょうと, staring up at the sky. Then when you see an arrow, run for it and try to catch it with your face!

meaning_mnemonic: If you think a <radical>tree</radical> looks <radical>sweet</radical> you have to be <kanji>certain</kanji> before you bite into it. Trees are really hard, and usually pretty bitter (believe me) so if you don't want a mouthful of broken teeth, sap, and sadness, you really need to be sure it's the one before you start munching.
reading_mnemonic: If you really want to be <kanji>certain</kanji>, and not just guess, take out your <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball and roll it as hard as you can into the tree. If its the right one it will let out a sweet smell, if it's not the tree will punch you.
meaning_hint: Which tree do you think is sweet? That one? Or that one? Are you absolutely certain? I hope you are!
reading_hint: So now let me ask you again, are you certain, or would you like to try the bowling ball test?

meaning_mnemonic: Inside this giant <radical>mouth</radical> is a <radical>person</radical>, trapped, because they're a <kanji>criminal</kanji>. They did something bad (they touched the mouth of another person without consent) and now they're in mouth jail for criminals.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>criminal</kanji> is only wearing <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). They're completely naked otherwise. They have to wear shoes because the mouth jail would be too slippery for them to stand otherwise.
meaning_hint: Picture the criminal, trapped inside the mouth. They don't even look sorry!
reading_hint: Wearing nothing but shoes is also really uncomfortable too, so it's actually part of the criminal's punishment. They don't seem to know that though.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>'s have a <radical>certain</radical> tendency to <kanji>secrete</kanji> things as they go back out to sea. When they bash themselves onto the land they just throw a bunch of sea water around, but there are only certain things that they'll secrete when they're leaving.
reading_mnemonic: Most tsunami's <kanji>secrete</kanji> <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). It takes a lot of energy for a tsunami to crash onto the land so the water is really really hot, and it has to secrete that heat as it's going back out to sea.
meaning_hint: Certain tsunami secretions are dangerous. Can you think of any?
reading_hint: The next time someone asks you what does a tsunami secrete? You'll say heat!

meaning_mnemonic: You're stuck in your <radical>car</radical> during a <radical>tsunami</radical> and all you have to break out is an <radical>axe</radical>. You whack and whack but you're only <kanji>gradually</kanji> making progress. You thought an axe would be an easy way to get out of a car but it's not working like you'd planned! You have to make more than gradual progress if you don't want to drown.
reading_mnemonic: As you're <kanji>gradually</kanji> making your door open with the axe you look up to see the New York Yankees floating by on top of their bus. You yell, "<reading>YO YA</reading>NKEES (ようや) to get their attention. They see your gradual progress and start throwing balls at your car to help dent it open.
meaning_hint: Imagine gradually denting your car door open with an axe while you're trapped in a tsunami. This isn't fast enough!
reading_hint: YO YANKEES! Gradually yelling that saved your life!

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>flowers</radical> that wash up on shore in <radical>tsunami</radical>s that we make into <radical>syrup</radical> is <kanji>seaweed</kanji>. Okay, so it's more like soup or tea than syrup, but you can make them both out of seaweed.
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Seaweed</kanji> is true <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) food. Which means it's good for your soul. Didn't you ever read those books about chicken soup being good for your soul? Well that was a lie. Miso soup with seaweed is what's really good for your soul.
meaning_hint: Seaweed is the flower of the sea!
reading_hint: Feed your soul with seaweed and it will thank you. Promise! <3

meaning_mnemonic: If the <radical>death</radical> of a <radical>woman</radical> occurs it's because she was being <kanji>reckless</kanji>. People don't just die out of nowhere! They have to have done something super crazy to cause it. Right? Yes... right.
reading_mnemonic: As you watch the news about this <kanji>reckless</kanji> woman you find out that she died from <reading>mow</reading>ing (もう) her lawn. You're not sure how, but now you really believe that she had to be doing it really recklessly to die doing it.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself watching the news about a woman's death. The word RECKLESS?! pops up on your screen and keeps flashing. Huh, she must have been, you think to yourself.
reading_hint: Imagine what reckless thing that woman had to have done to die while mowing the lawn. Eww.

meaning_mnemonic: If, <radical>again</radical> and again, you behave like an <radical>insect</radical>, you might as well be a <kanji>barbarian</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: "<reading>BAN</reading> (ばん) the barbarians! Ban them, I say!," you loudly declare as the lord of these lands.
meaning_hint: Again and again, these insect-like barbarians are harassing you and your lands. What shall we do, m'lord?
reading_hint: Picture yourself as the lord of your lands, screaming "BAN them" at the top of your lungs. You'll finally expel these insect-like barbarians that have been plaguing your lands again and again.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> <radical>squid</radical> tells all of its squid people to be <kanji>thrifty</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Who is a <kanji>thrifty</kanji> squid? <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai's squid buddy, who gets carried around in a bucket and never pays rent.
meaning_hint: Imagine the leader squid giving a speech about how you shouldn't buy new things, and how you should live with a roommate to save money. Every squid yen counts!
reading_hint:
Ken's squid is always shaking his head at Ken the samurai's obsession with wearing all silk. He should visit Thrifty Threads, the second hand shop, and get some cotton clothes that would do the job just fine.

Hint: Whenever there's a squid, remember that Ken (けん) the samurai is nearby.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>animal</radical> that hangs out in <radical>melon</radical> patches is the elusive <kanji>fox</kanji>! They like to steal melons then come back into town disguised as human melon sellers. Foxes are tricky like that.
reading_mnemonic: If you want to stop <kanji>fox</kanji>es from stealing your melons you can get one of those <reading>kits</reading> that <reading>neigh</reading> (きつね) when one is around. The kits neigh when they sense a fox, but because it sounds like a horse the fox won't run. But you'll be able to get out there and take your melons back!
meaning_hint: Imagine seeing a red fox scampering through a melon patch trying to take melons with it.
reading_hint: Imagine buying kits that neigh and putting them around your melon patch to warn you when foxes are around.

meaning_mnemonic: You're stuck in <radical>prison</radical> with nothing but a <radical>spoon</radical>, and the <kanji>smell</kanji> in here is so bad you’re using the prison spoon to waft it outside as best you can.
reading_mnemonic: You can’t believe how awful these <kanji>smell</kanji>s are. You’re new in prison, so you call them all <reading>neo</reading>-smells (にお). They're neo, they're new to you.
meaning_hint: It smells awful in this prison. You have to act fast with your prison spoon to get rid of all the bad smells!
reading_hint: Try to come up with some neo-smells right now. There’s the neo-food smell (prison cafeteria pizza, maybe) and the neo-laundry smell of course (your prison uniform). Can you think of any other neo-smells?

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> is <radical>rejoic</radical>ing. She must be <kanji>glad</kanji> and <kanji>happy</kanji> to rejoice out loud in front of you like this.
reading_mnemonic: "I'm so <kanji>glad</kanji>!" the woman shouts, "Because I found the <reading>key</reading> (き)!" You immediately start to wonder what "the key" is. The key to life? The key to learning Japanese? Then you see she's holding up some car keys.
meaning_hint: Picture a woman rejoicing loudly. She's got a big smile on her face and she wants you and everyone else to see how glad she is.
reading_hint: You'd be happy too if you'd just found your lost keys. Though maybe you wouldn't shout it for the entire world to hear.

meaning_mnemonic: Open your <radical>mouth</radical>. A <radical>tiger</radical> fell in there on some <radical>spikes</radical>! That's what you tell everybody, but when they look in your mouth there's nothing there. It's all a big <kanji>lie</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Lie</kanji> about spikes in your mouth and I'll put soap with spikes in it in your mouth to teach you a lesson. That's why I'm using <reading>u</reading>ni <reading>so</reading>ap (うそ).
meaning_hint: Of course there isn't a tiger in your mouth. And those spikes are just normal teeth. No matter how you look at it, you're saying a lie.
reading_hint: Now at least you're not lying about spikes being in your mouth.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>skewer</radical> radical and the <kanji>skewer</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
reading_mnemonic: Uh oh, this <kanji>skewer</kanji> is really... <reading>cushy</reading> (くし). It's so soft and cushy you can't get any meat or vegetables onto it.
meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo.
reading_hint: Imagine trying to use this cushy skewer. It's the right shape (long, thin, pointy at one end), but every time you try to put something on it it squishes in on itself like a long marshmallow.

meaning_mnemonic: Into your <radical>mouth</radical> the <radical>leader</radical> shot an <radical>arrow</radical> with an attached <radical>hook</radical> so that they'd be able to see into your <kanji>throat</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: From your <kanji>throat</kanji> you hear a voice. Not yours, but one you recognize. It's <reading>こう</reading>いち! What's that rascal doing down in your throat?
meaning_hint: Imagine the arrow with its hook shooting down into your mouth and hooking into your throat. Ouch! Now the leader is peering down in there, but you don't know why.
reading_hint: The leader starts to tug こういち out of your throat, but he resists. "No, I like it down here, it's dark and damp." The leader gets angry and pulls harder, which hurts! This is your throat, after all, don't you get a say?!

meaning_mnemonic: On your <radical>mouth</radical> is a <radical>stamp</radical> that you <kanji>tap</kanji>, <kanji>strike</kanji> and <kanji>slap</kanji> to create a beat! The stamp looks like a little drum, but you're really just hitting your mouth for fun (the stamp was an excuse to make it seem normal).
reading_mnemonic: When you <kanji>tap</kanji> and <kanji>strike</kanji> the stamp on your mouth, you make a <reading>ta-ta</reading> (たた) sound. Imagine yourself making a beat with the little stamp drum on your mouth. Now listen... たた! There! Did you hear it?
meaning_hint: Put a stamp on your mouth and look in your bathroom mirror. Now tap that stamp and make a fresh new beat the young kids can dance to!
reading_hint: Picture yourself as a drummer, tapping away on a miniature stamp drum on your mouth. With each tap, you create a lively ta-ta beat!

meaning_mnemonic: What do you <radical>eat</radical> like a <radical>machine</radical>? <kanji>Candy</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>candy</kanji> in particular is just drops of hardened, clear sugar that looks like <reading>雨</reading> (あめ) drops.
meaning_hint: Picture your mouth opening and closing really fast and hard as candy on a conveyor belt goes sliding in. Munch, munch, munch, you're a candy machine!
reading_hint: Picture this kanji rain as it goes into your chomping machine mouth. It tastes like sweet, sugary 雨. Yum!

meaning_mnemonic: Next to your <radical>mouth</radical> there are <radical>fins</radical> which react to <radical>alcohol</radical>. If you exceed a certain alcohol <radical>measurement</radical>, they start flapping about, which makes you spread <kanji>rumors</kanji> and <kanji>gossip</kanji> uncontrollably.
reading_mnemonic: The flapping fins are making you slur your words, but that doesn't stop you from spreading <kanji>rumors</kanji> and <kanji>gossip</kanji>: "<reading>Ooo</reading> <reading>whassa</reading>t (うわさ) I hear about so and so having an affair?!"
meaning_hint: Just imagine these fins forcing your mouth to move with all their flapping. This is what happens when you have too much alcohol, you can't control yourself.
reading_hint: Just imagine trying to talk eloquently and clearly when you have fins flapping around your mouth. And if you've had a few drinks on top of that, it stands to reason that you'd start slurring your words.

meaning_mnemonic: You have something to <radical>say</radical>, so you <radical>stand</radical> up, throw in the <radical>towel</radical>, and shout: "I <kanji>GIVE UP</kanji>!" The stand radical looks a little weird in this kanji, probably because you're all tired and ready to give up.
reading_mnemonic: What's making you want to <kanji>give up</kanji>? Well, there's <reading>a killer</reading> (あきら) behind you who's threatening to kill you if you don't.
meaning_hint: Imagine standing up and throwing in the towel (or even just waving it around, especially if it's white) — it's the universal signal for giving up, right? Just make sure you also say it out loud, to make it clear for everyone: "I GIVE UP!"
reading_hint: Hey, whatever you're doing, it's not worth losing your life over. If a killer is threatening you, you should probably just give up for now and live to fight another day.

meaning_mnemonic: Use your <radical>fingers</radical> to grab some guy's <radical>foot</radical> and <kanji>catch</kanji> him! Touching his foot kind of grosses you out, though, which is why you're just catching him with your fingers rather than your whole hand.
reading_mnemonic: What made you decide to <kanji>catch</kanji> this person? The <reading>Torah</reading> (とら). It was written in the Torah that you needed to catch this guy, and you always follow all the Torah's teachings.
meaning_hint: You have really strong fingers, so once you catch this guy's foot in your pincer grip, there's no way he's escaping.
reading_hint: Your copy of the Torah is pretty unique for having this list of must-catch people. No one else's Torah has this… Have you ever considered you might have a bootleg version?

meaning_mnemonic: Under the <radical>moon</radical> beneath a <radical>tree</radical> there is a <radical>hat</radical> filled with <radical>water</radical>. You stare down into the water and decide to stick your <kanji>knee</kanji> in it.
reading_mnemonic: Suddenly, in your <kanji>knee</kanji>, you feel <reading>he</reading>at and a <reading>za</reading>p (ひざ)! Your knee is simultaneously burned and electrocuted by the hot water!!
meaning_hint: Feel the water splash over your knee as you stick it into this hat. It fits really well, the hat is the perfect size for your knee. Now look up at the moonlight filtering through the tree above.
reading_hint: Imagine the heat then the zap going through your knee. It isn't a nice heat, it's a hot heat, then BZZZT zap! Your knee was heat zapped pretty bad.

meaning_mnemonic: You're holding a <radical>flag</radical> on a <radical>stick</radical> above your <radical>eye</radical>s to imitate <kanji>eyebrows</kanji>. You can't grow any of your own, but you really love eyebrows, so this was the best you could do.
reading_mnemonic: Underneath your fake <kanji>eyebrows</kanji> are two slabs of <reading>me</reading>at (み). Meat helps hair grow, according to many Internet specialists who also happen to sell meat. You're waiting for this meat to foster eyebrow hair, and when that sweet day comes you won't need these flags.
meaning_hint: Look at yourself in the mirror. You can see the stick holding up the flag and it's very clear you drew some eyebrows onto the flag with some markers, but you still look pretty good (in your opinion).
reading_hint: Imagine walking around town, holding your eyebrow flags. You can feel the meat sticking above your eyes. They stink and they're weirdly lukewarm, but you know your real eyebrows will grow under them any day now.

meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> hits, it's <radical>rain</radical>ing, and you're outside trying to use a <radical>rake</radical>, you're going to be very <kanji>wet</kanji> and <kanji>damp</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: You can't stand how <kanji>wet</kanji> and <kanji>damp</kanji> you feel in these clothes so you rip them off and rake in the <reading>nu</reading>de (ぬ)!
meaning_hint: Imagine how wet and damp you'd be with tsunami water up to your knees and rain pouring down on your head. You struggle with your rake anyway.
reading_hint: You're still wet, but at least you don't have the feeling of damp clothes touching your body now. You'll have to be really careful raking in the nude though.

meaning_mnemonic: You are so <radical>sick</radical> that you've come to look like the <radical>humble</radical> <radical>stool</radical>: <kanji>thin</kanji>. Eerily thin, in fact.
reading_mnemonic: When you're this <kanji>thin</kanji>, your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) is visible from outside of your body too.
meaning_hint: Imagine standing behind a stool and being able to disappear behind it completely. That's how thin this sickness has made you.
reading_hint: Picture your soul pulsing through your thin body. You have no body fat to cover it up, so everyone can see it pulsing and lurking in there. This is why you're hiding behind one of the humble stools in your house.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Sheep</radical> are always looking at the <radical>next</radical> animal, full of <kanji>envy</kanji> and <kanji>jealousy</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The reason the sheep feels such <kanji>envy</kanji> and <kanji>jealousy</kanji> is that the next creature is a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん)! Who wouldn't be jealous of a creature as noble as a centaur?
meaning_hint: Picture a sheep comparing itself to the next creature, and feeling deep envy and jealousy. Why does it feel that way?
reading_hint: Being a centaur is every sheep's dream, so they can't help feeling terrible jealousy when they see one.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> is going <radical>wild</radical> because you're <kanji>confused</kanji>. If you're confused, it's usually because you don't know what's going on or what to do. This makes the soul inside your body go absolutely wild!
reading_mnemonic: Whenever you're <kanji>confused</kanji>, <reading>こう</reading>いち appears to offer you guidance. He's confused a lot, so he knows how to help. And knowledge offered by こういち is the best medicine for confusion.
meaning_hint: Think back to the last time you were confused. Remember how your soul felt? You were so confused it went wild and made you feel dumb and bad, like a wild person.
reading_hint: Imagine being confused about Japanese, for example. In your confusion everything seems scary and wild and your soul is utterly bewildered, but then here comes こういち. He sits with you and pats your back and tries to help you stop being so confused.

meaning_mnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> when you're in <radical>private</radical> to use an <radical>arrow</radical> to <kanji>push open</kanji> things.
reading_mnemonic: Your favorite thing to <kanji>push open</kanji> is your own <reading>eye</reading> (あい). You take the arrow into your fingers and gently push each lid open until you can see.
meaning_hint: You're in private in your own room, no one else is around. Now you wrap your fingers around this big arrow, and you push open your door, your curtains, your windows. It's weird, but you really enjoy this.
reading_hint: Picture the arrow in your hands. You're facing a mirror, but you can't see because you're about to push open your eyes. The anticipation is killing you, push them open now!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> told you that a <radical>flood</radical> last <radical>evening</radical> would <kanji>draw close</kanji>. There was nothing you could do to stop it, it seemed to be <kanji>be imminent</kanji>, coming in the darkness of the evening.
reading_mnemonic: Luckily whenever you sense a flood starting to <kanji>draw close</kanji>, <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) prepares a raft. Remember Satsuki? Such a talented little girl. I guess floods are common enough that she's learned to make rafts?
meaning_hint: Think back to last evening, when your fingers twitched and twisted, warning you as the flood started to draw close.
reading_hint: This one is barely big enough for two people, so you have to draw close if you want to fit. Kind of uncomfortable, but it's better than being caught in an imminent flood.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>axe</radical> radical and the <kanji>axe</kanji> kanji are exactly the same, with one minor difference. This kanji is also used as the <kanji>bread loaf counter</kanji>, probably because bread was so big and hard in the past that they had to use axes to slice them.
reading_mnemonic: You imagined being back in time too good and now you're stuck using an <kanji>axe</kanji> to be a <kanji>bread loaf counter</kanji> for the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. You aren't sure which king he is or when in time you are, but he's mean and keeps eating all the bread you cut up with your axe.
meaning_hint: Imagine you've gone back in time a good hundred years, and you're trying to count loaves of bread after each swing of your axe. Axes and bread loaves will become intertwined in your brain.
reading_hint: Imagine being stuck using an axe on bread while the King of England glares down at you, munching and spilling crumbs everywhere.

meaning_mnemonic: Everyone likes your <radical>outfit</radical>. The <radical>reason</radical>? Your cool <kanji>sleeve</kanji>s. You are rocking some absolutely amazing sleeves, and that's reason enough for everyone to love your whole look.
reading_mnemonic: Little does everyone know, you wear these <kanji>sleeve</kanji>s because your arms would be <reading>so de</reading>ad (そで) without them. Why? Because they'd be all exposed and defenseless!
meaning_hint: Imagine these incredible sleeves you're wearing. Are they flashy and colorful? Puffed and billowing? Jeweled and ornate? Whatever the case, they really bring your outfit together.
reading_hint: Imagine all the ways your arms would be so dead if you didn't have sleeves. Sunburn, bug bites, miscellaneous cuts and scrapes... Just so, so dead. Good thing you've got these stylish, fancy sleeves to protect them.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>ice</radical> <radical>wife</radical> is <kanji>amazing</kanji>. She's also <kanji>terrible</kanji>, because she's very cold and has many ice powers, but that's also part of what makes her amazing!
reading_mnemonic: She turns her <kanji>amazing</kanji> gaze onto your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) and hits it with an ice beam. Woah! Instead of turning to ice or shattering, it steals her awful powers. Now your saber is amazing and terrible too!
meaning_hint: Picture your amazing and terrible ice wife. She's floating in your backyard cackling and shooting ice all around.
reading_hint: Now you're floating in the backyard cackling with your amazing wife as you use your terrible saber and freeze things with her!

meaning_mnemonic: What kind of <radical>woman</radical> comes down from <radical>heaven</radical>? A <kanji>supernatural</kanji> one. She is so <kanji>enchanting</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>supernatural</kanji> woman comes down in front of you and she's so <kanji>enchanting</kanji> that it takes a while to realize she's completely made of <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう).
meaning_hint: Imagine your idea of what an extremely supernatural woman would look like, and have her come down from heaven towards you. You're totally enchanted by her looks.
reading_hint: Touch the supernatural yogurt woman and feel the yogurt. Taste the yogurt, what flavor is it? It's totally enchanting you further.

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> of the <radical>World</radical> <radical>Tree</radical>, all it does is <kanji>chat</kanji> and <kanji>talk</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: To <kanji>chat</kanji> or <kanji>talk</kanji> with the World Tree, just head over to <reading>Sha</reading>man <reading>Bay</reading> (しゃべ).
meaning_hint: You'd think the World Tree's mouth would talk wisdom, but all it does is chat, chat, chat.
reading_hint: The locals shorten Shaman Bay to "Sha Bay," because they always go there to 喋る with the World Tree. Imagine all the shamans hanging about in the bay.

meaning_mnemonic: What kind of <radical>fish</radical> jumps from <radical>dirt</radical> to <radical>dirt</radical>? A <kanji>salmon</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: You make a drinking game out of watching the <kanji>salmon</kanji> going up stream. Every time one jumps onto the dirt, you drink a shot of <reading>酒</reading> (さけ). You realize you're drunk when you ask for more さけ but it comes out as <reading>しゃけ</reading>.
meaning_hint: When you're at the river watching salmon jumping up stream, they keep jumping onto the dirt, flopping back into the water, then doing it again.
reading_hint:
Salmon. Drink さけ. Salmon. Drink さけ. Salmon. Drink...

"Ughhh.... I'm feeling tipsy... Hey, where's my しゃけ!?"

meaning_mnemonic: On your <radical>roof</radical> every <radical>evening</radical> you meet with <radical>snake</radical>s. All snakes know your <kanji>address</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: The snakes come to your <kanji>address</kanji> every night to receive the greatest gift you can give a snake: <reading>a 手</reading> (あて).
meaning_hint: You're snake famous. Snakes go on a pilgrimage to your address every evening. Your address is passed down from snake generation to snake generation.
reading_hint:
"You only give a 手 a night. Newcomers might have a 手 or two, but some snakes who have been coming to your address for years have hundreds of 手s.

They are human hands."

meaning_mnemonic: In the <radical>foot</radical> <radical>capital</radical>, it's tough to be a <radical>dog</radical> because you're always getting <kanji>kick</kanji>ed.
reading_mnemonic: Imagine yourself <kanji>kick</kanji>ing a <reading>ke</reading>ttle (け). There's nothing that's more satisfying to kick than a good ol' kettle, you know?
meaning_hint: In the foot capital, you can only use your feet. So petting dogs looks a lot like kicking them.
reading_hint: You just love how a kettle gets dented as you kick it around.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>proclaim</radical>s "I am <kanji>noisy</kanji> and I am loud!" It keeps repeating this over and over to everyone you pass by.
reading_mnemonic: You are so <kanji>noisy</kanji> and loud that the townsfolk hired <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the Samurai to shut you up.
meaning_hint: The townsfolk agree with your mouth, you are noisy and you are picky and you need to shut up.
reading_hint:
"Ken, your armor color is so last year. That squid you have is gross. Your sword is so gaudy. You talk like you're from the Edo Period, but it's Reiwa right now."

Your noisy and picky self just goes off on Ken the Samurai. He skulks off, defeated by your noisy and picky words.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> is <radical>showy</radical> so it makes everybody around you <kanji>rowdy</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Everyone is getting <kanji>rowdy</kanji> so you get in your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) to escape.
meaning_hint: Your mouth is showy because it's covered in grills and bright red lipstick. People get rowdy because you're a celebrity and your showy mouth is your signature look, and everyone's so excited to see your mouth in person.
reading_hint: As you drive away in your car you flash that signature mouth at them one more time, and you can see them getting even more rowdy.

meaning_mnemonic: One way to put <radical>wheat</radical> in your <radical>face</radical> is with <kanji>noodles</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Once you're done stuffing noodles in your face, you can use your leftover noodles to make <kanji>noodle</kanji><reading>men</reading> (めん).
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself shoveling piles of wheaty noodles into your face. They're so wheaty and delicious!
reading_hint: Did you ever use to make pictures with your food? (Or maybe you still do? We won't judge.) Imagine you're doing that again, and think about how lifelike your noodle men are!

meaning_mnemonic: The best kind of flowers to grow in your window? Strawberry, obviously. Yes, there are strawberry flowers — it's actually the flowers that turn into the strawberries, you know. And they're perfect as window flowers because they need a ton of light. That's why all your window flowers are strawberries.
reading_mnemonic: Believe it or not, your <kanji>strawberry</kanji> is the only cure for an <reading>itchy Go</reading>dzilla (いちご).
meaning_hint: You don't waste time with non-edible flowers. Only strawberries for your windows. Now imagine that fresh, sweet strawberry smell that would drift in on the breeze every time you opened your windows. Nice, right?
reading_hint: Imagine Godzilla, covered in an itchy rash, squishing one of your strawberries and rubbing it all over his body to calm his itchy skin.

meaning_mnemonic: You keep your <radical>moon</radical> <radical>weapon</radical> in your <kanji>crotch</kanji>. Where else would you keep it?
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>crotch</kanji> has a moon weapon, and it's served you well, so you hope that if you have a <reading>子 </reading> (こ) one day, they'll have one too.
meaning_hint: Your moon weapon is a moon that you keep in your crotch. We'll let you imagine how this one works...
reading_hint: Your mother had a moon weapon, and her father before her. It's been in the family for generations, and you hope to pass it on to your <reading>子</reading> as well.

meaning_mnemonic: You've made yourself a <radical>tree</radical> <radical>bookshelf</radical> (that is, a bookshelf cut into a tree), so you build a <kanji>fence</kanji> to keep the riffraff out.
reading_mnemonic: On your <kanji>fence</kanji>, you hang up a <reading>sack</reading> (さく) to accept donations.
meaning_hint: Your tree bookshelf is often being raided by the neighbors, and since it's outside, all you can do is build a fence around it to protect it. Better check out that fence-building book in your tree bookshelf.
reading_hint: People put money and books in your fence sack to keep your tree bookshelf well stocked.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> has a <radical>tooth</radical> that you use to <kanji>bite</kanji> and <kanji>chew</kanji> with.
reading_mnemonic: Your one tooth is so strong that you can even use it to <kanji>bite</kanji> and <kanji>chew</kanji> up a whole  <reading>ca</reading>r (か).
meaning_hint: Imagine that one tooth in your mouth chewing through everything. It's not easy to chew with only one tooth, but luckily yours is huge and razor-sharp, so it helps you to bite and chew your way through anything you like.
reading_hint: Some people like eating cars, and you're one of them. You can't get enough of those delicious cars. You just bite them apart and chew.

meaning_mnemonic: One <radical>animal</radical> that is particularly  <radical>good</radical> is the <kanji>wolf</kanji>. Wolves have a bad reputation, but actually they're very, very good animals.
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>wolf</kanji> might be good, but if you run into one, all you can do is scream <reading>Oh 神</reading>!” (おおかみ).
meaning_hint:
When wolves are reintroduced to an ecosystem, good things happen. Overpopulation in deer and other animals decline, plants grow and create more habitats, and the balance of wildlife improves. It all starts with wolves, who are good dogs.

Also, wolves take care of wild, abandoned kids. Only wolves do that, because they're good. Just ask Mowgli!
reading_hint: "Oh 神," as in, "Oh god," because you're about to be eaten by a good wolf. You're part of the ecosystem now.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> was <radical>slice</radical>d open by a terrible <kanji>cough</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Oddly enough, some people find your <kanji>cough</kanji> <reading>sexy</reading> (せき).
meaning_hint: You've heard of a hacking cough, right? Well, this is worse. It's a slicing cough, and it's sliced right through your mouth, too. Imagine it flapping around while you cough.
reading_hint: That slicing cough opened up a nice gravelly, sexy voice. Between coughs, you start noticing all the looks you're getting. You realize your cough is making you really sexy to all the people around you. Cough cough cough.

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> have to <radical>stand</radical> up straight, to protect you from being <kanji>abduct</kanji>ed, or <kanji>pull</kanji>ed away against your will.
reading_mnemonic: Suddenly, someone really is trying to <kanji>abduct</kanji> you. They're <kanji>pull</kanji>ling you away. It's the ancient Egyptian god <reading>Ra</reading> (ら). Help!
meaning_hint: You're convinced someone is trying to abduct you, so you need to keep your fingers standing at all times, ready to defend yourself. Your fingers are feeling pretty stiff, but you still keep them standing, just in case.
reading_hint: Luckily, your fingers were standing to attention and you were able to fight off the mighty Ra. He won't try to abduct you again in a hurry. Thank goodness for those standing fingers.

meaning_mnemonic: Using <radical>flowers</radical> to make a <radical>lip ring</radical> is always going to end in <kanji>frustration</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: After some <kanji>frustration</kanji>, you made your flower lip ring. Now you look like an <reading>Egy</reading>ptian  (いじ) pharaoh. Because all ancient Egyptian monarchs wore flower lip rings (historical fact)!
meaning_hint: Have you ever made a daisy chain? Try making a daisy lip ring. Just imagine your frustation as you try to make it small enough to fit through your lip piercing.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself dressed as an ancient Egyptian pharaoh, wearing your newly-crafted flower lip ring. Think how classy (or strange) you feel.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Before</radical> you <radical>boil</radical> something, make sure that you <kanji>roast</kanji> it.
reading_mnemonic: Now you <kanji>roast</kanji> yourself some <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). Roasted centaur goes well with tea.
meaning_hint: Why would you roast something before you boil it? Because that something is tea! Or maybe coffee. Tea leaves and coffee beans are roasted before they're boiled.
reading_hint: Smell the wafting scent of roasted centaur as you sip on your freshly-roasted tea.

meaning_mnemonic: There's a <radical>slide</radical> with a <radical>drunkard</radical> <radical>above</radical> it, and they're making you feel <radical>small</radical>. And the worst part is that the drunkard is one of your <kanji>relatives</kanji>.
