reading_mnemonic: As if things couldn't get worse, your relative seems to think they're <reading>sexy</reading> (せき) when they're on the slide drunk. Sexy? More like irresponsible!
meaning_hint: A slide isn't the safest place for a drunkard to be. You try to help your relative down but they just keep shouting at you and making you feel small. Drunk relatives are the worst.
reading_hint: Imagine your intoxicated relative shouting about how sexy they are on that slide. Someone get them down from there!

meaning_mnemonic: What do you <radical>eat</radical> if you’re wearing <radical>horns</radical> and surrounded by <radical>lantern</radical>s? <kanji>Mochi</kanji> (or <kanji>sticky rice cake</kanji>s) of course!
reading_mnemonic: The reading of <kanji>mochi</kanji> is <reading>mochi</reading> (もち). Yay!
meaning_hint: Imagine yourself in the lantern light wearing horns and eating delicious mochi.
reading_hint: As long as you can remember the meaning of this kanji, you'll know the reading too.

meaning_mnemonic: Use a <radical>flag</radical> to <radical>compare</radical> your <kanji>flatulence</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic:
How do you read <kanji>flatulence</kanji>? <reading>Heh</reading>(へ)...heh..heh..heheheh. You can't NOT laugh at the word flatulence.

Note that some dictionaries incorrectly list おなら as a reading for this kanji. While おなら also means "flatulence" (as in "fart"), you’ll only ever encounter this reading in hiragana, as the kanji can only be read as へ.
meaning_hint: How do you compare your flatulence? I recommend a flag. You can see which fart is strongest by seeing how much the flag flutters.
reading_hint: Picture yourself unable to stop snickering over the word "flatulence", like a little schoolchild. Heh… heh…

meaning_mnemonic:
You offer a <radical>hot pepper</radical> to the <radical>king</radical>, and he gives you a <radical>drop</radical> of something. You look down at the drop to see it transforming into a <kanji>disc</kanji>. And not just any old disc at that. It's a <kanji>jade disc</kanji>.

Have you ever seen ancient Chinese jade discs with holes in the middle? That's what this kanji is referring to.
reading_mnemonic: Wait, did a drop really just change into a <kanji>disc</kanji>? What the <reading>heck i</reading>s (へき) going on?
meaning_hint: You took a long time to pick out the perfect hot pepper. Only the best for the king, right? It burned your hands a little as you held it on your way to the palace. You were hoping for something pretty special in return, but you never imagined it would be a drop that magically turns into a disc. Wow!
reading_hint: You start to realize just how crazy the situation is. Seriously. How the heck is a drop able to turn into a jade disc?

meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>sick</radical> <radical>sheep</radical> is <kanji>itchy</kanji> and it can barely sit still. Help the poor sheep!
reading_mnemonic: Even though the sheep is <kanji>itchy</kanji>, look how <reading>kah-yu</reading>te (かゆ) it is! It’s not just cute. It’s kah-yute!
meaning_hint: Imagine you were itchy but you couldn't scratch the spot. Now that you know how the sick sheep feels, give it a hand!
reading_hint: Say it out loud! Kah-yuuute!

meaning_mnemonic: You go outside to bask your <radical>forehead</radical> in the <radical>sun</radical>, but then you see these creatures with <radical>lid</radical>s for heads and <radical>fins</radical> for feet. Uh… where did they come from? From the <kanji>underworld</kanji>. Nothing like them exists in this world. They obviously came from the underworld.
reading_mnemonic: These <kanji>underworld</kanji> creatures must be out and about because it's <reading>May</reading> (めい), and the weather is so fantastic and sunny right now. They don't get to see the sun down there in the underworld, so every May they turn up in droves to experience it.
meaning_hint: Imagine the heat of the sun on your forehead as you watch these horrible creatures with lid-heads and fin-feet. There's no doubt they're from the underworld, probably here to enjoy the sun. You might want to get out of their way…
reading_hint: Yes, even underworld creatures love to frolic around in the beautiful May weather.

meaning_mnemonic: You have a <radical>greenhouse</radical> that is <kanji>endless</kanji>. Imagine your endless greenhouse, which goes on and on, as far as the eye can see. Lucky you.
reading_mnemonic: Your flowers have grown so big that now the only way you can fit into your <kanji>endless</kanji> greenhouse is by lying on your <reading>back</reading> (ばく).
meaning_hint: What will you grow in your endless greenhouse? Imagine all your favourite flowers and foods growing endlessly in your endless greenhouse.
reading_hint: Imagine yourself lying on your back in your greenhouse under a canopy of great big flowers, tending to them as they grow even bigger.

meaning_mnemonic: Did you know that those strange <radical>geoduck</radical> creatures were once used to make <kanji>page</kanji>s? Okay maybe not, but it makes for a memorable image!
reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>page</kanji> is a <reading>page</reading> (ページ).
meaning_hint: Imagine the strange, gooey books you'd end up with if you used geoducks for pages. Now imagine flicking through one of those books, and getting the slimy geoduck residue on your hands as you look at the pages.
reading_hint: Since the reading comes from the English word "page", this one shouldn't be too hard to remember, especially if you've already come across it in katakana.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>wing</radical> is something you <kanji>must not</kanji> do anything with. Wings are fragile (think bird's wings and butterfly's wings) and sometimes dangerous (like the wings of a plane) and you must not touch them, or even go near them! You MUST NOT. You. MUST. NOT.
reading_mnemonic: Apart from staying away from wings, the other thing you <kanji>must not</kanji> do is eat <reading>mochi</reading> (もち). Okay, they're not fragile or dangerous, but I love mochi and I don't want you eating them.
meaning_hint: Imagine a pair of wings with a prohibition sign (a red circle with a red line) on them. And next time you see a wing of any kind, think "I MUST NOT"!
reading_hint: Now imagine some mochi with red prohibition circles on them. You MUST NOT eat those, do you hear me? Those mochi are mine!

meaning_mnemonic: You've got a <radical>car</radical>, a <radical>weapon</radical>, and some <radical>thread</radical> lying around. So of course you decide to <kanji>connect</kanji> them all together.
reading_mnemonic: There's only one reason you've <kanji>connected</kanji> all this together: <reading>Tuna</reading> (つな) fishing. Obviously!
meaning_hint: Imagine using the thread to fasten the weapon onto your car, creating some kind of monstrosity. Surely this can't be legal?!
reading_hint: Your weapon of choice is a harpoon, which is perfect for catching big tuna. We'll just have to hope your car is amphibious, or we might run into some issues. Now let's go catch some tuna!

meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> <radical>before</radical> were <kanji>complete</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: To have your <kanji>complete</kanji> fingers back, you gotta run to the hospital <reading>solo</reading> (そろ).
meaning_hint: Imagine one of your fingers is missing, and they're no longer complete like before. When everyone told you not to join that yakuza gang, you just didn't listen.
reading_hint: You can't be seen with anyone because your former yakuza mates might be watching you. Seriously, you don't want anyone else involved in this business.

meaning_mnemonic: All the <radical>world</radical>'s <radical>shellfish</radical> are yours to <kanji>receive</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Why do you want to <kanji>receive</kanji> all these shellfish? Because it's the <reading>mora</reading>l (もら) thing to do. Oh benevolent, moral king of all the world's shellfish, please receive us.
meaning_hint: They're all for you. You're the king of the shellfish. Look at all of them, the entire world's shellfish, and they're all yours to receive.
reading_hint: Picture yourself as some enlightened, moral king of all the world's shellfish, with your sole duty to receive and take in all the world's shellfish into your flock.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Fire</radical> is one of a <radical>few</radical> things you'll need to <kanji>fry</kanji> things.
reading_mnemonic: You love to <kanji>fry</kanji> things and are also an adventurous <reading>eater</reading> (いた). This means you'll fry and eat just about anything. Fried ostrich, fried alligator, fried chocolate, you name it!
meaning_hint: You need a few other things like a pan and some oil too, but fire is gonna be the most important for frying!
reading_hint: Your being an adventurous eater of all things fried truly knows no bounds. What cursed combination of foods will you fry up tomorrow?!

meaning_mnemonic: Looking at the <radical>moon</radical>, you notice it's grown a bunch of <radical>branch</radical>es. Wait, are those all… <kanji>limbs</kanji>?!
reading_mnemonic: After some consideration, you know who put all those <vocabulary>limb</vocabulary>s on the moon. It must've been none other than the moon <reading>shee</reading>p (し)!
meaning_hint: You feel absolutely terrified looking at the moon with all its branches consisting of limbs, lifelessly flopping around. How did this happen?
reading_hint: The moon sheep are always up to no good, so this must all have been devised by them. Those darn moon sheep!

meaning_mnemonic: All you need are some <radical>flowers</radical>, <radical>fish</radical>, and <radical>grain</radical> and you've got yourself a potion that can <kanji>revive</kanji> just about anyone or anything!
reading_mnemonic: This potion able to <kanji>revive</kanji> any being just has one small downside: it tastes like <reading>so</reading>ap (そ).
meaning_hint: Just don't forget the ingredients you'll need: flowers, fish, and grain. Imagine yourself throwing them all into a big cauldron and brewing up a potion powerful enough to revive a person!
reading_hint: As long as you can get over the awful taste of soap, this is some good, powerful stuff! And considering this'll be administered to dead things… the taste is probably not so important.

meaning_mnemonic: Who is this <radical>woman</radical> <radical>mole</radical>?! It's your <kanji>niece</kanji>!
reading_mnemonic: Not only are they moles, all of your <kanji>niece</kanji>s were also born in <reading>May</reading> (めい)!
meaning_hint: Who else would it be but your niece? In fact, all of your nieces are actually moles! Picture what your family gatherings would look like with all those mole-nieces. Quite terrifying, really…
reading_hint: Picture your many nieces, all moles, and all born in the same month of May. Every year, like clockwork, a new mole-niece is born into your family in May.

meaning_mnemonic: You're using your <radical>fingers</radical> to do something important for this <radical>ceremony</radical>'s <radical>construction</radical>. What's really important for this ceremony's construction, using your fingers? Well… you <kanji>wipe</kanji> the floors.
reading_mnemonic: Now <kanji>wipe</kanji> these floors, you <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ)!
meaning_hint: Hey, somebody has to do it! Wiping the floors is as important for any ceremony as all the other work that goes into it… right?!
reading_hint: Oh wow, sorry, not sure where that just came from… Look, wiping floors for a living doesn't make anyone a fool, they're people just like you and me. Now hurry up, I want a nice sheen on these floors before the ceremony.

meaning_mnemonic: Beside your <radical>mouth</radical> you hold your <radical>cross</radical> as you pray for all your wishes to <kanji>come true</kanji>. Perhaps some divine power will <kanji>grant</kanji> what you wish for?
reading_mnemonic: And if you really want your wish to <kanji>come true</kanji>, make sure to write it down in <reading>kana</reading>!
meaning_hint: Picture yourself holding your cross right up next to your mouth as you speak your prayers. What will you wish to come true? It could be anything you want!
reading_hint: Legend has it that writing down your wishes in kana will lead to higher chances of them actually coming true! Whether you go with hiragana or katakana is up to you, just make sure it's all in some form of kana.

meaning_mnemonic: "Take your <radical>fingers</radical>. <radical>Now</radical>, put them on your foe's <radical>heart</radical> and <kanji>twist</kanji>." These are the words of your martial arts master as he teaches you his final, ultimate technique: the "finger-heart twister."
reading_mnemonic: The last challenger to appear is… your own master, who taught you the <kanji>twist</kanji> technique. Turns out he's a <reading>nen</reading>doroid (ねん), so he doesn't even have a heart for you to twist. Welp, you're screwed.
meaning_hint: No one can stop you now. Imagine how powerful you feel as you conquer dojo after dojo, using your bare fingers to twist the heart of any challenger who dares face you. What a twisted fiend you are!
reading_hint: Imagine the helplessness you'd feel facing him. Your master, who all along was a kind of plastic figurine called a nendoroid, is impervious to the twist technique he himself taught you! What a twist.

meaning_mnemonic: When you hold out your <radical>fingers</radical>, a <radical>person</radical> — and then a second <radical>person</radical> — stomps them into the <radical>dirt</radical>, leaving you with a serious finger <kanji>sprain</kanji>. That wasn't very nice of either person, and now you feel awfully <kanji>discourage</kanji>d about things.
reading_mnemonic: You show your <kanji>sprain</kanji> to a doctor, and he tells you to go see the C<reading>za</reading>r (ざ). In your country, only the Czar has the authority to treat a sprain of this severity.
meaning_hint: Feel the throbbing pain in your sprained, dirt-covered fingers, and imagine how intensely discouraged you are knowing that neither person who hurt you is going to face any consequences.
reading_hint: Once again, you face a crushing sense of discouragement — you'll never be able to afford the services of the Czar! It looks you're just going to have to live with this finger sprain for the rest of your life.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Shellfish</radical> cause <radical>landslide</radical>s because of all their <kanji>bustling</kanji> and <kanji>lively</kanji> underwater activity.
reading_mnemonic: You can't stand <kanji>bustling</kanji> and <kanji>lively</kanji> situations, so you get out your <reading>knee gui</reading>llotine (にぎ). What's a knee guillotine? It's exactly what it sounds like, and you're about to use it on these shellfish to prevent some landslides.
meaning_hint: You ever see cliffs falling into the sea? 99% of those landslides are due to bustling, lively shellfish communities, eroding the base of the cliff with all their hustle and bustle.
reading_hint: Any time you're in a bustling place, you reach for your knee guillotine. Imagine, now, the graveyard of shellfish legs that lies before you. Ahh, no more bustle… Also, that's dinner sorted.

meaning_mnemonic: Members of the <radical>tongue</radical> <radical>clan</radical> like to do one thing: <kanji>lick</kanji>. They go around licking everything to mark their tongue clan territory, you know? They even lick each other on the cheek by way of greeting.
reading_mnemonic: Unfortunately, the tongue clan members also <kanji>lick</kanji> your <reading>na</reading>chos (な). You'd been saving those nachos! They were your travel rations. Now each and every nacho has been licked clean.
meaning_hint: Imagine encountering the tongue clan in your travels and being greeted by a whole village of them. It's kinda gross at first, with your cheeks getting all slick from the saliva, but after a while you get used to it.
reading_hint: Seems this tongue clan only likes food that can be licked up, like nacho cheese and salsa. They lap up all the good stuff, so all that's left for you are some soggy, pale, naked nachos.

meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> in <radical>boot</radical>s has a <kanji>slight</kanji> edge over one without boots. It's not much, but even <kanji>a little</kanji> advantage is better than nothing.
reading_mnemonic: Getting that <kanji>slight</kanji> edge over other leaders allows your leader to become the <reading>kin</reading>g (きん).
meaning_hint: Imagine your leader wearing knee-high leather boots. Wouldn't you feel a little more confident in a leader with boots like that?
reading_hint: Your leader must've already been on the verge of becoming king if the slight advantage from those boots was enough to tip the scales.

meaning_mnemonic: What might a <radical>leader</radical> <radical>say</radical> to <radical>someone</radical> to make little <radical>drop</radical>s of sweat appear on their brow? "Go <kanji>make money</kanji> for me." When a leader entrusts someone with carrying out their latest money-making plan, that person's gonna feel a lot of pressure to succeed.
reading_mnemonic: So, what's your leader's grand plan to <kanji>make money</kanji>? Having you <reading>mow</reading> (もう) lawns, of course. Few money-making schemes are more tried-and-true than the ol' "mow your neighbor's lawn for twenty bucks" approach. Grass grows back forever, so it's basically nature's cash cow — moooow, get it?
meaning_hint: Imagine you're that someone: feel the drops of sweat rolling down your brow as your leader explains their money-making plan to you. It's a good plan — you just hope you can carry it out properly and bring back some money for your leader! (Note that the drop radical can be dropped sometimes — that is, it's fairly common for it to be omitted in handwriting or in certain fonts.)
reading_hint: You do a great job and pretty soon you're mowing lawns all over town, raking in profits hand over fist. Before you know it, you've built a full-blown money-making mow-nopoly, and your leader couldn't be prouder. Guess you didn't need to be so nervous about succeeding after all, huh?

meaning_mnemonic: Try not to let your <radical>mouth</radical> get <radical>droopy</radical> — you'll dribble <kanji>saliva</kanji> everywhere.
reading_mnemonic: If you need an outlet for all your extra <kanji>saliva</kanji>, try playing the <reading>tuba</reading> (つば). A tuba's even got a nifty little <kanji>spit</kanji> valve at the bottom, so you can fill it with saliva to your heart's content and then drain it all out when you're done.
meaning_hint: If you feel spit starting to dribble down your chin, it probably means you've let your mouth get all droopy again. Come on — flex those face muscles and get it together!
reading_hint: Imagine opening the spit valve of your tuba and watching a torrent of saliva pour out of it. This is the price you pay for those mellow brassy tones.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>s love to slam into <radical>valuable</radical> things and <kanji>crush</kanji> them into oblivion. A tsunami will crush your house and <kanji>squash</kanji> your car and never even think to say sorry about it.
reading_mnemonic: A tsunami can <kanji>crush</kanji> a house or a car as though it's a <reading>tube</reading> (つぶ) of toothpaste.
meaning_hint: Imagine looking on in horror as a tsunami surges in and crushes all your most valuable possessions. The despair is absolutely… crushing.
reading_hint: Think about how easy it would be for you to squash a tube of toothpaste. That's how easy it is for a tsunami to crush your all valuable stuff.

meaning_mnemonic: Take some <radical>leather</radical> and <radical>wrap</radical> your possessions in it. Now you have a <kanji>bag</kanji> to carry them around in. Neat!
reading_mnemonic:
You actually learned this reading with the vocabulary word カバン. It's been a while though, so here's a mnemonic to help you connect it to this kanji:

The reason you made your own <kanji>bag</kanji> is that there's been a <reading>car ban</reading> (かばん). With this car ban, you can't just throw all your stuff in your car anymore (come on — we all know you were using it like a second closet) so now you need a bag to carry it around in.
meaning_hint: Picture yourself carefully wrapping leather around a bundle of your things to make this bag. Look at you, being all DIY and stuff.
reading_hint: Thanks to this car ban, you were able to establish yourself as a successful maker of bespoke leather bags. Funny how the universe works out like that sometimes.

meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Heaven</radical>'s favorite <radical>insect</radical> is the <kanji>silkworm</kanji>. Silkworms are really prized in heaven because they produce all the silk used to make those flowy white angel robes and those fluffy clouds everyone walks around on.
reading_mnemonic: Lately, the <kanji>silkworm</kanji>s have been making "must-<reading>買い coa</reading>ts (かいこ)" that all the angels are going crazy over. Regular angel robes? Those are sooo last year. If you're not rocking one of these silkworm-crafted must-買い coats, then you simply can't be part of heaven's in-crowd.
meaning_hint: Silkworms and their silky secretions are actually really core to heaven's entire aesthetic.
reading_hint: Imagine the anticipation as you wait in line to get your must-買い coat. You're so grateful to the silkworms — thanks to them, you'll finally be able to hang out with the cool angels!

meaning_mnemonic: No one is more full of <radical>life</radical> than the little <radical>man</radical> you call your <kanji>nephew</kanji>.
reading_mnemonic: Your <kanji>nephew</kanji> is so lively that you need to yell "<reading>Oi</reading> (おい)!" at him sometimes when he comes to visit. "Oi! Nephew! Stop licking the window! Oi! Quit flushing my socks down the toilet!"
meaning_hint: Imagine your nephew as a rambunctious, lively little boy. He's always running around your brother or sister's house and livening the place up with his antics.
reading_hint: Imagine your nephew doing the most rascally, annoying little-kid thing you can imagine. Now let out your annoyance with a loud "OIIIIIIIII!!!!!!!"
